<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks):** *Define the term "implementation of maritime law through national legislation." Why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer:**
Implementation of maritime law through national legislation is the process by which a State converts its international maritime treaty obligations into binding domestic law through statutes, rules, and regulations. It is necessary because:
1. International conventions are generally **not self-executing** in dualist States like India.
2. It creates **enforceable rights and duties** for shipowners, seafarers, and cargo interests.
3. It enables **penal sanctions** and the creation of **administrative machinery** for enforcement.
4. It allows **national courts** to adjudicate maritime disputes under municipal law.

> **Question 2 (3 Marks):** *Distinguish between the Monist and Dualist theories of treaty implementation. Which one does India follow?*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Monist Theory | Dualist Theory |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal System** | International and national law are unified | Two separate, distinct systems |
| **Treaty Effect** | Automatic upon ratification | Requires legislative enactment |
| **Court Application** | Courts apply treaties directly | Courts apply only transformed law |
| **Examples** | Netherlands, France, Germany | **India**, UK, Australia, Canada |

**India follows the Dualist Theory.** Therefore, every major maritime convention (e.g., SOLAS, MARPOL) requires a separate domestic statute (e.g., Merchant Shipping Act, 1958) to become enforceable.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain the various **methods of implementing international maritime law** into Indian national legislation, with appropriate statutory examples. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Critically examine the **constitutional and legislative framework** governing India's implementation of international maritime conventions. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Part A (a) — 7 Marks

India, being a **dualist State**, employs three principal methods to implement international maritime conventions:

**Method 1: Transformation (7-Mark Key Point)**
Parliament enacts a **comprehensive new statute** that re-states the international rules in domestic legal language. The convention text is **not** literally incorporated; the Indian legislature re-drafts the substance. For example, the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** is a transformed statute that domesticates SOLAS, MARPOL (provisions), Load Lines, Tonnage Measurement, and several other conventions into a single, coherent Indian code. The Bill that became the **Merchant Shipping Bill, 2024** (proposed) continues this tradition by transforming the latest IMO instruments into a modern Indian statute.

**Method 2: Incorporation (7-Mark Key Point)**
The **text of the international convention is annexed as a Schedule** to a short enabling Act, and the schedule is declared to have the force of law. For example, the **Coast Guard Act, 1978** incorporates India's enforcement duties under UNCLOS and other maritime conventions. The **Indian Ports Act, 1908** incorporates certain international port rules through schedules and references.

**Method 3: Reference (Dynamic Incorporation) (7-Mark Key Point)**
An Indian statute simply **refers to the international convention "as amended from time to time"**. This creates a *rolling* legal effect — every time the IMO amends a convention, Indian law automatically tracks the change without further parliamentary action. This is found in the **Merchant Shipping (Safety Convention) Rules** and various chapters of the Merchant Shipping Act dealing with international safety standards.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** India uses a **hybrid** of all three methods depending on the convention's subject matter, complexity, and the need for dynamic updating.

### Model Solution for Part A (b) — 7 Marks

The Indian constitutional framework for implementing international maritime conventions rests on a robust foundation:

**Constitutional Foundation:**
- **Article 51(c)** of the Constitution of India directs the State to *foster respect for international law and treaty obligations* — a Directive Principle guiding maritime implementation.
- **Article 253** empowers **Parliament** to make laws implementing treaties, even on subjects otherwise in the State List. This is the *enabling clause* for maritime legislation.
- **Schedule VII, Union List, Entry 40** places *"shipping and navigation on inland waterways, railways and air routes, and national waterways in so far as regards shipping and navigation on those waterways"* under the **exclusive domain of the Union**. Maritime law is therefore **centrally legislated**, ensuring uniform implementation across all coastal States (including Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Maharashtra, Gujarat, West Bengal, etc.).

**Legislative Acts Implementing Maritime Conventions:**
1. **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** — implements SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, Tonnage, STCW, MLC.
2. **Maritime Zones of India Act, 1976** — implements UNCLOS provisions on territorial waters, EEZ, continental shelf.
3. **Coast Guard Act, 1978** — establishes the ICG to enforce maritime law in Indian zones.
4. **Civil Liability for Bunker Oil Pollution Damage Act, 2017** — implements the Bunker Convention, 2001.
5. **Admiralty (Jurisdiction and Settlement of Maritime Claims) Act, 2017** — provides for specialized High Court jurisdiction over maritime disputes.
6. **Indian Ports Act, 1908** — port-level implementation of international port rules.

**Critical Evaluation:**
While the framework is comprehensive, challenges include:
- **Time-lag** between IMO adoption and Indian implementation (sometimes 5–10 years).
- **Capacity constraints** in enforcement (limited ICG and DGS personnel).
- **Delegated legislation overload** — many conventions are implemented through Rules rather than Acts, weakening parliamentary oversight.
- **Need for periodic updating** of old statutes like the Indian Ports Act, 1908.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ratification with implementation** — Signing/ratifying a treaty is *not* the same as implementing it. Marks are deducted for treating them as identical.
> 2. **Forgetting the Constitutional basis** — Article 253 is *mandatory* to mention in any answer on Indian maritime implementation.
> 3. **Not naming the statute** — Generic statements like "India has a law on shipping" score zero. Always name: *Merchant Shipping Act, 1958; Coast Guard Act, 1978; Admiralty Act, 2017*, etc.
> 4. **Ignoring methods** — A 7-mark question on methods *requires* all three (transformation, incorporation, reference) with **at least one example each**.
> 5. **Conflating UNCLOS with SOLAS** — UNCLOS is the framework Law of the Sea treaty; SOLAS is a specific safety convention. They are different.

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, June 2025 Pattern]**
> **(a)** Trace the **step-by-step process** by which an international maritime convention becomes enforceable Indian law. Illustrate with the example of the **Bunker Convention, 2001** and the **Civil Liability for Bunker Oil Pollution Damage Act, 2017**. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the role of **Flag State, Port State, and Coastal State jurisdictions** in compelling national maritime legislation, with reference to the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Part B (a) — 7 Marks

The conversion of an international maritime convention into enforceable Indian municipal law follows a structured **10-step process** (elaborated in Section 3.1). For the **Bunker Convention, 2001 → Indian Act, 2017**, the steps are:

1. **International Need**: The *Erika* (1999, France) and *Prestige* (2002, Spain) spills revealed that ships' bunker fuel could cause catastrophic pollution, but the existing **Civil Liability Convention, 1969** and **Fund Convention, 1971** covered only persistent oils, not bunker fuel.

2. **Adoption**: The IMO Diplomatic Conference adopted the **International Convention on Civil Liability for Bunker Oil Pollution Damage** on **23 March 2001**.

3. **Domestic Consultation**: India's MoPSW, MEA, DGS, and Ministry of Law examined the Convention and identified that the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** and **Indian Ports Act, 1908** did not impose strict liability on shipowners for bunker pollution.

4. **Cabinet Approval**: The Union Cabinet approved India's signing and ratification.

5. **Signature and Ratification**: India signed the Convention in 2007 and ratified it subsequently.

6. **Drafting of the Bill**: The MoPSW drafted the **Civil Liability for Bunker Oil Pollution Damage Bill, 2017**.

7. **Parliamentary Passage**: The Bill was passed by Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha under **Article 253** of the Constitution.

8. **Presidential Assent**: The President assented to the Bill, and it was notified as the **Civil Liability for Bunker Oil Pollution Damage Act, 2017 (Act No. 14 of 2017)** in the Gazette of India.

9. **Subordinate Legislation**: The Central Government framed implementing Rules.

10. **Enforcement and Adjudication**: The DGS enforces the law; **High Courts** exercise **Admiralty Jurisdiction** under the **Admiralty Act, 2017** to adjudicate claims.

**Liability Formula (1 Mark bonus):** $L_{owner} = \min(C_{actual}, L_{LLMC})$.

### Model Solution for Part B (b) — 7 Marks

**UNCLOS, 1982** establishes three distinct jurisdictional bases that **compel** national maritime legislation:

**Flag State Jurisdiction (UNCLOS Articles 91–98):**
Every State has the right to sail ships under its flag (Article 91) and the **duty** to exercise jurisdiction and control over such ships on the high seas (Article 94). This **compels Flag States** to enact national laws implementing SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, STCW, and COLREG for vessels registered under their flag. For India, this is implemented through the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958**, the **Merchant Shipping (Standards of Training) Rules**, and the **Indian Register of Shipping** for ship registration.

**Port State Jurisdiction (UNCLOS Article 25 + 217–220):**
A port State has the right to enforce international rules on **foreign ships** that voluntarily enter its ports, particularly regarding safety, labour, and pollution (Article 217). This compels States to legislate port-state control (PSC) regimes. India's implementation is through the **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** read with **DGS port-state control inspections** and the **Coast Guard Act, 1978** for enforcement.

**Coastal State Jurisdiction (UNCLOS Articles 2–32, 55–75):**
A coastal State has sovereignty over its **territorial sea (12 nm)** and **sovereign rights** in its **Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ, 200 nm)** and over its **continental shelf**. This compels legislation on navigation, resource management, environmental protection, and EEZ policing. India implements this through:
- **Maritime Zones of India Act, 1976**
- **Territorial Waters, Continental Shelf, EEZ and Other Maritime Zones Act, 1976**
- **Coast Guard Act, 1978**
- **Maritime Zones of India (Regulation of Fishing by Foreign Vessels) Act, 1981**

**Synthesis (1 Mark):** Together, these three jurisdictional pillars create a **comprehensive obligation** on India to legislate on every aspect of maritime activity — whether the ship is on the high seas (flag), in an Indian port (port), or in Indian waters (coastal). The **UNCLOS** is the constitutional charter of the oceans, and national legislation is its *operational code*.

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **High-Loss Areas in This Topic — Examiner's Observations:**
> 1. **Treating the Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 as one big statute** — It actually **transforms multiple conventions**; recognizing this nuance scores higher marks.
> 2. **Not mentioning Article 253** — Almost every KTU question on this topic expects a reference to the constitutional provision.
> 3. **Skipping the dualist theory** — This is the *theoretical foundation*; skipping it is a 3-mark penalty.
> 4. **Missing the Admiralty Act, 2017** — A relatively new statute that KTU examiners love to test as it affects which High Court has jurisdiction.
> 5. **No mention of enforcement agencies** (DGS, ICG) — Implementation is meaningless without enforcement; always name at least two agencies.
> 6. **Treating UNCLOS as the only source** — UNCLOS is the *framework* convention; SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, etc. are *specialist* conventions. Both are needed.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **India is a Dualist State** — international maritime conventions are *not* automatically law; they require domestic legislation.
- ☐ **Article 253** of the Indian Constitution is the *constitutional doorway* for implementing treaties in maritime matters.
- ☐ **Article 51(c)** provides the directive principle to respect international law.
- ☐ **Schedule VII, Union List, Entry 40** makes maritime law a **central** (Union) subject.
- ☐ **Three Methods of Implementation**: (1) Transformation, (2) Incorporation as a Schedule, (3) Reference (dynamic).
- ☐ **Three Jurisdictional Pillars**: Flag State, Port State, Coastal State (all under UNCLOS, 1982).
- ☐ **Key Indian Statutes**:
  - Merchant Shipping Act, **1958** (proposed replacement: Merchant Shipping Bill, **2024**)
  - Indian Ports Act, **1908**
  - Coast Guard Act, **1978**
  - Maritime Zones Act, **1976**
  - Admiralty (Jurisdiction and Settlement of Maritime Claims) Act, **2017**
  - Civil Liability for Bunker Oil Pollution Damage Act, **2017**
- ☐ **Key International Conventions**:
  - **UNCLOS** (1982) — Law of the Sea framework
  - **SOLAS** (1974) — Safety of Life at Sea
  - **MARPOL** (73/78) — Marine Pollution
  - **STCW** (1978) — Training and Certification
  - **MLC** (2006) — Maritime Labour
  - **COLREG** (1972) — Collision Regulations
  - **Bunker Convention** (2001) — Bunker oil pollution liability
  - **LLMC** (1976/1996) — Limitation of Liability
- ☐ **Enforcement Agencies**: Directorate General of Shipping (DGS), Indian Coast Guard (ICG), Mercantile Marine Departments, High Courts (Admiralty Jurisdiction).
- ☐ **The 10-Step Conversion Process** (from international need to enforceable law) — memorize for the 7-mark sub-question.
- ☐ **The liability formula**: $L_{owner} = \min(C_{actual\_damage}, L_{LLMC\_cap})$ for the Bunker Convention context.
- ☐ **The Bunker Convention example** is the most commonly cited KTU case study for tracing implementation.
- ☐ **The 2017 Admiralty Act** designates specific High Courts (Bombay, Madras, Kerala, Karnataka, Calcutta, Gujarat, Delhi, etc.) as **Admiralty Courts** — relevant for jurisdiction questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of United Nations (UN), International Maritime Organization (IMO) and International Labour Organization (ILO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Role of United Nations (UN), International Maritime Organization (IMO) and International Labour Organization (ILO) # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The United Nations (UN) and Maritime Governance ### Formal Definition The **United Nations (UN)** is the pre-eminent global intergovernmental organisation founded on **24 October 1945** by **51 original Member States**, currently with **193 Member States**. In the maritime domain, the UN functions as the overarching political and legal umbrella under which specialised agencies regulate ocean governance, freedom of navigation, sustainable use of marine resources, and the equitable development of international shipping. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The UN's maritime relevance stems from three pillars — **(i)** the legal framework established by **UNCLOS 1982**, **(ii)** economic development through **UNCTAD**, and **(iii)** peacekeeping and dispute resolution via the **International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea (ITLOS)**. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The UN as the World's Parliament of the Oceans" Imagine the world's oceans as a colossal apartment complex shared by 193 families (nations). The UN acts as the **building's housing society** — it sets the master rules (UNCLOS), appoints specialised sub-committees for plumbing (IMO), electrical safety (ILO), and dispute handling (ITLOS), and ensures no single family occupies the lobby or corridor illegally. Without this overarching body, every country would act on its own rules, causing chaos similar to traffic without road signs. ### Key UN Bodies Relevant to Maritime Affairs | Body | Full Name | Maritime Function | |------|-----------|-------------------| | UNCLOS | UN Convention on the Law of the Sea | Constitutional charter of the oceans | | UNCTAD | UN Conference on Trade & Development | Shipping economics, liner conferences | | IMO | International Maritime Organization | Technical safety, security, prevention of pollution | | ILO | International Labour Organization | Seafarer working and living conditions | | ITLOS | International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea | Adjudication of maritime disputes | --- ## 1.2 The International Maritime Organization (IMO) ### Formal Definition The **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** is a **specialised agency of the United Nations** headquartered in **London, United Kingdom (4 Albert Embankment, London SE1 7SR)**. It was established by the **International Maritime Organization Convention** (originally signed as the **IMCO Convention**) on **6 March 1948** and came into force on **17 March 1958**. IMO's name was changed from IMCO (Inter-Governmental Maritime Consultative Organization) to **IMO in 1982**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Mandate:** "**Safe, secure and efficient shipping on cleaner oceans.**" IMO is the global standard-setting authority for the safety, security, and environmental performance of international shipping. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The IMO as the World's Ship Inspector" Think of IMO as the **international Bureau of Standards (BIS)** but for ships. When India manufactures a ship in Cochin or a tanker in Mumbai, IMO decides the thickness of steel that must be used, the colour of lifeboats, the type of fire extinguishers, how much sulphur can burn in the fuel, and even how the crew must be trained. Without IMO, ships built in Japan, registered in Panama, crewed by Indians, and sailing to Brazil would have no common technical language. ### Membership Statistics (Vital Data) - **Member States: 176** - **MoU Port State Control agreements: 9** (Paris, Tokyo, Indian Ocean, Caribbean, Mediterranean, Black Sea, Riyadh, Vina del Mar, Abuja) - **Major Conventions: 60+** (SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW, Load Lines, Tonnage, COLREG, etc.) --- ## 1.3 The International Labour Organization (ILO) ### Formal Definition The **International Labour Organization (ILO)** is the only **tripartite UN agency** — meaning it brings together **governments, employers, and workers** in its decision-making. Founded in **1919** (originally under the League of Nations) and operational from **1920**, it became the first specialised agency of the UN in **1946**. Its headquarters is in **Geneva, Switzerland**. In the maritime sphere, ILO's defining instrument is the **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006**, often called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights."** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Tripartite Structure:** Every ILO convention is shaped by **three voices** — 2 Government delegates, 1 Employer delegate, 1 Worker delegate per Member State — making ILO uniquely representative. ### Conceptual Analogy — "The ILO as the Seafarer's Trade Union Treaty" If IMO designs the ship and its navigation, ILO looks after the **human beings** who sail it. ILO is the equivalent of a globally-binding **HR Policy Manual** for the world's seafarers — specifying minimum wages, maximum working hours, leave entitlements, medical care, repatriation rights, and even standards for the food served in the ship's galley. Without ILO, a Filipino rating and a Greek officer might work under radically different and exploitative conditions. ### ILO Maritime Labour Convention (MLC 2006) — Key Stats - **Entered into force: 20 August 2013** (after ratification by **30 Member States representing 33% of world gross tonnage**) - **Current ratifying countries: 100+** (covering approximately **96% of world gross tonnage**) - **Amended in 2022** (Amendments effective **23 December 2024**) - Contains **5 Titles and 2 Annexes** plus **Guidelines** # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The UN's Three-Pillar Maritime Strategy The UN influences shipping through **three inter-locking mechanisms**: ### Pillar 1 — Legal & Constitutional (UNCLOS) - Adopted on **10 December 1982** (Montego Bay, Jamaica) - Entered into force on **16 November 1994** - Currently **168 parties** (including the EU) - Often called the **"Constitution for the Oceans"** - Establishes 7 maritime zones (Territorial Sea, EEZ, Continental Shelf, High Seas, etc.) ### Pillar 2 — Economic & Developmental (UNCTAD) - Established **30 December 1964** - Functions as the UN's focal point on trade, finance, and shipping economics - Critical for **developing maritime nations** like India, Brazil, Nigeria ### Pillar 3 — Dispute Settlement (ITLOS) - Established under **UNCLOS Annex VI** - Located in **Hamburg, Germany** - Operational since **1996** - Has **21 judges** elected for **9-year terms** ## 2.2 IMO's Regulatory Architecture — "How IMO Rules are Made" The IMO operates on a **committee-based consensus model**. The workflow is: 1. **Member State or Secretariat** identifies a maritime safety/security/environmental issue. 2. **Proposal** is tabled at the **Council** (40 Member States, 3 categories). 3. **Referral** to one of the **5 main committees**: - **MSC** — Maritime Safety Committee - **MEPC** — Marine Environment Protection Committee - **LEG** — Legal Committee - **TC** — Technical Co-operation Committee - **FAL** — Facilitation Committee 4. **Drafting** of Convention/Solution/Recommendation. 5. **Adoption** at the **Assembly** (all 176 Member States, biennial). 6. **Entry into force** after ratification threshold (typically countries representing a stated % of world tonnage). > [!NOTE] > **Why IMO standards are universally obeyed:** Because **85%+ of world trade by volume moves by sea**, almost every country depends on safe and environmentally sound shipping. Refusing to comply means a country's ships can be detained in any port worldwide under Port State Control. ## 2.3 ILO's Maritime Tripartite Plus One Strategy Unlike IMO (purely governmental), ILO's tripartite structure ensures that **workers and shipowners have an equal voice with governments** when framing maritime labour standards. The structure: | Level | Composition | |-------|-------------| | **International Labour Conference (ILC)** | Annual plenary — all Member States | | **Governing Body** | 56 members (28 Government, 14 Employer, 14 Worker) | | **Specialised Tripartite Maritime Committee** | Sets MLC amendments | | **National Action | National Focal Points** | Implement MLC domestically | ## 2.4 KTU Cheat Sheet — Key Conventions & Frameworks | # | Convention / Body | Year | Domain | Key Principle | |---|-------------------|------|--------|---------------| | 1 | **UN Charter** | 1945 | Global peace & law | Sovereign equality of states | | 2 | **UNCLOS** | 1982 | Law of the Sea | Freedom of navigation, EEZ | | 3 | **IMCO/IMO Convention** | 1948 | Maritime safety & environment | Safe, secure, efficient, clean | | 4 | **SOLAS** | 1974 | Ship construction & safety | "Lifeboats and lifejackets" | | 5 | **MARPOL** | 73/78 | Pollution prevention | Annex VI caps sulphur at 0.50% (from 2020) | | 6 | **STCW** | 1978 (revised 2010) | Crew training | Watchkeeping & certification | | 7 | **MLC 2006** | 2006 | Seafarer welfare | Minimum age 16, repatriation rights | | 8 | **ILO Constitution** | 1919 | Labour rights | Tripartism (Govt + Employer + Worker) | | 9 | **ILO Convention 147** | 1976 | Minimum standards for ships | Superseded partly by MLC | | 10 | **Ballast Water Convention** | 2004 | Invasive species | BWM Treatment Systems | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Shipping Management - For a **ship manager in Mumbai**, compliance with **SOLAS + MARPOL + MLC** is non-negotiable; failure means port state detention. - For a **shipowner in Liberia (open registry)**, **flag state compliance** with IMO standards is the price of doing business. - For **port authorities in India**, **FAL Convention** simplifies ship-shore interface and documentation. - For **seafarer welfare officers**, **MLC Regulation 4.3** mandates occupational safety and accident prevention. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Detailed Comparison ## 3.1 The UN Maritime Ecosystem — Detailed Structural Breakdown Since this is a legal/management topic rather than a numerical one, we replace algebraic derivations with **analytical reasoning frameworks**. Each step below is a "derivation" of how the international maritime regulatory pyramid is constructed. ### Step 1: The Apex — United Nations General Assembly (UNGA) **Logic:** The UNGA is the principal deliberative organ where all 193 Member States meet. **Reason:** It provides the **political legitimacy** for ocean governance through annual resolutions on oceans and the law of the sea (e.g., **UNGA Resolution 2749 (XXV)** — the "Seabed Resources for Mankind"). ### Step 2: Treaty-Making at UNCLOS **Logic:** UNCLOS was drafted over **11 years (1973–1982)** through **11 sessions of the Third UN Conference on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS III)**. **Reason:** Multilateral treaty-making requires sustained diplomatic engagement; UNCLOS III set the template for subsequent global conventions. ### Step 3: Operational Implementation by UNCTAD **Logic:** UNCTAD established the **Committee on Shipping** and manages the **Liner Code (UNCTAD Code of Conduct for Liner Conferences, 1974)**. **Reason:** Maritime trade economics must be balanced with the political interest of developing coastal states. The **40-40-20 rule** (40% cargo allocation to importing-exporting national lines, 40% to third-country lines, 20% to cross-traders) is a classic UNCTAD outcome. ### Step 4: Dispute Resolution at ITLOS **Logic:** ITLOS handles **compulsory procedures** when parties cannot resolve maritime boundary disputes under UNCLOS. **Reason:** Sovereignty over EEZ and continental shelf is worth trillions of dollars in oil, gas, and fisheries; an impartial tribunal is essential. ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — UN vs. IMO vs. ILO | Dimension | UN | IMO | ILO | |-----------|----|----|-----| | **Type** | Universal political body | Specialized UN agency | Specialized UN agency | | **Founded** | 1945 | 1948 (as IMCO) | 1919 (League era) | | **Headquarters** | New York, USA | London, UK | Geneva, Switzerland | | **Members** | 193 | 176 | 187 | | **Focus in shipping** | Law of the Sea, peace | Technical safety, security, environment | Working conditions of seafarers | | **Key Maritime Instrument** | UNCLOS 1982 | SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW | MLC 2006 | | **Decision-making** | Sovereign equality (1 state = 1 vote) | Weighted by tonnage + states | Tripartite (govt/employer/worker) | | **Enforcement Mechanism** | ICJ, ITLOS, UNSC | Port State Control, Flag State | Flag State + Maritime Labour Cert. | | **Subject Matter** | Ocean as a whole | The ship | The seafarer | ## 3.3 The IMO Standard-Setting Process — Logical Derivation For a maritime topic such as the **2020 sulphur cap (0.50% m/m)** in MARPOL Annex VI, the following analytical derivation shows how IMO functions: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Step 1: Scientific evidence} \rightarrow \text{IMO commissioned study on ship air pollution} \\ &\text{Step 2: MEPC debate} \rightarrow \text{Proposed cap reduction from 3.50\% to 0.50\%} \\ &\text{Step 3: Committee vote} \rightarrow \text{Adopted as Resolution MEPC.176(58) on 15 July 2008} \\ &\text{Step 4: Adoption at Assembly} \rightarrow \text{Entered into force on 1 January 2020} \\ &\text{Step 5: Global enforcement} \rightarrow \text{Port State Control inspections worldwide} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion logic:** Each step moved the policy from **theory → MEPC technical review → binding regulation → global implementation**. ## 3.4 The ILO MLC 2006 — Structural Derivation The MLC 2006 is the **fourth pillar** of international maritime regulation (along with SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW) and is analytically derived as follows: $$ \begin{aligned} &\textbf{Title 1:} \quad \text{Minimum requirements for seafarers to work on a ship} \\ &\textbf{Title 2:} \quad \text{Conditions of employment (wages, hours, leave)} \\ &\textbf{Title 3:} \quad \text{Accommodation, food, recreational facilities} \\ &\textbf{Title 4:} \quad \text{Health protection, medical care, social security} \\ &\textbf{Title 5:} \quad \text{Compliance and enforcement (flag + port state)} \end{aligned} $$ **Why this structure works:** It mirrors the **MECD Framework** — Minimum Entitlements, Conditions, Dignity, Care, Enforcement — making MLC the comprehensive seafarer rights instrument. ## 3.5 Inter-Linkage Map — How the Three Bodies Work Together The analytical "equation" of global maritime governance can be expressed as: $$ \text{Effective Maritime Governance} = f(\text{UN Legal Framework}) + g(\text{IMO Technical Standards}) + h(\text{ILO Labour Standards}) $$ Where: - $f$ represents **UNCLOS providing the constitutional ocean law**. - $g$ represents **SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW setting ship standards**. - $h$ represents **MLC setting human standards**. The **interaction effect** ($f \times g \times h$) ensures that no single dimension — law, ship, or person — is regulated in isolation. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 UN Maritime Governance — Hierarchical Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A[UN General Assembly
193 Members] --> B[UN Security Council] A --> C[ECOSOC
Economic and Social Council] A --> D[International Court of Justice] A --> E[UN Specialized Agencies] C --> F[UNCTAD
Maritime Trade Economics] E --> G[IMO
Safety, Security, Environment] E --> H[ILO
Seafarer Labour Standards] A --> I[UNCLOS
Law of the Sea] I --> J[ITLOS
Hamburg] I --> K[Continental Shelf Commission] I --> L[International Seabed Authority
Kingston, Jamaica] ``` ## 4.2 IMO Organizational Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A[IMO Assembly
176 Member States
Biennial] --> B[IMO Council
40 Member States
Category A B C] A --> C[Maritime Safety Committee MSC] A --> D[Marine Environment Protection MEPC] A --> E[Legal Committee LEG] A --> F[Technical Cooperation TC] A --> G[Facilitation Committee FAL] B --> H[Sub-Committees] C --> H D --> H H --> H1[Sub-Committee on Pollution Prevention] H --> H2[Sub-Committee on Human Element Training] H --> H3[Sub-Committee on Navigation] H --> H4[Sub-Committee on Ship Design] ``` ## 4.3 ILO Tripartite Structure ```mermaid graph TD A[International Labour Conference ILC
Annual Plenary] --> B[Governing Body
56 Members] A --> C[Specialized Tripartite Maritime Committee] B --> D[Governments
28 Seats] B --> E[Employers
14 Seats] B --> F[Workers
14 Seats] C --> D C --> E C --> F G[ILO Geneva HQ] --> A H[Regional Offices] --> G I[National Focal Points in 187 Countries] --> H ``` ## 4.4 The Maritime Regulatory Pyramid ```mermaid graph TD A[UN Charter 1945
Apex - Political Mandate] --> B[UNCLOS 1982
Constitution of the Oceans] B --> C[Maritime Conventions
SOLAS MARPOL STCW] B --> D[Maritime Labour Convention MLC 2006] C --> E[Flag State Implementation] D --> E C --> F[Port State Control
9 MoUs Worldwide] D --> F C --> G[Recognized Organizations
Classification Societies] D --> G E --> H[Ship Operators and Seafarers] F --> H G --> H ``` ## 4.5 Inter-Agency Workflow for a Maritime Incident ```mermaid graph LR A[Maritime Incident Occurs] --> B{Type of Issue?} B -->|Safety/Environment| C[IMO MSC / MEPC] B -->|Seafarer Welfare| D[ILO Special Tripartite Committee] B -->|Legal Dispute| E[ITLOS or Arbitration] C --> F[Amendments to Conventions] D --> F E --> G[Binding Judgment] F --> H[Flag State Implementation] G --> H H --> I[Shipowner Compliance] I --> J[Port State Verification] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the International Maritime Organization (IMO) and state its main objectives. **Model Answer:** The International Maritime Organization (IMO) is a specialised agency of the United Nations established by the **International Maritime Organization Convention** on **6 March 1948** (came into force on **17 March 1958**), headquartered in **London, UK**. Originally called **IMCO (Inter-Governmental Maritime Consultative Organization)**, it was renamed IMO in **1982**. **Main Objectives:** - To provide **machinery for co-operation** among governments in the field of governmental regulation and practices related to technical matters affecting shipping engaged in international trade. - To encourage the **highest practicable standards** in matters concerning maritime safety, efficiency of navigation, and prevention and control of marine pollution from ships. - To deal with **legal matters** related to international shipping. `[KTU University Exam - Model Q1 | CO1, Remembering & Understanding]` --- ### Question 2: What is the Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006 and why is it called the "Seafarers' Bill of Rights"? **Model Answer:** The **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006** was adopted at the **94th Session of the International Labour Conference** in Geneva on **23 February 2006** and entered into force on **20 August 2013**. It is often called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights"** because it consolidates and updates more than **68 existing ILO maritime labour conventions and recommendations** into a single, comprehensive, enforceable legal instrument. It establishes **minimum working and living standards** for all seafarers worldwide, covering working hours, wages, leave, accommodation, food, health, safety, and social security. `[KTU University Exam - Model Q2 | CO1, Understanding]` --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **(a)** Discuss in detail the role of the **United Nations (UN)** in international maritime affairs. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The UN plays a **four-fold role** in maritime affairs: 1. **Legal Framework via UNCLOS 1982:** The UN sponsored the **Third United Nations Conference on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS III)** which adopted the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)** on **10 December 1982** in Montego Bay, Jamaica. UNCLOS provides the comprehensive legal order for the seas and oceans, defining maritime zones (territorial sea, EEZ, continental shelf, high seas) and the rights/duties of states. It is the **"Constitution for the Oceans"** and has been ratified by **168 parties**. 2. **Economic Development via UNCTAD:** The **UN Conference on Trade and Development (UNCTAD)** established in **1964** addresses the maritime trade interests of developing countries. It formulated the **UNCTAD Code of Conduct for Liner Conferences (1974)** with its famous **40-40-20 cargo-sharing formula** and continues to facilitate fair competition between maritime nations. 3. **Dispute Settlement via ITLOS:** The **International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea (ITLOS)** in **Hamburg, Germany**, operational since **1996**, adjudicates disputes arising out of the interpretation and application of UNCLOS. 4. **Specialised Agencies (IMO, ILO):** The UN oversees two specialised agencies — **IMO (maritime safety)** and **ILO (maritime labour)** — which produce binding conventions on shipping. **(b)** Explain the structure and functions of the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Structure of IMO:** - **Assembly** — Supreme body, all 176 Member States, meets biennially. - **Council** — 40 Member States, divided into 3 categories: Category A (10 largest shipping nations), Category B (10 largest trading nations), Category C (20 states with special interests in maritime transport). - **Main Committees:** - **MSC** — Maritime Safety Committee - **MEPC** — Marine Environment Protection Committee - **LEG** — Legal Committee - **FAL** — Facilitation Committee - **TC** — Technical Co-operation Committee - **Sub-Committees** reporting to MSC and MEPC on technical issues. - **Secretariat** headed by a **Secretary-General**. **Functions:** 1. **Regulatory Function:** Adopt conventions (e.g., SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW) and resolutions. 2. **Advisory Function:** Issue recommendations and guidelines. 3. **Co-ordinating Function:** Facilitate international co-operation on maritime matters. 4. **Technical Co-operation:** Provide assistance to developing states (e.g., Indian Ocean MoU training). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only about IMO's headquarters and date but **forget to explain the committee structure**. For **7-mark questions**, you must describe **at least 3 committees** and the **Council's composition**. Vague answers score only 3–4 marks. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice) **(a)** Critically analyse the role of the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** in protecting seafarers' rights. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The ILO plays a **crucial role** in protecting seafarers through the following mechanisms: 1. **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006:** Codifies minimum standards for seafarers, including: - **Minimum age (16)** for shipboard work. - **Medical certificate** requirements. - **Repatriation rights** at shipowner's cost. - **Maximum 14 hours work / 72 hours rest per week**. - **Minimum wage standards**. 2. **Tripartite Structure:** Unlike IMO, ILO ensures that **workers and employers participate equally** with governments in framing labour standards, giving seafarers a voice. 3. **Enforcement via Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC):** Every ship above **500 GT** engaged in international voyages must carry a valid **Maritime Labour Certificate** and **Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC)**. 4. **Prior ILO Maritime Conventions:** Before MLC, ILO had conventions on seafarers such as **C147 (Minimum Standards in Merchant Ships)** and **C185 (Seafarers' Identity Documents)**. 5. **Complaints and Monitoring:** The **ILO Committee on Freedom of Association** and **Committee of Experts on the Application of Conventions and Recommendations (CEACR)** monitor implementation. **(b)** Compare and contrast the roles of **IMO and ILO** in maritime regulation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Basis | IMO | ILO | |-------|-----|-----| | **Subject Matter** | The ship — its design, safety, pollution prevention | The seafarer — working and living conditions | | **Major Conventions** | SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW | MLC 2006 | | **Decision-making** | Government-only | Tripartite (Govt, Employer, Worker) | | **HQ** | London, UK | Geneva, Switzerland | | **Year** | 1948 | 1919 | | **Compliance Certificate** | SOLAS Safety Construction Cert., MARPOL Cert. | Maritime Labour Certificate | | **Inspection Regime** | Port State Control MoUs (9 worldwide) | Flag State inspections under MLC Title 5 | **Similarities:** - Both are **specialised agencies of the UN**. - Both produce **binding international conventions**. - Both rely on **flag state and port state enforcement**. - Together with SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW and MLC, they form the **"four pillars"** of international maritime regulation. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse **IMO's role (safety/security/environment)** with **ILO's role (labour/welfare)**. Many students mix these up. Also remember the **fourth pillar** — the **STCW Convention** on training is under **IMO**, not ILO. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **UN** is the **apex body** with **193 members**; provides the **legal umbrella** via **UNCLOS 1982**. - ✅ **IMO** is a **specialised UN agency** in **London**; sets **technical standards** for shipping (SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW). - ✅ **ILO** is the **tripartite UN agency** in **Geneva**; protects **seafarer rights** via **MLC 2006**. - ✅ **UNCLOS** is the **constitution of the oceans** with **168 parties**. - ✅ **IMO Council** has **40 members** in **3 categories**. - ✅ **MLC 2006** entered into force on **20 August 2013** and is the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights."** - ✅ **ITLOS** is in **Hamburg, Germany**, with **21 judges**. - ✅ **UNCTAD's 40-40-20 rule** is a classic shipping economics formula. - ✅ **The Four Pillars of Maritime Regulation** = SOLAS + MARPOL + STCW + MLC. - ✅ **Sulphur cap of 0.50%** under MARPOL Annex VI entered into force on **1 January 2020**.

topics.title: Role of flag state and port state in implementation of law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Role of flag state and port state in implementation of law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

Under the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, 1982 (UNCLOS)**, the implementation of international maritime law is fundamentally distributed among three principal jurisdictional actors: the **Flag State**, the **Port State**, and the **Coastal State**. Each actor derives its authority from a distinct legal nexus — nationality of the vessel, geographic location of the port, and sovereignty over maritime zones respectively.

**Flag State** is the State whose flag a vessel is entitled to fly, and in whose register the ship is enrolled. Under **Article 91 of UNCLOS**, every State has the right to fix the conditions for the grant of its nationality to ships, and the right to sail ships flying its flag on the high seas. The Flag State is therefore the **primary enforcer** of maritime law on its vessels, wherever they may be in the world.

**Port State** is the State in whose internal waters or ports a foreign vessel is located. Under customary international law and codified in **UNCLOS Articles 217, 218, 219, 220, and 226**, the Port State enjoys prescriptive and enforcement jurisdiction in defined circumstances, particularly with respect to **safety, pollution prevention, and labor conditions** on visiting foreign ships.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 examines how the seemingly borderless maritime industry is regulated through a layered jurisdictional architecture. Memorise the distinction between **Prescriptive Jurisdiction** (law-making power) and **Enforcement Jurisdiction** (power to compel compliance).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **international road system** where cars (ships) carry licence plates issued by one country (Flag State), travel through neighbourhoods belonging to other countries (Coastal States), and occasionally pull into parking lots (Port States).

- The **Flag State** is like the **DMV that issued the licence plate** — it must ensure the car is roadworthy, the driver is qualified, and the vehicle meets emission standards, no matter where the car drives.
- The **Port State** is like a **parking lot attendant** — when a foreign car parks in their lot, they have the right to inspect it, but only for specific things (noise, fumes, parking violations) — they cannot dictate how the car is built back in its home country.
- The **Coastal State** is like the **homeowners' association** along the street — they control the road in front of their house (territorial sea), can ask you to slow down (innocent passage), and have limited right to inspect if you're being too noisy or polluting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two regulators?** Because ships spend 80%+ of their time in waters far from their flag. Without Port State oversight, substandard "flag of convenience" vessels could escape accountability forever. The Flag-Port cooperation closes this **regulatory loophole**.

### 1.3 Standard Maritime Metrics to Remember

| Metric | Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Breadth of Territorial Sea | **12 nautical miles** | UNCLOS Art. 3 |
| Contiguous Zone | **24 nautical miles** (from baselines) | UNCLOS Art. 33 |
| Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) | **200 nautical miles** | UNCLOS Art. 55 |
| High Seas | Beyond all jurisdictional zones | UNCLOS Art. 86 |
| 1 Nautical Mile | **1,852 metres** | IMO standard |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 The Flag State — Operational Breakdown

A Flag State's role is anchored in **UNCLOS Article 94**, often called the "Flag State Duties Charter." It prescribes the following operational obligations:

**Step 1 — Assumption of Jurisdiction (Sovereignty Linkage)**
- The State must register the ship in its **nationality register** (UNCLOS Art. 91).
- There must be a **genuine link** between the State and the ship (Art. 91(1)) — though in practice, this is a *debated* principle; the IMO has not enforced it strictly.

**Step 2 — Administrative Compliance**
- Flag State must ensure the ship is **commanded by a properly qualified master and officer** (STCW Convention compliance).
- Crew must be **adequately trained, certified, and familiarised**.
- Labour conditions must conform to **Maritime Labour Convention, 2006 (MLC)** standards.

**Step 3 — Technical Compliance**
- Ship construction, equipment, and **seaworthiness** must meet **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea)** standards.
- Load lines must comply with the **International Convention on Load Lines, 1966**.
- Tonnage measurement must follow **ITC 1969**.

**Step 4 — Pollution Prevention (MARPOL)**
- The ship must comply with **MARPOL 73/78** — oil, noxious liquids, packaged harmful substances, sewage, garbage, air pollution (Annex VI).

**Step 5 — Survey and Certification**
- The Flag State must arrange for **periodic surveys** (annual, intermediate, renewal).
- It must issue **statutory certificates** — Safety Construction, Safety Equipment, Safety Radio, Minimum Safe Manning Document, etc.
- Classification societies (Lloyd's, DNV, ABS, BV) act as **Recognised Organisations (ROs)** on behalf of the Flag State.

**Step 6 — Enforcement on the High Seas**
- On the high seas, only the Flag State may exercise jurisdiction (UNCLOS Art. 92).
- Hot pursuit exception (Art. 111) — but the pursuing State must notify the Flag State.

### 2.2 The Port State — Operational Breakdown

The Port State role is a **secondary but vital layer of enforcement**, designed to catch vessels that evade Flag State supervision. The legal basis lies in:

- **UNCLOS Art. 218** — Port State enforcement of violations committed outside its waters (a major enforcement power).
- **UNCLOS Art. 220** — Port State enforcement for violations of pollution rules in the territorial sea and EEZ.
- **UNCLOS Art. 226** — Investigation of foreign vessels and limits on physical inspection.

**Operational steps performed by Port State:**

**Step 1 — Initial Documentation Check**
- Verification of flag, certificates (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, STCW, Load Line, Sanitary).
- Crew list, port documents, last port clearance.

**Step 2 — Port State Control (PSC) Inspection**
- Triggered by: targeting factor (ship age, flag, RO performance, prior detentions), or random selection.
- Conducted under **regional Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs)** — *Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian Ocean MoU, Caribbean MoU, Black Sea MoU, Mediterranean MoU, Riyadh MoU, Vina del Mar MoU, Abuja MoU*.
- Three inspection levels: **Initial, More Detailed, Expanded**.

**Step 3 — Detention Decision**
- If deficiencies are **clearly hazardous to safety, health, or environment**, the ship is **detained** until rectified.
- Detention is governed by **Procedures for Port State Control, 2023 (Resolution A.1185(33))**.

**Step 4 — Reporting and Database Entry**
- Detention, refusal of access, and deficiencies are entered into databases (e.g., **EQUASIS**, **THETIS-EU**).
- Flag State and Recognised Organisation are notified.

**Step 5 — Prosecution or Release**
- The Port State may institute **criminal proceedings** for violations of its laws committed within its waters.
- For violations outside its waters (Art. 218), the Port State may only **institute proceedings** if the Flag State has not done so within 6 months, or if the violation caused pollution damage in the Port State's waters, or the violation is of MARPOL Annex VI (air pollution) rules.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Framework / Cheat Sheet

| Element | Flag State | Port State |
|---|---|---|
| **Legal Basis** | UNCLOS Art. 91, 92, 94 | UNCLOS Art. 218, 220, 226 |
| **Jurisdictional Trigger** | Nationality of ship (flag) | Geographic location of port |
| **Scope of Authority** | Worldwide over its flagged ships | Only over ships in its ports/waters |
| **Primary Functions** | Register, certify, survey, man, legislate | Inspect, detain, prosecute (limited) |
| **Conventions Enforced** | SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, STCW, Load Lines, COLREGS | Primarily MARPOL, MLC, AFS, BWM, Bunker Convention |
| **Recognition of ROs** | Yes (issues authorisation to IACS members) | No direct role, but uses RO certificates |
| **Instruments Issued** | Statutory certificates, Minimum Safe Manning Doc | Detention notices, inspection reports |
| **Limitations** | Often weak enforcement on high seas; "flags of convenience" | Cannot board in EEZ (only with coastal state consent for some acts) |
| **Liability for Non-compliance** | State responsibility to IMO for substandard fleet | State responsibility to ensure non-discriminatory PSC |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

- **Open-Register Economies:** Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Bahamas, Malta — these are called **"Flags of Convenience" (FoCs)**. The ITF estimates roughly **60% of world tonnage** flies such flags. This phenomenon makes Port State Control indispensable in practice.
- **Class Society Linkage:** Without ROs, no Flag State could technically inspect its global fleet; Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, ClassNK handle >90% of class work.
- **Risk-Based Targeting:** Modern PSC inspections use **risk profiles** — older ships (over 20 years), certain flag combinations, and prior detention histories are weighted higher for inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** The sinking of the **MV Prestige (2002)** off Spain was a watershed moment — it revealed catastrophic Flag State (Bahamas) failure and triggered the EU's **Port State Control Directive 2009/16/EC**, strengthening Port State powers across Europe.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix: Jurisdictional Cascade

### 3.1 The "Five-Element Jurisdictional Test"

This is the analytical method the examiner will test for any maritime enforcement scenario. Apply it as a 5-step deductive chain.

**Step 1 — Identify the Vessel's Nationality (Flag)**
The nationality is established by the flag it flies and the registration documentation. This determines the **primary jurisdiction**.

**Step 2 — Identify the Location of the Incident (Maritime Zone)**
The maritime zone determines the spatial reach of Coastal State jurisdiction. Apply UNCLOS Art. 2-55 framework.

**Step 3 — Identify the Nature of the Violation (Safety / Pollution / Labour / Customs)**
Different violations trigger different conventions, and different jurisdictional powers.

**Step 4 — Identify Which Convention/Rule is Engaged**
SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, STCW, Load Lines, COLREGS, SUA, etc.

**Step 5 — Allocate Jurisdiction using the Cascade**

Let us work this out as a step-by-step jurisdictional allocation matrix.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Jurisdiction} &= J_{\text{Flag}} + J_{\text{Coastal}} + J_{\text{Port}} \\
\text{where each } J_i &= \text{(Subject Matter)} \cap \text{(Territorial Reach)} \cap \text{(Enforcement Capacity)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Application Example — Worked Case:**
A bulk carrier flying the Liberian flag discharges oil 200 nm off the coast of India while en route to a Chinese port.

- **Liberian Authority (Flag):** Has prescriptive and enforcement jurisdiction per UNCLOS Art. 94 — but lacks physical presence on the high seas.
- **Indian Authority (Coastal EEZ):** The discharge occurred at 200 nm — at the **outer edge of India's EEZ**. MARPOL Art. 6 (now superseded by MARPOL Annex VI) and UNCLOS Art. 220 give India enforcement rights for pollution violations.
- **Chinese Authority (Port of Arrival):** Once the ship arrives in a Chinese port, China may inspect under MARPOL and prosecute per UNCLOS Art. 218 if Liberia fails to act within 6 months.

### 3.2 The Enforcement Cascade (Decision Tree)

The hierarchy of enforcement unfolds as follows:

**Step 1 — Flag State Primary Right**
The Flag State has the *first right and duty* to enforce. UNCLOS Art. 217(1) provides: *"States shall ensure compliance by vessels flying their flag..."*

**Step 2 — Coastal State Secondary Right**
In its territorial sea and EEZ, the Coastal State has concurrent jurisdiction for defined violations (pollution, customs, fiscal, immigration, sanitary — UNCLOS Art. 27-28).

**Step 3 — Port State Backstop**
The Port State may investigate and detain. UNCLOS Art. 218(1) specifies the conditions:
- Violation occurred **beyond the Port State's territorial sea**, OR
- Caused damage in Port State's waters, OR
- Violates MARPOL Annex VI (air pollution).

**Step 4 — Regional Cooperation**
Detentions and refusals of access are circulated through MoUs. The **Paris MoU** (Europe + Canada + Russia) operates the **"Targeted Inspection List"** — vessels on this list must be inspected at every port call.

**Step 5 — Universal Jurisdiction (rare)**
For piracy (UNCLOS Art. 100), slave trading, unauthorised broadcasting, drug trafficking (UN Convention on the Law of the Sea, Art. 108), any State may act.

### 3.3 Comparative Liability Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flag State Liability (FSL)} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\text{Cert Deficiency}_i \times \text{Surveillance Failure}_i) \\
\text{Port State Liability (PSL)} &= \sum_{j=1}^{m} (\text{Inspection Gap}_j \times \text{Detention Failure}_j) \\
\text{Where } n &= \text{number of unenforced Flag State obligations} \\
\text{and } m &= \text{number of inspection deficiencies} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** If FSL is high, the Flag State has failed in its primary duty and Port States must compensate by raising PSC. If PSL is high, the Port State itself is negligent in inspection.

### 3.4 Institutional Architecture of Implementation

International Maritime Organization (IMO) sets the rules. Flag States transcribe them into national law. Port States enforce them at the point of contact. Class Societies provide technical verification. The architecture is:

1. **Rule-Maker:** IMO (London Convention/Code regime)
2. **Rule-Transposer:** Flag State (national maritime authority)
3. **Rule-Applier:** Recognised Organisation / Class Society
4. **Rule-Enforcer (Primary):** Flag State inspectors
5. **Rule-Enforcer (Secondary):** Port State Control Officers (PSCOs)
6. **Rule-Auditor:** IMO Member State Audit Scheme (IMSAS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 14-mark answers**, the examiner expects you to **draw the cascade** — Flag → Coastal → Port → Universal — and identify *which convention* and *which UNCLOS article* is engaged at each step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Jurisdictional Cascade in Maritime Law Enforcement

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Vessel Incident Detected] --> B{Determine Flag State}
    B --> C[UNCLOS Art 94 - Flag State Primary Duty]
    C --> D{Flag State Capable of Enforcement?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Flag State Takes Action]
    D -- No --> F[Escalate to Backstop Layers]
    F --> G{Coastal State in whose waters?}

    G --> I{Port State in whose port?}
    I --> J[UNCLOS Art 218 / Art 220]
    J --> K[PSC Inspection by PSCO]
    K --> L{Deficiencies Hazardous?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Detain Ship - Issue Notice]
    L -- No --> N[Allow Departure with Observations]
    M --> O[Notify Flag State and RO]
    N --> P[Record in EQUASIS THETIS Database]
    E --> Q[Final Resolution - Sanction or Release]

    P --> Q
```


### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence: How a Detention Operates

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Vessel
    participant P as Port State PSCO
    participant F as Flag State
    participant R as Recognised Organisation
    V->>P: Arrives in port with certificates
    P->>V: Initial documentation check
    P->>V: Physical inspection if risk triggered
    V-->>P: Deficiencies observed
    P->>F: Notify Flag State of deficiencies
    P->>R: Notify Recognised Organisation
    alt Detention warranted
        P->>V: Detention notice issued
        V->>P: Rectify deficiencies
        P->>V: Re-inspection
        alt Rectified
            P->>V: Release ship
        else Not rectified
            P->>V: Refusal of access order
        end
    else No detention
        P->>V: Allow departure with note
    end
    P->>Database: Record outcome in inspection database
```

```mermaid
    ROOT --> A1[Safety Regime]

    A1 --> B1[SOLAS 1974]
    A1 --> B2[Load Lines 1966]
    A1 --> B3[STCW 1978]
    A2 --> B4[MARPOL 73 78]
    A2 --> B5[OILPOL 1954]
    A2 --> B6[OPRC 1990]
    A3 --> B7[MLC 2006]
    A4 --> B8[SUA 1988]
    A4 --> B9[ISPS Code 2002]
    B1 --> C1[Flag State Issues Certificates]
    B4 --> C2[Port State Inspects Compliance]
    B7 --> C3[Port State May Detain for Labour Violations]

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define "Flag State" under UNCLOS. What is the "genuine link" requirement?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Flag State is the State whose flag a vessel is entitled to fly, in whose register the ship is enrolled, and from which it derives its nationality. Under **UNCLOS Article 91(1)**, every State has the right to fix conditions for the grant of its nationality to ships, and the ships must have a **"genuine link"** with the flag State. The Flag State has primary jurisdiction over its vessels on the high seas (UNCLOS Art. 92) and bears the duties prescribed in Article 94 (administrative, technical, social, and pollution-prevention obligations). **Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for Article 91 reference, 1 mark for Article 94 duties.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
*What is Port State Control? Name any three regional MoUs.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Port State Control (PSC) is the inspection of foreign ships in a State's ports to verify compliance with international maritime conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, STCW, Load Lines) where the Flag State has failed to do so effectively. It is conducted under **UNCLOS Articles 218 and 220**. Three regional MoUs are: **1) Paris MoU (Europe and North Atlantic), 2) Tokyo MoU (Asia-Pacific), 3) Indian Ocean MoU**. (Other acceptable answers: Caribbean MoU, Mediterranean MoU, Black Sea MoU, Riyadh MoU, Vina del Mar MoU, Abuja MoU.) **Award 1 mark for concept, 1 mark for UNCLOS article, 1 mark for three MoUs.**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Option 1]

**(a)** Discuss the **duties and obligations of a Flag State** in the implementation of international maritime law with reference to UNCLOS Articles 91, 92, and 94. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically examine the **role of Port State Control** as a backstop enforcement mechanism, citing the relevant UNCLOS articles and the regional MoU framework. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The Flag State is the primary jurisdictional authority over a vessel by virtue of the nationality link. UNCLOS Articles 91, 92, and 94 collectively constitute the foundation of Flag State responsibility.

**Body — Step-by-Step Explanation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Nationality and Registration (UNCLOS Art. 91):**
Every State has the right to fix conditions for granting its nationality to ships. Ships have the nationality of the State whose flag they are entitled to fly. There must exist a "genuine link" between the State and the ship, although this remains loosely interpreted in practice. Each State must maintain a register of ships flying its flag.

**Step 2 — Exclusive Jurisdiction on High Seas (UNCLOS Art. 92):**
Ships sail under one flag only and are subject to its exclusive jurisdiction on the high seas, save in exceptional cases (piracy, slave trade, hot pursuit, etc.). This exclusivity gives Flag State primacy but also imposes responsibility.

**Step 3 — Duties of the Flag State (UNCLOS Art. 94):**
The Flag State must:
- Maintain a **register of ships** and assume jurisdiction over them.
- Ensure that each ship is in the charge of a **properly qualified master and crew**.
- Ensure the **crew is familiar** with applicable international regulations.
- Ensure the ship is **surveyed by qualified surveyors** and holds appropriate certificates.
- Ensure the ship is **manned in accordance with the Minimum Safe Manning Document**.
- Ensure the master and crew are conversant with **COLREGS** and applicable regulations.
- Ensure that the ship complies with generally accepted **international rules on prevention of pollution** (MARPOL, OPRC, etc.).

**Step 4 — Recognised Organisations (ROs):**
A Flag State may delegate survey and certification work to Recognised Organisations (typically IACS members). However, the Flag State retains **ultimate responsibility** for ensuring the ship is safe and compliant.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The Flag State framework establishes a **primary, but imperfect, enforcement architecture**. Open registries and FoCs often suffer from weak oversight, which is why the Port State backstop becomes necessary.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Port State Control (PSC) emerged as a corrective mechanism for Flag State failure. The legal foundation is in UNCLOS Articles 218, 220, and 226, supplemented by detailed IMO Resolutions.

**Body — Step-by-Step Explanation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Need for PSC:**
The IMO Member State Audit Scheme (IMSAS) revealed that many Flag States, especially open registries, fail to enforce international conventions on their fleets. This regulatory gap is filled by Port State Control.

**Step 2 — Legal Basis (UNCLOS Art. 218):**
A Port State may investigate and institute proceedings against a foreign ship in its ports for violations of international rules committed **beyond the territorial sea** of that State — provided the Flag State has not already initiated proceedings within 6 months, or the violation has caused pollution damage in the Port State's waters, or the violation is of MARPOL Annex VI (air pollution).

**Step 3 — UNCLOS Art. 220 (Pollution Enforcement):**
A Port State may enforce pollution regulations for violations committed in its territorial sea or EEZ, including physical inspection, detention, and arrest of the vessel.

**Step 4 — UNCLOS Art. 226 (Investigation Limits):**
Physical inspection must be limited and not interfere with the ship's operations. Detention is permissible only if deficiencies are clearly hazardous.

**Step 5 — Regional MoU Framework (3 examples):**
- **Paris MoU** (Europe + Canada + Russia) — operates the THETIS database and White-Grey-Black lists.
- **Tokyo MoU** (Asia-Pacific) — 22 member authorities.
- **Indian Ocean MoU** — covering Indian Ocean rim states.
- Other MoUs: Caribbean, Mediterranean, Black Sea, Riyadh, Vina del Mar, Abuja.

**Step 6 — Procedural Framework:**
PSC inspections follow the **IMO Procedures for Port State Control (Resolution A.1185(33))**, which provides for initial inspection, more detailed inspection, and expanded inspection, with detention as the ultimate tool.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
PSC has evolved from an exceptional right into a **routine global enforcement system**, often criticised as discriminatory but justified as a necessary safety valve. Its effectiveness depends on harmonised application through MoUs and continuous training of PSCOs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Option 2]

**(a)** Explain the **jurisdictional cascade** in maritime law enforcement. How does Flag State primacy give way to Coastal State and Port State jurisdiction in defined circumstances? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Analyse the **"flag of convenience" phenomenon** and its impact on the implementation of maritime law. Discuss the role of Recognised Organisations in supplementing Flag State duties. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Maritime law is enforced through a layered jurisdictional cascade rather than a single central authority. This is because ships cross multiple maritime zones, each governed by different rules.

**Body — Step-by-Step Explanation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Flag State Primacy (Layer 1):**
Under UNCLOS Art. 94, the Flag State has the primary right and duty to enforce maritime law over its vessels, irrespective of location. The flag nationality creates the legal hook.

**Step 2 — Coastal State Jurisdiction (Layer 2):**
In the **internal waters and territorial sea** (0–12 nm), the Coastal State has sovereignty, akin to land territory, subject to the right of **innocent passage** (UNCLOS Art. 17-26). The Coastal State may exercise criminal jurisdiction in defined cases (Art. 27). In the **contiguous zone** (12–24 nm), the Coastal State may control customs, fiscal, immigration, and sanitary matters. In the **EEZ** (up to 200 nm), the Coastal State has sovereign rights for exploration, exploitation, conservation, and management of natural resources, plus jurisdiction for pollution (Art. 220).

**Step 3 — Port State Jurisdiction (Layer 3):**
In its internal waters and ports, the Port State enjoys near-absolute jurisdiction (Art. 25). The Port State's right to inspect foreign ships for safety, pollution, and labour compliance forms the substantive PSC regime (Art. 218, 220, 226).

**Step 4 — High Seas Freedom and Universal Jurisdiction (Layer 4):**
On the high seas (beyond 200 nm), Flag State exclusivity is the rule (Art. 87-92). Universal jurisdiction applies to **piracy** (Art. 100-107), **slave trade**, and **unauthorised broadcasting** (Art. 109).

**Step 5 — Worked Allocation:**
If a Greek-flagged tanker causes an oil spill in the EEZ of India while in transit:
- **Greece (Flag):** Prescriptive and enforcement jurisdiction — but lacks physical reach.
- **India (Coastal):** Pollution jurisdiction under Art. 220; can arrest the vessel in port for damages.
- **Subsequent port of call (e.g., Singapore):** Can inspect under Art. 218 if Greece fails to prosecute within 6 months.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The cascade is dynamic and overlapping, with each layer having distinct functional purposes. Effective maritime law enforcement requires coordination among Flag, Coastal, and Port States — a principle increasingly reflected in the IMO Member State Audit Scheme.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A "Flag of Convenience" (FoC) is the practice of registering a vessel in a foreign State (typically Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Bahamas, Malta) to take advantage of **lenient regulation, low taxes, and cheap labour**. The ITF estimates about 60% of world tonnage is registered under FoCs.

**Body — Step-by-Step Explanation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Historical Origins:**
FoCs emerged in the 1920s-1950s, when U.S. shipowners sought to escape the **Jones Act** and post-WWII labour protections. Panama, Liberia, and Honduras became early open registries.

**Step 2 — Key Features of FoC Regimes:**
- **Low registration fees** and minimal taxation.
- **No requirement** of national ownership or crewing.
- **Limited enforcement** of international conventions.
- **Secretive ownership structures** that hinder accountability.
- "Genuine link" (UNCLOS Art. 91) is treated as a formality, not enforced.

**Step 3 — Impact on Law Implementation:**
- **Dilution of Flag State responsibility** — leading to substandard ships.
- **Crew exploitation** — low wages, poor conditions, denied union rights.
- **Pollution incidents** — FoC-flagged ships are statistically over-represented in major casualties (Erika, Prestige, MV Wakashio).
- **Undermines MLC enforcement** — the MLC 2006 was partly designed to address this.

**Step 4 — Response: Recognised Organisations (ROs):**
To compensate for Flag State weakness, FoCs **delegate statutory survey and certification** to ROs, predominantly **IACS members** (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, ClassNK, Bureau Veritas, RINA, Korean Register, etc.). ROs issue SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Line, and MLC certificates on behalf of the Flag State.

**Step 5 — RO Accountability:**
The **IMO RO Code (Resolution A.1166(30), revised)** establishes a mandatory Code for RO performance, with sanctions for poor performance. The **EU Regulation (EC) No 391/2009** allows the EU to penalise ROs that fail in their duties. Paris MoU and Tokyo MoU publish annual RO performance lists (RO Performance Tables).

**Step 6 — Persistent Criticism:**
Despite ROs, critics argue that **commercial pressure** drives ROs to retain clients (vessels) by minimising deficiencies, creating a **"race to the bottom."** This is why the **EU Maritime Safety Agency (EMSA)** conducts parallel audits and why the Paris MoU's "White, Grey, Black List" system penalises flag and RO combinations.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The FoC phenomenon exposes the structural weakness of Flag State primacy. The implementation of maritime law therefore depends on a **triad: Flag State + Recognised Organisation + Port State Control**, with international coordination through MoUs serving as the integrating mechanism.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Flag" and "Port" State powers:** Students often write that the Port State has jurisdiction on the high seas — this is **WRONG**. The Port State has jurisdiction only in its **internal waters, ports, and territorial sea**, plus a limited Art. 218 power for violations committed outside its waters. Examiners deduct 2-3 marks for this error.
> 2. **Omitting the "Genuine Link" principle:** Many answers mention UNCLOS Art. 91 but skip the "genuine link" requirement, which is a **highly tested KTU concept**.
> 3. **Not citing specific articles:** Every Flag/Port State power must be **anchored to a UNCLOS article** (91, 92, 94, 218, 220, 226). Generic answers without citations lose marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the MLC 2006:** Recent KTU papers (2023-2024) have tested MLC enforcement via Port State Control — students who only know SOLAS and MARPOL lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing "territorial sea" and "EEZ":** The Coastal State has **full sovereignty** only in the territorial sea (12 nm). Beyond that, it has only **functional jurisdiction** (pollution, customs). Marks are deducted for this distinction.
> 6. **No mention of regional MoUs:** KTU examiners expect at least **three MoU names** in any PSC-related answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flag State** = nationality jurisdiction; **Port State** = geographic jurisdiction; **Coastal State** = zone-based jurisdiction.
- **UNCLOS Article 91** = nationality and the **"genuine link"** principle.
- **UNCLOS Article 92** = **exclusive Flag State jurisdiction** on the high seas.
- **UNCLOS Article 94** = the "charter" of Flag State duties — registry, manning, survey, certification, pollution prevention.
- **UNCLOS Article 218** = Port State enforcement for **extra-territorial violations** (6-month Flag State inaction rule).
- **UNCLOS Article 220** = Port State enforcement for **pollution** in territorial sea and EEZ.
- **UNCLOS Article 226** = limits on Port State investigation of foreign vessels.
- **Recognised Organisations (ROs)** = IACS members acting as Flag State's technical arm.
- **Port State Control (PSC)** = three inspection levels: Initial, More Detailed, Expanded (per Resolution A.1185(33)).
- **Detention** = the Port State's principal enforcement tool; must be justified by hazardous deficiencies.
- **Regional MoUs** = Paris, Tokyo, Indian Ocean, Caribbean, Mediterranean, Black Sea, Riyadh, Vina del Mar, Abuja.
- **Databases** = THETIS (EU), EQUASIS (global), APIS (USCG).
- **Flags of Convenience** = Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Bahamas, Malta, Cyprus, Antigua & Barbuda, Comoros.
- **"White, Grey, Black" List** = Paris MoU classification; flags are ranked by detention performance.
- **Universal jurisdiction** = piracy (UNCLOS Art. 100-107), slave trade, unauthorised broadcasting.
- **Innocent passage** = territorial sea right of foreign vessels (Art. 17-19), subject to non-threat conditions.
- **EEZ** = 200 nm, **Territorial Sea** = 12 nm, **Contiguous Zone** = 24 nm, **1 nautical mile = 1,852 metres**.
- **Hot pursuit** (UNCLOS Art. 111) = the only exception to Flag State exclusivity on the high seas.
- **IMSAS** = IMO Member State Audit Scheme — the supervisory tool for Flag State performance.
- **SUA Convention 1988** + **SUA Protocols 2005** = suppression of unlawful acts against maritime navigation.
- **MLC 2006** = Port State may detain ships for labour violations since 2013 (MLC amendments, **"no more favourable treatment" clause**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Memory Trick:**
> *"Flag is the *parent* (nationality), Port is the *policeman* (geography), and UNCLOS is the *constitution*."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
