topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW and Maritime Labour Convention (MLC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Four Pillars of Modern Maritime Regulation

International maritime safety, environmental protection, and seafarer welfare are governed by a framework of **International Maritime Organization (IMO) Conventions** and **International Labour Organization (ILO) Conventions**. The four most critical conventions every maritime professional must understand are **SOLAS**, **MARPOL**, **STCW**, and **MLC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** These four conventions form the **regulatory backbone** of the global shipping industry. They are mutually complementary — SOLAS addresses ship safety, MARPOL addresses pollution, STCW addresses human competence, and MLC addresses working conditions.

---

## 1.2 SOLAS — International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea

### Formal Definition
**SOLAS (1974)** is the principal international convention concerning the **safety of merchant ships**. It was first adopted in **1914** in response to the *Titanic* disaster (1912), making it the **oldest** of all IMO conventions still in force today. The current version is the **1974 SOLAS Convention**, as amended.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **SOLAS as the "Building Code for Ships"** — just as a city has fire codes, electrical codes, and structural codes for buildings, SOLAS sets minimum construction, equipment, and operational standards for every ocean-going vessel. If your building cannot pass the building code, you cannot occupy it. If your ship cannot pass SOLAS, it cannot sail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Article (Article I):** The contracting governments undertake to give effect to the provisions of the present Convention, which shall apply to all ships flying the flag of a Contracting State.

---

## 1.3 MARPOL — International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships

### Formal Definition
**MARPOL 73/78** is the main international convention covering **prevention of pollution of the marine environment by ships** from operational or accidental causes. It was adopted in **1973** and the **1978 Protocol** (combined as MARPOL 73/78) addressed tanker accidents. It is the **most significant international treaty** in maritime environmental protection.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine **MARPOL as the "Environmental Protection Law of the Oceans"** — just as a factory cannot dump toxic waste, untreated sewage, or smoke into a river, ships cannot dump oil, chemicals, garbage, or harmful emissions into the sea. MARPOL specifies *what* can be discharged, *where*, and *in what quantities*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Annexes of MARPOL:**
> - **Annex I** — Oil (Operational and Accidental)
> - **Annex II** — Noxious Liquid Substances in Bulk
> - **Annex III** — Harmful Substances Carried in Packaged Form
> - **Annex IV** — Sewage
> - **Annex V** — Garbage
> - **Annex VI** — Air Pollution

---

## 1.4 STCW — International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Seafarers

### Formal Definition
**STCW 1978**, as substantially revised by the **2010 Manila Amendments**, establishes **minimum standards of training, certification, and watchkeeping** for seafarers. It was the first internationally agreed convention to address **human element** issues in shipping.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **STCW as the "Professional Licensing System for Seafarers"** — just as a doctor needs a medical degree, a pilot needs a flying license, and an engineer needs a professional registration, every officer and rating on a ship must hold a certificate proving they are competent to perform their duties. STCW ensures global uniformity in seafarer qualifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STCW applies to:** Masters, officers, and ratings serving on **seagoing merchant ships of 500 GT or more**, engaged on international voyages. The Manila Amendments (2010) added new requirements for **electricians, security personnel**, and stricter standards for **tanker, passenger ship, and high-speed craft** personnel.

---

## 1.5 MLC — Maritime Labour Convention, 2006

### Formal Definition
The **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC 2006)** is an **ILO Convention** that consolidates and updates over **68 existing ILO maritime labour instruments**. It is often called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights"** and creates a single, coherent instrument embodying all the latest standards of existing international maritime labour Conventions and Recommendations.

### Conceptual Analogy
**MLC is the "Labour Constitution for Seafarers"** — just as the Indian Constitution guarantees fundamental rights to workers, MLC guarantees minimum working conditions, wages, leave, medical care, social security, and repatriation to every seafarer, regardless of the flag of the ship or their nationality.

> [!NOTE]
> **MLC entered into force on 20 August 2013** and covers **nearly 1.2 million seafarers worldwide** (representing over 95% of world shipping tonnage by August 2024). It is the **fourth pillar** of the IMO/ILO regulatory regime for international shipping.

---

## 1.6 The Four Conventions at a Glance

| Convention | Adopted | Focus Area | Administered By |
|------------|---------|------------|-----------------|
| **SOLAS** | 1974 (first version 1914) | Ship Safety | IMO |
| **MARPOL** | 1973/1978 | Marine Pollution | IMO |
| **STCW** | 1978 (revised 2010) | Seafarer Competence | IMO |
| **MLC 2006** | 2006 (in force 2013) | Seafarer Welfare | ILO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Together, these four conventions are sometimes called the **"Four Pillars" or "Regulatory Pillars of International Shipping"** and are referenced as the foundation of modern maritime governance. Understanding their interrelationship is essential for Module 1.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 SOLAS — Detailed Structural Analysis

### 2.1.1 Historical Evolution
The evolution of SOLAS demonstrates progressive international response to maritime disasters:

1. **1912** — Sinking of RMS *Titanic* (1,517 deaths)
2. **1914** — First SOLAS Convention adopted
3. **1929, 1948, 1960, 1974** — Subsequent revisions
4. **1974 SOLAS** — Current version in force since **25 May 1980**
5. **Continuous amendments** — Adopted at IMO Maritime Safety Committee (MSC) sessions

> [!NOTE]
> The 1974 SOLAS Convention is **continuously updated** through tacit acceptance procedures, making it a "living instrument."

### 2.1.2 Structural Breakdown — Chapters of SOLAS 1974

| Chapter | Subject | KTU Significance |
|---------|---------|------------------|
| **Chapter I** | General Provisions | Definitions, application, surveys |
| **Chapter II-1** | Construction — Subdivision & Stability, Machinery & Electrical Installations | Watertight doors, stability |
| **Chapter II-2** | Construction — Fire Protection, Fire Detection, Fire Extinction | Fire safety code |
| **Chapter III** | Life-Saving Appliances & Arrangements | Lifeboats, lifejackets |
| **Chapter IV** | Radiocommunications | GMDSS requirement |
| **Chapter V** | Safety of Navigation | Bridge procedures, passage planning |
| **Chapter VI** | Carriage of Cargoes | Grain, dangerous goods |
| **Chapter VII** | Carriage of Dangerous Goods | IMDG Code |
| **Chapter VIII** | Nuclear Ships | Reactor standards |
| **Chapter IX** | Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code) | Safety Management System |
| **Chapter X** | Safety Measures for High-Speed Craft | HSC Code |
| **Chapter XI-1** | Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety | LRIT, continuous synopsis record |
| **Chapter XI-2** | Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Security | **ISPS Code** |
| **Chapter XII** | Additional Safety Measures for Bulk Carriers | Bulk carrier safety |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Chapter IX (ISM Code) and Chapter XI-2 (ISPS Code) are **favourite exam topics** because they integrate safety and security management systems into SOLAS.

### 2.1.3 SOLAS 2020 Amendments — Highlight
Major amendments adopted for **2020/2021** include:
- **Chapter II-1** — Subdivision and damage stability requirements
- **Chapter III** — New requirements for lifeboat release hooks
- **Chapter V** — New requirements for VDR/S-VDR (Voyage Data Recorders)

---

## 2.2 MARPOL — Detailed Structural Analysis

### 2.2.1 Annex-by-Annex Breakdown

| Annex | Pollution Type | Key Requirement | KTU Exam Focus |
|-------|---------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **I** | Oil | Oily Water Separator (15 ppm standard), **SOPEP** (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan) | Double hull tankers (post-ERIKA & PRESTIGE) |
| **II** | Noxious Liquid Substances (NLS) in bulk | Category A/B/C/D substances; **IBC Code** | Pre-wash procedures |
| **III** | Harmful Substances in Packaged Form | **IMDG Code**, marking, labelling, stowage | Limited quantities |
| **IV** | Sewage | **Sewage Treatment Plant** or holding tank | Discharge beyond 4 nm (regulated) |
| **V** | Garbage | **Garbage Management Plan**, **Garbage Record Book** | Plastic ban (no discharge anywhere) |
| **VI** | Air Pollution | **Sulphur Cap (0.50% m/m)**, EEDI, EEXI, CII | **ECA zones, 0.10% sulphur** |

### 2.2.2 MARPOL Air Pollution — Detailed Sub-Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Annex VI (Air Pollution)** was adopted in **1997** and entered into force in **2005**. It is the **most amended** annex and is critical for KTU 2024 scheme exams.

Key sub-regulations:
- **Regulation 13** — NOx Technical Code (Tier I, II, III)
- **Regulation 14** — **Sulphur Cap** (currently 0.50% globally, 0.10% in **ECA**)
- **Regulation 19** — Energy Efficiency for Ships (**EEDI**, **EEXI**, **CII**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sulphur Cap 2020:** Effective **1 January 2020**, IMO reduced global sulphur limit from 3.50% to **0.50% m/m** in fuel oil. This is a landmark regulation under MARPOL Annex VI.

---

## 2.3 STCW — Detailed Structural Analysis

### 2.3.1 Manila Amendments 2010 — Key Changes

The **2010 Manila Amendments** to STCW were adopted at a Diplomatic Conference held in **Manila, Philippines (21–25 June 2010)** and entered into force on **1 January 2012** with transitional arrangements until **1 January 2017**.

Major changes include:

1. **New training requirements** for:
   - Electro-Technical Officers (ETO)
   - Able Seafarers (Deck & Engine)
2. **Updated certification** standards for:
   - Tanker personnel (Oil, Chemical, LNG, LPG)
   - Passenger ship personnel
   - High-speed craft personnel
3. **Anti-piracy training** — New section on **security-related familiarization**
4. **Hours of rest** — Minimum **10 hours rest in any 24-hour period**, and **77 hours in any 7-day period**
5. **New IMO Model Courses** to support implementation

### 2.3.2 STCW Tables of Competence

| Table | Function | Level |
|-------|----------|-------|
| **Table A-II/1** | Officer in Charge of a Navigational Watch (OOW) | Operational |
| **Table A-II/2** | Master and Chief Mate | Management |
| **Table A-III/1** | Officer in Charge of an Engineering Watch | Operational |
| **Table A-III/2** | Chief Engineer and Second Engineer | Management |
| **Table A-IV/2** | GMDSS Radio Operators | Operational |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** "**Operational vs Management Level**" is a key STCW concept. **Operational** = routine duties; **Management** = overall responsibility and command.

---

## 2.4 MLC 2006 — Detailed Structural Analysis

### 2.4.1 The Five Titles of MLC 2006

| Title | Subject | KTU Exam Significance |
|-------|---------|----------------------|
| **Title 1** | Minimum Requirements for Seafarers to Work on a Ship | Minimum age (16/18), medical certificate, training |
| **Title 2** | Conditions of Employment | Wages, hours of work/rest, leave, repatriation |
| **Title 3** | Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food & Catering | On-board living standards |
| **Title 4** | Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare & Social Security | Medical care on board, social security |
| **Title 5** | Compliance & Enforcement | Flag State, Port State, Labour Inspectorate, Maritime Labour Certificate |

> [!NOTE]
> The **"Four Most Important Areas"** that flag states must certify compliance with are:
> 1. Minimum age
> 2. Medical certification
> 3. Training and qualifications
> 4. Recruitment and placement services

### 2.4.2 Key MLC Requirements — "The Seafarer's Bill of Rights"

- **Minimum wage** — Regular payment at no less than monthly intervals
- **Maximum working hours** — **14 hours/day** and **72 hours/week** (maximum)
- **Minimum rest hours** — **10 hours/day** and **77 hours/week** (minimum)
- **Annual leave** — **4.5 calendar days/month** minimum
- **Repatriation** — Right to return home at employer's cost
- **Medical care** — Free access to medical care on board and ashore
- **Repatriation of remains** — In case of death on board
- **Protection against abandonment** — Financial security system
- **Protection against piracy** — Specific rights when held captive

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MLC Regulation 2.1 (Standard A2.1):** Every seafarer has the right to **"a safe and secure workplace"** and **"fair terms of employment."** This is a foundational principle of MLC.

### 2.4.3 Compliance & Enforcement — Three-Tier System

1. **Flag State Inspection** — Issue of **Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC)** valid for **5 years**
2. **Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC)** — Ship-specific compliance document
3. **Port State Control** — Inspection in foreign ports, complaint handling, detention powers

> [!NOTE]
> The MLC creates a **"no-more-favourable-treatment"** clause — a port state can detain a foreign ship if conditions on board are clearly hazardous to safety, health, or security.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Summary Table — The Four Conventions

| Aspect | SOLAS | MARPOL | STCW | MLC |
|--------|-------|--------|------|-----|
| **Adopting Body** | IMO | IMO | IMO | ILO |
| **Year (Current)** | 1974 | 1973/78 | 1978 (Manila 2010) | 2006 |
| **Focus** | Ship Safety | Environmental Protection | Crew Competence | Seafarer Welfare |
| **Application** | All merchant ships | All ships (varying) | Seagoing ≥500 GT international | All ships engaged in commercial activity |
| **Key Document/Cert.** | Safety Construction Cert., Safety Equipment Cert. | IOPP Cert. | COC, COP | MLC + DMLC |
| **Enforcement** | Flag/Port State | Flag/Port State | Flag State | Flag/Port State |
| **Latest Major Update** | 2020 amendments | 0.50% S cap (2020) | Manila 2010 | 2018 amendments (Part B) |

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Conceptual Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Framework — How the Four Conventions Interrelate

### 3.1.1 Logical Step-by-Step Relationship

Let us derive the relationship between these four conventions using a **stepwise analytical approach**:

**Step 1 — Start with the SHIP (Asset):**
$$\text{Ship Construction} \rightarrow \text{Governed by SOLAS (Chapters II-1, II-2)}$$

**Step 2 — Protect the ENVIRONMENT:**
$$\text{Ship Operations} \rightarrow \text{Governed by MARPOL (Annexes I–VI)}$$

**Step 3 — Qualify the CREW:**
$$\text{Human Element on Board} \rightarrow \text{Governed by STCW (Training & Certification)}$$

**Step 4 — Protect the CREW's RIGHTS:**
$$\text{Working & Living Conditions} \rightarrow \text{Governed by MLC 2006 (Five Titles)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **4-step logic** is often asked in KTU exams: *"Explain the interrelationship between SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW and MLC."*

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Representation of Convention Coverage

We can represent the **completeness of maritime regulation** as a four-set union:

$$R_{\text{Total}} = R_{\text{SOLAS}} \cup R_{\text{MARPOL}} \cup R_{\text{STCW}} \cup R_{\text{MLC}}$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{SOLAS}}$ = Set of rules covering ship safety
- $R_{\text{MARPOL}}$ = Set of rules covering pollution prevention
- $R_{\text{STCW}}$ = Set of rules covering crew competence
- $R_{\text{MLC}}$ = Set of rules covering crew welfare

The **completeness condition** requires:
$$R_{\text{Total}} \supseteq \{\text{Asset Safety} \cup \text{Environment} \cup \text{Human Competence} \cup \text{Human Welfare}\}$$

This means **no aspect** of safe and sustainable shipping should fall outside the union of these four regulatory frameworks.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — SOLAS Chapter Significance

### 3.2.1 SOLAS Applicability Logic

The applicability of SOLAS is determined by the following logical flow:

$$\text{Ship Type} \times \text{Ship Size} \times \text{Voyage Type} \rightarrow \text{Applicable SOLAS Chapters}$$

**Where:**
- **Ship Type** = Passenger / Cargo / Tanker / Bulk Carrier / HSC
- **Ship Size** = <500 GT / 500 GT and above
- **Voyage Type** = International / Domestic

**Standard Application Rule (Article III):**
$$\text{International Voyage (any ship)} + \text{≥500 GT Cargo Ships} \rightarrow \text{All Chapters apply}$$

**For Cargo Ships <500 GT:**
$$\text{Specific Relaxations Apply} \rightarrow \text{Chapters II-1, II-2, III, IV modified}$$

**For Passenger Ships:**
$$\text{All Chapters Apply Regardless of Size (with safety intensification)}$$

### 3.2.2 MARPOL Annex Applicability Logic

MARPOL uses an **opt-in/opt-out system** for Annexes IV, V, and VI. The logic is:

$$\text{MARPOL Default Application} = \text{Annex I} \cup \text{Annex II} \cup \text{Annex III}$$

$$\text{Optional Annexes} = \text{Annex IV (Sewage)}, \text{Annex V (Garbage)}, \text{Annex VI (Air)}$$

A flag state must **deliberately accept** Annexes IV, V, and VI for them to apply to its ships. However, in practice, virtually all major maritime nations have accepted all six annexes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** India has accepted **all six Annexes** of MARPOL. The **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS), Mumbai** is the designated authority.

---

## 3.3 STCW Analytical Derivation

### 3.3.1 Training Path Logic

The STCW competence ladder follows a structured progression:

$$\text{Level 1: Basic Safety Training (BST)} \rightarrow \text{Ratings (Deck/Engine)}$$

$$\text{Level 2: Watchkeeping Certificate} \rightarrow \text{Operational Level Officers}$$

$$\text{Level 3: Master's/Chief Engineer's Certificate} \rightarrow \text{Management Level Officers}$$

**Expressed as a logical dependency:**
$$\text{COC Issuance} \Rightarrow \text{(Sea Service} \land \text{Approved Training} \land \text{Medical Fitness} \land \text{Competence Demonstration)}$$

Where:
- $\text{COC}$ = Certificate of Competency
- $\Rightarrow$ = "requires" (logical implication)
- $\land$ = logical AND (all conditions must be met)

### 3.3.2 Hours of Rest Calculation (STCW Manila 2010)

The minimum rest hours calculation can be expressed as:

$$\text{Rest}_{24h} \geq 10 \text{ hours}$$
$$\text{Rest}_{7d} \geq 77 \text{ hours}$$
$$\text{Maximum Work}_{24h} \leq 14 \text{ hours}$$

**Verification logic:**
$$\text{Work Hours} + \text{Rest Hours} = 24 \text{ hours per day}$$

If Maximum Work = 14 hours, then:
$$\text{Minimum Rest} = 24 - 14 = 10 \text{ hours (Satisfies the requirement ✓)}$$

Over 7 days:
$$\text{Work}_{7d} \leq 7 \times 14 = 98 \text{ hours (Max)}$$
$$\text{Rest}_{7d} \geq 7 \times 10 = 70 \text{ hours (Min)}$$

But STCW requires **77 hours in 7 days**, so:
$$\text{Rest}_{7d} \geq 77 \text{ hours} \rightarrow \text{Work}_{7d} \leq 91 \text{ hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The 77 hours in 7 days is a **stricter standard** than what the daily rule implies, requiring careful watchkeeping planning.

---

## 3.4 MLC Analytical Derivation

### 3.4.1 Seafarer Compliance Threshold

The MLC **compliance condition** for a ship is:

$$\text{MLC Compliance} = \text{MLC Certificate} \land \text{DMLC} \land \text{Onboard Inspection}$$

Where:
- **MLC Certificate** = Issued by Flag State, valid 5 years
- **DMLC** = Ship-specific Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance
- **Onboard Inspection** = Initial + Intermediate inspections during the 5-year period

### 3.4.2 Annual Leave Calculation (MLC)

$$\text{Annual Leave}_{min} = 4.5 \text{ calendar days} \times 12 \text{ months} = 54 \text{ calendar days/year}$$

This is a **mandatory minimum** and may be increased by national laws or collective bargaining.

**Leave Pay Logic:**
$$\text{Leave Pay} = \text{Average Normal Wage} \times \text{Days of Leave Taken}$$

### 3.4.3 Repatriation Entitlement

A seafarer acquires the right to repatriation after a **maximum period of service** which is determined by national laws or collective agreements, but typically:

$$\text{Maximum Service Before Repatriation} \leq 11 \text{ months (industry standard)}$$

The right arises under:
1. End of seafarer's employment contract
2. Seafarer's illness/injury
3. Shipwreck
4. Maternity-related reasons
5. Termination by employer (without valid cause)

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define SOLAS Convention. State its major chapters. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea), 1974** is the principal IMO Convention concerning the safety of merchant ships. It was first adopted in 1914 in response to the Titanic disaster and has been revised several times, with the current version being the 1974 SOLAS Convention.

**Major Chapters include:**
- **Chapter I** — General Provisions
- **Chapter II-1 and II-2** — Construction (Subdivision, Stability, Fire Protection)
- **Chapter III** — Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements
- **Chapter IV** — Radiocommunications (GMDSS)
- **Chapter V** — Safety of Navigation
- **Chapter IX** — Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
- **Chapter XI-2** — Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Security (ISPS Code)

---

### Question 2: List the six Annexes of MARPOL. (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The six Annexes of MARPOL 73/78 are:
1. **Annex I** — Prevention of Pollution by Oil
2. **Annex II** — Control of Pollution by Noxious Liquid Substances in Bulk
3. **Annex III** — Prevention of Pollution by Harmful Substances in Packaged Form
4. **Annex IV** — Prevention of Pollution by Sewage from Ships
5. **Annex V** — Prevention of Pollution by Garbage from Ships
6. **Annex VI** — Prevention of Air Pollution from Ships

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) Explain in detail the four pillars of international maritime regulation. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Discuss the structure and key provisions of STCW Convention with reference to the Manila Amendments 2010. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a): The Four Pillars of International Maritime Regulation (7 Marks)

The **four pillars** are the fundamental international conventions that govern the modern shipping industry. They are:

**1. SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea) — 1974**
SOLAS is the oldest IMO convention, addressing **ship safety**. It covers construction, fire protection, life-saving appliances, radio communications, navigation, and dangerous goods. It also integrates the **ISM Code** (safety management) and **ISPS Code** (security).

**2. MARPOL (Prevention of Pollution from Ships) — 73/78**
MARPOL addresses **marine environmental protection** through six annexes covering oil, noxious liquids, packaged goods, sewage, garbage, and air pollution. The 2020 sulphur cap (0.50% m/m) under Annex VI is its most recent landmark regulation.

**3. STCW (Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping) — 1978**
STCW governs **seafarer competence**. It ensures that masters, officers, and ratings meet minimum international training standards. The Manila Amendments 2010 introduced new requirements for electro-technical officers, able seafarers, anti-piracy training, and stricter hours of rest rules.

**4. MLC (Maritime Labour Convention) — 2006**
MLC is the ILO convention often called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights."** It consolidates labour standards covering minimum age, wages, hours of work, leave, repatriation, accommodation, medical care, and social security.

**Interrelationship:** SOLAS protects the ship; MARPOL protects the environment; STCW qualifies the crew; MLC protects the crew's rights. Together they form a complete regulatory framework.

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b): STCW and Manila Amendments 2010 (7 Marks)

**Historical Context:** STCW was first adopted in 1978 and entered into force in 1984. Due to technological advances and new types of ships, it was substantially revised.

**Manila Amendments 2010:** Adopted at a Diplomatic Conference in Manila, Philippines (21–25 June 2010), these amendments entered into force on **1 January 2012** with full implementation by **1 January 2017**.

**Key Provisions:**

**(i) New Training Requirements:**
- Electro-Technical Officers (ETO)
- Able Seafarers (Deck and Engine)
- Security Awareness Training

**(ii) Updated Certification Standards:**
- Oil, Chemical, LNG, LPG tanker personnel
- Passenger ship personnel
- High-speed craft personnel

**(iii) Hours of Rest:**
- Minimum 10 hours rest in any 24-hour period
- Minimum 77 hours rest in any 7-day period
- Maximum 14 hours work in 24 hours

**(iv) Anti-Piracy Measures:**
- All seafarers must receive security-related familiarization
- Specific training for those assigned to security duties

**(v) Revised Tables of Competence:**
- Table A-II/1, A-II/2, A-III/1, A-III/2 — Operational and Management levels

**Implementation:** Flag states must review their training and certification systems to ensure compliance with the Manila Amendments. New IMO Model Courses were developed to support implementation.

---

### Question B (Option 2): (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) Discuss the Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006 in detail, with reference to its Five Titles. (7 Marks)**
**(b) Explain the structure of MARPOL 73/78 with special emphasis on Annex VI (Air Pollution). (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a): MLC 2006 — Five Titles (7 Marks)

The **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006** is an ILO Convention that consolidates over 68 previous maritime labour instruments. It is often called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights"** and covers nearly 1.2 million seafarers worldwide.

**Title 1 — Minimum Requirements for Seafarers to Work on a Ship**
- Minimum age: 16 years (general) and 18 years for night work/hazardous duties
- Medical certificate requirement
- Training and qualifications (aligned with STCW)
- Recruitment and placement services (no fees charged to seafarers)

**Title 2 — Conditions of Employment**
- Wages: paid at no less than monthly intervals
- Hours of work and rest: Maximum 14 hours/day, 72 hours/week; Minimum 10 hours rest/day, 77 hours/week
- Annual leave: minimum 4.5 calendar days per month
- Repatriation: at employer's cost
- Manning levels: ensuring safe manning
- Social security protection

**Title 3 — Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food and Catering**
- Standards for sleeping rooms, mess rooms, sanitary facilities
- Ventilation, heating, lighting, noise levels
- Food quality and free provision
- Recreational facilities and welfare

**Title 4 — Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare and Social Security**
- Access to medical care on board and ashore
- Protection against piracy
- Maternity protection
- Welfare facilities and services

**Title 5 — Compliance and Enforcement**
- Flag state responsibilities: certification and inspection
- Port state control: inspection in foreign ports
- Maritime Labour Certificate (5 years validity) and DMLC
- Onboard complaint procedures for seafarers
- Labour inspectorate powers

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b): MARPOL Structure with Focus on Annex VI (7 Marks)

**Structure of MARPOL 73/78:**

MARPOL was adopted in **1973** and modified by the **1978 Protocol** in response to tanker accidents. It has **six annexes** covering different types of pollution from ships.

**Annex I — Oil:** Oily water separators (15 ppm standard), SOPEP, double hull requirements.

**Annex II — Noxious Liquid Substances:** Categorization of substances (A, B, C, D) and discharge controls.

**Annex III — Packaged Harmful Substances:** Use of IMDG Code for marking, labelling, and stowage.

**Annex IV — Sewage:** Sewage treatment plants and discharge restrictions.

**Annex V — Garbage:** Garbage management plans, garbage record books, and complete ban on plastic discharge.

**Annex VI — Air Pollution (Special Focus):**

Adopted in **1997**, entered into force in **2005**. This annex is critical for KTU 2024.

**Key Regulations:**

**Regulation 13 — NOx Emissions:**
- Tier I (2000): Applicable to ships built between 2000–2010
- Tier II (2011): 15–22% reduction
- Tier III (2016): 80% reduction (in ECA zones only)

**Regulation 14 — Sulphur Content of Fuel Oil:**
- Global cap: **0.50% m/m** (effective 1 January 2020)
- ECA cap: **0.10% m/m** (Baltic Sea, North Sea, North American ECA)
- Methods of compliance: Low-sulphur fuel, LNG, scrubbers

**Regulations 19, 20, 21, 22, 23 — Energy Efficiency:**
- **EEDI** (Energy Efficiency Design Index) — for new ships
- **EEXI** (Energy Efficiency Existing Ship Index) — effective 2023
- **CII** (Carbon Intensity Indicator) — annual rating A to E, effective 2023

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Significance:** Annex VI regulations on **sulphur cap 0.50%** and **EEDI/EEXI/CII** are extremely high-yield topics for KTU 2024 scheme exams.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Year Confusion:** Students often write "SOLAS 1978" (correct is **SOLAS 1974**) or "MARPOL 1973" (correct is **MARPOL 73/78**). One mark is deducted per such error.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Annex Count:** MARPOL has **6 Annexes**, not 5. Missing Annex VI is a common mistake.
> 
> 3. **STCW Body Confusion:** STCW is an **IMO** convention, not ILO. MLC is **ILO**. Confusing these loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Hours of Rest Inversion:** Students write "maximum 10 hours rest" instead of **minimum** 10 hours rest. The keyword "**minimum**" is critical.
> 
> 5. **MLC 2006 vs STCW Confusion:** MLC deals with **welfare and conditions**; STCW deals with **competence and certification**. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 
> 6. **Sulphur Cap:** The global sulphur cap is **0.50% m/m** since 2020 (not 3.50% — that was the pre-2020 limit). ECA limit is **0.10%**.
> 
> 7. **MLC Title Mistake:** Title 3 is **Accommodation**, not Medical Care. Medical is Title 4.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1

- [x] **SOLAS 1974** — IMO Convention on Ship Safety, oldest convention (1914 origin after Titanic)
- [x] **MARPOL 73/78** — IMO Convention on Marine Pollution, **6 Annexes** (Oil, NLS, Packaged, Sewage, Garbage, Air)
- [x] **STCW 1978** — IMO Convention on Seafarer Competence, **Manila Amendments 2010**
- [x] **MLC 2006** — ILO Convention on Seafarer Welfare, **5 Titles**, in force from **20 August 2013**
- [x] **STCW Manila:** 10 hrs rest/24h, 77 hrs rest/7 days, 14 hrs work max/day
- [x] **MLC Annual Leave:** 4.5 calendar days/month (minimum)
- [x] **MARPOL Annex VI:** Global Sulphur cap 0.50% m/m (since 1 Jan 2020), ECA Sulphur cap 0.10%
- [x] **SOLAS ISM Code** is in **Chapter IX**; **ISPS Code** is in **Chapter XI-2**
- [x] **MLC = ILO**, others = **IMO**
- [x] **Maritime Labour Certificate** — valid 5 years, with intermediate inspections
- [x] **IOPP Certificate** (MARPOL), **Safety Construction/Equipment Certificates** (SOLAS)
- [x] **STCW Tables of Competence** — A-II/1 (Operational Deck), A-II/2 (Master), A-III/1 (Operational Engine), A-III/2 (Chief Engineer)
- [x] **EEDI, EEXI, CII** — Energy Efficiency regulations under MARPOL Annex VI
- [x] **Interrelationship:** SOLAS → Ship, MARPOL → Environment, STCW → Crew Competence, MLC → Crew Welfare
- [x] **India is a party** to all four conventions; DG Shipping Mumbai is the administration
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need of survey and certification, classification societies, United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS) various zones and limits, Maritime Labour Convention: minimum requirements for seafarers to work on a ship, conditions of employment, accommodation, recreational facilities, food and catering, health protection, medical care, welfare and social security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Maritime Survey and Certification

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> A **Maritime Survey** is a systematic, technical inspection of a vessel, its machinery, equipment, structure, and operational compliance, conducted by qualified surveyors to ascertain seaworthiness, regulatory conformity, and adherence to international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, Load Lines).

**Certification** is the formal documentary attestation issued by an Administration, Classification Society, or Recognized Organization (RO) confirming that a vessel has satisfactorily complied with the applicable statutory and class requirements. It is the legal proof that a ship is fit to operate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Ship's Annual Health Check-Up":**
> Think of a ship like a human body. Just as we undergo routine medical check-ups, blood tests, and receive fitness certificates before flying or driving, a ship undergoes **statutory surveys** (compulsory, mandated by law) and **class surveys** (voluntary but commercially essential) to obtain "Certificates of Fitness." Without these certificates, the ship is legally barred from sailing — equivalent to a car being stopped by traffic police for lacking an FC (Fitness Certificate).

**Why is it needed?**
1. **Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** — prevention of casualties like the *Titanic* (1912) and *MV Herald of Free Enterprise* (1987).
2. **Prevention of Marine Pollution (MARPOL)** — environmental compliance.
3. **Commercial Viability** — insurance, chartering, port state control clearances.
4. **Liability Mitigation** — establishes due diligence of the owner.
5. **Crew Welfare** — MLC compliance ensures humane working conditions.

---

## 1.2 Classification Societies

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> A **Classification Society** is a non-governmental organization (NGO) that establishes and applies technical standards (Classification Rules) for the design, construction, and operational maintenance of ships, offshore structures, and other maritime assets. It assigns a **class notation** and certifies that the vessel conforms to its published rules.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Ship's Credit Rating Agency":**
Imagine a credit-rating agency like CIBIL or Moody's that grades individuals/companies (AAA, BB, etc.) based on financial trustworthiness. A classification society does the same for ships — issuing a "class" rating (e.g., ✠100A1, Class +A1) that globally communicates the vessel's structural and mechanical integrity to insurers, charterers, banks, and port authorities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Function:** Classification societies do NOT replace flag State Administrations. They act as **Recognized Organizations (ROs)** delegated by the flag State to perform statutory surveys on its behalf.

---

## 1.3 United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> **UNCLOS (1982)** is the international treaty that defines the rights and responsibilities of nations in their use of the world's oceans, establishing guidelines for businesses, the environment, and the management of marine natural resources. It is often called the **"Constitution for the Oceans"** and entered into force on **16 November 1994**.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Global Real-Estate Deed for the Seas":**
Imagine Earth as a housing society, where land (continents) has clear ownership deeds, but the vast ocean surrounding it was a "no-man's land." UNCLOS divided this ocean "property" into clearly demarcated zones (like apartment boundaries), specifying who can fish, drill, sail, or lay cables within each — and importantly, where everyone is free to pass through.

---

## 1.4 Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> The **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC 2006)** — also called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights"** — is an International Labour Organization (ILO) convention that consolidates and updates over 68 existing maritime labour standards into a single, legally binding instrument. It sets minimum working and living standards for seafarers worldwide.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Seafarer's Labour Law Handbook":**
If SOLAS protects the *ship* and MARPOL protects the *sea*, then MLC protects the *people* on the ship. Just like factories have Factories Act regulations (working hours, safety gear, canteen, rest rooms), MLC ensures every seafarer worldwide has guaranteed rights regarding wages, leave, accommodation, food, healthcare, and repatriation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Four Pillars" of MLC 2006:**
> 1. Minimum requirements for seafarers to work on a ship
> 2. Conditions of employment
> 3. Accommodation, recreational facilities, food & catering
> 4. Health protection, medical care, welfare & social security

---

## 1.5 Module-Level Interconnection Diagram (Conceptual Map)

```
MARITIME LAW (PENST863 MODULE 1)
        │
        ├── SHIP-LEVEL ─────► Surveys & Certification (SOLAS/MARPOL compliance)
        │                          │
        │                          └── Classification Societies (Class rules)
        │
        ├── SEA-LEVEL ───────► UNCLOS 1982 (Maritime Zones)
        │
        └── PEOPLE-LEVEL ───► MLC 2006 (Seafarer Rights)
                                   │
                                   ├── Title 1: Minimum Requirements
                                   ├── Title 2: Conditions of Employment
                                   ├── Title 3: Accommodation & Welfare
                                   └── Title 4: Health, Medical & Social Security
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Maritime Surveys

### 2.1.1 Statutory Surveys (Mandatory by International Convention)

| Survey Type | Convention | Purpose | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Survey** | SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC, Load Lines | Before ship enters service | Once (new-building) |
| **Renewal Survey** | SOLAS, MARPOL | Full compliance verification | Every 5 years |
| **Annual Survey** | SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines | General examination | Every 12 months |
| **Intermediate Survey** | SOLAS, MARPOL | Between renewals | Between 2nd & 3rd year |
| **Docking Survey** | Load Lines | Underwater hull inspection | At least twice in 5 yrs |
| **Unscheduled/Accident Survey** | All | Post-incident, damage | As required |

### 2.1.2 Class Surveys (By Classification Society)

- **Special Survey (Class)**: Major overhaul survey, every **5 years**.
- **Annual Class Survey**: Visual general examination, every **12 months**.
- **Continuous Survey (CS)**: Continuous machinery/hull inspection scheme.
- **Occasional Survey**: Damage, modification, or after grounding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Certificates Issued After Surveys:**
> - Safety Construction Certificate (SOLAS Ch. II-1, II-2)
> - Safety Equipment Certificate (SOLAS Ch. III)
> - Safety Radio Certificate (SOLAS Ch. IV)
> - International Oil Pollution Prevention (IOPP) Certificate
> - International Air Pollution Prevention (IAPP) Certificate
> - International Load Line Certificate (ICLL)
> - Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC)
> - Minimum Safe Manning Document
> - Class Certificate + Class Notation (e.g., ✠100A1 Bulk Carrier ESP)

---

## 2.2 Major Classification Societies (IACS Members)

> [!NOTE]
> **IACS = International Association of Classification Societies** — currently has **12 member societies** that collectively classify ~90% of world tonnage.

| Society | Abbreviation | Country | Founded |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lloyd's Register | **LR** | United Kingdom | **1760** (oldest) |
| Bureau Veritas | **BV** | France | 1828 |
| American Bureau of Shipping | **ABS** | USA | 1862 |
| DNV (Det Norske Veritas) | **DNV** | Norway | 1864 |
| Germanischer Lloyd | **GL** | Germany | 1867 (merged with DNV) |
| Nippon Kaiji Kyokai | **ClassNK / NK** | Japan | 1899 |
| Korean Register of Shipping | **KR** | South Korea | 1960 |
| Indian Register of Shipping | **IRS** | India | 1975 |
| China Classification Society | **CCS** | China | 1956 |
| Registro Italiano Navale | **RINA** | Italy | 1861 |
| Russian Maritime Register of Shipping | **RS** | Russia | 1913 |
| Polski Rejestr Statków | **PRS** | Poland | 1946 |

---

## 2.3 UNCLOS — Maritime Zones & Limits (Comprehensive Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **UNCLOS divides maritime space into zones with distinct sovereign rights and jurisdictions. Memorize the nautical mile (NM) limits — these are HIGH-YIELD for KTU exams.**

| Zone | Article | Breadth | Coastal State Rights | Other States' Rights |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal Waters** | Art. 8 | Landward of baselines | Full sovereignty (like land) | No right of innocent passage |
| **Territorial Sea (TS)** | Art. 3 | Up to **12 NM** from baseline | Sovereignty (subject to INN passage) | Innocent passage (Art. 17–26) |
| **Contiguous Zone (CZ)** | Art. 33 | **24 NM** (extends 12 NM beyond TS) | Control to prevent/ punish infringement of customs, fiscal, immigration, sanitary laws | Freedom of navigation, overflight |
| **Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ)** | Art. 55–75 | Up to **200 NM** from baseline | Sovereign rights for exploring, exploiting, conserving & managing natural resources (living & non-living) | Freedom of navigation, overflight, cables/pipelines, other lawful uses |
| **Continental Shelf (CS)** | Art. 76 | Up to **200 NM** natural; up to **350 NM** or **100 NM beyond 2500 m isobath** (whichever applies) | Sovereign rights over seabed & subsoil resources | Same freedoms as EEZ |
| **High Seas** | Art. 86–120 | Beyond EEZ | None (Res communis — common heritage) | Freedoms: navigation, overflight, cables, fishing, marine scientific research |
| **Area (International Seabed)** | Art. 1(1), 133 | Beyond national jurisdiction (deep seabed) | None | Regulated by **International Seabed Authority (ISA)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:**
> - Territorial Sea: Sovereignty is FULL but qualified (right of innocent passage exists for foreign vessels).
> - EEZ: Sovereignty is LIMITED — only over resources (fish, minerals, energy); navigation freedom remains for all.

**Contiguous Zone vs EEZ:**
- CZ is a **control zone** for enforcement of specific laws.
- EEZ is a **resource jurisdiction** zone.

### 2.3.1 UNCLOS Baseline Rules

- **Normal Baseline**: Low-water line along the coast (as shown on large-scale charts).
- **Straight Baselines**: Permissible for deeply indented coasts or fringed with islands (Art. 7).
- **Bay Closing Line**: Max **24 NM** between natural entrance points (Art. 10).
- **Islands**: An island can have its own TS, CZ, EEZ & CS (Art. 121). **Rocks** that cannot sustain human habitation or economic life have **NO EEZ or CS**.
- **Artificial Islands, Installations & Structures (AIIS)**: Have **no legal status** affecting baselines, TS, EEZ, or CS (Art. 60(8), 80).

### 2.3.2 Right of Innocent Passage (Territorial Sea)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Passage is "continuous and expeditious" through TS, without entering internal waters, calling at a roadstead/port, or threatening the coastal State.
> **Activities deemed NON-innocent (Art. 19):** Threat/use of force, weapons exercise, spying, propaganda, launching aircraft, submarine activities without surfacing, fishing, pollution, research, interference with coastal systems.

---

## 2.4 MLC 2006 — The Five Titles Architecture

### Title 1: Minimum Requirements for Seafarers to Work on a Ship

| Standard | Key Requirement |
|---|---|
| **Minimum Age** (Regulation 1.1) | **16 years** (general); **18 years** for night work, overtime, hazardous work |
| **Medical Certificate** (Reg. 1.2) | Valid certificate from approved medical practitioner; color-vision for watchkeepers |
| **Training & Qualifications** (Reg. 1.3) | STCW-compliant training certificates |
| **Recruitment** (Reg. 1.4) | Through certified recruitment agencies if from countries lacking adequate facilities |

### Title 2: Conditions of Employment

| Condition | Standard |
|---|---|
| **Seafarers' Employment Agreement (SEA)** | Written, signed, contains particulars (Reg. 2.1) |
| **Wages** | Paid at no greater than monthly intervals; directly to seafarer's bank account (Reg. 2.2) |
| **Hours of Work & Rest** | Max **14 hours/day** and **72 hours/week**; min **10 hours rest in 24 hrs** and **77 hours in 7 days** (Reg. 2.3) |
| **Entitlement to Leave** | Min annual paid leave = **2.5 calendar days per month** of service = **30 days/year** (Reg. 2.4) |
| **Repatriation** | Right to return at no cost; covered conditions include illness, injury, sale of ship, etc. (Reg. 2.5) |
| **Seafarer Compensation for Ship's Loss/Foundering** | Equivalent to **2 months wages** in case of total loss (Reg. 2.6) |
| **Manning Levels** | Sufficient for safe operation (Reg. 2.7) |
| **Career & Skill Development** | Opportunities for advancement (Reg. 2.8) |

### Title 3: Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food & Catering

| Element | Key Requirement |
|---|---|
| **Accommodation** (Reg. 3.1) | Adequate size, ventilation, heating, lighting, noise/vibration insulation, sanitary facilities |
| **Recreational Facilities** (Reg. 3.1) | Furnishings, social/welfare facilities, access to communication (phone/internet) |
| **Food & Catering** (Reg. 3.2 / 3.3) | Qualified cook (with certificate of competency); varied, nutritious meals; **free of charge** for crew |
| **Minimum Headroom** | **≥ 203 cm** (about 6 ft 8 in) |
| **Bed Dimensions** | Single berth min **198 cm × 80 cm**; mattress min **10 cm** thick |

### Title 4: Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare & Social Security

| Element | Key Requirement |
|---|---|
| **On-board Medical Care** (Reg. 4.1) | Medical cabinet, equipment, medicines per flag State requirements |
| **Medical Access Ashore** | Access to port-based medical facilities |
| **Sick Wages & Repatriation** (Reg. 2.5) | Seafarer entitled to repatriation if unfit for service |
| **Health & Safety** (Reg. 4.3) | Protective equipment, accident prevention, occupational safety |
| **Welfare** (Reg. 4.4) | Access to welfare facilities ashore (port-based welfare programs) |
| **Social Security** (Reg. 4.5) | Protection in branches: medical, sickness, unemployment, old-age, employment injury, family, maternity, invalidity, survivors' benefits |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Formula/Value Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision — covers every numeric value tested in KTU Module 1.**

| Concept | Value/Rule | Article/Reg. |
|---|---|---|
| Territorial Sea breadth | **12 NM** | UNCLOS Art. 3 |
| Contiguous Zone breadth | **24 NM** | UNCLOS Art. 33 |
| EEZ breadth | **200 NM** | UNCLOS Art. 57 |
| Continental Shelf (normal) | **200 NM** | UNCLOS Art. 76 |
| Continental Shelf (extended) | **350 NM** or **100 NM beyond 2500 m isobath** | UNCLOS Art. 76(4)–(6) |
| Bay Closing Line | **24 NM** | UNCLOS Art. 10 |
| Minimum Age (MLC) | **16 years** general; **18 years** for hazardous/night work | MLC Reg. 1.1 |
| Max Daily Working Hours | **14 hours** | MLC Reg. 2.3 |
| Max Weekly Working Hours | **72 hours** | MLC Reg. 2.3 |
| Min Daily Rest Hours | **10 hours in any 24 h** | MLC Reg. 2.3 |
| Min Weekly Rest Hours | **77 hours in 7 days** | MLC Reg. 2.3 |
| Min Annual Paid Leave | **30 days/year (2.5 days/month)** | MLC Reg. 2.4 |
| Ship Loss Compensation | **2 months' wages** | MLC Reg. 2.6 |
| Accommodation Headroom | **≥ 203 cm** | MLC Reg. 3.1 |
| Single Berth Size | **198 cm × 80 cm** | MLC Reg. 3.1 |
| Mattress Thickness | **≥ 10 cm** | MLC Reg. 3.1 |
| UNCLOS Adoption | 1982 | — |
| UNCLOS Entry into Force | **16 November 1994** | — |
| MLC Adoption & Entry into Force | **23 February 2006** | — |
| MLC Effective Date | **20 August 2013** | — |
| Number of IACS Members | **12** (as of 2024) | — |
| Oldest Classification Society | **Lloyd's Register (1760)** | — |
| Largest Classification Society | **DNV (by tonnage classified)** | — |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ship Design**: Naval architects use classification rules to design hull scantlings (plate thickness, frame spacing).
- **Offshore Structures**: Class rules apply to FPSOs, drilling rigs, jack-ups.
- **Port State Control (PSC)**: Officers in ports like Singapore, Rotterdam check validity of certificates.
- **Insurance Premiums**: P&I Clubs and Hull insurers base premiums partly on class status.
- **EEZ Disputes**: UNCLOS zones determine fishing rights, OCS oil blocks, submarine cable routing (e.g., recent South China Sea disputes).
- **Seafarer Welfare**: MLC inspections directly affect flag state performance in PSC inspections and ILO complaint mechanisms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Survey & Certification Process Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Understand the chronological process from ship construction to operational certification.

### Step 1 — New-Building Survey (Pre-Launch)

$$
\text{Stage}_1 : \text{Keel Laying} \rightarrow \text{Steel Survey} \rightarrow \text{Machinery Survey} \rightarrow \text{Outfitting Survey}
$$

The classification surveyor inspects:
- Hull steel quality, weld testing (radiographic, ultrasonic).
- Main engine, auxiliary machinery on test bed.
- Electrical installations, automation systems.
- Lifeboats, fire-fighting equipment (statutory survey parallel).

### Step 2 — Initial Statutory Surveys (Concurrent)

After successful class survey, **flag State (or RO delegated)** conducts:
- SOLAS initial survey
- MARPOL initial survey
- Load Lines initial survey
- MLC inspection (declaration of maritime labour compliance — DMLC)

### Step 3 — Issuance of Certificates (All Valid for 5 Years, with Annual/Intermediate)

$$
\text{Validity Period} = 5 \text{ years}, \quad \text{Renewed} = \text{Every 5 yrs}, \quad \text{Annual Check} = \text{Yearly}
$$

Intermediate Survey is held between the **2nd and 3rd anniversary** of the certificate (i.e., within 3 months either side of the 3rd anniversary date).

### Step 4 — Continuous Maintenance

- Owner maintains ship "in class" through planned maintenance system (PMS).
- Class surveyor issues **"Recommendation"** if deficiencies found.
- If not rectified within due date → class suspension → commercial disaster.

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanatory Logic:** Surveys are not one-time events; they are cyclical. A ship without valid certificates cannot trade; without valid class, insurers withdraw cover. Hence, the "Survey Cycle" is the operational heartbeat of every vessel.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough: UNCLOS Zone Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Type:** Given coastal baseline geometry, determine a country's maritime entitlements.

### Worked Example 1: A country with a straight coastline on the open sea

$$
\text{Baseline} = \text{low-water line marked on chart}
$$

**Step 1 — Territorial Sea (TS):**
$$
\text{Outer Limit of TS} = \text{Baseline} + 12 \text{ NM} \quad (\text{Article 3})
$$

**Step 2 — Contiguous Zone (CZ):**
$$
\text{Outer Limit of CZ} = \text{Baseline} + 12 + 12 = 24 \text{ NM} \quad (\text{Article 33})
$$

**Step 3 — Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ):**
$$
\text{Outer Limit of EEZ} = \text{Baseline} + 200 \text{ NM} \quad (\text{Article 57})
$$

**Step 4 — Continental Shelf (CS):**
$$
\text{Outer Limit of CS} = \max\left(200 \text{ NM}, \ 350 \text{ NM}, \ \text{Baseline} + 100 \text{ NM beyond 2500 m isobath}\right)
$$

### Worked Example 2: A bay with two natural entrance points 18 NM apart

**Step 1 — Internal Waters within Bay:**
The bay is enclosed within the headlands. The closing line of **18 NM** ≤ **24 NM** allowed limit (Art. 10), so the waters inside are **internal waters**.

**Step 2 — TS measured from baseline:**
For the bay, baselines = low-water line of the shore (Art. 9).
TS extends **12 NM outward from each headland** of the bay.

**Step 3 — CZ, EEZ, CS** measured from the same baselines following Art. 33, 57, 76.

### Worked Example 3: Bays of two states facing each other across a 30 NM-wide strait

If both coasts are directly opposite and the strait is ≤ **24 NM** at any point, both states can claim **internal waters up to the median line** but the central waterway remains open to **non-suspendable innocent passage** (Art. 45 — similar to TSS).

$$
\text{Median Line} = \frac{\text{Point}_A + \text{Point}_B}{2}
$$

### Worked Example 4: Island "X" that is a rock (no human habitation, no economic activity)

By Art. 121(3):
$$
\text{TS} = 12 \text{ NM}; \quad \text{CZ, EEZ, CS} = \text{None (zero)}
$$

### Worked Example 5: MLC — Working Hours Audit

A Chief Officer claims he worked **84 hours in 7 days**. Is this compliant?

$$
\text{Max Allowed per week} = 72 \text{ hours} \quad (\text{Reg. 2.3, Standard A2.3})
$$

$$
\text{Excess} = 84 - 72 = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation:** The Chief Officer has breached MLC Reg. 2.3. The master must record the excess in the rest-hours log, schedule compensatory rest, and (if persistent) report to flag State. PSC inspectors will flag this violation.

### Worked Example 6: MLC — Annual Leave Entitlement

A seafarer signs off after **8 months of continuous service**.

$$
\text{Leave Earned} = 8 \times 2.5 = 20 \text{ calendar days} \quad (\text{Reg. 2.4})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation:** Although the MLC minimum is 30 days/year (i.e., 2.5 days/month), the seafarer has only completed 8 months, so pro-rata entitlement is 20 days. The remaining 10 days complete on subsequent service or are paid as "leave encashment" depending on the SEA.

### Worked Example 7: MLC — Ship Loss Compensation

A bulk carrier founders in the Indian Ocean. The Chief Engineer's monthly wage was **USD 4,500**.

$$
\text{Compensation} = 2 \times 4500 = \text{USD } 9{,}000 \quad (\text{Reg. 2.6})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation:** Reg. 2.6 guarantees the seafarer (or dependents) compensation equal to at least 2 months' wages if the ship is lost or foundered. This is independent of any contractual insurance, contractual claim, or accident compensation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Survey × Class × UNCLOS × MLC Interrelations

| Function | Survey & Cert. | Classification | UNCLOS | MLC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Legal Authority** | Flag State + ROs | Private society | Treaty-based | ILO Convention |
| **Subject Matter** | Ship & equipment | Ship integrity | Sea zones & resources | Seafarers' rights |
| **Compliance Enforced Through** | PSC inspections, certificates | Class maintenance | Coastal state enforcement | DMLC Part I & II |
| **Failure Consequence** | Detention, de-flagging | Class withdrawal, insurance loss | Diplomatic disputes, sanctions | Detention, sanctions |
| **Tied With** | SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines | IACS rules | UN Charter, ITLOS | STCW, ILO Conventions |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough: Classification Notation Decoding

Example notation: **✠ 100A1 Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **✠** | Maltese Cross — society mark (Lloyd's Register) |
| **100** | 100A1 means vessel maintained in accordance with LR Rules and classification character assigned |
| **A1** | Hull and machinery in good and efficient condition |
| **ESP** | Enhanced Survey Programme (for tankers/bulk carriers > 20 years) |
| **CSR** | Common Structural Rules (post-2006 oil tankers/bulk carriers) |
| **BC-A** | Bulk Carrier — Type A (double-side skin alternative design) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Explanation:** Each symbol in class notation is meaningful. Reading class notation is a high-yield KTU question.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**
> **Define a Classification Society. List any four major classification societies with their countries of origin.**

**Model Answer:**
A classification society is a non-governmental technical organization that establishes and applies rules for the design, construction, and survey of ships and offshore structures, certifying that they meet its standards.

| Society | Country |
|---|---|
| Lloyd's Register (LR) | United Kingdom |
| Bureau Veritas (BV) | France |
| American Bureau of Shipping (ABS) | USA |
| DNV | Norway |

*(Any 4 from the 12 IACS members are acceptable.)*

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2023, 3 Marks]**
> **What is the significance of UNCLOS in international maritime law?**

**Model Answer:**
UNCLOS 1982 is the comprehensive international treaty that governs all uses of the world's oceans. Its significance lies in:
1. Establishing **legal order** over maritime spaces (zones with clear boundaries).
2. Defining **rights and duties** of coastal and flag states.
3. Codifying **freedom of navigation** and **right of innocent passage**.
4. Creating the **International Seabed Authority (ISA)** for deep seabed resources.
5. Establishing **International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea (ITLOS)** for dispute resolution.
6. Promoting **equitable resource sharing** between developed and developing nations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Instructions:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both carry equal marks (7 marks each sub-part).

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the various types of surveys conducted on a merchant ship during its operational life. Distinguish between **statutory surveys** and **classification surveys**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Surveys are systematic inspections to verify compliance with international rules and regulations. They are of two broad categories:

**Statutory Surveys** are mandatory under international conventions and enforced by the flag State or its delegated Recognized Organization (RO). They include:
- **Initial Survey** — conducted when a ship is new or before re-registration.
- **Annual Survey** — general verification of compliance every 12 months.
- **Intermediate Survey** — between 2nd and 3rd anniversary of certificate.
- **Renewal / Periodical Survey** — comprehensive survey every 5 years for renewal.
- **Docking Survey** — underwater hull inspection, at least twice in 5 years.
- **Unscheduled / Occasional Surveys** — after damage, grounding, or major repair.

**Classification Surveys** are performed by classification societies (e.g., LR, ABS, DNV) under their own rules, and ensure that the ship meets the society's technical standards. They include:
- **Special Survey** — every 5 years (the most extensive class survey).
- **Annual Class Survey** — visual check yearly.
- **Continuous Survey (CS)** — continuous scheme replacing special/annual surveys.
- **Occasional Survey** — for damages, modifications, repairs.

**Distinction Table:**

| Aspect | Statutory Survey | Classification Survey |
|---|---|---|
| Authority | Flag State / RO | Classification Society |
| Mandatory? | Yes (by law/convention) | Voluntary (commercial necessity) |
| Purpose | Compliance with conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL) | Compliance with class rules |
| Outcome | Statutory Certificates | Class Certificate / Notation |
| Frequency | Annual/Intermediate/Renewal | Annual/Special |

---

**(b)** Discuss the role, functions, and importance of **Classification Societies** in the international maritime industry. Highlight the role of IACS. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Classification Societies are independent, non-governmental organizations that develop and apply technical standards (rules) for the design, construction, and maintenance of ships and offshore units, certifying compliance through a "class notation."

**Functions:**
1. **Rule Development** — Continuous updating of technical rules based on research and casualty feedback.
2. **New-building Surveys** — Verification of design, materials, workmanship during construction.
3. **Class Surveys** — Periodic inspections (annual, special, continuous).
4. **Certification** — Issue of Classification Certificate and statutory certificates on behalf of flag States (as Recognized Organizations).
5. **Statutory Services** — On delegation from flag States, conduct surveys under SOLAS, MARPOL, Load Lines, MLC.
6. **Research & Development** — Hydrodynamic testing, structural analysis, FEM modeling.
7. **Advisory Services** — Risk assessment, ISM audits, ISO certifications, decarbonization consulting.

**Importance:**
- Provides a **common global standard** for ship quality.
- Reduces risk for **insurers, charterers, banks, and cargo owners**.
- Essential for **port state control** clearance.
- Supports **maritime safety** and **environmental protection**.
- **IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** — founded 1969, currently has 12 members including LR, BV, ABS, DNV, ClassNK, KR, etc. IACS:
  - Acts as a **forum for harmonization** of rules.
  - Sets **Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)**.
  - Provides unified technical resolutions and unified interpretations.
  - Recognized by **IMO** as a consultative body.
  - Together classifies over **90% of world merchant tonnage**.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Describe the **maritime zones** established under **UNCLOS 1982**. Explain the rights of coastal states and other states in each zone. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

UNCLOS 1982 divides maritime space into **9 distinct zones**, each with specific legal status:

| Zone | Breadth | Coastal State Rights | Other States' Rights |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal Waters** | Landward of baselines | Full sovereignty like land | No right of innocent passage |
| **Territorial Sea** | 0–12 NM | Full sovereignty (subject to innocent passage) | Innocent passage (Art. 17–19) |
| **Contiguous Zone** | 12–24 NM | Control for customs, fiscal, immigration, sanitary laws | Navigation, overflight |
| **EEZ** | 0–200 NM | Sovereign rights over living & non-living resources; jurisdiction over artificial islands, marine scientific research, environmental protection | Freedom of navigation, overflight, cables/pipelines, fishing (subject to coastal state regulations) |
| **Continental Shelf** | Up to 200/350 NM | Sovereign rights over seabed & subsoil resources | Cables/pipelines (subject to coastal state consent for delimitation) |
| **High Seas** | Beyond EEZ | None — res communis | Six freedoms: navigation, overflight, cables, fishing, marine scientific research, artificial islands |
| **The Area** | Deep seabed beyond national jurisdiction | None | Administered by **ISA** for benefit of mankind |

**Special Provisions:**
- **Straits used for international navigation**: Non-suspendable transit passage (Art. 37–44).
- **Archipelagic States** (e.g., Indonesia, Philippines): Archipelagic baselines and archipelagic sea lanes (Art. 46–54).
- **Islands**: Generate full maritime zones; **Rocks** generate only TS (Art. 121).
- **Enclosed / Semi-enclosed Seas**: Special cooperation obligations (Art. 123).

**Significance:** The EEZ concept is revolutionary — extending coastal state jurisdiction to vast ocean areas for resource control while preserving global navigation freedoms.

---

**(b)** Explain the **Maritime Labour Convention (MLC) 2006** in detail. Discuss its applicability, the four major titles, and the **minimum requirements** for a seafarer to work on a ship. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Background:**
The MLC 2006 was adopted at the 94th (Maritime) Session of the International Labour Conference on **23 February 2006** and entered into force on **20 August 2013**. Often called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights"**, it consolidates over 68 earlier ILO conventions and recommendations.

**Applicability:**
- Applies to **all ships**, whether publicly or privately owned, engaged in commercial activities, except ships engaged in fishing or similar activities and traditional ships.
- Applies to **all seafarers** working on such ships.

**Five Titles:**

1. **Title 1 — Minimum Requirements for Seafarers to Work on a Ship**
2. **Title 2 — Conditions of Employment**
3. **Title 3 — Accommodation, Recreational Facilities, Food & Catering**
4. **Title 4 — Health Protection, Medical Care, Welfare & Social Security**
5. **Title 5 — Compliance and Enforcement**

**Minimum Requirements (Title 1):**

| Regulation | Key Standard |
|---|---|
| **Reg. 1.1 — Minimum Age** | **16 years** for general work; **18 years** for night work, overtime, or hazardous work. |
| **Reg. 1.2 — Medical Fitness** | Seafarers must hold a valid medical certificate issued by a practitioner approved by the flag State; includes eyesight/hearing; watchkeepers must pass color-vision test. |
| **Reg. 1.3 — Training & Qualifications** | Must be trained, certified, and competent per **STCW Convention** (as amended). |
| **Reg. 1.4 — Recruitment & Placement** | Use of certified recruitment agencies; prohibits charging fees to seafarers. |

**Compliance Mechanism:**
- **Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC)** — Part I (flag State) + Part II (shipowner).
- **Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC)** — issued after inspection, valid for **5 years**.
- **Annual inspections** + **interim inspections** for new ships.
- **On-board complaints procedure** mandatory on every ship.

**Significance:**
- Universal application — once ratified, applies to all ships in ratifying country's ports.
- Provides seafarers with **port-of-call protection**.
- Strengthened by the **ILO MLC Complaint Procedure**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Module 1):**
> 1. **Confusing CZ and EEZ breadth**: CZ = **24 NM**, EEZ = **200 NM**. Students often write "EEZ is 24 NM" — instant 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Wrong Article Numbers**: UNCLOS Article 3 (TS), Article 33 (CZ), Article 57 (EEZ), Article 76 (CS). Mix-up loses marks.
> 3. **MLC Hour Limits**: Maximum **14 hours/day** and **72 hours/week** of WORK. Minimum **10 hours/24h** and **77 hours/7 days** of REST. Writing these backwards costs 1 mark easily.
> 4. **Class Survey vs Statutory Survey**: Forgetting the word "voluntary/commercial" for class surveys is a frequent deduction.
> 5. **Minimum Age**: 16 years general, **18 years** for night work / hazardous — students often give only one value.
> 6. **Continental Shelf**: Many students forget that CS extends up to **350 NM** under the "Irish formula" — beyond 200 NM requires submission to CLCS.
> 7. **Innocent Passage**: Forgetting that submarines must **surface** and show flag during innocent passage (Art. 20).
> 8. **Ship Loss Compensation**: Reg. 2.6 = **2 months' wages**, not 2 weeks — common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

### A. Survey & Certification
- [ ] 5-year statutory certificate cycle with annual + intermediate inspections.
- [ ] Class surveys are voluntary; statutory surveys are mandatory.
- [ ] Docking survey: minimum twice in 5 years.
- [ ] Recognized Organizations (ROs) act on behalf of flag State.

### B. Classification Societies
- [ ] Lloyd's Register (1760) is the **oldest**.
- [ ] DNV and Lloyd's Register are among the largest by tonnage.
- [ ] IACS has **12 members** (as of 2024).
- [ ] Classification notation **100A1** at LR means ship in full compliance with rules.
- [ ] Class withdrawal = insurance refusal = operational paralysis.

### C. UNCLOS 1982
- [ ] TS = **12 NM**, CZ = **24 NM**, EEZ = **200 NM**, CS = up to **350 NM**.
- [ ] Bay closing line limit = **24 NM**.
- [ ] EEZ = sovereign rights over resources (not full sovereignty).
- [ ] TS = full sovereignty + innocent passage for foreign ships.
- [ ] Rocks have only TS; islands have full zones (Art. 121).
- [ ] AIIS have **no territorial status**.
- [ ] High Seas freedoms = **6 freedoms** (navigation, overflight, cables/pipelines, fishing, marine scientific research, artificial islands).
- [ ] **ISA** regulates the Area (international seabed).
- [ ] **ITLOS** in Hamburg is the dispute-resolution body.

### D. MLC 2006 — Five Titles
- [ ] **Title 1**: Min age 16/18; medical cert; STCW training; recruitment standards.
- [ ] **Title 2**: SEA in writing; max 14/72 hours; min 10/77 hours rest; 2.5 days/month leave (= 30 days/year); 2 months wages on ship loss.
- [ ] **Title 3**: Headroom ≥ 203 cm; berth ≥ 198 × 80 cm; mattress ≥ 10 cm; qualified cook; free food.
- [ ] **Title 4**: On-board medical cabinet; shore medical access; social security 9 branches; PPE; accident prevention.
- [ ] **Title 5**: MLC certificate 5 years; DMLC Parts I & II; PSC inspections.

### E. Numerical Values (HIGH YIELD)
- [ ] 12 / 24 / 200 / 350 NM
- [ ] 16 / 18 years
- [ ] 14 / 72 / 10 / 77 hours
- [ ] 2.5 / 30 days
- [ ] 203 / 198 / 80 / 10 cm
- [ ] 2 months wages
- [ ] 12 IACS members
- [ ] 1760 (LR)

### F. Key Acronyms
- [ ] **IACS**, **RO**, **DMLC**, **MLC**, **SEA**, **STCW**, **PSC**, **P&I**, **EEZ**, **TS**, **CZ**, **CS**, **ISA**, **ITLOS**, **CLCS**
