topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SOLAS: Introduction, subdivision and stability, fire protection, detection and extinction, life saving appliances and arrangements, carriage of grain, carriage of dangerous goods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The SOLAS Convention — Formal Definition

The **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS)** is the most important international treaty concerning the safety of merchant ships. The first version was adopted in **1914** in response to the *Titanic* disaster of 1912. The current version is the **SOLAS 1974 Convention**, which came into force on **25 May 1980**, and has been amended many times to keep pace with technical and operational developments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS 1974** is the principal international convention governing the safety of merchant ships engaged on international voyages. It is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, a specialized agency of the United Nations headquartered in **London, United Kingdom**.

The Convention consists of:
- **12 Chapters** (Chapters I–XII, with Chapter II split into II-1 and II-2)
- **Annex of Regulations**
- Associated **Codes** (FSS Code, LSA Code, IMDG Code, Grain Code, etc.)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Building Code" of the Sea**
> Just as a city building code specifies the number of fire exits, the strength of walls, the width of staircases, and the installation of sprinklers in a building, **SOLAS specifies how a ship must be built, equipped, and operated** so that lives at sea are protected. The ship is the "building," the ocean is the "occupancy," and SOLAS is the safety code enforced by flag states and port state control officers.

### 1.3 Scope of Module 2 Topics

The following Chapters of SOLAS 1974 are covered under Module 2:

| Chapter | Subject Area |
|:-------:|:-------------|
| General | Introduction, structure, applicability, contracting government duties |
| **Chapter II-1** | Construction — **Subdivision and Stability**, machinery and electrical installations |
| **Chapter II-2** | Construction — **Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction** |
| **Chapter III** | **Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements** |
| **Chapter VI** | **Carriage of Grain** |
| **Chapter VII** | **Carriage of Dangerous Goods** |

### 1.4 Applicability of SOLAS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SOLAS applies to the following types of ships engaged on international voyages:
> - **Passenger ships** of any size (regardless of tonnage)
> - **Cargo ships** of **300 GT and above**

A ship is on an **international voyage** when it travels between ports of different countries. SOLAS does NOT generally apply to:
- Warships
- Ships not propelled by mechanical means
- Pleasure yachts not engaged in trade
- Fishing vessels (governed by separate FAO/IMO instruments)
- Cargo ships below 300 GT on international voyages

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 SOLAS Chapter II-1 — Subdivision and Stability

#### 2.1.1 Concept of Subdivision

**Subdivision** means dividing the hull of a ship into a number of **watertight compartments** using **watertight bulkheads** (transverse and longitudinal). The purpose is to ensure that if one or two compartments are flooded due to collision or grounding, the ship does not sink.

> [!NOTE]
> **Watertight Bulkhead**: A metal partition running across (transverse) or along (longitudinal) the ship designed to prevent water passage between compartments. Each bulkhead must be tested to a head of water at least **0.3 m above the bulkhead deck** at the deepest subdivision load line.

#### 2.1.2 Bulkhead Decks and the Damage Stability Line

- **Bulkhead Deck**: The deck up to which the watertight bulkheads are carried.
- **Margin Line**: A line drawn parallel to the bulkhead deck at a distance of **76 mm (3 inches)** below it.
- **Deepest Subdivision Load Line (DSLL)**: The waterline corresponding to the deepest permissible draught for the ship.

The **permissible floodable length** at any point is determined so that, with the assumed compartment flooded, the margin line is not submerged.

#### 2.1.3 Floodable Length

**Floodable length** at any point on the ship is the maximum length of the ship, having that point as its centre, which, under the assumed flooded condition, may be flooded without submerging the margin line.

If $L_s$ is the length of the ship, and the flooding of a compartment of length $\ell$ extends from $x_1$ to $x_2$, the **permissible length** of the compartment at that location is obtained from the floodable length curve.

#### 2.1.4 Permeability (μ)

The **permeability** $\mu$ of a space is the ratio of the volume within that space which can be occupied by water to the total volume of that space.

| Space | Permeability (μ) |
|:------|:-----------------:|
| Machinery spaces | **0.85** |
| Accommodation spaces | **0.95** |
| Dry cargo spaces | **0.70** |
| Tanks intended to carry liquids | **0 or 0.95** (depending on whether full or empty) |

#### 2.1.5 Factor of Subdivision (f)

The **factor of subdivision** $f$ is a factor representing the degree of subdivision required. It depends on the ship's length and the number of passengers.

For **cargo ships**, the factor of subdivision is:
$$f = 0.04 + \frac{L}{10{,}000}$$
where $L$ is the moulded length (in metres). Note that for $L < 100$ m, $f = 0.05$.

#### 2.1.6 Required Subdivision Index (R)

The **Required Subdivision Index R** is the level of survival capability the ship must have. It is calculated based on the length $L$, number of passengers $N$, and a criterion function $s_i$ depending on the stage of flooding.

The standard SOLAS formulation is:

$$R = 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{P}{1000} \cdot F}}$$

where $P$ is the criterion (passenger criterion) and $F$ is the floodable length factor.

A simplified expression commonly cited in maritime education:
$$R = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \frac{P}{100}}$$

The interpretation is:
- A ship with a **higher R** must be **more subdivided**.
- A ship with a **higher R** has **more compartments**, each of which must be smaller.

#### 2.1.7 Attained Subdivision Index (A)

The **Attained Subdivision Index A** is the actual capability of the ship, determined by:
- The geometry of the watertight compartments,
- The assumed damage scenarios (number of compartments flooded),
- The resulting residual stability (GZ curve, range of positive stability ≥ 20°, heeling angle ≤ 25°),
- Final equilibrium draught.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Rule**: A ship is considered to comply with damage stability requirements if and only if:
> $$A \geq R$$

If **A is less than R**, the ship fails the SOLAS damage stability requirement. The ship operator must either:
- Increase the size of the watertight compartments (more compartments),
- Install additional fixed bilge pumps,
- Reduce the permissible draught, or
- Increase the margin line elevation.

#### 2.1.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Subdivision & Stability

| Concept | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Value |
|:--------|:------:|:---------------------|:--------------:|
| Factor of subdivision | $f$ | $f = 0.04 + \dfrac{L}{10{,}000}$ | 0.33 to 0.70 |
| Required Index | $R$ | Function of $L, N, f$ | 0.40 – 0.85 |
| Attained Index | $A$ | Sum of contributions from each compartment | Must be $\geq R$ |
| Permeability | $\mu$ | Volume of water / Total volume | 0.70 – 0.95 |
| Margin line | – | 76 mm below bulkhead deck | 0.076 m |
| Range of positive stability | – | Heel angle for positive GZ | $\geq 20°$ |
| Maximum GZ angle | – | Peak of GZ curve | 25° to 40° |
| Heeling angle | – | Final heel after flooding | $\leq 25°$ |

### 2.2 SOLAS Chapter II-2 — Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction

#### 2.2.1 Structural Fire Protection

The aim of fire protection in SOLAS Chapter II-2 is to:
1. **Prevent fire** (segregation, structural protection).
2. **Detect fire** (detection systems).
3. **Suppress fire** (extinguishing systems).
4. **Provide means of escape**.

#### 2.2.2 Fire Zones

A **fire zone** is a section of the ship separated from the rest by steel boundaries (typically A-class divisions). SOLAS divides passenger ships into main vertical fire zones, each not exceeding **40 m** in length, to limit the spread of fire and smoke.

#### 2.2.3 Fire Classifications (per FSS Code and IMO standards)

| Class | Fuel Type | Extinguishing Agent |
|:-----:|:----------|:--------------------|
| **A** | Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth) | Water, foam |
| **B** | Flammable liquids (oil, fuel, paint) | Foam, CO₂, dry powder |
| **C** | Flammable gases (LPG, LNG, methane) | CO₂, dry powder |
| **D** | Combustible metals (magnesium, sodium) | Special dry powder |
| **K** (or F) | Cooking oils and fats | Wet chemical |

#### 2.2.4 Fire Detection Systems

| System | Application | Response Time |
|:-------|:------------|:--------------:|
| Smoke detector (ionization) | Accommodation, corridors | Seconds |
| Heat detector (fixed temperature) | Galleys, machinery | Seconds |
| Flame detector (IR/UV) | Engine rooms, helidecks | Milliseconds |
| Aspirating smoke detector (ASD) | Cargo holds, control rooms | Very fast |

#### 2.2.5 Fire Extinction Systems

| System | Use | Notes |
|:-------|:----|:------|
| Fixed CO₂ system | Engine rooms, pump rooms | Releases 85% CO₂; personnel must evacuate |
| Fixed foam system | Chemical tankers, deck fires | AFFF (Aqueous Film Forming Foam) |
| Water mist system | Engine rooms, accommodation | Low pressure, environment-friendly |
| Sprinkler system | Accommodation, balconies | Automatic activation at 68–79°C |
| Dry chemical powder | LPG / LNG carriers, gas fires | Interrupts chemical reaction |
| Fire main system | All areas | Hydrants, hoses, international shore connection |
| Portable extinguishers | All areas | Carried in accommodation and machinery |

> [!NOTE]
> **FSS Code (Fire Safety Systems Code)** — adopted under SOLAS Chapter II-2 Regulation 3, gives detailed international standards for fire-extinguishing systems.

### 2.3 SOLAS Chapter III — Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements

#### 2.3.1 LSA Code

The **Life-Saving Appliance (LSA) Code** is an international code for life-saving appliances required by SOLAS Chapter III, made mandatory by Regulation 34.

#### 2.3.2 Categories of Life-Saving Appliances

| Category | Equipment | Minimum Requirement |
|:---------|:----------|:--------------------|
| **Survival craft** | Lifeboats (fully enclosed / partially enclosed) | 100% persons on each side |
| **Survival craft** | Liferafts (inflatable) | 100% persons total |
| **Rescue boats** | Rescue boat | At least 1 per ship |
| **Personal** | Lifejackets | 100% + 5% for crew |
| **Personal** | Immersion suits | 100% for each lifeboat person |
| **Visual signals** | Life buoys (with light, smoke) | 8 minimum |
| **Communication** | EPIRB, SART, VHF radio | 1 EPIRB, 2 SARTs |
| **Launching** | Davits, falls, winches | For each lifeboat |

#### 2.3.3 Muster List and Emergency Drills

- A **Muster List** must be posted in conspicuous places showing each crew member's station, duties, and survival craft assignment.
- **Abandon ship drills** must be conducted **monthly**, and each crew member must participate in at least one drill every month.
- **Fire drills** must also be conducted monthly.

### 2.4 SOLAS Chapter VI — Carriage of Grain

#### 2.4.1 Why Grain Carriage is Special

Bulk grain shifts when the ship rolls. If not properly secured, the free surface effect of the shifted grain can capsize the ship. Grain is therefore treated differently from other bulk cargo.

#### 2.4.2 International Grain Code

The **International Code for the Safe Carriage of Grain in Bulk** is mandatory under SOLAS Chapter VI. It specifies:
- **Filling ratio** of holds (typically not exceeding a stated fraction).
- **Trimming** procedures (grain must be trimmed to eliminate voids).
- **Shifting boards** — vertical partitions to resist grain shifting.
- **Stability criteria** — residual GM and righting lever curves.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A ship loading grain in bulk must have a **Document of Authorization** issued by the flag state, certifying compliance with the International Grain Code.

#### 2.4.3 Stability Requirements for Grain Ships

| Parameter | Typical Requirement |
|:----------|:--------------------|
| Area under GZ curve (0° to 40°) | $\geq 0.075$ m·rad |
| Area under GZ curve (30° to 40°) | $\geq 0.015$ m·rad |
| Maximum GZ value | $\geq 0.20$ m |
| Range of positive stability | $\geq 30°$ |
| Angle of vanishing stability | $\geq 55°$ |

### 2.5 SOLAS Chapter VII — Carriage of Dangerous Goods

#### 2.5.1 IMDG Code

SOLAS Chapter VII Regulation 1 makes the **International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code** mandatory. The IMDG Code classifies dangerous goods into **9 Classes**.

| Class | Category | Example |
|:-----:|:---------|:--------|
| 1 | Explosives | Dynamite, fireworks |
| 2 | Gases | LPG, oxygen, chlorine |
| 3 | Flammable liquids | Petrol, diesel, methanol |
| 4 | Flammable solids | Matches, sodium |
| 5 | Oxidizers / Organic peroxides | Hydrogen peroxide |
| 6 | Toxic / Infectious substances | Cyanide, pesticides |
| 7 | Radioactive material | Uranium, medical isotopes |
| 8 | Corrosives | Sulfuric acid, caustic soda |
| 9 | Miscellaneous dangerous goods | Lithium batteries, asbestos |

#### 2.5.2 Documentation and Marking

- **Dangerous Goods Manifest / Stowage Plan** must be carried on board.
- Each package must display the **UN Number**, **Proper Shipping Name**, and **Hazard Class Label**.
- Stowage and segregation must comply with the IMDG Code.

> [!NOTE]
> **MARPOL Annex III** complements SOLAS Chapter VII by preventing pollution by harmful substances carried in packaged form.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Required Subdivision Index (R)

The **Required Subdivision Index (R)** in SOLAS depends on:
- Length of the ship ($L$),
- Number of passengers ($N_s$),
- Service notation, and
- The criterion standard $s_i$.

The general SOLAS formulation in Reg. II-1/6 is:

$$R = 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \frac{P \cdot F}{1000}}}$$

where:
- $P$ is the passenger-related criterion,
- $F$ is a floodable length factor (function of $L$ and $N$).

A simplified educational version used in maritime colleges:

$$R = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \frac{P}{100}}$$

#### Worked Derivation

Let us evaluate $R$ for a typical passenger ship with $P = 60$.

Step 1: Substitute $P = 60$:

$$R = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + \frac{60}{100}}$$

Step 2: Compute the fraction:

$$\frac{60}{100} = 0.60$$

Step 3: Add 1:

$$1 + 0.60 = 1.60$$

Step 4: Take the reciprocal:

$$\frac{1}{1.60} = 0.625$$

Step 5: Subtract from 1:

$$R = 1 - 0.625 = 0.375$$

So a passenger ship with $P = 60$ requires $R = 0.375$.

If the attained subdivision index $A$ is computed (by summing contributions from each compartment) and found to be $A = 0.42$, then:

$$A = 0.42 \geq R = 0.375 \quad \text{(COMPLIES)}$$

If, however, $A = 0.30$, then:

$$A = 0.30 < R = 0.375 \quad \text{(DOES NOT COMPLY — redesign required)}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Factor of Subdivision

For a cargo ship of moulded length $L = 150$ m:

$$f = 0.04 + \frac{L}{10{,}000}$$

Substituting:

$$f = 0.04 + \frac{150}{10{,}000}$$

$$f = 0.04 + 0.015$$

$$f = 0.055$$

This means the ship needs very modest subdivision (typical for cargo ships below 100 m, $f = 0.05$).

For a passenger ship of length $L = 250$ m, $N = 1200$ passengers:

The factor of subdivision $f$ is computed from a more complex SOLAS table:

$$f = 0.50 + \frac{L}{6000} - \frac{N}{20{,}000}$$

For $L = 250$ m, $N = 1200$:

$$f = 0.50 + \frac{250}{6000} - \frac{1200}{20{,}000}$$

$$f = 0.50 + 0.0417 - 0.06$$

$$f = 0.4817$$

This means each passenger compartment must have a length such that the ship can survive flooding in several locations.

### 3.3 Damage Stability Calculation — Sample Walk-Through

For a passenger ship after flooding of a single compartment:
1. Compute the **lost buoyancy** = displaced volume of flooded compartment.
2. Apply the **permeability** $\mu$ to find the actual volume of water.
3. Calculate the new waterline based on residual buoyancy.
4. Verify the **margin line** is not submerged.
5. Compute the **residual GZ curve**.
6. Verify:
   - Range of positive stability $\geq 20°$
   - Max GZ occurs between $20°$ to $40°$
   - Area under GZ curve $\geq 0.075$ m·rad

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of SOLAS Chapters

| Parameter | Ch. II-1 (Stability) | Ch. II-2 (Fire) | Ch. III (LSA) | Ch. VI (Grain) | Ch. VII (Dangerous Goods) |
|:----------|:--------------------|:----------------|:--------------|:---------------|:--------------------------|
| Goal | Prevent sinking | Prevent / fight fire | Save lives at sea | Prevent grain shift | Prevent accident / pollution |
| Key Code | Probabilistic Damage Stability | FSS Code | LSA Code | International Grain Code | IMDG Code |
| Mandatory Tool | Index R, A | CO₂, sprinkler, detection | Lifeboat, liferaft, lifejacket | Document of Authorization | Dangerous Goods Manifest |
| Inspection | Every 5 years (PAS) | Annual | Annual | Each grain loading | Per shipment |
| Penalty | Detention | Detention | Detention | Refusal to load | Refusal to load |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Classification of Dangerous Goods under IMDG

Step 1: Identify the substance.
Step 2: Look up the **UN Number** in the IMDG Code (e.g., UN 1090 = Acetone).
Step 3: Determine the **Class** (Acetone = Class 3, Flammable Liquid).
Step 4: Identify the **Packing Group** (I, II, or III — based on danger level).
Step 5: Determine **stowage category** (A to E).
Step 6: Determine **segregation** from other classes (e.g., away from food).
Step 7: Mark and label the package with UN number and class label.
Step 8: Prepare **Dangerous Goods Declaration** and update the **Cargo Plan**.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024]**
> **Define the SOLAS Convention. Mention its year of adoption and the administering organization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SOLAS stands for the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea**. It was first adopted in **1914** in response to the *Titanic* disaster. The current version, **SOLAS 1974**, was adopted on **1 November 1974** and entered into force on **25 May 1980**. It is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, a United Nations specialized agency headquartered in **London, United Kingdom**. SOLAS is the most important international treaty concerning maritime safety and sets minimum standards for the construction, equipment, and operation of merchant ships.

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**
> **What is the FSS Code? What is its relevance to SOLAS?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **FSS Code (Fire Safety Systems Code)** is the **International Code for Fire Safety Systems**, adopted under **SOLAS Chapter II-2, Regulation 3**. It provides detailed international standards and specifications for fire-extinguishing systems, including fixed gas systems (CO₂, halon replacements), water mist, foam, sprinkler systems, and detection systems. The FSS Code makes SOLAS Chapter II-2 regulations practical by specifying the design, installation, testing, and maintenance requirements for all fire safety systems on board ships.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the concept of **Subdivision and Damage Stability** under SOLAS Chapter II-1. Define the **Required Subdivision Index R** and the **Attained Subdivision Index A**, and explain the **Attained Index Rule**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Describe the various **life-saving appliances** required under SOLAS Chapter III and the **LSA Code**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Subdivision and Damage Stability (7 marks):**

1. **Subdivision Concept (2 marks):** Subdivision means dividing the ship's hull into **watertight compartments** by transverse (and sometimes longitudinal) **watertight bulkheads**. The aim is to ensure that the ship can survive flooding of one or more compartments without sinking. SOLAS Chapter II-1 Part B-1 sets the technical rules.

2. **Required Subdivision Index R (2 marks):** The Required Index R is a measure of the level of subdivision that a ship must achieve. It is calculated based on the ship's length $L$ and the number of passengers $N$ using a SOLAS formula. Higher R = more compartments.

3. **Attained Subdivision Index A (2 marks):** The Attained Index A is the actual subdivision capability of the ship, calculated by summing contributions from each compartment based on the residual stability and survival conditions.

4. **Attained Index Rule (1 mark):**
$$A \geq R$$
If true, the ship complies with damage stability; otherwise, redesign is needed.

**(b) Life-Saving Appliances (7 marks):**

1. **LSA Code (2 marks):** The Life-Saving Appliance Code is mandatory under SOLAS Chapter III, Regulation 34. It specifies the design, construction, performance, and testing of all life-saving appliances.

2. **Survival Craft (2 marks):** Lifeboats (fully enclosed, partially enclosed, free-fall) and liferafts (inflatable) must accommodate **100% of persons on board** on each side (passenger ships) or distributed.

3. **Rescue Boats (1 mark):** At least **one rescue boat** must be carried.

4. **Personal Appliances (1 mark):** Lifejackets for 100% + 5%, immersion suits for cold-water services, life buoys (minimum 8 per ship).

5. **Communication & Signalling (1 mark):** **EPIRB** (1), **SART** (2), **VHF handhelds**, line-throwing appliances, parachute rockets.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **fire protection, detection, and extinction** requirements under SOLAS Chapter II-2. Explain the **FSS Code** and various fire classifications. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain the rules for the **carriage of grain** and **carriage of dangerous goods** under SOLAS Chapters VI and VII. Mention the **Grain Code** and the **IMDG Code**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Fire Protection, Detection, and Extinction (7 marks):**

1. **SOLAS Chapter II-2 (2 marks):** Provides the regulatory framework for fire prevention, suppression, and escape. The chapter is supported by the **FSS Code** (Fire Safety Systems Code) which is mandatory.

2. **Structural Protection (2 marks):** Includes **A-class and B-class divisions**, fire zones (passenger ship zones not exceeding 40 m), and **means of escape**. A-class divisions must withstand a standard fire test for 60 minutes; B-class for 30 minutes.

3. **Detection Systems (1 mark):** Smoke detectors (ionization/photoelectric), heat detectors, flame detectors, and aspirating smoke detection systems (ASD).

4. **Extinction Systems (1 mark):** CO₂ for engine rooms, foam for oil fires, water mist, sprinkler systems (automatic), and portable extinguishers.

5. **Fire Classes (1 mark):** Class A (ordinary), Class B (flammable liquids), Class C (gases), Class D (metals), Class K (cooking oils).

**(b) Carriage of Grain and Dangerous Goods (7 marks):**

1. **SOLAS Chapter VI — Grain (3 marks):** Grain is dangerous because it can shift when the ship rolls, creating a free-surface effect that can capsize the vessel. The **International Grain Code** specifies filling ratios, trimming procedures, shifting boards, and stability criteria. A **Document of Authorization** is required.

2. **SOLAS Chapter VII — Dangerous Goods (4 marks):** The **IMDG Code** (International Maritime Dangerous Goods Code) is mandatory. It classifies dangerous goods into **9 classes**:
   - Class 1: Explosives
   - Class 2: Gases
   - Class 3: Flammable liquids
   - Class 4: Flammable solids
   - Class 5: Oxidizers / peroxides
   - Class 6: Toxic / infectious
   - Class 7: Radioactive
   - Class 8: Corrosives
   - Class 9: Miscellaneous
   Each package must display **UN number**, **Proper Shipping Name**, and **hazard class label**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Many students write **"SOLAS 1948"** instead of **SOLAS 1974** — this loses **1 mark**.
> - Confusing the **FSS Code** (Fire Safety Systems) with the **FSC Code** (Fire Safety Code) costs marks.
> - Forgetting to state **"A ≥ R"** as the SOLAS criterion (not "A = R") loses 1 mark.
> - Mistaking **EPIRB** for a transmitter on board an aircraft or a satellite beacon with continuous transmission.
> - Not stating **100% persons** capacity for lifeboats / liferafts is a common error.
> - In grain, students often forget the requirement of a **Document of Authorization** issued by the flag state.
