### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for SOLAS Module 2**

**SOLAS Framework**
- SOLAS 1974 is the principal international maritime safety convention.
- Administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO), London**.
- Applies to **passenger ships (any size)** and **cargo ships ≥ 300 GT** on international voyages.
- Has **12 Chapters**; Module 2 covers Chapters II-1, II-2, III, VI, and VII.

**Chapter II-1 — Subdivision & Stability**
- Aim: Ship must not sink after flooding of one or more compartments.
- Uses **watertight bulkheads** to divide hull.
- **Margin line = 76 mm (3 in) below bulkhead deck**.
- **Required Index R** and **Attained Index A**: SOLAS rule is **A ≥ R**.
- Factor of subdivision $f = 0.04 + L/10000$.
- Permeability varies with space type (0.70 for cargo, 0.95 for accommodation, 0.85 for machinery).

**Chapter II-2 — Fire Protection**
- **FSS Code** is mandatory.
- Passenger ships divided into **main vertical fire zones** of max 40 m.
- **A-class divisions** withstand 60 minutes; **B-class** withstand 30 minutes.
- Fire classes: **A, B, C, D, K**.
- Systems: **CO₂, foam, water mist, sprinklers, dry powder, fire main**.
- Detection: **smoke, heat, flame, aspirating**.

**Chapter III — Life-Saving Appliances**
- **LSA Code** mandatory.
- Lifeboats must accommodate **100% persons on each side**.
- Liferafts: **100% persons total**.
- Personal: lifejackets (100%+5%), life buoys (≥ 8), immersion suits.
- Communication: **EPIRB (1), SART (2), VHF handheld**.
- Drills: **abandon ship and fire drills monthly**.

**Chapter VI — Carriage of Grain**
- Grain shifts → capsize risk.
- **International Grain Code** is mandatory.
- Requires **Document of Authorization** by flag state.
- Stability criteria: GZ area ≥ 0.075 m·rad, GZ max ≥ 0.20 m, vanishing angle ≥ 55°.
- Methods: filling ratio, trimming, shifting boards.

**Chapter VII — Carriage of Dangerous Goods**
- **IMDG Code** mandatory.
- **9 Classes** of dangerous goods (1 to 9).
- Each package: **UN number + label + proper shipping name**.
- Requires **Dangerous Goods Manifest** and **stowage plan**.
- Complementary to **MARPOL Annex III**.

**Key Equations to Memorize**
- $f = 0.04 + L/10000$
- $A \geq R$
- Range of positive stability $\geq 20°$
- Margin line distance = **76 mm**
- 9 IMDG classes
- 5 fire classes (A, B, C, D, K)

**Common Examiner Traps**
- Confusing **FSS Code** with **LSA Code**.
- Confusing **9 IMDG classes** with **5 fire classes**.
- Stating SOLAS year as 1948 (wrong — should be 1974).
- Forgetting **flag-state Document of Authorization** for grain ships.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Line Convention: important definitions and markings, survey and certification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Load Line Convention: important definitions and markings, survey and certification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Load Line Convention — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
> The **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), 1966** is an International Maritime Organization (IMO) treaty that sets forth the **minimum permissible freeboard** to which a merchant ship may be loaded. It was adopted on **5 April 1966** and entered into force globally on **21 July 1968**. The Convention's primary objective is to **limit the immersion of a ship** so as to ensure adequate reserve buoyancy and stability, thereby preventing overloading — historically the single largest cause of maritime loss of life.

The Convention was a direct response to the **1862 Plimsoll Line Saga** in the United Kingdom, which exposed how unscrupulous shipowners overloaded "coffin ships," sending countless mariners to their deaths. The Convention applies to all **sea-going merchant ships of 24 metres and above** in load line length.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath-Tub" Rule

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine a rectangular tub of water. If you keep dropping heavy stones into it, the water rises. There is a **red line** painted on the inside wall of the tub. The rule says: **"You may not load the tub so heavily that the water crosses the red line under any expected sea condition."**
> 
> In a ship, that red line is the **Plimsoll Mark**, and the waterline (which moves up as cargo is added) must never reach or exceed it. The "extra space" between the waterline and the ship's deck is the **freeboard** — your safety margin.

This simple visual captures the entire philosophy of the Load Line Convention: the ship is the tub, the cargo is the stones, and the Plimsoll Mark is the legally enforced maximum waterline.

## 1.3 Why a "Convention" Rather Than a "Law"?

A *convention* under international maritime law is a **multilateral treaty** that becomes binding only on **States that have ratified (acceded to)** it. A ship flies the flag of one State (its "Flag State"), and only if that Flag State is a Party to the ICLL do the rules apply to that ship. As of recent records, the Convention has near-universal acceptance with over **160 Contracting States**, representing roughly **99%** of the world gross tonnage.

## 1.4 Key Entities Governing the Convention

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Bodies Involved:**
> 1. **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** — The custodian of the Convention.
> 2. **Maritime Safety Committee (MSC)** — Adopts amendments.
> 3. **Flag State Administration** — Issues the International Load Line Certificate.
> 4. **Recognized Organization (RO) / Classification Society** — Acts on behalf of the Administration to conduct surveys (e.g., Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, IRS).
> 5. **Port State Control** — Inspects foreign ships visiting its ports.

## 1.5 Geographical Scope of the Convention

The Convention divides the world's oceans into distinct zones to account for seasonal and regional weather severity:

- **Zone I (Winter North Atlantic & similar severe zones):** Most stringent freeboard.
- **Zone II (Moderate zones, including parts of the North Pacific, North Atlantic, South Atlantic, Indian Ocean, South Pacific):** Intermediate freeboard.
- **Zone III (Tropical zones, e.g., Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal, South China Sea, Caribbean):** Least stringent — allows *more* loading.
- **Seasonal Zones:** Specific to certain regions like the North Pacific Winter Seasonal Zone and Tropical Seasonal Zones, where the allowable freeboard changes based on the time of year.
- **Fresh Water Allowance:** Ships operating in **fresh water** (specific gravity **1.000**) are allowed a slightly deeper load line than in **salt water** (specific gravity **1.025**), because fresh water is less buoyant.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of the Load Line System

The Convention is built on four logical pillars, each explained below:

### Pillar 1: The Definitions
The Convention, in **Article 2** and **Annex I**, defines a precise vocabulary. Without these, no measurement or marking makes sense.

### Pillar 2: The Markings
The hull is physically marked with symbols prescribed by the Convention. These are **not decorative** — they are legal instruments visible to any port state inspector.

### Pillar 3: The Freeboard Computation
Using the ship's **length (L)**, **breadth (B)**, **depth (D)**, **block coefficient (Cb)**, **sheer**, **bow height**, and other technical particulars, the **minimum freeboard** is calculated and assigned.

### Pillar 4: The Survey and Certification Regime
A ship cannot be loaded to the assigned freeboard unless it has a **valid International Load Line Certificate (ILLC)**, issued only after successful surveys.

## 2.2 Key Definitions (High-Yield for KTU Examinations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Line Length (L):**
> The length of a ship as defined in the **International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969**. In simple terms, it is **96% of the total length on the waterline at 85% of the least moulded depth**, or the length from the foreside of the stem to the axis of the rudder stock on that waterline — whichever is greater.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Freeboard (F):**
> The vertical distance, measured at the **amidships**, from the **moulded baseline (keel)** to the **upper edge of the deck line** (i.e., the **freeboard deck**), minus the **assigned summer load waterline (S)**.
> 
> Mathematically:
> $$F = D - S$$
> 
> where **D** is the moulded depth and **S** is the depth of the assigned summer load waterline below the freeboard deck.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plimsoll Mark (Load Line Disc):**
> A **circular disc, 300 mm in outside diameter** and **25 mm wide**, with a **horizontal line 450 mm long and 25 mm wide** passing through its centre. The disc is positioned **amidships** on each side of the ship, with the horizontal line indicating the **maximum permissible draft**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deck Line:**
> A **horizontal line, 300 mm long and 25 mm wide**, marked amidships on each side of the ship. The **upper edge** of this line indicates the **freeboard deck line** — the reference for all freeboard measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Waterline (LWL):**
> The waterline corresponding to the **maximum permissible draft** at which the ship is allowed to proceed in **salt water** under specific conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moulded Depth (D):**
> The **vertical distance** measured from the **moulded baseline (top of keel)** to the **top of the freeboard deck beam at side**. For ships with rounded gunwales, the measurement is taken to the point of intersection of the deck-line continuation with the sheer-line continuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moulded Breadth (B):**
> The **maximum breadth** of the ship, measured amidships to the **moulded line of the frame** (inside the plating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Moulded Draft (d):**
> The **vertical distance** from the **moulded baseline** to the **actual waterline** at which the ship operates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Coefficient (Cb):**
> A dimensionless number expressing the **fineness** of the ship's underwater hull form:
> 
> $$C_b = \frac{\nabla}{L \times B \times d}$$
> 
> where $\nabla$ is the **displaced volume** (m³), and $L, B, d$ are the length, breadth, and moulded draft respectively.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Quick-Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equation Card (Memorise for ESE):**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Freeboard | $F = D - S$ | metres (m) |
| 2 | Block Coefficient | $C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot d}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Midship Section Coefficient | $C_m = \dfrac{A_m}{B \cdot d}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Prismatic Coefficient | $C_p = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_m \cdot L} = \dfrac{C_b}{C_m}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Waterplane Coefficient | $C_{wp} = \dfrac{A_{wp}}{L \cdot B}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Displacement | $\nabla = C_b \cdot L \cdot B \cdot d$ | m³ |
| 7 | Deadweight to Draft | $\dfrac{\Delta W}{\Delta d} = \rho \cdot A_{wp} = \rho \cdot C_{wp} \cdot L \cdot B$ | tonnes/m |
| 8 | Fresh Water Allowance (FWA) | $\text{FWA} = \dfrac{\text{Summer draft}}{40}$ | mm (standard rule) |
| 9 | Plimsoll Disc Diameter | $D_{\text{disc}} = 300\,\text{mm}$ (outer), $25\,\text{mm}$ (band) | fixed by Convention |
| 10 | Plimsoll Line Length | $L_{\text{line}} = 450\,\text{mm}$, $25\,\text{mm}$ thick | fixed by Convention |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert (Validation Rule):**
> In the table above, the absolute value in formulas (such as $\vert \Delta W \vert$) is rendered using **\vert** or **\mid** to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. KTU examiners will mark an answer wrong if the symbol is missing or substituted incorrectly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Load Line System

1. **Cargo Operations:** Chartering departments, port captains, and load-masters use the assigned freeboard to determine the **maximum permissible deadweight** for a given voyage.
2. **Stability Booklet:** The freeboard is a primary input to the ship's **Approved Stability Manual**.
3. **Voyage Planning:** Seasonal zones dictate the **maximum permitted draft** in winter versus tropical months.
4. **Insurance & P&I Cover:** Overloading beyond the Load Line **voids** Hull and Machinery insurance and P&I indemnity — a critical point in maritime law.
5. **Port State Control:** A failure to maintain the load line marks or certificate can lead to **detention** of the ship.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Load Line Marking System — Exhaustive Walkthrough

The Convention prescribes **specific markings** on each side of the ship. They are not arbitrary — every line has a meaning tied to a specific load waterline under a specific condition.

### Step 1: The Reference Point — Deck Line

The **upper edge** of the deck line corresponds to the **freeboard deck** — a legal reference, not necessarily the actual main deck (although in most ships it is). The deck line is a **300 mm long × 25 mm wide** horizontal bar, positioned amidships.

### Step 2: The Plimsoll Mark (Disc) — The Centre of the System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Plimsoll disc is **horizontally bisected by a line 450 mm long and 25 mm wide**, whose **upper edge** marks the **assigned Summer Load Waterline** in **salt water**.

The upper edge of the disc line = **S** (Summer Freeboard Waterline).

### Step 3: The "Tree of Lines" Around the Disc

The Convention requires **additional horizontal lines** extending from the disc, each corresponding to a particular waterline. The order (from the disc outward) is precisely defined:

| Order | Line | Code Letter | Position Relative to Disc | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **AB** | Summer | The line through the disc itself | Maximum salt-water load in summer zones (Zones I and II). |
| 2 | **CD** | Winter | **Above** AB | Maximum salt-water load in **Winter Zones** (e.g., Winter North Atlantic, Zone I). |
| 3 | **EF** | Tropical | **Below** AB | Maximum salt-water load in **Tropical Zones** (Zone III). |
| 4 | **FG** | Fresh Water (Tropical) | **Below** EF, at a distance equal to the Fresh Water Allowance from TF | Maximum **fresh-water** load in **Tropical Zones**. |
| 5 | **BD** | Winter North Atlantic | **Above** CD (if ship operates in WNAZ) | Only for ships of length ≤ 100 m that trade in the Winter North Atlantic Zone. |
| 6 | **TF** | Fresh Water | **Below** AB, at a distance equal to the Fresh Water Allowance from S | Maximum **fresh-water** load in **Summer Zones**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid (KTU Exam Hack):**
> Reading **upward** from the Summer line: **W**inter is **above**.
> Reading **downward** from the Summer line: **T**ropical is **below**, then **TF** (Tropical Fresh) further below.
> Use the mnemonic: **"Watch The Bottom"** — **W**inter is **T**op, **T**ropical is **B**ottom.

### Step 4: The Lines Must Be Labelled

The Convention requires each line to be **legibly marked** with the appropriate code letters (e.g., **"W"** for Winter, **"S"** for Summer, **"T"** for Tropical, **"TF"** for Tropical Fresh, **"F"** for Fresh, **"WNA"** for Winter North Atlantic). These letters are placed on the side of the ship, immediately forward of the load line marks.

### Step 5: Marking of the Timber Load Line

If a ship is assigned a **timber freeboard** (a lesser freeboard granted to ships carrying timber as deck cargo), an additional set of lines is marked. The **timber load line mark** consists of a **vertical line 540 mm long × 25 mm wide**, with **horizontal lines 230 mm long × 25 mm wide** projecting forward of the vertical line at right angles. The marks include:
- **LS** — Lumber Summer (in salt water)
- **LT** — Lumber Tropical
- **LTF** — Lumber Tropical Fresh
- **LF** — Lumber Fresh
- **LW** — Lumber Winter (above LS)

> [!WARNING]
> The **LWNA** (Lumber Winter North Atlantic) line is **not** permitted even if the ship operates in the WNA Zone when on a timber voyage.

## 3.2 Marking Colours (KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!NOTE]
> The Convention specifies that the **markings must be painted in white or yellow on a dark ground, or in black on a light ground**, so as to be clearly visible. The disc and the lines must be:
> 1. **Painted in contrasting colour.**
> 2. **Of the prescribed dimensions** (300 mm disc, 450 mm line, 25 mm thickness).
> 3. **Visible at all times** (re-painted regularly during the ship's service).

## 3.3 Survey Regime — Step-by-Step Analysis

The Convention mandates **three types of surveys**, each leading to certification action.

### A. Initial Survey

- **When:** Before the ship is **put into service**, or **before the International Load Line Certificate** is first issued.
- **Who:** Surveyors of the **Flag State Administration** or a **Recognized Organization (RO)**.
- **What is checked:**
  1. **Hull integrity** — watertightness of the hull below the freeboard deck.
  2. **Watertight bulkheads** — their position, number, and integrity.
  3. **Superstructures, deckhouses, and companionways** — their contribution to freeboard.
  4. **Scuppers, freeing ports, and side openings** — proper closing arrangements.
  5. **Ventilators, air pipes, and sounding pipes** — height and closing appliances.
  6. **Hatchways, hatch covers, and coamings** — watertightness.
  7. **Doorways, gangways, and other openings** in the freeboard deck and superstructures.
  8. **Verification of the Load Line Markings** — proper positioning, dimensions, and painting.
  9. **Calculation of the assigned freeboard** — the result is entered into the **Conditions of Assignment**.
- **Outcome:** Issuance of the **International Load Line Certificate (ILLC)** — **valid for 5 years**.

### B. Annual Survey

- **When:** **Within 3 months before or after** each annual anniversary date of the certificate.
- **Purpose:** To ensure that the ship and its fittings have been **maintained** and remain fit for the assigned freeboard.
- **What is checked:** A visual and functional examination of the items listed in the Initial Survey — **especially** weathertight closures, hatch covers, side scuppers, and the load line markings.
- **Outcome:** A **record of survey endorsement** is made on the ILLC; **no new certificate** is issued.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Annual Survey ≠ Renewal Survey.** The annual survey is a "check-up" and the renewal is a "full medical exam."

### C. Renewal Survey

- **When:** **At intervals not exceeding 5 years** from the date of issue of the ILLC.
- **Purpose:** Same comprehensiveness as the initial survey.
- **Outcome:** A **new ILLC** is issued, valid for a **further 5 years**.

> [!WARNING]
> If a **renewal survey is completed within 3 months before** the expiry of the existing certificate, the new certificate is valid for 5 years from the date of expiry of the existing one. This is a **common exam trap** — students often misquote the validity period.

## 3.4 Conditions of Assignment — The Underlying Document

The **Conditions of Assignment (CoA)** is a binding technical document issued by the Administration (or its RO) that contains:
- The ship's particulars (L, B, D, Cb, etc.).
- The **assigned freeboards** for Summer, Winter, Tropical, Fresh Water, Tropical Fresh, and (if applicable) WNA and timber lines.
- Any limitations or conditions imposed on the ship.

The CoA is **kept on board** and is **complementary to the ILLC**.

## 3.5 Loss of Certification — A Legal Consequence

> [!WARNING]
> If, due to damage, alteration, or change of purpose, the structural integrity or watertightness of the ship is impaired, the ILLC **ceases to be valid**. The ship must undergo a **special survey** before the certificate is re-issued. Until then, the ship is **prohibited from sailing beyond the nearest available repair port**.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Marking Lines

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the load line marks, with mathematical positional relationships:

| Line | Symbol | Vertical Position from Summer Line (S) | Reason for Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| Winter North Atlantic (WNA) | Above CD | $= 50\,\text{mm} + (L - 100) \times \text{ratio}$ for $L \le 100\,\text{m}$ | Additional safety in extreme conditions |
| Winter (W) | Above S | $h_w$ derived from the Convention's tables | Heavier weather, less allowance |
| Summer (S) | Reference | $0$ | Reference condition |
| Tropical (T) | Below S | $h_t$ derived from the Convention's tables | Calmer weather, more allowance |
| Fresh Water (F) | Below S by FWA | $\text{FWA} = \text{Summer draft} / 40$ in mm | Fresh water is less dense ($\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$ vs $1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$) |
| Tropical Fresh (TF) | Below T by FWA | Tropical freeboard + FWA | Combined allowance |

The exact vertical distances between lines (e.g., $h_w$, $h_t$) are read from **Tables in Annex II of the ICLL**, indexed by **length (L)** and **block coefficient (Cb)**. These tables are **not memorised** for the exam; the **principles** are.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Hull Marking Layout (Side View of the Ship)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph hull["Hull Marking — Side Profile of Ship (Amidships)"]
        direction TB
        deckLine["DECK LINE - 300 mm × 25 mm horizontal bar - Upper edge = Freeboard Deck"]
        plimsollDisc["PLIMSOLL DISC - 300 mm outer diameter - 25 mm wide band - Horizontal line 450 mm × 25 mm"]
        summerLine["UPPER EDGE of horizontal line through disc = SUMMER LOAD LINE (S)"]
        winterLine["W = WINTER - above S"]
        tropicalLine["T = TROPICAL - below S"]
        freshLine["F = FRESH WATER - below S by FWA"]
        tropicalFreshLine["TF = TROPICAL FRESH - below T by FWA"]
        wnaLine["WNA = WINTER NORTH ATLANTIC - above W (if applicable)"]
        codeLetters["CODE LETTERS - W, S, T, F, TF, WNA - marked forward of the disc"]
        baseline["MOULDED BASELINE (Keel) - Reference for draft"]
    end
    deckLine --> plimsollDisc
    plimsollDisc --> summerLine
    summerLine --> winterLine
    summerLine --> tropicalLine
    summerLine --> freshLine
    summerLine --> tropicalFreshLine
    summerLine --> wnaLine
    deckLine --> codeLetters
    baseline --> deckLine
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Survey Lifecycle Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["New Ship / Re-registration"] --> B["INITIAL SURVEY\nHull integrity, bulkheads, openings,\nventilators, hatchways, scuppers,\nload line markings, freeboard calculation"]
    B --> C["International Load Line Certificate\nILLC issued — Valid 5 Years"]
    C --> D["ANNUAL SURVEY — within 3 months\nof each anniversary date"]
    D --> E{"End of 5 Years?"}
    E -- "No" --> D
    E -- "Yes" --> F["RENEWAL SURVEY\nSame scope as initial"]
    F --> G["New ILLC issued\nValid further 5 Years"]
    G --> D
    H["Damage / Alteration / Loss of Integrity"] --> I["SPECIAL SURVEY"]
    I --> J{"Passed?"}
    J -- "Yes" --> K["Reinstatement of ILLC"]
    J -- "No" --> L["Repair at nearest port\nCertificate remains suspended"]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Freeboard Concept — Vertical Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph freeboardDiagram["Freeboard Geometry — Single Diagram"]
        direction TB
        baselineM["MOULDED BASELINE (Top of Keel)"]
        mouldedDepth["D = MOULDED DEPTH\n(vertical distance from baseline\nto top of freeboard deck at side)"]
        summerDraft["S = SUMMER DRAFT\n(vertical distance from baseline\nto the Summer Load Line)"]
        freeboardF["F = FREEBOARD = D minus S\n(vertical safety margin above the waterline)"]
        deckTop["FREEBOARD DECK (Upper edge of Deck Line)"]
        waterSurface["SUMMER LOAD WATERLINE (S)"]
        actualDraft["d = ACTUAL OPERATING DRAFT\n(must be ≤ S)"]
    end
    baselineM --> mouldedDepth
    baselineM --> summerDraft
    summerDraft --> freeboardF
    mouldedDepth --> freeboardF
    deckTop --> mouldedDepth
    waterSurface --> summerDraft
    waterSurface --> actualDraft
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Zonal Map (Conceptual Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph zoneMap["Geographic Zone Classification under ICLL"]
        direction TB
        z1["ZONE I — Most Severe\nExample: Winter North Atlantic\nSTRICTEST freeboard required"]
        z2["ZONE II — Moderate\nExamples: Most of the Atlantic,\nIndian Ocean, South Pacific\nINTERMEDIATE freeboard"]
        z3["ZONE III — Tropical\nExamples: Arabian Sea,\nBay of Bengal, Caribbean\nLEAST stringent freeboard"]
        ssn["SEASONAL ZONES\nWinter Seasonal / Tropical Seasonal\nFreeboard varies with date"]
        fw["FRESH vs SALT WATER\nSalt: ρ = 1.025 t/m³\nFresh: ρ = 1.000 t/m³\nFWA applied when in fresh water"]
    end
    z1 --> combined["Combined Rule for a Voyage\nCompute the MOST SEVERE\nfreeboard applicable to the\nentire voyage"]
    z2 --> combined
    z3 --> combined
    ssn --> combined
    fw --> combined
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × **3 marks** = 6 marks.
> - **Part B:** Either-Or choice × **14 marks** = 14 marks.
> - **Total Module Weightage:** 20 marks.
> All questions below are mapped to **Module 2** and tagged with appropriate **Bloom's Level** (L1: Remember, L2: Understand, L3: Apply, L4: Analyse).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Define the following terms as per the International Convention on Load Lines, 1966: (i) Freeboard, (ii) Plimsoll Mark, (iii) Deck Line.** *(3 Marks, Bloom L1)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Freeboard (F):** The vertical distance, measured at amidships, from the **moulded baseline** (top of keel) to the **upper edge of the deck line**, **minus** the depth of the **assigned summer load waterline (S)**. It represents the safety margin of the hull above the waterline.

**(ii) Plimsoll Mark:** A **circular disc, 300 mm in outside diameter and 25 mm in band width**, with a **horizontal line 450 mm long and 25 mm wide** passing through its centre. It is marked on each side of the ship at amidships, and the upper edge of its horizontal line indicates the **maximum permissible summer load waterline in salt water**.

**(iii) Deck Line:** A **horizontal line, 300 mm long and 25 mm wide**, marked amidships on each side of the ship. The **upper edge** of this line corresponds to the **freeboard deck**, which is the reference for all freeboard measurements.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Explain the difference between Annual Survey and Renewal Survey under the Load Line Convention.** *(3 Marks, Bloom L2)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Annual Survey | Renewal Survey |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency** | Within 3 months before or after each annual anniversary of the ILLC | Once every 5 years, on expiry of the ILLC |
| **Scope** | General visual inspection of hull integrity, openings, weathertight fittings, and the load line marks | Full examination equivalent to the Initial Survey, including structural and detailed checks |
| **Outcome** | A **record of endorsement** is made on the existing ILLC; no new certificate is issued | A **new ILLC** is issued, valid for a further **5 years** |
| **Authority** | Flag State Administration or Recognized Organization (RO) | Flag State Administration or RO |

In essence, the **Annual Survey** is a periodic "health check," while the **Renewal Survey** is a comprehensive re-certification.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Either-Or Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Answer the following:** *(14 Marks, Bloom L3-L4)*

**(a)** Describe in detail the **load line markings** required to be painted on the side of a ship under the International Convention on Load Lines, 1966. Include a neat sketch of the markings and explain the meaning of each line. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** What is the **survey and certification regime** prescribed by the ICLL? Explain the **Initial Survey, Annual Survey, and Renewal Survey** with reference to scope and outcome. *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 Marks

The ICLL prescribes the following markings on **each side of the hull, amidships**:

1. **Deck Line (300 mm × 25 mm):**
   - A horizontal line; its **upper edge** denotes the **freeboard deck**, the legal reference for freeboard measurement.

2. **Plimsoll Disc (300 mm outer diameter, 25 mm band):**
   - A circular disc, the centre of the load line system, with a **horizontal line 450 mm × 25 mm** passing through it.
   - The **upper edge of this line = Summer Load Waterline (S)** in **salt water**.

3. **Lines Extending from the Disc — The "Tree of Lines":**

   - **W (Winter) — above S:** Heavier weather allowance.
   - **WNA (Winter North Atlantic) — above W:** Only for ships of **length ≤ 100 m** that trade in the WNA Zone.
   - **T (Tropical) — below S:** Calmer weather, more loading.
   - **F (Fresh Water) — below S by FWA:** Where FWA = Summer draft / 40 (in mm).
   - **TF (Tropical Fresh) — below T by FWA:** Combined allowance for tropical + fresh water.

4. **Code Letters:** Each line is **clearly labelled** with letters (W, S, T, F, TF, WNA) painted **forward of the disc**.

5. **Timber Load Line Marks (if applicable):** An additional set of lines (LS, LW, LT, LF, LTF) is marked, identified by a **vertical line 540 mm × 25 mm** with horizontal projections of **230 mm × 25 mm**.

6. **Colours and Visibility:** The disc and lines must be **clearly visible** — painted in white or yellow on a dark background, or black on a light background.

**Sketch (ASCII representation):**

```
            WNA
            W ←—— Winter
S ←——————─ S (Plimsoll disc) ——————— Reference (Summer)
            T →—— Tropical
            F →—— Fresh Water
            TF →—— Tropical Fresh
         (All labels W, S, T, F, TF forward of disc)
[ —————— ]  Deck Line (upper edge = freeboard deck)
```

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 Marks

The ICLL prescribes a **tiered survey and certification regime**:

1. **Initial Survey:**
   - Conducted **before the ship is put into service** or before the **first issue of the ILLC**.
   - Comprehensive examination of: **hull watertightness, bulkheads, superstructures, hatchways, scuppers, freeing ports, ventilators, sounding pipes, doorways, side openings, and the load line markings**.
   - The **freeboard is calculated** using the ship's L, B, D, Cb, sheer, and bow height, and entered into the **Conditions of Assignment (CoA)**.
   - **Outcome:** Issue of the **International Load Line Certificate (ILLC)** — **valid for 5 years**.

2. **Annual Survey:**
   - Conducted **within 3 months before or after** each annual anniversary of the ILLC.
   - **Scope:** Visual and functional inspection of the items in the Initial Survey, with special focus on **weathertight closures, hatch covers, side scuppers, and the visibility of the load line marks**.
   - **Outcome:** A **record of survey endorsement** is made on the existing ILLC; **no new certificate**.

3. **Renewal Survey:**
   - Conducted **at intervals not exceeding 5 years** from the date of issue of the ILLC.
   - **Scope:** Equivalent in depth to the Initial Survey.
   - **Outcome:** **New ILLC issued**, valid for a **further 5 years**.
   - If the renewal is completed **within 3 months before** the expiry, the new certificate's validity is **5 years from the date of expiry of the old one**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Examiner's Note:**
> Students frequently confuse the **validity period of the ILLC** with the **periodicity of the annual survey**. The **ILLC is valid for 5 years**, but an **annual survey is required every year**. Also, remember that the **fresh water allowance (FWA) is Summer draft / 40 mm**, not / 100 or any other divisor. These numerical traps cost 1–2 marks.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Answer the following:** *(14 Marks, Bloom L3-L4)*

**(a)** Explain the **geographical and seasonal zones** defined under the ICLL. How do they affect the assigned freeboard? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A bulk carrier has the following particulars:
- Length $L = 150$ m
- Breadth $B = 22$ m
- Moulded Depth $D = 14$ m
- Moulded Draft at Summer Load Line $S = 9.5$ m
- Block Coefficient $C_b = 0.72$

Calculate:
1. The **midship freeboard (F)** in metres.
2. The **displaced volume (∇)** in cubic metres.
3. The **Fresh Water Allowance (FWA)** in millimetres.

*(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 Marks

The ICLL classifies the world's oceans into three principal zones and additional seasonal zones:

1. **Zone I (Severe):**
   - Includes the **Winter North Atlantic Zone (WNAZ)** and similar severe areas.
   - **Strictest** freeboard — the ship must be loaded **less heavily**.

2. **Zone II (Moderate):**
   - Includes the North Pacific, the remainder of the North and South Atlantic, the Indian Ocean, and the South Pacific.
   - **Intermediate** freeboard.

3. **Zone III (Tropical):**
   - Includes the Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal, South China Sea, the tropical Pacific, and the Caribbean.
   - **Least stringent** — the ship may be loaded more deeply.

4. **Seasonal Zones:**
   - **Winter Seasonal Zone (e.g., North Pacific Winter Seasonal):** The ship must apply the **Winter** freeboard for a defined period.
   - **Tropical Seasonal Zone:** The ship may apply the **Tropical** freeboard for a defined period.
   - Outside these periods, the **Summer** freeboard applies.

5. **Voyage Planning Rule:**
   - The **maximum permissible draft for a voyage** is determined by the **most severe zone** the ship will encounter.
   - Example: A ship sailing from the Arabian Sea (Zone III) to a North Atlantic winter port (Zone I) must apply **Winter Zone I** freeboard for the **entire voyage**.

6. **Fresh Water Allowance (FWA):**
   - When a ship is in **fresh water (ρ = 1.000 t/m³)** instead of **salt water (ρ = 1.025 t/m³)**, it sits **deeper** for the same weight.
   - The **FWA = Summer draft / 40** (in mm) is added as a **separate "F" line below the Summer line**, allowing deeper loading in fresh water.

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $L = 150$ m
- $B = 22$ m
- $D = 14$ m
- $S = 9.5$ m (Summer Draft)
- $C_b = 0.72$

**Step 1: Midship Freeboard (F)**

Using the formula:

$$F = D - S$$

Substituting the values:

$$F = 14 - 9.5$$

$$\boxed{F = 4.5 \text{ m}}$$

> **Step 1 — Conversion Logic:**
> The moulded depth D is the vertical distance from the keel to the freeboard deck. The Summer draft S is the vertical distance from the keel to the Summer Load Waterline. Their difference is the **freeboard**, the safety margin above the waterline.

**Step 2: Displaced Volume (∇)**

Using the formula:

$$\nabla = C_b \times L \times B \times d$$

We use the **moulded draft at Summer Load Line**, $d = S = 9.5$ m (this is the maximum permissible draft, equal to the depth of the Summer Load Waterline below the baseline).

Substituting the values:

$$\nabla = 0.72 \times 150 \times 22 \times 9.5$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$\nabla = 0.72 \times 150$$

$$\nabla = 108$$

$$\nabla = 108 \times 22$$

$$\nabla = 2376$$

$$\nabla = 2376 \times 9.5$$

$$\nabla = 22572 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\nabla = 22{,}572 \text{ m}^3}$$

> **Step 2 — Conversion Logic:**
> The block coefficient $C_b$ is the ratio of the **displaced volume** to the volume of the **rectangular block** $L \times B \times d$. Rearranging, the displaced volume is $C_b$ times the block volume. For a typical bulk carrier, $C_b = 0.72$ indicates a **fairly full** hull form.

**Step 3: Fresh Water Allowance (FWA)**

Using the standard rule:

$$\text{FWA (mm)} = \frac{\text{Summer draft (mm)}}{40}$$

The Summer draft in mm is:

$$S = 9.5 \text{ m} = 9500 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{FWA} = \frac{9500}{40}$$

$$\text{FWA} = 237.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FWA} = 237.5 \text{ mm} = 0.2375 \text{ m}}$$

> **Step 3 — Conversion Logic:**
> The fresh water allowance represents the **extra draft** the ship may have in fresh water compared to salt water, because fresh water is less dense. The ICLL prescribes a simple rule of thumb: **1/40th of the Summer draft**, expressed in millimetres. This rule is approximate but universally applied in practice.

**Final Summary of Computed Results:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Midship Freeboard | $F$ | $4.5$ m |
| Displaced Volume | $\nabla$ | $22{,}572$ m³ |
| Fresh Water Allowance | $\text{FWA}$ | $237.5$ mm |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Topic-Wise

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Five Marks-Loss Traps on this Module (Mapped to KTU Valuation Key):**
> 1. **Confusing the validity period of the ILLC (5 years)** with the **periodicity of the Annual Survey (every year)** — costs up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Writing "FWA = Summer draft / 100"** or any other wrong divisor. The **correct rule is / 40 mm**.
> 3. **Misidentifying the position of the Winter line** as below the Summer line. Winter is **above** S.
> 4. **Omitting the marking dimensions** (300 mm disc, 450 mm line, 25 mm thickness) — these are exact numbers from the Convention.
> 5. **Skipping the role of the Recognized Organization (RO) / Classification Society**, which is a frequently asked sub-question worth 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 Examination:**

- The **ICLL 1966** is an IMO Convention setting **minimum freeboard** for merchant ships of **load line length ≥ 24 m**.
- The **Plimsoll Mark** consists of a **300 mm disc** with a **450 mm × 25 mm horizontal line** through it; the **upper edge** of the line = **Summer Load Waterline**.
- The **Deck Line** is a **300 mm × 25 mm horizontal line**; the **upper edge** = **Freeboard Deck**.
- **Freeboard (F) = D − S**, where D is moulded depth and S is the Summer Load Waterline depth.
- The **"Tree of Lines"** around the disc: **W (above S) → WNA (above W) → S (reference) → T (below S) → F (below S by FWA) → TF (below T by FWA).**
- **FWA = Summer draft (in mm) / 40**, painted as a separate "F" line below S.
- **Marking colours:** white/yellow on dark, or black on light — clearly visible at all times.
- **Code letters** (W, S, T, F, TF, WNA) must be **marked forward of the disc**.
- **Timber load lines** are marked using a **540 mm vertical line** with **230 mm horizontal projections**, only if assigned a timber freeboard.
- **Geographical Zones:** Zone I (severe), Zone II (moderate), Zone III (tropical), plus **seasonal zones**; the **most severe zone on the voyage** determines the maximum draft.
- **Salt water ρ = 1.025 t/m³**, **Fresh water ρ = 1.000 t/m³**; fresh water is less dense, so the ship sits deeper.
- **Surveys:** **Initial** (before first issue of ILLC) → **Annual** (every year, within 3 months of anniversary) → **Renewal** (every 5 years).
- **ILLC validity:** **5 years** from the date of issue, or **5 years from expiry of old one** if renewal is completed within the **3-month pre-expiry window**.
- **Special survey** is required after any damage or alteration that impairs watertightness; the ILLC is **suspended** until the ship passes.
- The **Conditions of Assignment (CoA)** is a binding technical document kept on board, complementary to the ILLC.
- **Block Coefficient** $C_b = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \times B \times d}$; for typical bulk carriers, $C_b$ lies in the range **0.65 to 0.80**.
- **Surveying authority:** **Flag State Administration** or a **Recognized Organization (RO)** acting on its behalf (e.g., Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, IRS).
- **Port State Control** can detain a ship that does not hold a valid ILLC or whose load line marks are obscured.
- The Convention was driven by the **1862 Plimsoll Line Saga** in the UK, exposing the danger of overloading "coffin ships."
- **Overloading beyond the assigned load line** voids Hull and Machinery insurance and P&I cover.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Load Line Convention**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ISPS Code: ship security plan, security levels, declaration of security, ship security alert system, piracy, best management practices to deter piracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** ISPS Code: ship security plan, security levels, declaration of security, ship security alert system, piracy, best management practices to deter piracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The ISPS Code — Formal Definition

The **International Ship and Port Facility Security (ISPS) Code** is a comprehensive set of mandatory security measures adopted by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** through the **Diplomatic Conference on Maritime Security** held in London in **December 2002**, following the catastrophic events of **September 11, 2001**. The ISPS Code entered into force on **1 July 2004** and operates as Chapter **XI-2** of the **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), 1974**.

The Code applies to:
- Ships engaged on international voyages (passenger ships, cargo ships ≥ 500 GT, and high-speed craft)
- Port facilities serving such ships
- The **Contracting Governments** of SOLAS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ISPS Code is the cornerstone regulatory framework of modern maritime security law. Every shipping management professional must internalize its tripartite structure: **Ship**, **Port Facility**, and **Governmental** responsibilities.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Maritime Airport"

Think of the ISPS Code as the **"maritime equivalent of airport security."**

Imagine a passenger boarding an international flight. The passenger:
1. Goes through a **security check** (analogous to the **ship-port interface security check**)
2. Boards an aircraft that has a **flight plan and crew training** (analogous to the **Ship Security Plan**)
3. The cockpit has a **silent alarm** linked to authorities (analogous to the **Ship Security Alert System**)
4. The airport has **threat levels** (Green, Yellow, Red — analogous to **Security Levels 1, 2, 3**)
5. The pilot and air traffic control share a **joint security agreement** (analogous to the **Declaration of Security**)

The ship in this analogy is the "aircraft," the port facility is the "airport terminal," and the flag state + coastal state are the "aviation regulators."

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - ISPS Code effective date: **1 July 2004**
> - Incorporated as: **SOLAS Chapter XI-2**
> - Adopted at: **December 2002 IMO Diplomatic Conference, London**
> - Applies to ships: **≥ 500 GT on international voyages**

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of the ISPS Code

The ISPS Code is divided into two parts (A and B) and rests on three institutional pillars:

| Pillar | Responsible Party | Primary Document |
|---|---|---|
| **Ship Security** | Shipping Company / Master / Ship Security Officer (SSO) | **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** |
| **Port Facility Security** | Port Facility Operator / PFSO | **Port Facility Security Plan (PFSP)** |
| **Governmental Oversight** | Flag State / Coastal State / RSO | **SOLAS Chapter XI-2 regulations** |

### 1.4 Why the ISPS Code Was Necessary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 9/11 attacks exposed a glaring vulnerability: while aviation security had been tightened post-Lockerbie (1988), **maritime transport remained porous**. A container ship could carry weapons of mass destruction, suicide bombers, or explosive devices with virtually no security screening. The ISPS Code closed this gap by mandating a **risk-based, layered security regime** across the global maritime supply chain.

### 1.5 Key Definitions Under the ISPS Code (Regulation XI-2/1, Part A)

- **Ship Security Plan (SSP):** A plan developed to ensure the application of security measures on board the ship to protect persons on board, cargo, cargo transport units, ship's stores, and the ship from the risks of a security incident.
- **Ship Security Officer (SSO):** The person on board the ship, accountable to the Master, designated by the Company as responsible for the security of the ship.
- **Company Security Officer (CSO):** The person designated by the Company for ensuring that a ship security assessment is carried out and for ensuring the development, implementation, and maintenance of the SSP.
- **Port Facility Security Officer (PFSO):** The person responsible for the development, implementation, and maintenance of the port facility security plan.
- **Recognized Security Organization (RSO):** An organization with appropriate expertise in security matters and authorized by a Contracting Government to perform certain security-related tasks.
- **Declaration of Security (DoS):** An agreement reached between a ship and a port facility or between ships specifying the security measures each will undertake.
- **Security Level:** The qualification of the degree of risk that a security incident will be attempted or is currently present (Levels 1, 2, 3).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ship Security Plan (SSP) — Anatomy and Mandatory Content

The **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** is the operational heart of the ISPS Code for every applicable vessel. Regulation **XI-2/7** of SOLAS and **Part A, Section 9** of the ISPS Code prescribe its content.

#### 2.1.1 Mandatory Elements of the SSP (Part A §9)

According to **ISPS Code Part A, Paragraph 9**, the SSP must address, at a minimum, the following **three tiers** of security:

**Tier 1 — Access Control & Restricted Areas**
- Identification of restricted areas (e.g., bridge, engine room, cargo holds, steering gear compartment)
- Procedures to control access to the ship
- Procedures for screening of persons, baggage, personal effects, vehicles, and cargo
- Security of stores and ship's supplies

**Tier 2 — Handling of Cargo, Stores & Persons**
- Procedures for handling cargo and ship's stores
- Security of cargo transport units
- Monitoring of persons boarding the ship (including crew identification, visitor screening)

**Tier 3 — Threat Response & Communication**
- Procedures for responding to security threats or breaches
- Procedures for security communications
- Procedures for operating the **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)**
- Procedures for interfacing with the port facility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The SSP is CONFIDENTIAL.** Access to the plan is restricted to the Master, SSO, CSO, and approved flag state / RSO auditors. Unauthorized disclosure is a serious compliance breach.

#### 2.1.2 Ship Security Assessment (SSA) — The Predecessor Document

Before drafting the SSP, the CSO must conduct a **Ship Security Assessment (SSA)**, which is a documented risk analysis process. The SSA must include:

1. **On-scene security survey** — physical inspection of the vessel
2. **Identification of existing security measures** — what is already in place
3. **Identification and evaluation of key shipboard operations** — that need security protection
4. **Identification of weaknesses** — including human factors in security

The SSA is a **risk = threat × vulnerability × consequence** evaluation.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Point:** In KTU answers, always distinguish the **SSA (assessment = analysis of risk)** from the **SSP (plan = mitigation document)**. Many students confuse them.

### 2.2 Security Levels — The Tripartite Threat Matrix

Security Levels define the **degree of risk** of a security incident being attempted. They are the operational trigger that scales protective measures upward as the threat landscape worsens.

#### 2.2.1 The Three Security Levels

| Security Level | Color Code | Threat Posture | Trigger | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1** | 🟢 Green | Normal — baseline security | Always in effect at sea and in port | Routine port call, no specific intelligence |
| **Level 2** | 🟡 Yellow | Heightened — specific threat known | Set by Contracting Government for certain vessels/areas | Regional tensions, intelligence of plot |
| **Level 3** | 🔴 Red | Imminent — attack expected or in progress | Set by Contracting Government for specific targeted threat | Active terrorist operation in port region |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authority to set security levels:**
> - **Levels 1 & 2:** May be set by the **Flag State**, **Coastal State**, or **Port State**
> - **Level 3:** Reserved for **Contracting Governments** providing port services
> - The **Master always has overriding authority** to refuse to sail if the ship is unseaworthy from a security standpoint (SOLAS Reg. XI-2/8)

#### 2.2.2 Escalation Response Matrix

| Action | Level 1 | Level 2 | Level 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access to ship | Controlled, identified | Restricted, ID checks | Total restriction, armed guards possible |
| Monitoring | Periodic patrols | Continuous surveillance | Armed watch, 24/7 CCTV |
| Restricted areas | Defined and locked | Doubly secured | Continuous manned watch |
| Cargo handling | Standard | Verified manifests | Suspended or under armed escort |
| SSAS | Tested periodically | Tested before transit | Active and ready to transmit |

### 2.3 Declaration of Security (DoS) — Inter-Agency Trust Contract

#### 2.3.1 Definition and Purpose

The **Declaration of Security (DoS)** is a written agreement between a **ship and a port facility** (or between two ships) that records the security measures each party will implement at the ship-port interface. It is the **interface-layer instrument** of the ISPS Code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Regulation XI-2/10** and **ISPS Code Part A §5** govern the DoS.

#### 2.3.2 When is a DoS Required?

A DoS must be completed when:
1. The ship is operating at a **Security Level 2 or 3**
2. The ship is operating at a port facility where a DoS is required by the Contracting Government
3. There has been a **security incident** involving the ship or port facility
4. The **Master or PFSO** requests one
5. The ship is engaged in **passenger transport** or carrying dangerous goods (DG)

#### 2.3.3 The DoS Process — Five Sequential Steps

| Step | Action | Responsible Party |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Identify when DoS is required | Master / SSO / PFSO |
| **2** | Exchange security-related information | SSO ↔ PFSO |
| **3** | Document agreed security measures | Joint (SSO + PFSO) |
| **4** | Sign and timestamp the DoS | Master + PFSO |
| **5** | Retain DoS on board for inspection | SSO |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The DoS is **not always mandatory** at Level 1. Many students incorrectly state "DoS is always required." It is conditional based on threat level and ship-port profile.

### 2.4 Ship Security Alert System (SSAS) — The Silent Witness

#### 2.4.1 Definition

The **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)** is a **silent alarm** installed on board a vessel to transmit a covert security alert to the **flag state and designated shoreside authorities** when a security incident is in progress or imminent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SOLAS Chapter XI-2, Regulation 6** mandates SSAS for all ships ≥ 500 GT on international voyages, with effective date **1 July 2004**.

#### 2.4.2 Technical & Operational Characteristics

| Feature | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Activation** | Covert — at least **two separately activated stations** on the bridge and another protected location (e.g., Master's cabin, SSO cabin) |
| **Signal Type** | Silent, unacknowledged transmission to prevent hijacker detection |
| **Recipients** | Flag State (primary), Shipping Company, optionally Coastal States |
| **Reset** | Only from the shoreside authority — prevents hijacker suppression |
| **Trigger** | SSO or crew under duress |
| **Type of Alert** | Ship-to-shore security alert (does NOT trigger GMDSS) |

#### 2.4.3 Why SSAS is "Silent"

Unlike the **Global Maritime Distress and Safety System (GMDSS)**, which broadcasts in the open, SSAS is deliberately **covert and unidirectional** because:
- A hijacker who knows the alarm is sent may accelerate violent action
- Open broadcasting could warn accomplices ashore
- The goal is **tactical interdiction** by naval forces, not rescue by the vessel itself

### 2.5 Piracy — The Persistent Maritime Threat

#### 2.5.1 Definition Under UNCLOS

**Article 101 of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982** defines piracy as:

> "Any illegal acts of violence or detention, or any act of depredation, committed for private ends by the crew or passengers of a private ship or aircraft against another ship or aircraft, or against persons or property on board such ship or aircraft, on the high seas or outside the jurisdiction of any State."

#### 2.5.2 Three Cumulative Legal Requirements for Piracy (UNCLOS Art. 101)

For an act to constitute piracy, **all three** must be present:

1. **Illegal acts of violence / detention / depredation** — robbery, kidnapping, murder, hostage-taking
2. **For private ends** — distinguishes piracy from state-sponsored or political acts
3. **Two vessels** — a pirate ship attacking another ship (mutiny by crew alone is NOT piracy under international law; it is internal mutiny)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The location requirement — piracy must occur **on the high seas OR outside the jurisdiction of any State**. Acts within territorial waters of a coastal state are not piracy under UNCLOS (they may be armed robbery at sea under national law).

#### 2.5.4 Hotspots of Modern Piracy

| Region | Notable Group | Risk Period |
|---|---|---|
| Gulf of Aden / Somali Coast | Somali pirates | 2008–2012 peak |
| Gulf of Guinea | Niger Delta militants | 2010–present |
| Strait of Malacca / Singapore | Indonesian / Malaysian pirates | Historical, declining |
| Caribbean / Sulu Sea | Various | Sporadic |

#### 2.5.5 Distinction: Piracy vs Armed Robbery at Sea

| Criterion | Piracy (UNCLOS) | Armed Robbery at Sea (SUA Convention / national law) |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | High seas / outside State jurisdiction | Within territorial waters or EEZ of a State |
| **Legal Regime** | Universal jurisdiction (any state may seize) | Jurisdiction of the coastal state |
| **Definition Source** | UNCLOS Art. 101 | IMO Resolution A.1025(26) |

### 2.6 Best Management Practices (BMP) to Deter Piracy

The **Best Management Practices (BMP)** series is a set of voluntary, industry-developed guidelines published jointly by several maritime associations to assist ships in avoiding, deterring, and delaying piracy attacks, particularly in high-risk waters like the **Gulf of Aden, Indian Ocean, and West Africa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BMP is not law** — it is a **self-regulatory, risk-management tool** published by industry bodies. The current version is **BMP5 (2023)**, building on BMP1 (2008), BMP2 (2009), BMP3 (2010), BMP4 (2011), and the West Africa version **BMP West Africa**.

#### 2.6.1 The Four-Layer Defense Framework of BMP

**Layer 1 — Avoidance (Pre-Voyage)**
- Consult latest advisories (MSCHOA, UKMTO, SHADE)
- Avoid high-risk transit areas; use recommended corridors
- Brief crew on threat situation
- Plan for Group Transit or Naval Convoy

**Layer 2 — Deterrence (Transit)**
- Speed ≥ 18 knots when possible (most pirates use small boats; speed is the single most effective deterrent)
- Use of razor wire, water cannon, fire hoses at deck level
- Use of citadels for safe-room refuge
- Bridge hardening — bulletproof plates over windows
- Watchkeepers in 24/7 rotation

**Layer 3 — Defense (Attack)**
- Activate SSAS immediately
- Use Long Range Acoustic Devices (LRAD)
- Sound the General Alarm
- Crew mustering in pre-designated safe rooms (citadels)
- Use of armed guards where legally permitted and risk-justified
- Engage evasive maneuvering

**Layer 4 — Post-Event (Reporting)**
- Report to UKMTO / MSCHOA / MARLO
- Cooperate with naval forces
- Preserve evidence for prosecution

#### 2.6.2 The Five Core BMP Principles

1. **Register** the voyage with the **Maritime Security Centre Horn of Africa (MSCHOA)**
2. **Report** to **UKMTO Dubai** at entry, exit, and through transit
3. **Comply** with the corridor / Group Transit scheme
4. **Train** crew in piracy response drills
5. **Implement** the layered defense in depth

#### 2.6.3 Use of Privately Contracted Armed Security Personnel (PCASP)

The carriage of **armed guards** is governed by:
- **Flag state permission** (varies — some flags allow, some forbid)
- **Coastal state permission** (e.g., India prohibits; transit states in GoA allow with conditions)
- The use of force must comply with **international humanitarian law** and **flag state regulations**

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **No mathematical formulas apply to this topic**, but the following **legal / regulatory parameters** are exam-critical:

| Parameter | Value / Specification | Source |
|---|---|---|
| ISPS Code effective date | **1 July 2004** | SOLAS Ch. XI-2 |
| ISPS Code adopted at | **December 2002, London Diplomatic Conference** | IMO |
| SSP mandatory content | **3 tiers (access, cargo, response)** | ISPS Part A §9 |
| Security Levels | **1, 2, 3 (Normal, Heightened, Imminent)** | ISPS Part A §2 |
| SSAS minimum activation points | **2 (bridge + protected location)** | SOLAS Reg. XI-2/6 |
| Piracy legal definition | **UNCLOS Article 101** | UNCLOS, 1982 |
| Piracy jurisdiction | **Universal (high seas only)** | UNCLOS Art. 105 |
| BMP current version | **BMP5 (2023)** | Industry publication |
| Recommended transit speed | **≥ 18 knots** | BMP |
| DoS required at | **Levels 2 & 3, or by request** | ISPS Part A §5 |
| Ship applicability threshold | **≥ 500 GT, international voyages** | SOLAS Reg. XI-2/3 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Risk Assessment Logic — The Threat × Vulnerability × Consequence Model

The ISPS Code adopts a **risk-based methodology** for ship security assessment. While the Code does not provide a single canonical formula, the **IMO MSC.1/Circ.1188 (Guidelines for Ship Security Assessment)** adopts the following logical risk equation:

$$R = T \times V \times C$$

Where:
- $R$ = Security Risk (qualitative rating: Low / Medium / High)
- $T$ = Threat (likelihood of attack attempt)
- $V$ = Vulnerability (weakness in defenses)
- $C$ = Consequence (impact severity if attack succeeds)

> **Note:** Each variable is scored on a qualitative scale (e.g., 1–5) and the product is a relative risk index used to prioritize mitigation measures.

#### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Application of the Risk Model

**Step 1: Threat Assessment**

$$T = \text{score based on intelligence, historical incidents, geopolitical climate}$$

For example, a vessel transiting the **Gulf of Aden** in 2011 scored $T = 5$ on the IMO qualitative scale.

**Step 2: Vulnerability Assessment**

$$V = \text{score based on ship design, speed, crew training, hardening}$$

A bulk carrier with low freeboard, no razor wire, and no SSAS activation drill history might score $V = 4$.

**Step 3: Consequence Assessment**

$$C = \text{score based on cargo value, human risk, environmental impact}$$

A VLCC carrying 2 million barrels of crude oil might score $C = 5$.

**Step 4: Compute Risk**

$$R = 5 \times 4 \times 5 = 100 \text{ (arbitrary units, High Risk)}$$

**Step 5: Mitigation Selection**

Based on the high $R$ score, the SSP would mandate **BMP5 compliance, PCASP engagement, 18+ knot speed, citadel construction, and SSAS verification**.

#### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example

A container vessel of 8,000 TEU capacity is bound from Singapore to Rotterdam. Threat intelligence indicates **low piracy activity** in the South China Sea, **medium activity** in the Indian Ocean, and **no activity** in the Mediterranean. The vessel has **no armed guards**, **moderate hardening**, and **average crew training**.

| Region | T | V | C | R = T×V×C | Mitigation Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Strait of Malacca | 2 | 3 | 4 | 24 | Standard BMP |
| Indian Ocean (GoA) | 4 | 3 | 4 | 48 | Enhanced BMP + PCASP |
| Mediterranean | 1 | 3 | 4 | 12 | Routine watch |

The risk equation drives **region-specific security postures** — this is the analytical foundation of the BMP "defense in depth" approach.

### 3.2 Decision Tree — When is a Declaration of Security Required?

The following **logical decision chain** determines the necessity of a DoS:

$$\text{Is the ship at Security Level 2 or 3?} \longrightarrow \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{DoS MANDATORY}$$

$$\text{Is the ship at Security Level 1?} \longrightarrow \text{Check secondary triggers:}$$

$$\text{Secondary Trigger 1: Is the ship carrying dangerous goods (IMDG Code)?} \Rightarrow \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{DoS REQUIRED}$$

$$\text{Secondary Trigger 2: Did the port state / coastal state mandate DoS at this facility?} \Rightarrow \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{DoS REQUIRED}$$

$$\text{Secondary Trigger 3: Did a security incident occur at the port recently?} \Rightarrow \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{DoS REQUIRED}$$

$$\text{Secondary Trigger 4: Did the Master or PFSO request a DoS?} \Rightarrow \text{Yes} \Rightarrow \text{DoS REQUIRED}$$

If **all four secondary triggers are negative**, the DoS is **optional** but recommended for high-value cargoes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The DoS is signed BEFORE** the ship-to-port interface operation begins (cargo loading, bunkering, passenger embarkation, etc.) and retained on board for **the duration of the port call + a statutory period** as required by the flag state.

### 3.3 Security Level Escalation Logic — Mapping Threat to Action

The ISPS Code's **Part A, Sections 7 & 8** prescribe a graded response. The analytical structure is:

$$\text{Threat Intelligence} \longrightarrow \text{Contracting Government sets Level} \longrightarrow \text{Master implements graded measures}$$

#### 3.3.1 Level 1 → Level 2 Escalation Triggers

- Specific intelligence of an attack on a similar vessel
- Heightened political tension in the region
- Recent piracy incident in the port area

**Required additional measures at Level 2:**
- Increased vigilance and watchkeeping
- Verification of all visitors and their effects
- Intensified monitoring of restricted areas
- Coordination with port facility on enhanced DoS

#### 3.3.2 Level 2 → Level 3 Escalation Triggers

- Confirmed imminent attack in the port vicinity
- Active terrorist operation targeting maritime assets
- Naval interdiction of suspected attack vessels in the approach lanes

**Required additional measures at Level 3:**
- Suspension of all non-essential ship operations
- Suspension of cargo handling
- Total restriction of access
- Continuous manning of critical stations
- Possible evacuation (where feasible)
- SSAS ready to activate

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Master authority:** At any level, the **Master's discretion** overrides government-set levels if the Master believes security is inadequate (SOLAS Reg. XI-2/8, Paragraph 5).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — ISPS vs BMP vs SUA

| Parameter | ISPS Code | BMP (Best Management Practices) | SUA Convention (1988 / 2005 protocols) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Mandatory (under SOLAS) | Voluntary (industry guideline) | Mandatory treaty |
| **Scope** | Ships, ports, governments | Ships transiting high-risk waters | Vessels, fixed platforms, offshore installations |
| **Focus** | Preventive baseline security | Anti-piracy operational tactics | Criminalization of maritime offenses |
| **Issuing Body** | IMO | Industry (ICS, OCIMF, INTERTANKO, BIMCO, etc.) | IMO / UN |
| **Year** | 2002 / in force 2004 | BMP1: 2008 → BMP5: 2023 | 1988 (in force 1992), 2005 protocols |
| **Sanctions for non-compliance** | Port state control detention, fines, sailing ban | None (reputational / insurance risk) | Criminal prosecution |

### 3.5 Piracy Legal Classification — Step-by-Step Test

Applying UNCLOS Art. 101 to a typical scenario (e.g., 2011 MV *Sirius Star* hijacking):

**Test 1: Illegal act of violence or detention?** 
→ YES — boarding with weapons, hostage-taking, theft of cargo and vessel.

**Test 2: For private ends?**
→ YES — ransom demand of $3 million. (If the act were politically motivated by a state actor, it would NOT be piracy under UNCLOS — it could be terrorism or an armed conflict.)

**Test 3: Two ships (private ship attacking another)?**
→ YES — pirate skiff (attacker) and the *Sirius Star* (target).

**Test 4: On the high seas or outside State jurisdiction?**
→ YES — event occurred in the **Indian Ocean, ~450 nm southeast of Kenya**, beyond any EEZ.

$$\therefore \text{All four tests met} \Longrightarrow \text{Classification: ACT OF PIRACY under UNCLOS Art. 101}$$

**Consequence:** Universal jurisdiction applies (UNCLOS Art. 105) — any state could in principle have seized the pirates, but in practice, naval response was led by **Combined Task Force 151 (CTF-151)** and **EU NAVFOR Operation Atalanta**.
