## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Define the term **"Declaration of Security"** as envisaged under the ISPS Code. State **any two situations** when a DoS becomes mandatory.

**Model Answer:**

A **Declaration of Security (DoS)** is a written agreement reached between a **ship and a port facility** (or between two ships) under **ISPS Code Part A, Section 5** and **SOLAS Regulation XI-2/10**, which records the security measures each party will implement to maintain the security of the ship-port interface.

**Two mandatory situations:**
1. When the ship is operating at **Security Level 2 or 3**.
2. When the **port facility has experienced a security incident** or when there has been a threat to the facility.
3. (Any other acceptable answer: dangerous goods, passenger ships, port state mandate, Master/PFSO request)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** What is the **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)**? Why is it designed as a **silent** and **covert** system rather than an open broadcast?

**Model Answer:**

The **Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)** is a mandatory security device fitted on all ships of **500 GT and above** engaged on international voyages, as required by **SOLAS Regulation XI-2/6** and the **ISPS Code Part A, Section 9.4**. The SSAS transmits a covert ship-to-shore security alert to the **flag state and designated shoreside authorities** when a security threat or incident occurs on board.

**Why silent/covert:**
- To prevent **hijackers from learning** that an alert has been raised, which could escalate violence against the crew.
- To allow **tactical response** by naval or law enforcement forces without warning the attackers.
- The signal is **unacknowledged** and is only resettable by the shoreside authority, preventing suppression by hostile actors on board.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Module 2, Model Question, 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose A OR B**

**Q(a). [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **three Security Levels** prescribed under the ISPS Code. State the **authority to set** each level and the **graded response measures** at each level.

**Q(b). [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Best Management Practices (BMP)** framework developed to deter piracy. Explain the **four-layer defense in depth** model with relevant examples.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q(a) [7 Marks]**

The ISPS Code, in **Part A, Section 2**, defines **three security levels** that correspond to the **degree of risk** of a security incident:

**Security Level 1 — Normal (Green):**
- This is the **baseline level** at which ships and port facilities normally operate.
- Set as a default by all parties; no special intelligence or threat is required.
- Measures include routine access control, identification of restricted areas, monitoring of cargo and stores, and periodic security drills.

**Security Level 2 — Heightened (Yellow):**
- Triggered when there is a **specific threat** of a security incident, though the attack is not imminent.
- Can be set by the **Flag State, Coastal State, or Port State**.
- Measures include **increased vigilance**, verification of all persons boarding, intensified monitoring of restricted areas, and enhanced DoS coordination.

**Security Level 3 — Imminent (Red):**
- Triggered when a **security incident is imminent or in progress**.
- Set by the **Contracting Government** for a specific vessel, port, or geographical area.
- Measures include **total restriction of access**, suspension of cargo operations, continuous manning of critical stations, and readiness to activate the SSAS.

> **Authority summary:**
> - Levels 1 & 2 → Flag / Coastal / Port States
> - Level 3 → Contracting Government
> - The **Master always retains overriding authority** to refuse sailing if security is inadequate (SOLAS Reg. XI-2/8).

> **Examiner's note:** A 7-mark answer should cover all three levels with **both the trigger and the response**, plus a brief mention of authority and Master discretion. (2 marks per level + 1 mark for the Master authority).

---

#### **Model Solution to Q(b) [7 Marks]**

The **Best Management Practices (BMP)** are voluntary, industry-developed guidelines — currently **BMP5 (2023)** — published by maritime associations (ICS, OCIMF, BIMCO, INTERTANKO, etc.) to assist ships in **avoiding, deterring, and delaying** piracy attacks, particularly in the **Gulf of Aden, Indian Ocean, and West African waters**.

**The Four-Layer Defense in Depth:**

**Layer 1 — Avoidance (Pre-Voyage Planning):**
- Register the voyage with **MSCHOA** (Maritime Security Centre Horn of Africa).
- Report to **UKMTO Dubai** at entry, mid-transit, and exit.
- Follow **recommended transit corridors** and consider **group transits** with naval escorts.
- Brief the crew on the prevailing threat situation.

**Layer 2 — Deterrence (In-Transit Measures):**
- Maintain a speed of **≥ 18 knots** (the single most effective passive deterrent, as pirate skiffs cannot match modern ships at high speed).
- Install **razor wire, water cannons, and fire hoses** at the deck and access points.
- Implement **bridge hardening** (bulletproof / shatter-resistant glass).
- Maintain **24/7 watchkeeping** with binoculars and radar.

**Layer 3 — Defense (During Attack):**
- Activate the **SSAS** immediately to alert flag state and naval forces.
- Sound the **General Alarm** and muster the crew in a **citadel** (pre-built safe room).
- Deploy **Long Range Acoustic Devices (LRAD)** for acoustic deterrence.
- Engage **Privately Contracted Armed Security Personnel (PCASP)** where legally permitted by the flag state and transit states.
- Execute **evasive maneuvers** (hard turns, speed bursts, disabling lights).

**Layer 4 — Post-Event (Reporting & Recovery):**
- Report the incident to **UKMTO, MSCHOA**, and **MARLO** (Maritime Liaison Office).
- Cooperate with naval forces for response and evidence recovery.
- Preserve all CCTV, communication logs, and witness statements for **post-incident prosecution** or ransom negotiation.

> **Examiner's note:** A 7-mark answer should cover the **four layers with examples** (approx. 1.5 marks per layer) plus a brief mention of the BMP governance (0.5 mark) and the current BMP5 version (0.5 mark).

---

#### **Alternative Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**Q(a). [7 Marks]** Explain the **mandatory contents** of a **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** as per the ISPS Code. Distinguish between **SSP** and **SSA**.

**Q(b). [7 Marks]** Critically examine the **legal definition of piracy** under **UNCLOS Article 101**. How does it differ from **armed robbery at sea**?

---

#### **Model Solution to Alternative Q(a) [7 Marks]**

The **Ship Security Plan (SSP)** is developed by the **Company Security Officer (CSO)** after conducting a **Ship Security Assessment (SSA)**. Its content is prescribed by **ISPS Code Part A, Section 9** and **SOLAS Regulation XI-2/7**.

**Mandatory contents of the SSP (organized into three tiers):**

**Tier 1 — Access Control & Restricted Areas:**
- Identification of restricted areas (bridge, engine room, steering gear, communication room, etc.)
- Procedures for controlling access to the ship
- Screening of persons, baggage, personal effects, vehicles
- Security of stores and ship's supplies

**Tier 2 — Handling of Cargo, Stores, and Persons:**
- Procedures for handling cargo and ship's stores
- Security of cargo transport units (CTUs)
- Identification of crew, passengers, and visitors
- Monitoring of persons boarding the ship

**Tier 3 — Threat Response & Communication:**
- Procedures for responding to security threats, breaches, or incidents
- Security communication procedures
- Operating procedures for the **SSAS**
- Procedures for interfacing with the **port facility** and the PFSO

**Distinction — SSP vs SSA:**

| Parameter | SSA (Assessment) | SSP (Plan) |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Risk analysis — identifies threats and weaknesses | Risk mitigation — specifies countermeasures |
| **Nature** | Analytical, diagnostic | Operational, prescriptive |
| **Conducted by** | CSO with shore-based expertise | CSO with the Master and SSO |
| **Output** | Risk report | Confidential operational plan |
| **Frequency** | Renewed every 5 years or on major change | Continuous maintenance and review |

> **Examiner's note:** A 7-mark answer should cover (i) the three tiers of SSP (3 marks), (ii) the SSA definition (2 marks), and (iii) the tabular distinction (2 marks).

---

#### **Model Solution to Alternative Q(b) [7 Marks]**

**UNCLOS Article 101** defines piracy as:
> "Any illegal acts of violence or detention, or any act of depredation, committed for private ends by the crew or passengers of a private ship or aircraft, against another ship or aircraft, or against persons or property on board such ship or aircraft, on the high seas or outside the jurisdiction of any State."

**Critical Examination — Three Cumulative Elements:**

1. **Illegal acts of violence, detention, or depredation:** The acts must be unlawful. Includes murder, robbery, hostage-taking, kidnapping. (1.5 marks)

2. **For private ends:** Distinguishes piracy from political or state-sponsored acts. A terrorist attack by a non-state actor **for ideological ends** may not be piracy under strict UNCLOS interpretation, although it is captured under the **SUA Convention 2005 Protocol**. (1.5 marks)

3. **Two vessels involved (private ship attacking another ship):** Mutiny by crew alone is NOT piracy under UNCLOS; it is an internal matter. (1 mark)

4. **Location — on high seas OR outside the jurisdiction of any State:** Acts within territorial waters are not piracy under UNCLOS. (1 mark)

**Difference from Armed Robbery at Sea:**

| Aspect | Piracy (UNCLOS Art. 101) | Armed Robbery at Sea (SUA / national law) |
|---|---|---|
| Location | High seas / outside State jurisdiction | Within territorial waters or EEZ |
| Jurisdiction | Universal — any State may seize | Coastal State has primary jurisdiction |
| Definition | Treaty-based (UNCLOS) | IMO Resolution A.1025(26); national criminal law |
| Legal regime | Public international law | Domestic criminal law |

(2 marks for the distinction)

> **Examiner's note:** A 7-mark answer should cover the three/four elements of UNCLOS Art. 101 (4 marks) and the difference from armed robbery (3 marks). Use of case examples (e.g., *Sirius Star* 2008, *Maersk Alabama* 2009) earns extra credit.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on ISPS Code / Piracy Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing SSP with SSA:** Many students write them interchangeably. SSA = assessment (analysis); SSP = plan (mitigation). Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this confusion.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the location element of piracy:** Students often define piracy using only "violence at sea." They forget the **high seas / outside State jurisdiction** requirement. Marks lost: **1–2**.
> 
> 3. **Treating BMP as law:** BMP is **voluntary**, not mandatory. Stating "BMP is required by SOLAS" is wrong. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
> 
> 4. **Misattributing the SSAS to GMDSS:** The SSAS is **silent and unacknowledged**, distinct from the open GMDSS distress system. Mark deduction: **1–2 marks**.
> 
> 5. **Omitting the "two-ship" requirement in piracy:** UNCLOS Art. 101 requires **two vessels**. An internal mutiny by crew against Master is NOT piracy under international law. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
> 
> 6. **Conflating the three levels' authority:** Some students say "Master sets all levels." In fact, Levels 2 and 3 are set by **Contracting Governments**; the Master can only **request or refuse sailing**. Mark deduction: **1 mark**.
> 
> 7. **Forgetting that the SSP is CONFIDENTIAL:** Always state in your answer that the SSP is **confidential** and access is restricted.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2, ISPS Code & Piracy**

**A. Foundational Pillars**
- ISPS Code is **Chapter XI-2 of SOLAS, 1974**, adopted at the **December 2002 IMO Diplomatic Conference**, in force **1 July 2004**.
- Applies to **ships ≥ 500 GT on international voyages**, **port facilities**, and **Contracting Governments**.
- Three pillars: **Ship**, **Port Facility**, **Governmental Oversight**.

**B. Ship Security Plan (SSP)**
- Developed by the **CSO** after conducting the **SSA**.
- Three tiers: **Access Control**, **Cargo/Stores/Persons**, **Threat Response/Comms**.
- **Confidential** document.
- Reviewed every 5 years or after a major change.

**C. Security Levels**
- **Level 1 (Normal, Green)** — baseline.
- **Level 2 (Heightened, Yellow)** — specific threat.
- **Level 3 (Imminent, Red)** — attack expected.
- Levels 2 & 3 set by **Contracting Government**.
- **Master's overriding authority** to refuse sailing.

**D. Declaration of Security (DoS)**
- Mandatory at **Level 2 or 3**, and for dangerous goods, passenger ships, port state mandates, or incident history.
- Signed between **Master and PFSO** BEFORE ship-port operations begin.
- Retained on board for inspection.

**E. Ship Security Alert System (SSAS)**
- **SOLAS Reg. XI-2/6** mandate.
- **At least two activation points** (bridge + protected location).
- **Covert, silent, unacknowledged**, only resettable from shore.
- Distinct from GMDSS.

**F. Piracy (UNCLOS Art. 101)**
- **Four cumulative elements**: violence, private ends, two ships, high seas.
- **Universal jurisdiction** (Art. 105) — any state may seize.
- Distinguished from **armed robbery at sea** (within territorial waters, coastal state jurisdiction).

**G. BMP (Best Management Practices)**
- **Voluntary, industry-driven**, not law.
- Current version: **BMP5 (2023)**, plus **BMP West Africa**.
- Four-layer model: **Avoidance, Deterrence, Defense, Post-Event**.
- Key tools: **MSCHOA registration, UKMTO reporting, ≥18 knots speed, citadel, PCASP, SSAS**.

**H. Critical Terminology — Do Not Confuse**
- SSA (assessment) ≠ SSP (plan)
- Piracy (UNCLOS) ≠ Armed robbery at sea (national law)
- SSAS (covert) ≠ GMDSS (open)
- BMP (voluntary) ≠ ISPS (mandatory)

**I. Hotspot Areas**
- **Gulf of Aden / Somali Coast** (historically most active; declined post-2012).
- **Gulf of Guinea / Niger Delta** (rising threat; kidnapping for ransom).
- **Strait of Malacca** (declining).
- **Caribbean / Sulu Sea** (sporadic).

**J. Famous Cases for Reference**
- **MV Sirius Star (2008)** — Saudi VLCC, $3M ransom, 60 nm off Somalia.
- **MV Maersk Alabama (2009)** — Captain Phillips hijacking, US Navy rescue.
- **MT Brillante Virtuoso (2011)** — Gulf of Aden, suspected false flag.
- **MT Nave Constellation (2016)** — Gulf of Guinea, kidnapping.
- **Stolt Sakura (2017)** — Gulf of Guinea, chemical tanker.

---

> **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — ISPS CODE & PIRACY**
> 
> **Next Module Preview:** Module 3 typically covers *MARPOL, Ballast Water Management, and Annex VI — Air Pollution from Ships*. Continue with KTU-prepped notes from this series.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to other conventions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to other conventions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Other Conventions" in Maritime Law?

In the architecture of international maritime law, the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, a specialised agency of the United Nations headquartered in **London, United Kingdom**, has promulgated a vast body of conventions that govern almost every facet of shipping — from the design of a hull to the discharge of ballast water, from the working hours of a seafarer to the financial liability in case of a wreck. After studying the foundational instruments in earlier modules, Module 2 turns to the **"other conventions"** — the supplementary international treaties that complete the regulatory framework, addressing safety, pollution prevention, training, labour, navigation, and salvage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Other conventions* in maritime law refer to the secondary yet equally binding international treaties adopted under the auspices of the IMO (and related bodies such as the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** and the **United Nations**) that supplement the core conventions. They regulate specific operational, environmental, navigational, and social aspects of shipping, and become legally binding on a State only upon **ratification, acceptance, approval, or accession**, in accordance with their respective final clauses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Maritime Rule-Book Library"

Imagine international shipping as a **massive, complex global highway system** where ships of every nationality travel across oceans carrying trillions of dollars' worth of cargo. Without rules, this system would collapse into chaos. The IMO conventions act like a **multi-volume library of rule-books**:

- **Volume 1 (Core)** — SOLAS, MARPOL — the "big books" covered in Module 1.
- **Volume 2 (This Module)** — Load Lines, COLREGs, STCW, MLC, BWM, OPRC, AFS, FAL, Hong Kong, Nairobi, etc. — the **"specialised handbooks"** for specific scenarios.
- **Volume 3** — Regional protocols, codes, and circulars.

Just as a driver must know both the *highway code* and the *parking regulations*, a maritime professional must understand both the **primary** and the **specialised** conventions. Each "other convention" tackles a specific problem — for example, the **Load Lines Convention** stops ships from being overloaded (like a weight limit on a truck), while the **COLREGs** act as the **"road traffic rules of the sea"** (the equivalent of traffic signals and right-of-way).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Suffix Convention Used by IMO:**
> The year that appears in parentheses — e.g., **SOLAS, 1974** or **MARPOL 73/78** — is the **year of adoption**, NOT the year of entry into force. The convention only becomes binding internationally after a stipulated number of states (usually tied to a percentage of world tonnage) have ratified it.

### 1.3 Classification of the "Other Conventions"

For systematic study aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme, these conventions are best classified into **five functional pillars**:

| S.No. | Functional Pillar | Principal Conventions Covered |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ship Design & Structural Safety** | Load Lines (LL), 1966; Tonnage Measurement, 1969 |
| 2 | **Navigation & Traffic Management** | COLREGs, 1972; FAL, 1965; UNCLOS, 1982 |
| 3 | **Environmental Protection** | OPRC, 1990; AFS, 2001; BWM, 2004; Hong Kong, 2009 |
| 4 | **Human Element & Labour** | STCW, 1978 (as amended); MLC, 2006 |
| 5 | **Civil Liability & Wreck Removal** | Nairobi Wreck Removal, 2007; CLC, Bunker, Athens, HNS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 2):**
> The course outcome mapped to this topic is **CO2**: *"Describe the major international maritime conventions other than SOLAS and MARPOL and explain their application to shipping operations."* The cognitive level, per **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**, is predominantly **Understand (L2)** and **Apply (L3)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

### 2.1 The International Convention on Load Lines (LL), 1966

#### 2.1.1 Background and Rationale

The LL Convention was adopted at the **IMO Conference in London** in **1966** and entered into force on **21 July 1968**. It replaced a patchwork of earlier national load-line rules. Its purpose is to **establish limits on the permissible draught** to which a ship may be loaded, ensuring adequate **reserve buoyancy** and **freeboard** to prevent overloading — the historical cause of catastrophic losses like the sinking of the *SS Princess Sophia* in 1918.

> [!NOTE]
> **Freeboard** is the vertical distance measured on the ship's side between the **Plimsoll line** (the load line mark) and the upper edge of the **deck line**. It is the *visible safety margin* against sinking.

#### 2.1.2 Structural Logic of the Convention

- **Annex I — Regulations for Determining Load Lines**: Contains the formulas, tables, and conditions (Zone, Season, Type of Ship) used by classification societies to assign load lines.
- **Annex II — Zones, Areas, and Seasonal Periods**: Divides the world's oceans into **Summer Zones, Winter Zones, Tropical Zones, and Seasonal Tropical/ Winter Zones**.
- **Conditions of Assignment**: A ship is assigned a load line only if it **survives a survery** and the **plimsoll mark** is permanently marked on each side of the hull.

#### 2.1.3 Key Concepts for Examinations

- **Plimsoll Mark** — a circular disc with a horizontal line through its centre, with letter markings such as **TF (Tropical Fresh), F (Fresh), T (Tropical), S (Summer), W (Winter), WNA (Winter North Atlantic)**.
- **Load Line Certificate** — issued for a period not exceeding **5 years**.
- **Convention amended by the 1988 Protocol** — introducing the harmonised system of survey and certification.

---

### 2.2 Convention on the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs), 1972

Often called the **"Rules of the Road at Sea"**, COLREGs were adopted on **20 October 1972** and entered into force on **15 July 1977**. They apply to **all vessels upon the high seas and all waters connected therewith navigable by seagoing vessels**.

The convention is divided into **five parts** and contains **38 Rules**:

| Part | Subject | Key Rules |
|---|---|---|
| **A — General** | Application, Responsibility | Rules 1–3 |
| **B — Steering & Sailing** | Conduct of vessels in any condition of visibility | Rules 4–19 |
| **C — Lights and Shapes** | Mandatory lights and day-shapes | Rules 20–31 |
| **D — Sound and Light Signals** | Maneuvering, warning, fog signals | Rules 32–37 |
| **E — Exemptions** | Grandfathering of existing vessels | Rule 38 |

#### 2.2.1 The 4 Pillars of Steering & Sailing Rules

- **Look-out** (Rule 5) — every vessel must maintain a proper look-out at all times.
- **Safe Speed** (Rule 6) — speed must allow proper and effective action to be taken.
- **Risk of Collision** (Rule 7) — every vessel must use all available means to determine if risk exists.
- **Action to Avoid Collision** (Rule 8) — maneuvers must be **positive, made in ample time, and follow good seamanship**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 18 — Priority Between Vessels:**
> A **vessel not under command (NUC)**, a **vessel restricted in her ability to maneuver (RAM)**, a vessel **constrained by her draught**, and a vessel **engaged in fishing** when not making way have priority over power-driven vessels, sailing vessels, and seaplanes — *in that descending order of priority*.

---

### 2.3 International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Seafarers (STCW), 1978

Adopted **7 July 1978**, entered into force **28 April 1984**, STCW is the **single most important convention for the human element in shipping**. It was substantially revised by the **2010 Manila Amendments**, which entered into force on **1 January 2012**.

#### 2.3.1 Core Architecture

- **Chapter I — General Provisions**
- **Chapter II — Master and Deck Department** (Navigation, Cargo handling, etc.)
- **Chapter III — Engine Department**
- **Chapter IV — Radio Communication and Radio Personnel**
- **Chapter V — Special requirements for tankers, passenger ships, high-speed craft**
- **Chapter VI — Emergency, occupational safety, medical care, security**
- **Chapter VII — Watchkeeping arrangements and principles**
- **Chapter VIII — Fitness for duty** (added in Manila Amendments — hours of rest, drug and alcohol abuse)

> [!NOTE]
> **STCW Manila Amendments — Major New Features:**
> - **Rest Hours:** Minimum **10 hours of rest in any 24-hour period**, with **77 hours in any 7-day period**.
> - **CoCs (Certificates of Competency):** Mandatory for masters, officers, and ratings.
> - **Security Awareness Training:** Every seafarer must receive security-related familiarisation training.

---

### 2.4 Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006

Adopted by the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** on **23 February 2006** and entered into force on **20 August 2013** — MLC 2006 is the **"Fourth Pillar"** of maritime regulation alongside SOLAS, MARPOL, and STCW. It is often called the **"Seafarers' Bill of Rights"**.

#### 2.4.1 Five Titles Structure

| Title | Subject | Example Provision |
|---|---|---|
| **Title 1** | Minimum requirements for seafarers to work on a ship | Minimum age (**16**), medical certificate |
| **Title 2** | Conditions of employment | Repatriation, manning levels, wages, leave |
| **Title 3** | Accommodation, recreational facilities, food & catering | Minimum floor area per seafarer, ventilation standards |
| **Title 4** | Health protection, medical care, welfare, social security | On-board medical chest, occupational safety |
| **Title 5** | Compliance and enforcement | **Maritime Labour Certificate (MLC)** and **Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance (DMLC)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** MLC 2006 is the only convention in maritime law that uses the **"no more favourable treatment"** clause — meaning, port states can enforce its standards on non-ratifying states' ships entering their ports, on the principle of *level playing field*. This is unique and frequently examined.

---

### 2.5 Convention on Facilitation of International Maritime Traffic (FAL), 1965

Adopted on **9 April 1965**, the FAL Convention aims to **prevent unnecessary delays in maritime traffic** by standardising documents, formalities, and procedures between ships and port states. It contains **Standards** (mandatory) and **Recommended Practices** (non-mandatory).

---

### 2.6 Oil Pollution Preparedness, Response and Co-operation (OPRC) Convention, 1990

Adopted **30 November 1990** in response to major tanker disasters like the *Exxon Valdez* (1989). It obliges parties to establish **national systems for oil pollution emergency preparedness and response**. The **OPRC-HNS Protocol 2000** extended the scope to **Hazardous and Noxious Substances**.

---

### 2.7 International Convention on the Control of Harmful Anti-Fouling Systems on Ships (AFS), 2001

Adopted **5 October 2001**, entered into force **17 September 2008**. The convention **prohibits the use of harmful organotin compounds (TBT — tributyltin)** in anti-fouling paints used on ship hulls and establishes a mechanism to prevent the transfer of harmful anti-fouling systems to non-party states.

---

### 2.8 International Convention for the Control and Management of Ships' Ballast Water and Sediments (BWM), 2004

Adopted **13 February 2004**, entered into force **8 September 2017**. The BWM Convention addresses the **transfer of invasive aquatic species** through ships' ballast water, which is estimated to be responsible for **~60% of marine bio-invasions globally**.

> [!NOTE]
> **BWM Convention — The D-1 / D-2 Standard:**
> - **Regulation D-1 — Ballast Water Exchange Standard:** Ships must perform ballast water exchange with **>95% volumetric exchange**, achieving **>95% efficiency**.
> - **Regulation D-2 — Ballast Water Performance Standard:** Limits the concentration of viable organisms and specified indicator microbes discharged.

---

### 2.9 Hong Kong International Convention for the Safe and Environmentally Sound Recycling of Ships, 2009

Adopted **15 May 2009** in Hong Kong. It addresses the **dangers of ship-breaking**, particularly in South Asia (India, Bangladesh, Pakistan). It regulates the design, construction, operation, and preparation of ships to facilitate safe and environmentally sound recycling, without compromising safety and operational efficiency.

> [!WARNING]
> **Status Note:** Although adopted in 2009, the Hong Kong Convention entered into force only on **26 June 2025** after the required threshold of ratifications (15 states, ≥40% of world gross tonnage, ≥3% of world tonnage recycled) was met. This is a common KTU question.

---

### 2.10 Nairobi International Convention on the Removal of Wrecks, 2007

Adopted **18 May 2007** in Nairobi, entered into force **14 April 2015**. The Convention provides a **legal basis for the removal of wrecks beyond the territorial sea** that may pose a hazard to navigation or to the marine environment, and imposes **strict liability on the registered shipowner** for the costs of locating, marking, and removing the wreck.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula Note — Nairobi Convention Liability Limits (Article 6):**
> Liability shall not exceed — calculated using the **LLMC 1996 limits formula**:
>
> $$\text{Liability Limit} = \left( 2{,}000{,}000 \right) \times \text{SDR} + \left( 300 \right) \times \text{SDR} \times \left( \text{GT} - \text{Threshold GT} \right)$$
>
> where **SDR** is the Special Drawing Right, **GT** is the gross tonnage of the ship, and the threshold GT differs based on the type of damage (property, personal injury, etc.).

### 2.11 KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix — "At a Glance"

| Convention | Adopted | EIF | Primary Focus | Key Document Issued |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Load Lines (LL) | 1966 | 21 Jul 1968 | Hull design, freeboard | International Load Line Certificate |
| COLREGs | 1972 | 15 Jul 1977 | Navigation rules | (No certificate) |
| STCW | 1978 | 28 Apr 1984 | Seafarer competence | CoC (Certificate of Competency) |
| FAL | 1965 | 5 Mar 1967 | Port formalities | Standardised documents |
| OPRC | 1990 | 13 May 1995 | Oil spill response | National contingency plans |
| AFS | 2001 | 17 Sep 2008 | Anti-fouling paints | IAFS Certificate |
| BWM | 2004 | 8 Sep 2017 | Invasive species | BWM Certificate |
| MLC | 2006 | 20 Aug 2013 | Seafarer welfare | MLC Certificate + DMLC |
| Hong Kong | 2009 | 26 Jun 2025 | Ship recycling | IPRC / SRP Certificates |
| Nairobi | 2007 | 14 Apr 2015 | Wreck removal | Insurance Certificate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "LLC S FAM BAN"** to recall all conventions in Module 2:
> **L**oad Lines, **L**abour (MLC), **C**OLREGs, **S**TCW, **F**AL, **A**FS, **M**ARPOL (cross-ref), **B**WM, **A**FS, **N**airobi.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Decision Matrices

### 3.1 Walkthrough — How Load Line Is Computed (Freeboard Assignment)

The **LL Convention Annex I, Chapter III** prescribes a tabular method. Below is the analytical walk-through that KTU examiners expect for **14-mark derivative questions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Underlying Principle:** The **Minimum Freeboard (F_min)** of a ship is a function of the ship's **Length (L)**, **Type**, **Block Coefficient (C_b)**, **Sheer**, **Bow Height**, and the **Zone & Season** in which the ship operates.

**Step 1 — Tabular Freeboard (F_T)**
The first step is to read the **tabular freeboard** for a Type B ship from Table 2.2.2 of Annex I.

$$F_T = a \times \left( \frac{L}{100} \right)^b$$

where **L** is the **Rule Length** in metres, and **a, b** are constants from the relevant table.

**Step 2 — Correction for Block Coefficient (C_b)**

The tabular freeboard must be **increased** if the block coefficient exceeds the standard 0.68 (for ship length under 100 m) and **decreased** if it is lower.

$$\Delta F_{C_b} = \left( \frac{C_b + 0.68}{1.36} \right) \times k$$

where **k** is a length-dependent correction factor from the regulation.

**Step 3 — Correction for Depth (D)**

The tabular freeboard is **multiplied by** a depth correction factor when the standard depth-to-length ratio of 0.02 (L ≤ 100 m) is not met.

$$F_c = F_T \times \frac{(L + 50)}{1.36 L} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad F_c = F_T \times \left( 1 + \frac{50 - L}{1.36 L} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Final Minimum Freeboard**

After all corrections — for **sheer, bow height, superstructure**, and **zone/season deduction** — the final freeboard is:

$$F_{final} = F_T + \Delta F_{C_b} + \Delta F_{D} + \Delta F_{sheer} + \Delta F_{bow} - F_{reduction}$$

The **Plimsoll mark** corresponding to this $F_{final}$ is then permanently marked on the hull.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zone and Season Reduction (Annex II):**
> - **Summer (S):** No reduction.
> - **Winter (W):** Freeboard *increased* by $\frac{1}{48}$ of $F_{tabular}$.
> - **Tropical (T):** Freeboard *decreased* by $\frac{1}{48}$ of $F_{tabular}$.
> - **Fresh Water (F):** Freeboard *decreased* by $\frac{\Delta}{40T}$ inches.
> - **Tropical Fresh (TF):** Combination of tropical and fresh-water deductions.

---

### 3.2 Walkthrough — Applying the COLREGs "Priority" Decision Matrix

For 14-mark examination questions asking *"Which vessel has right of way?"*, examiners test **Rule 18 — Responsibility Between Vessels**. The following decision matrix is the canonical approach.

**Step 1 — Identify the Visibility Condition**

- *Restricted Visibility (Fog)?* → Apply **Rule 19** only.
- *Clear Visibility?* → Proceed to Step 2.

**Step 2 — Classify the Vessels in Order of Restriction**

- **Hierarchy of "Less Maneuverable" Vessels (Rule 18):**
  1. **Vessel not under command (NUC)** — Rule 3(g).
  2. **Vessel restricted in her ability to maneuver (RAM)** — Rule 3(i).
  3. **Vessel constrained by her draught** — Rule 3(j).
  4. **Vessel engaged in fishing (when not making way)** — Rule 3(c).
  5. **Sailing vessel** — Rule 3(c).
  6. **Power-driven vessel** — Rule 3(b).
  7. **Seaplane on water** — Rule 3(f).

**Step 3 — Apply the Hierarchy**

A vessel lower in the hierarchy must **give way** to a vessel higher in the hierarchy. If both vessels are in the **same category** (e.g., two power-driven vessels crossing), the **crossing rule (Rule 15)** applies: the vessel that has the other on her **starboard side** is the *give-way* vessel.

**Step 4 — Determine the Action of the Give-way Vessel**

- Pass **astern** of the stand-on vessel.
- Cross safely, with early and substantial alteration of course.
- If **in doubt**, sound **at least 5 short and rapid blasts** on the whistle (Rule 34(d)).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 18(c) — Sailing vessels & power-driven vessels:**
> *"A power-driven vessel which is proceeding in a narrow channel or fairway shall keep as near to the outer limit of the channel or fairway which lies on her starboard side as is safe and practicable."* This is a high-yield exam point.

---

### 3.3 Walkthrough — BWM Convention Compliance Decision Tree

For questions on BWM (a high-frequency KTU topic), the decision tree is:

**Step 1 — Does the ship carry ballast water?**
- **Yes** → BWM applies; obtain BWM Certificate.
- **No** → Exempt; obtain BWM Exemption Certificate.

**Step 2 — Capacity > 5000 m³ or Not?**

- **Capacity > 5000 m³** → Must install a **BWMS (Ballast Water Management System)**.
- **Capacity < 5000 m³** → May follow the **D-1 standard** (ballast water exchange) until BWMS is fitted.

**Step 3 — D-1 vs D-2**

- **D-1 (Exchange Standard):** $>95\%$ volumetric exchange; efficiency $>95\%$.
- **D-2 (Performance Standard):** Organisms $\leq 10$ per m³ (size $\geq 50 \mu m$); indicator microbes meeting IMO guidelines.

**Step 4 — Compliance Verification**

- **BWM Plan** must be on board.
- **BWRB (Ballast Water Record Book)** must record every operation.
- **Survey and certification** every 5 years (Renewal) and 2-3 years (Intermediate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:**
> KTU frequently asks: *"Compare D-1 and D-2 standards."* Always mention: **D-1 = Process Standard; D-2 = Outcome Standard.** D-1 is a *means*; D-2 is an *end*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following block-level flow diagram maps the interaction and classification of all the conventions studied in Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*State any three objectives of the International Convention on Load Lines, 1966.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. To establish uniform principles and rules relating to **limitation of draught** and **assignment of freeboard** for ships engaged on international voyages, ensuring **adequate reserve buoyancy** (1 mark).
2. To provide a **uniform international system** of load line marking (the Plimsoll mark) recognised by all contracting states, eliminating conflicting national rules (1 mark).
3. To enhance the **safety of life and property at sea** by preventing overloading, which historically was a leading cause of ship loss (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is the Plimsoll Mark? Name any four load lines marked on it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Plimsoll Mark** is a **permanently marked symbol** on the side of a ship, comprising a circular disc with a horizontal line passing through its centre. It indicates the **maximum permissible draught** to which a ship may be loaded under various conditions (1 mark).
The four load lines are:
- **TF** — Tropical Fresh Water (½ mark)
- **F** — Fresh Water (½ mark)
- **S** — Summer (½ mark)
- **W** — Winter (½ mark)
*(Any four of TF, F, T, S, W, WNA accepted.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) with Internal Choice

---

#### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
*(a)* Discuss the **STCW Convention 1978** in detail. Highlight the **major changes introduced by the Manila Amendments 2010**. (7 marks)
*(b)* Explain the **five Titles of the Maritime Labour Convention (MLC), 2006** and bring out its significance as the *"Fourth Pillar"*. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) STCW 1978 — Detailed Discussion**

The **International Convention on Standards of Training, Certification and Watchkeeping for Seafarers (STCW), 1978** was the first international convention to establish **minimum qualification standards** for masters, officers, and watchkeeping personnel on seagoing merchant ships (1 mark).

**Adoption and Entry into Force:**
- Adopted: **7 July 1978** at the IMO Conference in London.
- Entered into force: **28 April 1984** (1 mark).

**Salient Features:**
- **Chapters I–VII** (and Chapter VIII post-Manila) lay down training requirements, watchkeeping principles, and certification standards.
- It applies to **seafarers serving on board seagoing ships** of contracting states, irrespective of flag.
- It mandates the issuance of a **Certificate of Competency (CoC)** to officers and a **Certificate of Proficiency (CoP)** to ratings (1 mark).

**Major Changes by the Manila Amendments 2010** (entered into force 1 January 2012) (1 mark):

1. **New Chapter VIII — Fitness for Duty:** Introduced limits on **alcohol consumption (max 0.05% BAC)** and minimum **rest hours** — minimum **10 hours of rest in any 24-hour period** and **77 hours in any 7-day period** (1 mark).
2. **Special Requirements for Tankers, Passenger Ships, and High-Speed Craft:** Enhanced training for personnel on these high-risk vessels.
3. **Security-Related Training:** Mandatory for all seafarers following the **ISPS Code** post-9/11.
4. **Updated Training Requirements:** New competencies for **ECDIS (Electronic Chart Display and Information System)** — making paper charts optional if ECDIS is used (1 mark).
5. **Refresher and Re-validation Training:** Every 5 years for certificates of proficiency in survival craft, fire-fighting, and medical first aid (1 mark).
6. **New definitions** and **modernised certification pathways** to address emerging technologies.

**(b) MLC 2006 — Five Titles and Significance**

The **Maritime Labour Convention, 2006** was adopted under the auspices of the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** and entered into force on **20 August 2013** (1 mark).

**The Five Titles:**

| Title | Subject | Key Provision |
|---|---|---|
| **Title 1** | Minimum Requirements | Minimum age 16, medical fitness certificate |
| **Title 2** | Conditions of Employment | Repatriation rights, manning levels, wages, leave |
| **Title 3** | Accommodation & Catering | Minimum space, ventilation, food quality |
| **Title 4** | Health, Welfare, Social Security | Medical care, occupational safety, social security |
| **Title 5** | Compliance & Enforcement | **MLC Certificate**, **DMLC** (Declaration of Maritime Labour Compliance) (1 mark each = 5 marks for tabulated content) |

**Significance as the "Fourth Pillar"** (2 marks):
- It consolidates **over 68 earlier ILO maritime conventions** into a single, comprehensive instrument.
- It is the **first convention to use the "no more favourable treatment" clause** — port states can inspect non-ratifying states' ships to ensure seafarers' rights, addressing the **"level playing field"** concern.
- Combined with **SOLAS, MARPOL, and STCW**, MLC completes the **four-pillar framework** of modern maritime regulation.
- The **MLC Certificate and DMLC** are now mandatory for most commercial ships worldwide, making it a de facto universal standard.

---

#### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*(a)* Explain the **BWM Convention 2004** in detail, with emphasis on the **D-1 and D-2 standards**. (7 marks)
*(b)* Write a detailed note on the **Nairobi International Convention on the Removal of Wrecks, 2007**, covering its **objectives, scope, and liability provisions**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BWM Convention 2004 — Detailed Analysis**

The **International Convention for the Control and Management of Ships' Ballast Water and Sediments (BWM), 2004** was adopted on **13 February 2004** and entered into force on **8 September 2017** (1 mark).

**Why BWM was needed:** Ships take in ballast water for stability, but the **discharge of this water in foreign ports** transfers **invasive aquatic species**, pathogens, and sediments that disrupt local marine ecosystems. It is estimated to be a **~60 billion USD problem** annually (1 mark).

**Salient Provisions:**
- **Article 2 — General Obligations:** Parties must prevent, minimise, and ultimately eliminate the transfer of harmful aquatic organisms and pathogens via ballast water.
- **Regulation A-2:** General provision applying to all ships.
- **Regulation B-1:** Ships must have a **BWM Plan** onboard.
- **Regulation B-2:** Must maintain a **Ballast Water Record Book (BWRB)** (1 mark).

**D-1 — Ballast Water Exchange Standard:**
- Ships must perform ballast water exchange **>200 nautical miles from the nearest land** in water at least **200 m deep**, OR
- In areas designated by the port state, achieving **>95% volumetric exchange** with **>95% efficiency** (1 mark).
- This is a **process standard** — it specifies *how* the operation is to be done.

**D-2 — Ballast Water Performance Standard:**
- Limits on the **concentration of viable organisms** discharged:
  - Organisms $\geq$ 50 $\mu m$ in size: $\leq$ **10 per cubic metre** (1 mark).
  - Organisms $<$ 50 $\mu m$ and $\geq$ 10 $\mu m$: $\leq$ **10 per mL** (1 mark).
- Indicator microbes (e.g., *Vibrio cholerae*, *E. coli*, intestinal enterococci) must meet specific thresholds.
- This is an **outcome standard** — it specifies *what* must be achieved.

**Implementation Timeline:** All ships must comply with the **D-2 standard** by the **next IOPP renewal survey** after the convention's entry into force (1 mark).

**(b) Nairobi Wreck Removal Convention 2007**

**Background:** Many shipwrecks (e.g., the *MV Prestige* off Spain) remained because no state had clear legal authority or obligation to remove them. The Nairobi Convention fills this legal gap (1 mark).

**Adoption & Entry into Force:** Adopted **18 May 2007** in Nairobi, Kenya. Entered into force **14 April 2015** (1 mark).

**Objectives:**
1. To ensure the **prompt and effective removal of wrecks** beyond the territorial sea that pose hazards to navigation or the marine environment.
2. To provide a **legal basis for liability and compensation** for wreck removal costs.
3. To **standardise wreck removal rules internationally** (1 mark).

**Scope:**
- Applies in the **Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ) or equivalent** of a state party.
- Excludes measures taken under **SOLAS** or **UNCLOS** that the master is already obliged to undertake (1 mark).

**Liability Provisions (Article 6 & 7):**
- The **registered shipowner** is **strictly liable** for the costs of locating, marking, and removing a wreck.
- The owner must maintain **compulsory insurance or financial security** (1 mark).
- Liability is **limited** per the **LLMC 1996 limits** based on gross tonnage (1 mark).
- The **Certificate of Insurance** must be carried on board; a **wreck may be reported** by the affected coastal state to the flag state.

**Significance:** Closes a **major legal gap** — pre-Nairobi, wrecks beyond territorial seas were often *legally orphaned*, posing a real danger to navigation, fishing, and offshore installations (1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Year confusion:** Students often write the **year of entry into force (EIF)** instead of the **year of adoption** in questions like *"When was the BWM Convention adopted?"* — read the verb carefully!
> 2. **D-1 vs D-2:** Most students write only one of the standards in BWM questions. Both must appear with at least three numerical thresholds.
> 3. **Plimsoll mark lines:** Examiners ask *all six* lines; students remember only S and W. Use the **mnemonic "TF F T S W WNA"**.
> 4. **MLC authorship:** MLC is an **ILO** convention, not an IMO convention. This is a single-mark question but frequently lost.
> 5. **Nairobi Convention:** Many students write "Athens" or "Salvage" instead of *Nairobi*. Read carefully.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Module 2 covers the "Specialised" Maritime Conventions** beyond SOLAS and MARPOL, each addressing a specific problem.
- **Load Lines (1966)** — governs the **Plimsoll mark** and freeboard; EIF **21 July 1968**; amended by 1988 Protocol.
- **COLREGs (1972)** — *"Rules of the Road at Sea"* with **38 rules** in **5 parts**; EIF **15 July 1977**.
- **STCW (1978)** — Seafarer competence; Manila Amendments 2010 added Chapter VIII (rest hours, fitness for duty); EIF **28 April 1984**.
- **FAL (1965)** — Standardisation of port formalities; EIF **5 March 1967**.
- **OPRC (1990)** — Oil spill response; EIF **13 May 1995**.
- **AFS (2001)** — Bans harmful anti-fouling paints (TBT); EIF **17 September 2008**.
- **BWM (2004)** — Ballast water management; D-1 (process) + D-2 (outcome); EIF **8 September 2017**.
- **MLC (2006)** — ILO's "Seafarers' Bill of Rights"; 5 Titles; EIF **20 August 2013**; uses "no more favourable treatment" clause.
- **Hong Kong (2009)** — Safe ship recycling; EIF **26 June 2025**.
- **Nairobi (2007)** — Wreck removal beyond territorial sea; strict liability; EIF **14 April 2015**.
- **Mnemonic "LLC S FAM BAN"** for quick recall.
- For each convention, remember **3 things: Year of Adoption, EIF, and the Key Document Issued.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Convention on Salvage, Convention on Limitation of Liability for Maritime Claims
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** International Convention on Salvage, Convention on Limitation of Liability for Maritime Claims

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The International Convention on Salvage, 1989

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **International Convention on Salvage, 1989** (adopted in London on 28 April 1989, entered into force 14 July 1996) is a comprehensive international treaty administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** that modernized the traditional law of salvage, replacing the earlier **1910 Brussels Convention for the Unification of Certain Rules of Law respecting Assistance and Salvage at Sea**. It governs the obligations of the salvor, the duties of the salved party, the criteria for fixing salvage reward, and introduces the revolutionary concept of **"Special Compensation"** under Article 14 to protect salvors from environmental damage liability.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine a stranger risks his life to pull your **drowning child** from a fast-flowing river. Common decency and law say he deserves a **reward** — but *how much*? The International Convention on Salvage is essentially the **global rulebook** that decides:
- Who is a **rescuer of ships/cargo** (the "salvor")?
- How much **money** does the rescuer deserve?
- What if the rescue was of an **oil tanker** threatening an environmental disaster but the property itself was barely worth saving?

**Real-World Analogy:** Think of it like a **"Good Samaritan Maritime Law"** — globally standardized. If a tugboat pulls a sinking container ship off a reef, the law ensures the tugboat owner is fairly rewarded *and* protected from absurd claims. The 1989 Convention was triggered by the **Amoco Cadiz disaster (1978)** where salvors refused to help tankers for fear of huge pollution liability — the law now *bribes* salvors with **Special Compensation** to save the environment.

---

## 1.2 The Convention on Limitation of Liability for Maritime Claims, 1976 (LLMC 1976)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The **Convention on Limitation of Liability for Maritime Claims (LLMC), 1976** (adopted in London on 19 November 1976, entered into force 1 December 1986) is the principal international treaty that allows shipowners and other maritime operators to **limit their financial liability** for certain maritime claims arising from shipping operations, by establishing maximum monetary limits calculated on a **tonnage-based formula** tied to the ship's gross tonnage (GT).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Imagine a taxi driver accidentally causes an accident. Without a liability cap, a single accident could **financially destroy** him, his family, and the entire taxi company — making the taxi business impossible to run. The LLMC is like **insurance law for shipowners**:
- It says: *"Yes, you made a mistake, but you don't have to pay MORE than [calculated limit] per ton of your ship."*
- This prevents the **annihilation** of the global shipping industry by a single catastrophic claim.
- It balances the interests of **shipowners** (to stay in business) and **claimants** (to get fair compensation).

**Real-World Example:** The **Erika (1999)** and **Prestige (2002)** tanker disasters led to a **1996 Protocol** amending the LLMC, significantly **raising the limits** because the original 1976 limits had become outdated and woefully inadequate for modern supertankers.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Know KTU Glossary Terms (2024 Scheme):**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Salvage** | The act of rescuing a vessel, cargo, or freight from maritime peril |
| **Salvage Operation** | Any act or activity undertaken to assist a vessel or property in danger |
| **Salvage Reward** | Monetary compensation payable to the salvor for successful rescue |
| **Special Compensation** | Additional payment under **Article 14** when salvor prevents/minimises environmental damage |
| **LLMC** | Limitation of Liability for Maritime Claims Convention, 1976 |
| **Limitation Fund** | A monetary fund established by the shipowner to limit liability for claims |
| **Tonnage** | The gross tonnage (GT) of a ship used in LLMC limit calculation |
| **Convention Limit** | The maximum amount a shipowner must pay under the Convention |
| **Sister Ship** | Another ship owned by the same person as the ship causing the claim |
| **In Rem** | A legal action against the ship itself (Latin: "against the thing") |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The International Convention on Salvage, 1989 — Detailed Articles

### 2.1.1 Scope and Applicability

> The Convention applies to **judicial or arbitral proceedings** brought after its entry into force relating to salvage operations, regardless of where the salvage operation took place. It applies to **vessels** and **property** of any kind, including **State-owned vessels** (with certain exceptions for warships and non-commercial State vessels — *Article 4*).

### 2.1.2 The Salvor's Duties (Article 8)

The salvor has the following enumerated duties:

- **(a)** To carry out the salvage operations with **due care**;
- **(b)** To perform the operations with **reasonable speed** (no unnecessary delay);
- **(c)** To exercise due care to **prevent or minimise damage to the environment**;
- **(d)** To seek the assistance of other salvors when circumstances reasonably require;
- **(e)** To accept intervention of other salvors when reasonably requested by the owner/master.

### 2.1.3 Duties of the Owner and Master (Article 9)

- The salved vessel's **owner, master, and the owner of property on board** must **co-operate fully** with the salvor during the operations.
- The master must **accept delivery** of the salved property once it is brought to a safe place.

### 2.1.4 Criteria for Fixing the Salvage Reward (Article 13) ⭐

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Article 13 — The Salvage Reward Formula:**
> The salvage reward shall be fixed with a view to **encouraging salvage operations**, taking into account the following criteria **without regard to the order in which they are presented below**:

The Six (6) Criteria are:

1. **The salved value of the vessel and other property** (the bigger the saved asset, the bigger the reward);
2. **The skill and efforts of the salvors** in preventing or minimising damage to the environment;
3. **The measure of success obtained** by the salvor;
4. **The nature and degree of the danger** faced;
5. **The skill and efforts** of the salvors in salving the vessel, other property, and the lives of persons;
6. **The time used and expenses** incurred by the salvors, and the risk of liability and other risks run by them.

### 2.1.5 The "No Cure — No Pay" Principle (Article 12) ⭐

> **Article 12 (1):** *"Salvage operations which have had a useful result give right to a reward."*
> **Article 12 (2):** *"Except as otherwise provided, no payment is due under this Convention if the salvage operations have had no useful result."*

This is the cornerstone principle — the salvor gets paid **only if the salvage is successful** (has "useful result").

### 2.1.6 Special Compensation (Article 14) — The Environmental Innovation ⭐⭐

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most important and most-tested Article in KTU examinations.**

When a salvor has **carried out salvage operations in respect of a vessel which by itself or its cargo threatened damage to the environment**, and has **failed to earn a reward** (or the reward is **less than the special compensation** assessable), the salvor shall be entitled to **Special Compensation** from the owner of that vessel.

**Formula for Special Compensation (Article 14.2):**

$$\text{SC} = \text{Salvor's Expenses} + \text{Increment (up to 100\% of Expenses)}$$

Where:
- **Salvor's Expenses** = Actual out-of-pocket expenditure + fair rate for equipment and personnel;
- **Increment** = An additional amount up to **100%** of the above expenses, reflecting the skill and efforts of the salvor in preventing/minimising environmental damage.

> **Article 14.3:** If the salvor has, by his fault, caused or contributed to the pollution damage, the Special Compensation may be **reduced or even denied** entirely.

### 2.1.7 Apportionment Between Salvors (Article 15)

Where salvage is carried out by **multiple salvors** working together, the reward is **apportioned between them** based on their individual contribution.

### 2.1.8 Salvage of Human Life (Article 16)

> *"No payment is due from persons whose lives are saved, but salvors of human life are entitled to a fair share of the payment awarded to the salvor for salving the vessel or other property."*

This means **human life salvage** is rewarded only indirectly — life-savers get a slice of the property-salvage pie.

### 2.1.9 The "SCOPIC" Clause (Industry-Standard Addition)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Bonus Concept:**
> The **Special Compensation P&I Club Clause (SCOPIC)** is an industry-developed (Lloyd's) optional contractual supplement to Article 14, which guarantees salvors a **minimum payment** even if no useful result is obtained, in return for waiving certain Article 14 protections. The **SCOPIC Clause** is **invoked by the salvor** and is binding once invoked.

---

## 2.2 The Convention on Limitation of Liability for Maritime Claims, 1976 (LLMC) — Detailed Provisions

### 2.2.1 Scope of Application (Article 2)

The Convention applies to the following categories of claims, however they may be brought:

1. **Claims in respect of loss of life or personal injury**;
2. **Claims in respect of damage to property** occurring on board or in direct connection with the operation of the ship;
3. **Claims in respect of consequential loss** resulting from (1) and (2);
4. **Claims in respect of wreck removal**;
5. **Claims in respect of damage to harbour works, basins, waterways, and aids to navigation**;
6. **Claims in respect of damage in connection with the carriage of cargo, passengers, or their baggage**;
7. **Claims by public authorities** for the costs of measures taken to avert or minimise pollution damage.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Convention does NOT apply to:**
> - **Oil pollution damage** under the **CLC 1992**;
> - **Nuclear damage**;
> - **Public authority claims under nuclear liability conventions**;
> - Claims against the **Hull insurer** of a nuclear ship;
> - Claims by **servants of the shipowner** whose duties are connected with the ship (crew wages, etc.).

### 2.2.2 The Two-Tier Limitation System (Article 6) ⭐⭐

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Article 6 — The Heart of the LLMC Convention:**

The limits of liability are calculated as follows:

#### **For Claims for Loss of Life or Personal Injury (Article 6.1(a)):**

The limit shall be calculated as follows:

| Ship's Gross Tonnage (GT) | Limit Calculation (SDR) |
|---|---|
| For ships **up to 2,000 GT** | **2,000,000 SDR** |
| For ships **between 2,001 and 30,000 GT** | A **fixed amount of 2,000,000 SDR** + an additional amount per ton above 2,000 GT |
| For ships **between 30,001 and 70,000 GT** | A further step-up |
| For ships **above 70,000 GT** | Capped at the maximum for 70,000 GT |

#### **For Other (Property) Claims (Article 6.1(b)):**

The limit shall be **half** the amount calculated for personal injury claims for the relevant tonnage.

### 2.2.3 Limits of Liability Under the 1996 Protocol (As Amended) ⭐⭐

> [!NOTE]
> **The 1996 Protocol to the LLMC substantially raised the limits. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the amended figures:**

| Category of Ship (Tonnage) | Personal Injury (Loss of Life) | Property & Other Claims |
|---|---|---|
| **Up to 2,000 GT** | **2,000,000 SDR** | **1,000,000 SDR** |
| **2,001 – 30,000 GT** | **2,000,000 SDR + 800 SDR per ton above 2,000** | **1,000,000 SDR + 400 SDR per ton above 2,000** |
| **30,001 – 70,000 GT** | (Calculated) | (Calculated) |
| **Above 70,000 GT** | (Capped at maximum) | (Capped at maximum) |

### 2.2.4 The Unit of Account — SDR (Special Drawing Rights)

The limits are denominated in **SDR (Special Drawing Rights)** — an international reserve asset created by the **International Monetary Fund (IMF)**. The SDR value is calculated daily by the IMF against a basket of major currencies (US Dollar, Euro, Chinese Yuan, Japanese Yen, British Pound).

> **1 SDR (approximate 2024 value) ≈ 1.33 USD ≈ 1.23 EUR**

### 2.2.5 Conduct Barring Limitation (Article 4) ⭐

> **The Right to Limit — Loss (Breach) Rule:**

A person liable shall **not be entitled to limit** liability if it is proved that the loss resulted from his **personal act or omission**, committed with the intent to cause such loss, or **recklessly and with knowledge** that such loss would probably result (**"wilful misconduct"** test).

This is a high threshold — simple negligence does NOT defeat the right to limit.

### 2.2.6 Constitution of the Limitation Fund (Article 11)

The shipowner may constitute a **Limitation Fund** by:
- Depositing the limit amount with the **court** of the State where the action is brought; OR
- By **furnishing a guarantee** acceptable to the court.

Once the fund is constituted:
- **No other assets** of the shipowner can be arrested/attached for the same claims;
- The fund is distributed **pari passu** (equally, rateably) among all claimants;
- Each claimant's share cannot exceed the amount they would have received from the fund.

### 2.2.7 The "Sister Ship" Provision (Article 3.1.b) ⭐

> A claim may be made against a **sister ship** (another vessel owned by the same person at the time of the incident) for purposes of limitation. However, claims **for ownership of a ship** itself and **for salvage remuneration** cannot be brought against a sister ship.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Quick-Reference Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The ONLY Numerical Formula You Need:**

### **The LLMC Limitation Calculation**

$$\boxed{\text{Limit} = \text{Base SDR} + (\text{Variable SDR per ton}) \times (\text{GT} - \text{Threshold Tonnage})}$$

#### **For Loss of Life / Personal Injury (1996 Protocol):**

| Tonnage Range | Formula |
|---|---|
| **GT ≤ 2,000** | $\text{Limit} = 2{,}000{,}000 \text{ SDR}$ |
| **2,000 < GT ≤ 30,000** | $\text{Limit} = 2{,}000{,}000 + 800 \times (GT - 2{,}000)$ |
| **30,000 < GT ≤ 70,000** | $\text{Limit} = [\text{above}] + 600 \times (GT - 30{,}000)$ |
| **GT > 70,000** | $\text{Limit (capped)} = 3{,}000{,}000 \times (\text{factor for 70,000 GT})$ |

#### **For Property / Other Claims (1996 Protocol):**

| Tonnage Range | Formula |
|---|---|
| **GT ≤ 2,000** | $\text{Limit} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ SDR}$ |
| **2,000 < GT ≤ 30,000** | $\text{Limit} = 1{,}000{,}000 + 400 \times (GT - 2{,}000)$ |
| **30,000 < GT ≤ 70,000** | $\text{Limit} = [\text{above}] + 300 \times (GT - 30{,}000)$ |
| **GT > 70,000** | Capped at maximum |

### **Salvage Special Compensation Formula**

$$\text{SC} = \text{Expenses} + \text{Increment}, \quad \text{where } 0 \le \text{Increment} \le 100\% \text{ of Expenses}$$

### **Master Quick-Reference Table — Convention Comparison**

| Feature | Salvage 1989 | LLMC 1976 |
|---|---|---|
| **Administered by** | IMO | IMO |
| **Year adopted** | 1989 (in force 1996) | 1976 (in force 1986) |
| **Amended by** | — (no protocol yet) | 1996 Protocol |
| **Core principle** | "No Cure — No Pay" + Special Compensation | Right to limit liability |
| **Unit of account** | SDR | SDR |
| **Major innovation** | Article 14 (Special Compensation) | Tonnage-based liability caps |
| **Loss of right** | Wilful misconduct of salvor | Wilful misconduct of shipowner |
| **Most tested KTU topic** | Article 13 (Criteria) + Article 14 (SC) | Article 6 (Limits) + Article 4 (Bar to limit) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

| Convention | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| **Salvage 1989** | Governs the operations of global **salvage tug companies** (e.g., SMIT Salvage, Boskalis, Ardent), underpins **Lloyd's Open Form (LOF)** salvage contracts, and provides the framework for **SCOPIC Clause** activations. |
| **LLMC 1976** | Determines the **insurance premiums** (P&I cover) for shipowners; limits the **financial exposure** of the global shipping fleet; influences **flag-state registration** decisions. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Salvage Reward Calculation Logic

> **Scenario:** A bulk carrier *MV Vishakh* (25,000 DWT) ran aground off the Kerala coast. The salvor tug *Sagar Ratna* (Oceanic Salvage Pvt. Ltd.) refloated the vessel after 5 days, saving property valued at **USD 8 million** and preventing an oil spill of 200 tonnes (environmental cost avoidance: USD 5 million). The salvor's expenses were **USD 1.2 million**.

### Step 1: Apply the "No Cure — No Pay" Test (Article 12)

$$\text{Useful Result?} = \text{Yes (vessel refloated)} \implies \text{Proceed to Article 13}$$

The salvor has earned a useful result — therefore entitled to a reward.

### Step 2: List the Article 13 Criteria Values

| Criterion | Article Reference | Weightage Indication |
|---|---|---|
| Salved value of vessel/property | Article 13.1(a) | USD 8 million (high) |
| Skill/efforts in preventing environmental damage | Article 13.1(b) | USD 5 million (very high — oil spill averted) |
| Measure of success obtained | Article 13.1(c) | Total success (100%) |
| Nature and degree of danger | Article 13.1(d) | High (grounding, weather) |
| Skill and efforts of salvors | Article 13.1(e) | 5 days of work, expert team |
| Time, expenses, and risks | Article 13.1(f) | USD 1.2 million expenses |

### Step 3: Apply the Standard "Salvage Award" Industry Rule (in the absence of specific statutory formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Convention does NOT prescribe a rigid mathematical formula for the salvage reward. It only lists the criteria. However, the industry-standard approach (used by courts and LOF arbitrators) is:**

$$\text{Reward} = \text{Salved Value} \times \text{Fair Percentage}$$

For typical successful cases: **15% – 25%** of salved value is a common range.

### Step 4: Calculate the Indicative Reward

$$\text{Reward} = 8{,}000{,}000 \times 20\% = 1{,}600{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

### Step 5: Check the Special Compensation Trigger (Article 14)

Since the vessel threatened **environmental damage** (oil spill risk), the salvor is entitled to **Special Compensation** (if reward is less than expenses, or as an *additional* payment over and above reward, in some interpretations).

$$\text{SC} = \text{Expenses} + \text{Increment} = 1{,}200{,}000 + (0 \text{ to } 100\%) \times 1{,}200{,}000$$

$$\text{SC Range} = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ to } 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

### Step 6: Final Recovery by Salvor

$$\text{Total Recovery} = \text{Reward} + \text{SC} = 1{,}600{,}000 + 1{,}200{,}000 = \text{USD } 2{,}800{,}000$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: LLMC Limit Calculation

> **Scenario:** A container ship *MV Kochi Express* of **15,000 GT** collides with another vessel in the Arabian Sea, causing both loss of life (3 crew of the other vessel) and significant cargo damage. The shipowner seeks to limit liability under the LLMC Convention.

### Step 1: Identify the Applicable Limits (1996 Protocol)

The shipowner is claiming limitation for **both** loss-of-life and property damage.

### Step 2: Apply the Loss of Life / Personal Injury Formula

For **GT = 15,000**, the applicable range is **2,000 < GT ≤ 30,000**:

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} = 2{,}000{,}000 + 800 \times (15{,}000 - 2{,}000)$$

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} = 2{,}000{,}000 + 800 \times 13{,}000$$

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} = 2{,}000{,}000 + 10{,}400{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} = 12{,}400{,}000 \text{ SDR}}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Property / Other Claims Formula (Half the Life Limit)

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Property}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 12{,}400{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Limit}_{\text{Property}} = 6{,}200{,}000 \text{ SDR}}$$

### Step 4: Aggregate Limit (Where Claims Overlap)

If both personal injury and property claims arise from the same incident:

$$\text{Total Limit} = \text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} + \text{Limit}_{\text{Property}}$$

$$\text{Total Limit} = 12{,}400{,}000 + 6{,}200{,}000 = 18{,}600{,}000 \text{ SDR}$$

> **Note:** If the property claims consume the full property limit, the **personal injury limit is added on top** (a 50% overflow provision per Article 6.2).

### Step 5: Convert to Indian Rupees (For Practical Context)

Using 1 SDR ≈ ₹110 (approximate):

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} \text{ in INR} = 12{,}400{,}000 \times 110 = \text{₹ } 1{,}364{,}000{,}000$$

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Property}} \text{ in INR} = 6{,}200{,}000 \times 110 = \text{₹ } 682{,}000{,}000$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: "Bar to Limitation" Application (Article 4)

> **Scenario:** The master of *MV Vizhinjam Pride* deliberately scuttles (sinks) the ship to claim insurance money. Insurance claim is brought by cargo owners.

### Step 1: Identify the Issue

The cargo owners argue the shipowner should **not** be allowed to limit liability.

### Step 2: Apply the Article 4 Test

For the bar to limitation to apply, the loss must result from:
- **Personal act or omission** of the person liable (here, the shipowner/master), **AND**
- Committed with **intent to cause such loss**, OR **recklessly and with knowledge** that such loss would probably result.

### Step 3: Analysis

The deliberate scuttling (a wilful, intentional act) clearly meets the test. The shipowner is:

$$\boxed{\text{Barred from Limitation} \implies \text{Unlimited Liability}}$$

The shipowner is personally liable for the **full amount** of all valid claims, with no cap.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Simple negligence (e.g., navigational error, engine failure) does **NOT** defeat limitation. The test is **"wilful misconduct"** — a very high bar.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison & Contrast

| Analytical Dimension | Salvage Convention 1989 | LLMC 1976 |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | To reward & regulate rescue at sea | To cap financial liability of shipowners |
| **Beneficiary** | The **salvor** (rescuer) | The **shipowner** (defendant) |
| **Trigger** | Maritime peril + successful rescue | Maritime claim arising from ship operation |
| **Core principle** | "No Cure — No Pay" + Special Compensation | Right to limit liability by tonnage |
| **Calculation method** | Discretionary (court/arbitrator decides) | Strict mathematical formula |
| **Loss of right conditions** | Salvor's wilful misconduct, dishonest conduct | Shipowner's wilful misconduct, recklessness |
| **Unit of account** | SDR | SDR |
| **Fund mechanism** | No compulsory fund | Limitation fund (Article 11) |
| **Sister ship applicability** | Not directly applicable | Yes (Article 3.1.b) |
| **Related conventions** | CLC 1992, Fund Convention | CLC 1992, Athens Convention, Hague-Visby Rules |

---

## 3.5 Comparative Legal Pedigree

| Convention | Predecessor Convention | Year Adopted | Forum |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Salvage 1989** | Brussels Convention 1910 | 1910 | Brussels |
| **LLMC 1976** | Brussels Convention 1957 (relating to Limitation of Liability) | 1957 | Brussels |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Historical Insight:**
> The Salvage 1989 Convention was the IMO's first major rewrite of the 1910 Brussels Convention in **79 years**, prompted primarily by the **Torrey Canyon (1967)** and **Amoco Cadiz (1978)** oil pollution disasters. The LLMC 1976 was a similar modernisation of the 1957 Convention.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Salvage Convention 1989 — Logical Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Maritime Peril Occurs] --> B{Salvage Operation Initiated}
    B --> C[Salvor Performs Duties<br/>Article 8]
    C --> D{Salved Party Co-operates?<br/>Article 9}
    D -- Yes --> E{Successful Result?<br/>Article 12 No Cure No Pay}
    D -- No --> E
    E -- Yes --> F[Determine Salvage Reward<br/>Article 13 Six Criteria]
    E -- No --> G{Environmental Threat?<br/>Article 14 Trigger}
    F --> H{Vessel/Cargo Threatened<br/>Environmental Damage?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Special Compensation Analysis<br/>Article 14.2]
    H -- No --> J[Final Reward Apportionment<br/>Article 15]
    I --> K[Total = Reward + Special Compensation]
    G -- Yes --> L[Special Compensation Only<br/>Expenses + 0-100% Increment]
    G -- No --> M[No Payment Due]
    J --> N[Salvor Receives Reward]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> O[Operation Concluded]
    N --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The salvor only gets paid if there is a "useful result." If environmental damage was threatened, even an unsuccessful salvage triggers Special Compensation (limited to expenses + increment).

---

## 4.2 The LLMC Convention 1976 — Liability Limitation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Maritime Claim Arises<br/>e.g., Collision / Wreck] --> B{Is Claim Within<br/>Convention Scope?<br/>Article 2}
    B -- No --> X[Unlimited Liability Applies]
    B -- Yes --> C{Is Shipowner<br/>Seeking Limitation?}
    C -- No --> X
    C -- Yes --> D{Category of Claim?}
    D -- P1[Loss of Life /<br/>Personal Injury] --> E[Apply Life Limit Formula<br/>Article 6.1.a]
    D -- P2[Property / Other<br/>Claims] --> F[Apply Property Limit Formula<br/>Article 6.1.b]
    E --> G{Wilful Misconduct?<br/>Article 4 Test}
    F --> G
    G -- Yes --> X
    G -- No --> H[Constitute Limitation Fund<br/>Article 11]
    H --> I[Distribute Fund Pari Passu<br/>Among Claimants]
    I --> J[Shipowner Liability Capped<br/>at the Calculated Limit]
```

---

## 4.3 The Salvage Reward — Criteria Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Article 13<br/>Salvage Reward Fixing] --> B[Criterion 1<br/>Salved Value of Property]
    A --> C[Criterion 2<br/>Skill in Preventing<br/>Environmental Damage]
    A --> D[Criterion 3<br/>Measure of Success Obtained]
    A --> E[Criterion 4<br/>Nature and Degree of Danger]
    A --> F[Criterion 5<br/>Skill and Efforts of Salvors]
    A --> G[Criterion 6<br/>Time, Expenses, and Risks]
    B --> H[Final Award by Court<br/>or LOF Arbitrator]
    C --> H
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

---

## 4.4 LLMC Limits — Tonnage Tier Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Calculate Ship's<br/>Gross Tonnage GT] --> B{GT Bracket?}
    B -- GT ≤ 2,000 --> C[Tier 1<br/>Base Limit Applies<br/>2,000,000 SDR Life /<br/>1,000,000 SDR Property]
    B -- 2,000 < GT ≤ 30,000 --> D[Tier 2<br/>Base + 800 SDR per ton<br/>Life / 400 SDR per ton Property]
    B -- 30,000 < GT ≤ 70,000 --> E[Tier 3<br/>Additional 600 SDR per ton<br/>Life / 300 SDR per ton Property]
    B -- GT > 70,000 --> F[Tier 4<br/>Capped at Maximum]
    C --> G[Apply Article 4<br/>Wilful Misconduct Test]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- No Misconduct --> H[Limit Stands]
    G -- Misconduct Found --> I[Limit Denied<br/>Unlimited Liability]
```

---

## 4.5 Sister Ship & Limitation Fund — Interaction Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Claimant Files Suit] --> B{Against which Ship?}
    B -- C1[Original Ship<br/>causing the loss] --> D[Limitation Available]
    B -- C2[Sister Ship<br/>same owner] --> E[Limitation Available<br/>Article 3.1.b]
    B -- C3[Ownership Dispute<br/>of Ship] --> F[NO Limitation<br/>Article 3.2]
    B -- C4[Salvage Reward Claim] --> F
    D --> G[Court Directs Fund<br/>Constitution Article 11]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Fund Distributed<br/>Pari Passu]
    H --> I[Claims Satisfied<br/>Up to Limit]
    F --> J[Full Claim Recovery<br/>No Cap]
```

---
