# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define **"Special Compensation"** under Article 14 of the International Convention on Salvage, 1989. When is it payable? **(3 Marks)**

### **Model Answer:**

> **Special Compensation** is a unique and innovative payment introduced by **Article 14** of the 1989 Salvage Convention to incentivise salvors to assist vessels that **threaten damage to the environment** (e.g., oil tankers carrying hazardous cargo), even when the salvage operation fails to produce a commercially useful result.
>
> It is payable when:
> 1. The salvor has carried out salvage operations on a vessel that **by itself or its cargo threatened environmental damage**; **AND**
> 2. The salvor has **failed to earn a salvage reward** under Article 13, **OR** the reward earned is **less than the special compensation** assessable.
>
> The amount is calculated as the salvor's **out-of-pocket expenses** plus an **increment of up to 100%** of those expenses, depending on the skill and efforts displayed in preventing environmental damage. If the salvor's own fault caused or contributed to the pollution, the Special Compensation may be reduced or denied.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern Q2]**
> **Q2.** What is the **"No Cure — No Pay"** principle under the Salvage Convention 1989? Are there any exceptions? **(3 Marks)**

### **Model Answer:**

> The **"No Cure — No Pay"** principle, codified in **Article 12** of the Salvage Convention 1989, provides that a salvor is entitled to a reward **only if the salvage operations have produced a useful result**. If the operations fail to produce any useful result, no payment is due.
>
> **Exception — Special Compensation (Article 14):** Despite the "No Cure — No Pay" rule, a salvor who assists a vessel threatening environmental damage is entitled to **Special Compensation** covering expenses (and an increment up to 100%) even if no useful result is achieved, in order to incentivise environmental protection.
>
> **Exception — SCOPIC Clause:** Under the **SCOPIC Clause** (industry contractual supplement), the salvor can invoke an enhanced minimum payment guarantee.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - ESE Pattern - Module 2 - Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **International Convention on Salvage, 1989** with special reference to the duties of the salvor and the salved party. Explain the **"No Cure — No Pay"** principle. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **Special Compensation** under Article 14. How does it differ from the traditional salvage reward? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

The **International Convention on Salvage, 1989** was adopted in London on 28 April 1989 and entered into force on 14 July 1996. It replaced the **1910 Brussels Convention** and is administered by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**. The Convention modernised the law of salvage and introduced important environmental considerations.

**1. Scope of the Convention (Article 1):**
The Convention applies to judicial or arbitral proceedings relating to salvage operations of vessels and property of any kind, regardless of where the operation was conducted. State-owned vessels (except warships and non-commercial State vessels) are included.

**2. Duties of the Salvor (Article 8):**
The salvor has the following duties:
- **(a)** To carry out salvage operations with **due care**;
- **(b)** To perform the operations with **reasonable speed**;
- **(c)** To exercise due care to **prevent or minimise damage to the environment**;
- **(d)** To seek assistance of other salvors when circumstances reasonably require;
- **(e)** To accept the intervention of other salvors when reasonably requested by the owner or master.

**3. Duties of the Owner and Master (Article 9):**
- The owner, master of the vessel, and the owner of property on board must **co-operate fully** with the salvor during the operations.
- The master must **accept delivery** of the salved property once it is brought to a safe place.

**4. The "No Cure — No Pay" Principle (Article 12):**
This is the cornerstone principle of salvage law. It provides that:
- A salvor is entitled to a reward **only if the salvage operations have produced a useful result**.
- If there is no useful result, **no payment is due** under the Convention.
- This principle is intended to **incentivise effective and successful salvage operations**, while ensuring that salvors do not profit from failed attempts.

**Conclusion:** The 1989 Convention strikes a balance between protecting the salvor, the salved party, and the environment, while ensuring that the **reward is commensurate with the actual success achieved**.

---

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

**Special Compensation under Article 14 — A New Concept:**

The traditional "No Cure — No Pay" principle deterred salvors from assisting vessels that threatened **environmental damage** (e.g., oil tankers), because:
- Such vessels often had **low salved value** (the cargo was already polluting);
- Salvors feared being held liable for **pollution damage** during the operation;
- Without a useful result, no reward was payable.

The **Amoco Cadiz disaster (1978)** highlighted this problem, leading to the introduction of **Article 14 Special Compensation**.

**Key Features of Special Compensation:**

1. **Trigger Conditions:**
   - The vessel, by itself or its cargo, **threatened damage to the environment**;
   - The salvor has **failed to earn a reward** under Article 13, **OR** the reward earned is **less than the Special Compensation** assessable.

2. **Calculation (Article 14.2):**
   $$\text{SC} = \text{Expenses} + \text{Increment (0\% to 100\% of Expenses)}$$
   - **Expenses** = out-of-pocket costs + fair rate for equipment and personnel.
   - **Increment** = an additional amount up to **100%** of expenses, reflecting the skill/efforts of the salvor in preventing or minimising environmental damage.

3. **Reduction Clause (Article 14.3):**
   If the salvor's own fault caused or contributed to the pollution damage, the Special Compensation may be **reduced or denied** entirely.

**Differences from Traditional Salvage Reward:**

| Parameter | Salvage Reward (Article 13) | Special Compensation (Article 14) |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Successful salvage (useful result) | Environmental threat + (low reward or no reward) |
| **Basis of calculation** | Article 13 criteria (6 factors) | Expenses + Increment |
| **Payable by** | All salved parties (vessel, cargo, freight) | Shipowner (vessel owner) |
| **Environmental premium** | Only one of six criteria | Central purpose |
| **Cap on increment** | Discretionary | 100% of expenses |

**Conclusion:** Special Compensation is a revolutionary provision that aligns the salvor's economic interest with environmental protection, ensuring that no salvor is left to bear the cost of preventing environmental disasters.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **(a)** Explain the **Convention on Limitation of Liability for Maritime Claims, 1976 (LLMC)** in detail. Discuss the **scope of application** and the **categories of claims** covered. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A bulk carrier of **25,000 GT** is involved in a collision causing loss of life and cargo damage. Calculate the **shipowner's limitation of liability** under the 1996 Protocol to the LLMC Convention. What conditions, if any, can **bar the right to limit**? **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks**

**The Convention on Limitation of Liability for Maritime Claims, 1976 (LLMC):**

The LLMC Convention was adopted in London on 19 November 1976 and entered into force on 1 December 1986. It was amended by the **1996 Protocol** (entered into force 13 May 2004) to substantially raise the limits of liability.

**Purpose:**
The Convention allows shipowners and other maritime operators (such as charterers, managers, operators, salvors) to **limit their financial liability** for certain maritime claims, on a **tonnage-based formula** using the ship's Gross Tonnage (GT).

**Scope of Application (Article 2):**
The Convention applies to the following categories of claims, however they may be brought:
1. Claims for **loss of life or personal injury**;
2. Claims for **damage to property** (occurring on board or in direct connection with the operation of the ship);
3. **Consequential loss** from (1) and (2);
4. Claims for **wreck removal**;
5. Claims for damage to **harbour works, basins, waterways, and aids to navigation**;
6. Claims for damage in connection with the **carriage of cargo, passengers, or baggage**;
7. Claims by **public authorities** for costs of measures to avert/minimise pollution damage.

**Claims Excluded from the Convention:**
- Oil pollution damage (governed by CLC 1992);
- Nuclear damage (governed by Paris/Brussels/Vienna Conventions);
- Claims by servants of the shipowner (crew wages, etc.);
- Public authority claims under nuclear liability conventions;
- Claims against the hull insurer of a nuclear ship.

**Persons Entitled to Limit:**
- The shipowner, charterer, manager, operator of the ship;
- The salvor (and their employees/duties);
- Any person for whose act/omission the shipowner/salvor is liable;
- Insurers of the above persons.

**Unit of Account:**
All limits are expressed in **SDR (Special Drawing Rights)**, the IMF's reserve asset, converted to national currency at the rate prevailing on the date of constitution of the limitation fund.

**The Limitation Fund (Article 11):**
The shipowner may constitute a fund in the court where the action is brought, either by **depositing the limit amount** or by **furnishing a guarantee**. Once the fund is constituted:
- No other assets of the shipowner can be arrested/attached for the same claims;
- The fund is distributed **pari passu** among all claimants;
- A claimant's share cannot exceed the amount they would have received from the fund.

**Conclusion:** The LLMC Convention provides a critical legal framework that protects the global shipping industry from catastrophic financial ruin while ensuring claimants receive fair compensation up to a defined limit.

---

#### **Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** Ship's Gross Tonnage (GT) = 25,000

**Step 1: Identify the Applicable Tonnage Tier (1996 Protocol)**

Since 2,000 < GT ≤ 30,000, the Tier 2 formula applies.

**Step 2: Calculate the Loss of Life / Personal Injury Limit**

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} = 2{,}000{,}000 + 800 \times (GT - 2{,}000)$$

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} = 2{,}000{,}000 + 800 \times (25{,}000 - 2{,}000)$$

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} = 2{,}000{,}000 + 800 \times 23{,}000$$

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} = 2{,}000{,}000 + 18{,}400{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Limit}_{\text{Life}} = 20{,}400{,}000 \text{ SDR}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Property / Other Claims Limit (Half the Life Limit)**

$$\text{Limit}_{\text{Property}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 20{,}400{,}000$$

$$\boxed{\text{Limit}_{\text{Property}} = 10{,}200{,}000 \text{ SDR}}$$

**Step 4: Aggregate Limit**

$$\text{Total Limit} = 20{,}400{,}000 + 10{,}200{,}000 = 30{,}600{,}000 \text{ SDR}$$

> **Note:** Under Article 6.2, if property claims consume the full property limit, the personal injury limit is **added on top** (an overflow provision of 50% of the property limit can be used for personal injury claims if needed).

**Step 5: Conditions Barring the Right to Limit (Article 4)**

A person liable is **not entitled to limit** if it is proved that the loss resulted from:
- **Personal act or omission** of the person liable, **AND**
- Committed with the **intent to cause such loss**, OR **recklessly and with knowledge** that such loss would probably result.

This is the **"wilful misconduct"** test — a high threshold. Simple negligence or ordinary navigational errors do **NOT** defeat limitation.

**Examples of Conduct Barring Limitation:**
- Deliberate scuttling of the ship (e.g., to claim insurance);
- Operating the ship with **known unseaworthy condition** despite clear warning;
- **Reckless navigation** in known dangerous conditions (e.g., the *Heranger* case).

**Examples of Conduct NOT Barring Limitation:**
- Navigational error by a tired watchkeeper;
- Mechanical failure without negligence;
- Collision in heavy weather due to ordinary negligence.

**Conclusion:** The 1996 Protocol limits provide substantial financial protection to the shipowner (₹3,366 Crore approx. for personal injury claims), but this protection is lost if the shipowner's conduct meets the wilful misconduct threshold.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Article 14 miscalculation:** Students often confuse the **salvor's expenses** (which form the base) with the **salvage reward** (Article 13). Remember: Special Compensation is calculated as **Expenses + Increment (0-100%)**, not as a percentage of salved value.
> 2. **Tier confusion in LLMC:** Mixing up the **personal injury** and **property** limits. The property limit is **half** the personal injury limit for the same tonnage. This is the most common 14-mark answer mistake.
> 3. **Tonnage confusion:** The LLMC uses **Gross Tonnage (GT)**, not **Deadweight Tonnage (DWT)** or **Net Tonnage (NT)**. This is a 1-mark penalty trap.
> 4. **Wilful Misconduct confusion:** Students write "negligence" or "gross negligence" as the bar to limitation. The correct standard is **"wilful misconduct"** — i.e., intentional or reckless conduct with knowledge of probable loss.
> 5. **Article 12 "No Cure" being absolute:** Some students write that no payment is ever due without a useful result. This overlooks the **Article 14 Special Compensation** exception.
> 6. **Sister ship misunderstanding:** Some students think the LLMC applies to *all* claims against sister ships. **Ownership disputes** and **salvage reward** claims are explicitly excluded under Article 3.2.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

### **A. International Convention on Salvage, 1989 — Must-Know Points**

- ✅ Adopted in **1989, London, IMO**, entered into force **1996**.
- ✅ Replaced the **1910 Brussels Convention**.
- ✅ Applies to **vessels and property** of any kind (including State-owned, with some exceptions).
- ✅ **"No Cure — No Pay"** principle — Article 12.
- ✅ Salvage reward fixed using **6 criteria** in **Article 13** (no rigid formula).
- ✅ **Article 14 Special Compensation** — for environmental protection.
- ✅ Special Compensation formula: **Expenses + Increment (0-100%)**.
- ✅ **Article 4** — exclusion of warships and non-commercial State vessels.
- ✅ **SCOPIC Clause** — industry contractual supplement.
- ✅ Human life salvage — no direct payment, but share of property salvage (Article 16).
- ✅ Apportionment among co-salvors — Article 15.

### **B. Convention on Limitation of Liability, 1976 (LLMC) — Must-Know Points**

- ✅ Adopted in **1976, London, IMO**, entered into force **1986**.
- ✅ Amended by the **1996 Protocol** (entered into force 2004).
- ✅ Uses **Gross Tonnage (GT)** for calculation.
- ✅ Limits expressed in **SDR**.
- ✅ **Article 6** — Two-tier limit: Loss of Life + Property.
- ✅ **Loss of Life Tier 2 formula:** 2,000,000 + 800 × (GT - 2,000) SDR.
- ✅ **Property Tier 2 formula:** 1,000,000 + 400 × (GT - 2,000) SDR.
- ✅ **Article 4** — Wilful misconduct bars limitation.
- ✅ **Article 11** — Limitation fund mechanism.
- ✅ **Article 3.1.b** — Sister ship liability.
- ✅ **Article 3.2** — Ownership disputes and salvage claims excluded.
- ✅ **Article 2.2** — Excludes oil pollution (CLC), nuclear damage, crew wage claims.

### **C. Frequently Confused Distinctions to Memorise**

| Distinction | Why It Matters |
|---|---|
| **Salvage Reward (Art. 13) vs. Special Compensation (Art. 14)** | Different triggers, different basis, different payer. |
| **Gross Tonnage (LLMC) vs. Deadweight Tonnage** | Use GT for LLMC calculations. |
| **Wilful Misconduct (LLMC) vs. Gross Negligence** | Only wilful misconduct bars limitation. |
| **SDR vs. Gold Francs** | Older conventions used Gold Francs; modern ones use SDR. |
| **LLMC 1976 vs. 1996 Protocol** | Limits are different — always specify which is being applied. |
| **"No Cure — No Pay" (Salvage) vs. "No Cure — Some Pay" (Special Comp.)** | The latter is the exception to the former. |
| **CIL (Civil Liability Convention) vs. LLMC** | CIL is for oil pollution specifically; LLMC is the general regime. |

### **D. KTU-Favourite One-Liners for Last-Minute Recall**

- **"No Cure — No Pay"** → Article 12, except Article 14 Special Compensation.
- **"Six Criteria"** → Article 13 — salved value, environmental skill, success, danger, salvor skill, time/expenses.
- **"SC = Expenses + 0-100% Increment"** → Article 14.2.
- **"GT × 800 (Life) / GT × 400 (Property)"** → 1996 Protocol, Tier 2.
- **"Wilful misconduct bars limitation"** → Article 4 of LLMC.
- **"Sister ship subject, ownership/salvage excluded"** → Article 3.1.b / Article 3.2 of LLMC.
- **"SDR is the unit"** → Both conventions.

### **E. Cross-Reference to KTU 2024 Course Outcomes (COs)**

| CO Mapped | Topic Coverage |
|---|---|
| **CO 2** | Demonstrate knowledge of international maritime conventions (Salvage 1989, LLMC 1976). |
| **CO 3** | Apply liability rules and limitation principles to factual scenarios. |
| **CO 4** | Analyse modern trends in maritime law including environmental protection (Art. 14 Salvage Convention). |

---

topics.title: Oil Pollution Compensation Conventions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Oil Pollution Compensation Conventions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oil Pollution Compensation Conventions** refer to the body of international maritime treaties and protocols designed to establish a comprehensive legal regime for **liability, compensation, and insurance** for damage caused by oil spills from tankers and other ships, whether in territorial waters, EEZ, or on the high seas. They operate primarily through the principle of **strict liability** of the shipowner, **mandatory civil liability insurance**, and **multi-tiered compensation funds**.

The main conventions and protocols that form this regime include:

| Convention / Protocol | Year | Short Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| International Convention on Civil Liability for Oil Pollution Damage | 1969 | **CLC 1969** | Imposes strict liability on the shipowner |
| International Convention on the Establishment of an International Fund for Oil Pollution Damage | 1971 | **FUND 1971** | Provides supplementary compensation |
| Protocol to CLC 1969 | 1992 | **CLC 1992 Protocol** | Raises liability limits |
| Protocol to FUND 1971 | 1992 | **FUND 1992 Protocol** | Enhances fund compensation |
| Protocol establishing a Supplementary Fund | 2003 | **SUPP FUND 2003** | Adds a third tier of compensation |
| International Convention on Civil Liability for Bunker Oil Pollution Damage | 2001 | **BUNKER CONVENTION 2001** | Extends strict liability to non-tanker ships |
| International Convention on Oil Pollution Preparedness, Response and Co-operation | 1990 | **OPRC 1990** | Mandates contingency planning |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Three-Layer Insurance Cake" Analogy**

Imagine a huge oil tanker crashes and spills crude oil, devastating a coastal fishing village. The villagers need compensation. Where does the money come from?

Think of the compensation system as a **three-layer cake** baked by international law:

- **Layer 1 (The Shipowner's Plate)**: The tanker owner is **strictly liable** — they are responsible even if they were not at fault, and they must carry **compulsory insurance** (the "Certificate of Financial Responsibility"). Their insurer pays up to a calculated ceiling.
- **Layer 2 (The Industry Pie)**: If Layer 1 is insufficient, a separate fund — the **IOPC Fund** — kicks in. The money for this fund is collected from oil *receivers* (the oil companies that import crude by sea). This spreads the cost across the whole industry.
- **Layer 3 (The Supplementary Tier)**: A voluntary third tier provides even more compensation, again funded by oil receivers in participating states.

The **rationale** is brilliant: instead of letting victims suffer, the legal system forces the **shipping industry** and the **oil industry** (the two main beneficiaries of maritime oil transport) to collectively bear the cost of pollution they help create.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why was this needed?**
> The 1967 **Torrey Canyon** disaster off the coast of England (118,000 tonnes of crude oil spilled) exposed the fact that victims of oil pollution had no reliable source of compensation. This directly triggered the CLC and FUND Conventions.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical / Legal Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The conventions use **Special Drawing Rights (SDR)** as the unit of account. This is a basket currency of the **International Monetary Fund (IMF)**, replacing the old **Poincaré Gold Francs** used in early conventions.

- **1 SDR ≈ USD 1.33 (approximate, fluctuates with IMF rates)**
- **1 tonne (t) = 1,000 kg of oil cargo / 1 GT (Gross Tonnage)**
- The "Strict Liability" threshold is **0 to unlimited** liability depending on whether a *Fund Convention* state is involved.

---

## 1.4 Module Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Focus:** Students must understand the **evolution**, **key provisions**, **liability limits**, **fund structure**, and **interplay** of the CLC, FUND, and Supplementary FUND Conventions. The 1992 Protocols (amended versions) are the **current operational versions** in force for most major maritime nations, including **India** (which is a party to the 1992 Protocols).

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Historical Evolution: The "Why" Behind the Conventions

### Step-by-Step Logic:
- **Pre-1967**: Maritime law treated pollution as a private tort. Victims had to prove negligence — extremely difficult and slow.
- **1967 — Torrey Canyon Disaster**: The SS Torrey Canyon, a 297 m Liberian-flagged supertanker, ran aground off Land's End, England, spilling 119,000 tonnes of crude. Victims (fishermen, hoteliers, councils) had **no effective legal remedy** because the ship was owned by a single-purpose shell company.
- **1969**: IMO adopts **CLC 1969** — first convention imposing *strict liability* on the shipowner, with *mandatory insurance*.
- **1971**: IMO adopts **FUND 1971** — recognising that shipowner's limits were inadequate.
- **1984**: CRISTAL and TOVALOP (voluntary schemes by oil industry) phased out as FUND gained acceptance.
- **1992 — Major Revision**: Liability limits raised dramatically to reflect inflation and larger tanker sizes (VLCCs, ULCCs).
- **2000 — Erika Disaster (France)**: 30,000 tonnes spilled; triggered FURTHER review.
- **2002 — Prestige Disaster (Spain)**: 77,000 tonnes spilled; led to creation of the **2003 Supplementary Fund**.
- **2001**: **Bunker Convention** — extended strict liability regime to ships *other than tankers* using heavy fuel oil (bunker oil) for propulsion.

---

## 2.2 Core Legal Principles of the CLC Regime

### A. Strict Liability of the Shipowner

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under CLC Article III, the shipowner is liable for oil pollution damage **even without proof of fault**. The victim does NOT need to prove negligence. The burden of proof reverses — the *shipowner* must prove an *exception* applies to escape liability.

### B. The Limited Number of Exemptions (CLC Article III, paragraph 2)
The shipowner is NOT liable if he proves the damage resulted from:
1. **An act of war, hostilities, civil war, or insurrection** (Acts of God by man).
2. **A natural phenomenon of an exceptional, inevitable, and irresistible character** (Acts of God of nature — e.g., once-in-a-century tsunami).
3. **An act or omission of a third party** with intent to cause damage (e.g., sabotage).
4. **The negligence or wrongful act of any government** in maintaining navigational aids/lights.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Point:** "Negligence of the master/crew" is **NOT** a defence. This is what makes the liability "strict."

### C. Compulsory Liability Insurance
- Every tanker over **2,000 GT** carrying persistent oil in bulk to/from a CLC state must carry insurance.
- A **Certificate of Insurance** must be carried on board.
- The insurer is **directly liable** to victims (this is the legal innovation — victims can sue the insurer directly without first suing the shipowner).

### D. Right of Limitation of Liability
- The shipowner may limit his liability based on the ship's tonnage **up to a statutory ceiling**.
- This is the most argued aspect in litigation.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liability Limit Formulas (CLC 1992 Protocol, Article V)**

| Ship Type / Tonnage | Liability Limit (SDR) |
|---|---|
| Tanker ≤ 5,000 GT | **3 million SDR** (flat) |
| Tanker > 5,000 GT | **3 million SDR + 420 SDR per additional GT above 5,000** |
| Maximum Liability (any tanker) | **59.7 million SDR** (capped) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fund Convention (1992) Compensation Limits:**
> - Maximum compensation by IOPC Fund: **135 million SDR per incident**
> - If three or more states contribute, an additional amount up to a higher aggregate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Supplementary Fund (2003):**
> - Adds up to **750 million SDR** in extra compensation (combined with CLC + FUND 1992, total can be **~944.7 million SDR**).

### LaTeX Rendered Formula for Shipowner's Liability Limit:

$$
L_{\text{CLC}} = \begin{cases}
3{,}000{,}000 \text{ SDR} & \text{if } GT \leq 5{,}000 \\
3{,}000{,}000 + 420 \times (GT - 5{,}000) \text{ SDR} & \text{if } GT > 5{,}000 \\
\leq 59{,}700{,}000 \text{ SDR} & \text{absolute ceiling}
\end{cases}
$$

Where $L_{\text{CLC}}$ is the **shipowner's maximum liability** and $GT$ is the ship's **Gross Tonnage**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Management

> [!NOTE]
> This legal framework directly affects:
> - **Naval Architecture**: Tankers are designed with double-hulls (mandated by **MARPOL Annex I**, Reg 19) to reduce spill risk and indirectly affect insurance premiums.
> - **Ship Management**: Owners must budget for P&I (Protection & Indemnity) Club insurance.
> - **Risk Assessment**: Ports require bunker convention certificates for non-tankers.
> - **Crisis Management**: SOPEP (Shipboard Oil Pollution Emergency Plan) required by MARPOL.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation — Calculating Shipowner's Liability Limit

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A tanker of **30,000 GT** spills crude oil in coastal waters. Calculate the shipowner's maximum liability under **CLC 1992 Protocol**.

### Step 1: Identify which sub-formula applies
We check: $GT = 30{,}000 > 5{,}000$ → Use the **second case** of the formula.

### Step 2: Substitute the values
We use the formula:
$$
L_{\text{CLC}} = 3{,}000{,}000 + 420 \times (GT - 5{,}000)
$$

### Step 3: Calculate the tonnage difference
$$
GT_{\text{excess}} = 30{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 25{,}000 \text{ GT}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate the additional liability
$$
L_{\text{additional}} = 420 \times 25{,}000 = 10{,}500{,}000 \text{ SDR}
$$

### Step 5: Add to the base amount
$$
L_{\text{CLC}} = 3{,}000{,}000 + 10{,}500{,}000 = 13{,}500{,}000 \text{ SDR}
$$

### Step 6: Verify against the ceiling
$$
13{,}500{,}000 \text{ SDR} < 59{,}700{,}000 \text{ SDR (ceiling)}
$$
Therefore, the **liability limit is 13,500,000 SDR** (approximately USD 17.95 million at 1.33 rate).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Derivation — Total Available Compensation (3-Tier System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** In the above incident, victims file claims totalling **800 million SDR**. The spill occurs in a state that is a party to the **1992 CLC, 1992 FUND, AND 2003 Supplementary Fund**. Calculate the compensation breakdown.

### Step 1: Tier 1 — Shipowner's Liability (CLC 1992)
$$
L_{\text{Tier 1}} = 13{,}500{,}000 \text{ SDR (from Section 3.1)}
$$

### Step 2: Tier 2 — IOPC FUND 1992 Compensation
Maximum IOPC Fund compensation per incident: **135,000,000 SDR**.
However, this is the *maximum*, not always the full amount disbursed. For this calculation:
$$
L_{\text{Tier 2}} = 135{,}000{,}000 \text{ SDR (assume full available)}
$$

### Step 3: Tier 3 — Supplementary Fund 2003
The Supplementary Fund pays the *difference* between actual claims and CLC + FUND payments, up to a total system cap of **~750 million SDR** additional.

### Step 4: Combined Liability Ceiling (CLC + FUND + SUPP)
$$
L_{\text{combined}} = 13{,}500{,}000 + 135{,}000{,}000 + 750{,}000{,}000
$$
$$
L_{\text{combined}} = 898{,}500{,}000 \text{ SDR}
$$

### Step 5: Compare with the claim
$$
\text{Claim} = 800{,}000{,}000 \text{ SDR} < 898{,}500{,}000 \text{ SDR (ceiling)}
$$
Since claims are less than the total available, victims are **fully compensated**.

### Step 6: Source of funds
- 13.5 million SDR: Shipowner's P&I Club insurance.
- 135 million SDR: IOPC Fund (paid by oil receivers — importers of crude by sea).
- 651.5 million SDR: Supplementary Fund (paid by oil receivers in Supplementary Fund member states).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Conventions

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison of the Three Compensation Conventions**

| Feature | CLC 1992 | FUND 1992 | Supplementary Fund 2003 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Liability imposed on** | Shipowner | Oil receivers (via state contributions) | Oil receivers (in member states) |
| **Strict Liability** | YES | N/A (Fund) | N/A (Fund) |
| **Maximum Amount** | 59.7 million SDR (capped by tonnage formula) | 135 million SDR (per incident) | 750 million SDR (combined system cap) |
| **Payer** | Shipowner / Insurer | IOPC Fund | Supplementary Fund |
| **Triggered when** | Any spill damage exceeds shipowner's coverage | CLC limits insufficient | CLC + FUND insufficient |
| **Compulsory?** | YES (for tankers > 2,000 GT) | YES (for member states) | Optional (ratified by states) |
| **Shipowner's Defences** | War, act of God, sabotage, govt. negligence | No strict liability (covers gaps) | N/A |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bunker Convention vs. CLC

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Bunker Convention (2001) was needed**

| Aspect | CLC 1992 | Bunker Convention 2001 |
|---|---|---|
| **Applies to** | Oil tankers (cargo oil pollution) | All ships (bunker oil / fuel oil pollution) |
| **Type of ship** | Tanker | Bulk carrier, container ship, cruise ship, etc. |
| **Pollution source** | Cargo oil in bulk | Bunker fuel (engine fuel, heavy fuel oil) |
| **Strict liability** | YES | YES |
| **Defences** | Same 4 CLC defences | Same 4 defences (mirrored) |
| **Insurance limit** | Tonnage-based formula | Tonnage-based formula (separate limits in Annex) |
| **Certificate required** | Yes, for tankers | Yes, for ships > 1,000 GT |

---

## 3.5 Layered Compensation Logic — The "Cascade Principle"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cascade Principle:** The conventions operate on a *tiered cascade*. Each tier is exhausted before the next is triggered.

$$
\text{Victim Claims} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Tier 1: CLC (Shipowner)}} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Tier 2: FUND 1992}} \rightarrow \boxed{\text{Tier 3: SUPP FUND 2003}} \rightarrow \text{Uncompensated (no more money)}
$$

> Each tier has a **cap** beyond which victims simply bear the loss, creating strong political pressure to expand coverage (which led to the Supplementary Fund).
