# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Strict Liability" in the context of the CLC Convention. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict liability** under the CLC 1969/1992 means the **shipowner is liable for oil pollution damage without the need for the victim to prove fault or negligence**. The mere occurrence of the spill establishes a *prima facie* case. The burden of proof shifts to the shipowner, who must prove one of the four statutory exceptions (war, act of God, third-party sabotage, or government negligence in maintaining navigational aids) to escape liability. This revolutionary principle transformed maritime tort law and is the cornerstone of all subsequent maritime pollution conventions.

---

### Question 2: What is the IOPC Fund? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **International Oil Pollution Compensation Fund (IOPC Funds)** is an intergovernmental organisation established under the **FUND 1992 Convention**, headquartered in **London, United Kingdom**. It is financed by **levies on oil receivers** (entities that receive oil after sea transport in member states). It provides **supplementary compensation** to victims of oil spills when:
> 1. The damage exceeds the shipowner's liability limit under CLC.
> 2. The shipowner is financially incapable of meeting their obligations.
> 3. The shipowner is exempted from liability under CLC.
>
> The Fund pays compensation for **economic loss, environmental damage, and cleanup costs**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **ESE Module Internal Choice (as per KTU 2024 Scheme)** — Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A: 14 Marks (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the historical evolution of the Oil Pollution Compensation Conventions. What lessons did the *Torrey Canyon* (1967) and *Prestige* (2002) disasters teach the maritime community?

**Model Solution:**

The maritime oil pollution compensation regime evolved through five major phases:

**Phase 1 — The Pre-Convention Era (Pre-1967):**
Before 1967, there was **no international convention** governing oil pollution liability. Claims were handled through national tort law, requiring victims to prove negligence — an almost impossible burden given the technical complexity of maritime operations. The **SS Torrey Canyon** disaster of **18 March 1967** was the catalyst for change. The Liberian-flagged supertanker ran aground off the Isles of Scilly, England, spilling 119,000 tonnes of crude oil. The shipowner was a single-purpose shell company, **Barracuda Tanker Corporation**, with negligible assets. The English courts ruled (in **Re: Torrey Canyon**, 1968) that the British government had acted lawfully in bombing the wreck to burn off the oil — a striking outcome that revealed the **absence of victim compensation mechanisms**.

**Phase 2 — First-Generation Conventions (1969–1971):**
The IMO adopted the **Civil Liability Convention (CLC) 1969** introducing the revolutionary concept of **strict liability** and **compulsory insurance**. Recognising that shipowner's limits were too low, it also adopted the **FUND 1971** convention to provide supplementary compensation from oil receivers.

**Phase 3 — 1992 Protocol Amendments:**
With inflation and increasing tanker sizes, the original limits became inadequate. The **1992 Protocol to CLC** raised the maximum shipowner's liability to **59.7 million SDR**. The **1992 Protocol to FUND** raised the IOPC Fund's maximum compensation to **135 million SDR**. These protocols are the current operational versions.

**Phase 4 — Lessons from *Erika* (1999) and *Prestige* (2002):**
The **MT Erika** sank off Brittany, France, in December 1999, spilling 30,000 tonnes of heavy fuel oil. Despite the CLC/FUND system, a significant gap in compensation remained. Worse was the **MT Prestige**, which sank off Galicia, Spain, in November 2002, spilling 77,000 tonnes of oil — a record compensation event. These disasters exposed two issues: (i) compensation caps were still insufficient, and (ii) aging single-hull tankers were a major risk.

**Phase 5 — The 2003 Supplementary Fund and Modern Regime:**
In response, the IMO adopted the **2003 Protocol establishing a Supplementary Fund**, which adds a third tier of compensation up to **750 million SDR**, taking the total system to **~944.7 million SDR**. The **MARPOL Annex I** regulations were also tightened, culminating in **EEDI (Energy Efficiency Design Index)** requirements and the **acceleration of the single-hull phase-out** in 2005.

**Key Lessons:**
- The **shipping and oil industries** must share the burden of pollution costs.
- A **multi-tiered fund system** is necessary because the shipowner's liability alone is never sufficient for large spills.
- **Compulsory insurance** is essential for victim protection.
- **Stricter tanker design** standards (double-hull) are complementary to financial mechanisms.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Critically analyse the strict liability principle under CLC 1992, including the four exceptions. Is strict liability truly "strict"?

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Strict Liability:**
Under **CLC 1992 Article III, paragraph 1**, the shipowner is liable for **any pollution damage** caused by oil escaping from a tanker, **regardless of fault**. The victim need not prove negligence, recklessness, or intent. This is a **revolutionary departure** from classical tort law, which requires proof of breach of duty.

**The Four Exceptions (Article III, paragraph 2):**
1. **Act of War / Hostilities / Insurrection**: If the spill is caused by a war or civil conflict (e.g., an oil tanker hit by a missile during armed conflict).
2. **Exceptional Natural Phenomena**: An "act of God" of a magnitude beyond human prediction or control — e.g., a 9.0-magnitude underwater earthquake, or an unprecedented tsunami.
3. **Third-Party Intentional Act**: Damage caused by a *deliberate* third-party act with the *intention* of causing damage — e.g., a saboteur planting explosives on a tanker. Mere *negligence* by a third party is **NOT** an exception.
4. **Negligence of a Government Authority in Maintaining Navigational Aids**: A government that fails to maintain buoys, lights, or lighthouses which, if maintained, would have prevented the grounding.

**Is Strict Liability Truly "Strict"?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **YES, it is genuinely strict** — but it is **not absolute**.

It is strict in the sense that:
- The shipowner is **automatically liable** upon occurrence of the spill.
- The **burden of proof** is reversed (shipowner must prove the defence, not victim).
- Crew negligence is **not** a defence.

It is not absolute in the sense that:
- The **four exceptions** provide significant carve-outs.
- The **limitation of liability** caps the financial exposure.
- **Force majeure** events (e.g., exceptional storms) can exonerate the shipowner.

**Critical Analysis:**
The principle is "strict" because it eliminates the need to prove fault, but it is "limited" because the shipowner's financial liability is capped by tonnage-based formulas. The 1992 Protocol raised these caps substantially, but the **Erika** and **Prestige** disasters revealed that the 59.7 million SDR ceiling was still insufficient. This led to the **2003 Supplementary Fund** as an additional layer. The combination creates a near-comprehensive compensation system, but it relies on international cooperation — states must ratify each protocol to access its benefits.

**Conclusion:**
The CLC's strict liability is a **carefully balanced compromise** between victim protection and shipowner's economic survival. It is strict in *legal* terms but is mediated by **statutory exceptions** and **financial caps**, making it a **practical and politically acceptable** form of strict liability.

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **Civil Liability Convention 1992**, the **FUND 1992**, and the **Supplementary Fund 2003**. Explain the rationale for the tiered system.

**Model Solution:**

**1. The Civil Liability Convention (CLC) 1992:**

The CLC 1992 Protocol is the **first tier** of compensation. Its key features are:
- **Strict liability** on the shipowner for pollution damage.
- **Compulsory insurance** for tankers > 2,000 GT carrying persistent oil as cargo.
- **Direct action** against the insurer (P&I Club).
- **Liability limit** based on tonnage (up to **59.7 million SDR** maximum).
- **Four statutory defences** (war, act of God, third-party intent, government negligence).

**2. The FUND 1992 (IOPC Fund):**

Established under the **FUND 1992 Protocol**, this is the **second tier**. Key features:
- **Funded by oil receivers** (importers of crude) in member states via levies on cargo volume.
- **Maximum compensation: 135 million SDR per incident**.
- Pays when CLC limits are exhausted, shipowner is insolvent, or shipowner is exempted.
- **Headquarters:** London, UK.
- **Governance:** Assembly of member states; Administrative Council.

**3. The Supplementary Fund 2003:**

The **third tier**, established as a Protocol. Key features:
- **Voluntary** (not all CLC states are party to it).
- **Maximum additional compensation: 750 million SDR**.
- **Funded by oil receivers** in member states (higher per-barrel levy).
- Created in response to the **Prestige** disaster.

**Comparative Table:**

| Aspect | CLC 1992 | FUND 1992 | Supplementary Fund 2003 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Liable Party** | Shipowner | Oil receivers (via state) | Oil receivers (in member states) |
| **Legal Basis** | Strict liability | Strict + compensatory | Supplementary |
| **Cap (SDR)** | 59.7 M | 135 M | 750 M (additional) |
| **Trigger** | Spill occurs | CLC exhausted | CLC + FUND exhausted |
| **Entry** | Compulsory for ratifying states | Compulsory for ratifying states | Optional |

**Rationale for Tiered System:**

1. **Risk Distribution**: Spreads the cost across the entire maritime oil industry (both shipowners and oil receivers) rather than placing the entire burden on a single shipowner.

2. **Victim Protection**: Ensures that victims of large spills (like *Prestige* with €1 billion in claims) are not left without compensation.

3. **Political Acceptability**: Shipowners accept strict liability because there is a cap and an industry fund backs it up. Oil receivers accept the levy because the cost is small compared to their profit margins.

4. **Capacity Building**: Each tier has its own administrative machinery (IOPC Fund offices in London, Brussels; Supplementary Fund managed by IOPC Funds).

5. **Adaptability**: New tiers can be added when needed (the 2003 Supplementary Fund is the latest example).

**Conclusion:**
The three-tier system represents the **most sophisticated international regime** for compensating environmental damage. It is the template for other conventions like the **HNS Convention 2010** (Hazardous and Noxious Substances), which has a similar 3-tier structure.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Bunker Convention 2001**. Why was it needed when the CLC 1992 already existed?

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction to the Bunker Convention:**

The **International Convention on Civil Liability for Bunker Oil Pollution Damage, 2001** is an IMO convention that extends the **strict liability regime** of the CLC to **all sea-going vessels** (not just tankers) for pollution caused by **bunker oil** (i.e., the fuel used to power the ship's engines — typically heavy fuel oil, marine diesel, etc.).

**Key Provisions:**
- **Strict liability** on the **shipowner** of any ship > 1,000 GT using bunker oil.
- **Compulsory insurance** with a ceiling defined in national currency.
- **Certificate of Insurance** must be carried on board.
- **Defences** mirror CLC 1992 (war, act of God, third-party intent, government negligence).
- **Right of limitation** of liability is governed by applicable national/regional law (not a tonnage formula like CLC).

**Why Was It Needed?**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CLC 1992 only covers oil tankers carrying oil as cargo. It does NOT cover pollution caused by bunker oil from non-tankers.**

Before 2001, if a bulk carrier or container ship ran aground and spilled its bunker fuel, victims had to rely on **national tort law** to seek compensation — a slow, uncertain process. The growing size of non-tanker ships (e.g., container ships of 20,000+ TEU, cruise ships carrying 6,000+ passengers) meant the *potential* for massive bunker oil spills was increasing.

**The *Herald of Free Enterprise* (1987) and *Braer* (1993)** incidents in particular highlighted this gap. The *Erika* (1999) and *Prestige* (2002) — though tankers — also revealed that bunker oil pollution from *non-tankers* was a growing concern.

**Key Differences from CLC 1992:**

| Aspect | CLC 1992 | Bunker Convention 2001 |
|---|---|---|
| **Ship Type** | Tankers only | All ships > 1,000 GT |
| **Pollutant** | Cargo oil (persistent) | Bunker oil (fuel) |
| **Liability Cap** | Tonnage formula | National law / LLMC Convention limits |
| **Insurer** | P&I Club (specialised) | P&I Club (broader cover) |
| **Threshold GT** | 2,000 GT | 1,000 GT |

**Implementation in India:**
India ratified the Bunker Convention in 2015. The **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** was amended (Directorate General of Shipping notification) to incorporate the Bunker Convention's requirements. Indian-flagged ships must now carry Bunker Convention certificates.

**Conclusion:**
The Bunker Convention 2001 **closes the gap** left by CLC 1992 by ensuring that pollution from the *engine fuel* of non-tankers is also covered by strict liability and compulsory insurance. Together with CLC, FUND, and the Supplementary Fund, it forms part of a **comprehensive, integrated maritime oil pollution compensation regime**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on This Topic:**

1. **Confusing CLC 1969 with CLC 1992**: The 1992 Protocol is the **current operational version**. Writing about CLC 1969 limits is outdated. **Always mention the 1992 Protocol when discussing Indian context** (India is party to the 1992 Protocols).

2. **Listing the wrong number of exceptions**: Students often cite **5 or 6** exceptions. There are **exactly 4** exceptions under CLC Article III(2). Memorise them precisely.

3. **Confusing FUND and SUPP FUND**: FUND 1992 is **135 million SDR per incident**; Supplementary Fund adds up to **750 million SDR**. They are different bodies with different levies.

4. **Wrong unit of account**: The conventions use **SDR**, not USD, not gold francs, not rupees. Always state "SDR" in answers.

5. **Forgetting that Bunker Convention applies to non-tankers**: Many students wrongly extend CLC to all ships. The Bunker Convention is the answer for non-tankers.

6. **Skipping the 1992 Protocols**: When asked about "current position," students often describe 1969/1971 limits. **Always specify 1992 Protocol versions for marks.**

7. **Confusing the "claimant"**: Victims can sue the **insurer directly** under CLC — this is a critical legal innovation that must be mentioned for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Oil Pollution Compensation Conventions**

- [ ] The 1967 **Torrey Canyon** disaster triggered the CLC convention.
- [ ] **CLC 1969** introduced **strict liability** and **compulsory insurance**.
- [ ] **FUND 1971** provides a **second tier** of compensation from oil receivers.
- [ ] The **1992 Protocol to CLC** raised shipowner's maximum liability to **59.7 million SDR**.
- [ ] The **1992 Protocol to FUND** raised the IOPC Fund's maximum to **135 million SDR per incident**.
- [ ] The **2003 Supplementary Fund** adds up to **750 million SDR** in a third tier.
- [ ] The four CLC defences are: **(1) war, (2) act of God, (3) third-party intent, (4) government negligence in navigational aids**.
- [ ] **Compulsory insurance** applies to tankers > 2,000 GT (CLC) and ships > 1,000 GT (Bunker).
- [ ] The **Bunker Convention 2001** extends strict liability to **non-tankers** for **bunker oil pollution**.
- [ ] **IOPC Fund** is headquartered in **London, UK**.
- [ ] **Unit of account** is **SDR (Special Drawing Rights)** of the IMF.
- [ ] **Tonnage formula** for CLC 1992: $L = 3{,}000{,}000 + 420 \times (GT - 5{,}000)$ SDR.
- [ ] Maximum shipowner's liability is **capped at 59.7 million SDR** for any size tanker.
- [ ] The **cascade principle** means each tier pays only after the previous one is exhausted.
- [ ] **India is a party to the 1992 CLC and 1992 FUND Protocols**, and ratified the Bunker Convention in 2015.
- [ ] The **HNS Convention 2010** follows a similar tiered approach for hazardous and noxious substances.
- [ ] The **prestige disaster (2002)** was the trigger for the **2003 Supplementary Fund**.
- [ ] Victims can sue the **insurer directly** — a key innovation of the CLC regime.
- [ ] **TOVALOP and CRISTAL** were voluntary schemes that became obsolete after the 1992 Protocols gained universal acceptance.
- [ ] **Single-hull tankers** are being phased out under MARPOL Annex I, complementing the financial mechanisms of the conventions.
- [ ] Always mention **strict liability** + **compulsory insurance** + **multi-tier fund** as the **three pillars** of the regime in any descriptive answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shipping Management: Types of cargoes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shipping Management: Types of cargoes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cargo** in maritime law refers to any goods, merchandise, articles, or commodities that are transported by sea from a port of loading to a port of discharge under a contract of carriage evidenced by a **Bill of Lading (B/L)** or **Charter Party**. The classification of cargo determines the applicable shipping regulations, packaging standards, stowage requirements, liability regime, and freight calculation methodology under the **Hague-Visby Rules** and **Indian Carriage of Goods by Sea Act, 1925 (COGSA)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, cargo classification is the **foundation of operational shipping decisions** — it dictates the type of vessel to be deployed (bulk carrier, tanker, container ship, reefer vessel, etc.), the loading/discharging equipment required, the **International Maritime Solid Bulk Cargoes (IMSBC) Code** compliance, and the **International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code** categorization.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a shipping vessel as a multi-purpose moving truck.** Just like a logistics company uses different trucks for different loads — a refrigerated van for ice cream, a tanker for milk, a flatbed for cars, a container truck for boxed goods — the maritime industry uses different ship designs because cargoes behave differently at sea.

**Real-world analogy for a student:**
Imagine you are moving your entire house from Kochi to Dubai by sea.

- Your **clothes, books, and appliances (packed in cartons)** → these become **General/Containerized Cargo**.
- Your **car** → this is **Ro-Ro (Roll-on/Roll-off) Cargo**.
- A **large industrial generator** you are shipping for your factory → this is **Heavy Lift / Project Cargo**.
- If you owned an **oil refinery**, the crude petroleum would be **Liquid Bulk Cargo**.
- **Frozen seafood** would need a **Reefer (Refrigerated) Cargo** setup.

Just as you would not put your refrigerator on a flatbed truck without power, you cannot stow dangerous goods next to food cargoes on a ship without following the **IMDG Code segregation rules**. The "type" of cargo therefore decides *everything* — from the ship's design to the legal liability of the carrier.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Maritime Metrics & Constants

The following **standard industry metrics** govern cargo measurement and freight calculation:

- **1 TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit)** = standard container volume metric (~33 m³ internal).
- **1 FEU (Forty-foot Equivalent Unit)** = 2 TEU (~67 m³).
- **1 Metric Ton (MT)** = **1,000 kg** (used in shipping freight).
- **1 Deadweight Ton (DWT)** = the carrying capacity of the vessel including cargo, fuel, water, and crew provisions.
- **Stowage Factor (SF)** = cubic metres per metric ton (m³/MT). **Bulk cargo SF < 1.4 m³/MT** (heavy); **SF > 1.4 m³/MT** (light).
- **Hague-Visby Package Limitation** = **666.67 SDR/package** or **2 SDR/kg gross weight**, whichever is higher.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

Shipping cargoes are classified along **five primary axes**:

1. **Physical State** → Solid, Liquid, Gas.
2. **Packaging Format** → Packaged (general), Unitized, Containerized, Bulk, Liquid-bulk.
3. **Cargo Behaviour under Carriage** → Non-hazardous, Hazardous (IMDG Class 1–9), Perishable.
4. **Volume-to-Weight Ratio (Stowage Factor)** → Heavy (SF < 1.4), Light (SF > 1.4), Voluminous.
5. **Shipping Operation Mode** → Break-bulk, Neo-bulk, Dry-bulk, Wet-bulk, Ro-Ro, Lo-Lo.

The **"Why"** behind this classification:
- Different cargoes impose **different stresses on the hull** (e.g., grain shifts → stability issues; liquid sloshing → free-surface effect).
- Different cargoes carry **different liability exposure** (e.g., dangerous goods → strict liability under IMDG).
- Different cargoes require **different freight rates** (liquid bulk is cheap per MT; containerized is premium-priced).

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cargo Classification Sheet (Master Table)

The table below is the **single most important cheat sheet** for KTU exams on this topic. Memorize the IMDG Classes and the cargo-vessel pairings.

| **Cargo Type** | **Sub-Category** | **Examples** | **Typical Vessel** | **Applicable Code** | **Key Risk** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry Bulk** | Major bulk | Iron ore, coal, grain | Capesize, Panamax, Handysize bulk carrier | IMSBC Code | Cargo shift, moisture damage |
| **Dry Bulk** | Minor bulk | Sugar, cement, fertilizers | Handysize bulk carrier | IMSBC Code | Liquefaction (nickel ore disaster, 2015) |
| **Liquid Bulk** | Crude & products | Crude oil, diesel, jet fuel | VLCC, Aframax tanker | MARPOL Annex I | Oil spill, fire |
| **Liquid Bulk** | Chemicals | Acids, methanol, veg oils | Chemical tanker (stainless steel coated) | MARPOL Annex II, IBC Code | Toxicity, reactivity |
| **Gas** | Liquefied gases | LNG, LPG, ammonia | Gas carrier (Moss / Membrane type) | IGC Code, MARPOL Annex VI | Cryogenic boil-off, explosion |
| **General Cargo (Break-bulk)** | Bagged, baled, drummed | Cement bags, cotton bales, steel coils | Multi-purpose / General cargo ship | Hague-Visby Rules | Handling damage, theft |
| **Neo-Bulk** | Unitized pre-slung | Paper rolls, timber, steel plates | Multi-purpose vessel with cranes | Hague-Visby Rules | Securing failures |
| **Containerized** | FCL / LCL | Manufactured goods, electronics | Container ship (Post-Panamax, ULCV) | IMDG (if haz), CSS Code | Container stack collapse |
| **Reefer (Refrigerated)** | Perishable | Fruits, fish, pharma | Reefer ship / Reefer containers | ATP Agreement | Cold-chain break |
| **Ro-Ro** | Wheeled / self-propelled | Cars, trucks, tractors | Pure car carrier (PCC), Ro-Ro | CSS Code, IMDG (EV battery) | Vehicle fire, lashings |
| **Heavy Lift** | Over-dimensional | Turbines, transformers | Heavy-lift vessel (semi-submersible) | CSS Code, project-specific | Capsize, structural failure |
| **Project Cargo** | Engineered for one consignment | Refinery modules, drilling rigs | Semi-submersible, deck barge | Bespoke engineering | Schedule slippage |
| **Dangerous Goods** | IMDG Classes 1–9 | Explosives, radioactive, batteries | Depends on class — specialised | **IMDG Code (mandatory)** | Contamination, fire, pollution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Students frequently confuse *IMSBC* (for dry solid bulk) with *IMDG* (for packaged dangerous goods). Memorize: **IMSBC = Solid Bulk; IMDG = Packaged Hazardous; IGC = Liquefied Gases; IBC = Bulk Chemicals**.

---

### 2.3 IMDG Dangerous Goods Classes — Mandatory Memorization

Under the **International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code**, hazardous cargoes are divided into **9 classes**. This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

1. **Class 1** — Explosives (sub-divisions 1.1 to 1.6).
2. **Class 2** — Gases (compressed, liquefied, dissolved under pressure).
3. **Class 3** — Flammable liquids.
4. **Class 4** — Flammable solids; substances liable to spontaneous combustion; substances which emit flammable gases on contact with water.
5. **Class 5** — Oxidizing substances and organic peroxides.
6. **Class 6** — Toxic and infectious substances.
7. **Class 7** — Radioactive material.
8. **Class 8** — Corrosives.
9. **Class 9** — Miscellaneous dangerous substances and articles (e.g., lithium batteries, asbestos, dry ice).

---

### 2.4 Cargo Measurement Formulas (Cheat-Sheet)

Although this is a management/law subject, the following **measurement formulas** appear in freight calculations and **must** be memorized.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stowage Factor | $SF = \dfrac{\text{Bulk Volume (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Weight (MT)}}$ | m³/MT | Break-bulk & bulk planning |
| Deadweight Tonnage | $DWT = \text{Displacement} - \text{Lightweight}$ | MT | Vessel capacity check |
| TEU Conversion | $FEU = 2 \times TEU$ | TEU units | Container freight |
| Freight Tonnage (W/M) | $\text{Freight} = \max(W, M) \times \text{Rate}$ | MT or m³ | LCL consolidated cargo |
| Cargo Density Check | $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$ where $M$ is mass and $V$ is volume | MT/m³ | Heavy vs light classification |
| Package Limitation Liability | $L = \max\!\left(666.67 \text{ SDR}, 2 \times \text{GW (kg)}\right)$ | SDR | Hague-Visby claim |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule:** In the formula above, the "max" function is enclosed using `\left(` and `\right)` (NOT vertical bars `\vert x \vert`) to avoid breaking KTU-Premium-Engine markdown tables.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Maritime Law & Shipping

- **Under a Voyage Charter Party**, the description of cargo in the **Recap Box** determines freight, laytime, and demurrage.
- **Under a Time Charter**, owners usually forbid the charterer from carrying **IMDG Class 1 (Explosives)** or **Class 7 (Radioactive)** without express consent.
- **Under a Bill of Lading**, the **"said to contain"** clause shifts cargo-description risk to the shipper, but the carrier remains liable for **misdelivery and dangerous-cargo contamination**.
- **The IMSBC Code (mandatory under SOLAS Chapter VI)** requires a **Bulk Cargo Shipping Name (BCSN)** and a **Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS)** for every dry-bulk shipment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Stowage Factor Determination

**Problem (KTU-style 3-mark):**
A consignment of **iron ore** has a total weight of **50,000 MT** and occupies a stowage volume of **32,500 m³**. Determine its stowage factor and classify it as heavy, medium, or light.

**Step 1 — Identify given data.**
$$M = 50{,}000 \ \text{MT}$$
$$V = 32{,}500 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Stowage Factor formula.**

The stowage factor relates the volume occupied by one metric ton of cargo.

$$SF = \frac{V}{M}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$SF = \frac{32{,}500 \ \text{m}^3}{50{,}000 \ \text{MT}}$$

$$SF = 0.65 \ \text{m}^3/\text{MT}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the classification threshold.**

The shipping industry standard is **SF < 1.4 m³/MT for heavy bulk cargoes**.

$$0.65 < 1.4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Classified as HEAVY BULK}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**
A low SF means each cubic metre weighs a lot — iron ore is **dense, heavy, and requires tight stowage planning** to prevent excessive hull stress concentration. The vessel must be **double-skin bulk carrier** with strong tank-top reinforcement.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Freight Tonnage Under W/M Principle

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):**
A consignment has a **gross weight of 18 MT** and a **measurement of 32 m³**. The freight rate is **USD 75 per W/M ton** (whichever is higher). Compute the freight payable and the W/M differential.

**Step 1 — Understand W/M freight principle.**
Under **W/M (Weight or Measurement)** basis, freight is calculated on whichever is greater:
- Weight tonnes = **18 MT**.
- Measurement tonnes = **32 m³** (since 1 m³ ≈ 1 MT in shipping convention for general cargo).

**Step 2 — Apply the max function.**

$$\text{Freightable Tons} = \max(W, M) = \max(18, 32) = 32$$

**Step 3 — Compute freight.**

$$\text{Freight} = \text{Freightable Tons} \times \text{Rate}$$

$$\text{Freight} = 32 \times 75 = \text{USD } 2{,}400$$

**Step 4 — Calculate W/M differential.**

$$\Delta_{W/M} = M - W = 32 - 18 = 14 \ \text{tons}$$

**Step 5 — Legal interpretation.**
The cargo is classified as **"measurement cargo"** (voluminous but light) — typical of textile bales, furniture, or appliances. The carrier recovers the deadweight opportunity cost via the W/M rule.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — LCL Consolidation & IMDG Segregation

**Problem (KTU-style 7-mark):**
A consolidator combines two consignments in an LCL container:
- **Consignment A:** 12 MT of machinery, 30 m³, IMDG **Class 9** (lithium batteries).
- **Consignment B:** 8 MT of cotton garments, 40 m³, non-hazardous.

The freight rate is **USD 60/W/M**. Container stuffing volume = 60 m³. Determine (a) freight payable, (b) IMDG segregation requirement, (c) Bill-of-Lading issuing practice.

**Part (a) — Freight calculation.**

$$\text{Total Weight} = 12 + 8 = 20 \ \text{MT}$$

$$\text{Total Measurement} = 30 + 40 = 70 \ \text{m}^3$$

$$\text{Freightable Tons} = \max(20, 70) = 70$$

$$\text{Freight} = 70 \ \times 60 = \text{USD } 4{,}200$$

**Part (b) — IMDG segregation.**
Lithium batteries (**Class 9**) require **segregation from foodstuff (Class 0)** but are **acceptable with general cargo (Class 0)** provided:
- Battery terminals are protected against short-circuit.
- Packaging bears **Class 9 diamond label**.
- **Container Dangerous Goods Declaration (DGD)** is filed.

Since Consignment B (cotton garments) is non-hazardous, **no segregation by IMDG code is mandatory**, but the consolidator must still ensure **electrical isolation** of batteries from flammable textiles.

**Part (c) — Bill of Lading practice.**
- **Master B/L** issued by carrier to consolidator.
- **Two House B/Ls** issued by consolidator to each shipper.
- The master B/L must declare the **Class 9 dangerous goods** entry on its face; failure voids the carrier's limitation of liability.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Hague-Visby Package Limitation Calculation

**Problem:**
A package of industrial machinery weighing **2,000 kg** is lost due to carrier negligence. The SDR exchange rate is **USD 1 = 0.75 SDR**. Compute the carrier's maximum liability.

**Step 1 — Apply the dual-criterion rule.**
Under **Article IV Rule 5 of Hague-Visby Rules**, liability is the **higher of**:
- **666.67 SDR per package**, or
- **2 SDR per kilogram of gross weight**.

$$L_1 = 666.67 \ \text{SDR} \quad \text{(per package)}$$

$$L_2 = 2 \times 2{,}000 = 4{,}000 \ \text{SDR} \quad \text{(per kg)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the max function.**

$$L = \max(666.67, 4{,}000) = 4{,}000 \ \text{SDR}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to USD.**

$$L_{USD} = \frac{4{,}000}{0.75} = \text{USD } 5{,}333.33$$

**Step 4 — Legal note.**
For **heavy industrial cargoes**, the per-kg limit almost always exceeds the per-package limit — making Hague-Visby a *de facto* **weight-based liability regime** for industrial shipping.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Cargo Classification Flowchart

The diagram below maps the complete hierarchical classification of maritime cargoes as per KTU 2024 syllabus and IMO codes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Cargo Carried by Sea] --> nodeB{Physical State?}
    nodeB -- Solid --> nodeC{Is it Packaged?}
    nodeB -- Liquid --> nodeD{Is it Hazardous?}
    nodeB -- Gas --> nodeE[Gas Cargo - IGC Code]

    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeF[General Cargo - Break-Bulk]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeG[Bulk Cargo - IMSBC Code]
    nodeF --> nodeH{Needs Cold Chain?}
    nodeH -- Yes --> nodeI[Reefer / Refrigerated Cargo]
    nodeH -- No --> nodeJ[Containerized Cargo - CSS Code]

    nodeD -- Yes --> nodeK[IMDG Dangerous Goods - 9 Classes]
    nodeD -- No --> nodeL[Liquid Bulk - MARPOL Annex I or II]

    nodeG --> nodeM{Heavy or Light by Stowage Factor}
    nodeM -- SF less than 1 point 4 --> nodeN[Heavy Bulk - Iron Ore, Coal]
    nodeM -- SF greater than 1 point 4 --> nodeO[Light Bulk - Grain, Bauxite]

    nodeJ --> nodeP{FCL or LCL}
    nodeP -- FCL --> nodeQ[Full Container Load]
    nodeP -- LCL --> nodeR[Less than Container Load - Consolidation]

    nodeK --> nodeS{Class Number}
    nodeS -- 1 --> nodeT[Explosives]
    nodeS -- 2 --> nodeU[Gases]
    nodeS -- 3 --> nodeV[Flammable Liquids]
    nodeS -- 4 --> nodeW[Flammable Solids]
    nodeS -- 5 --> nodeX[Oxidizers]
    nodeS -- 6 --> nodeY[Toxic Substances]
    nodeS -- 7 --> nodeZ[Radioactive]
    nodeS -- 8 --> nodeAA[Corrosives]
    nodeS -- 9 --> nodeAB[Miscellaneous - Lithium Batteries]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Vessel Selection Logic per Cargo Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeShipType[Select Vessel Type] --> nodeCargoType{Cargo Type Decision}
    nodeCargoType -- Iron Ore / Coal --> nodeBulkVessel[Bulk Carrier - Capesize or Panamax]
    nodeCargoType -- Crude Oil --> nodeTanker[VLCC Tanker - Double Hull]
    nodeCargoType -- LNG --> nodeGasCarrier[Moss or Membrane LNG Carrier]
    nodeCargoType -- Cars --> nodeRoRoVessel[PCC Pure Car Carrier]
    nodeCargoType -- Containers --> nodeContainerShip[Post-Panamax or ULCV]
    nodeCargoType -- Heavy Lift --> nodeHeavyVessel[Semi-Submersible Heavy Lift]
    nodeCargoType -- Chemicals --> nodeChemTanker[Chemical Tanker - Coated]
    nodeCargoType -- Perishable --> nodeReeferVessel[Reefer Ship or Reefer Container]
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Cargo Handling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStep1[Shipper Tenders Cargo Description] --> nodeStep2[Booking with Carrier]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3{Cargo Type Identified}
    nodeStep3 -- Hazardous --> nodeStep4[File IMDG DGD with Carrier]
    nodeStep3 -- Bulk --> nodeStep5[Submit IMSBC Shipper Declaration]
    nodeStep3 -- General --> nodeStep6[Standard Booking]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep7[Vessel Selection per IMDG Code]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep8[Vessel Selection per IMSBC Code]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep9[Standard Container or Break-Bulk Allocation]
    nodeStep7 --> nodeStep10[Bill of Lading Drafted with Haz Declaration]
    nodeStep8 --> nodeStep11[Bill of Lading Drafted with BCSN]
    nodeStep9 --> nodeStep12[Standard Bill of Lading Issued]
    nodeStep10 --> nodeStep13[Loading and Stowage Plan Finalized]
    nodeStep11 --> nodeStep13
    nodeStep12 --> nodeStep13
    nodeStep13 --> nodeStep14[Voyage - Hague-Visby Rules Apply]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

> Define "Dry Bulk Cargo" and state **three** examples. Name the IMO Code that governs its safe carriage.

**Model Answer (3 marks — Board-style):**

Dry Bulk Cargo refers to homogeneous, unpackaged solid cargoes loaded directly into the ship's holds without any intermediate packaging. Examples: **iron ore, coal, and grain (wheat)**.

The governing IMO Code is the **International Maritime Solid Bulk Cargoes (IMSBC) Code**, mandatory under **SOLAS Chapter VI, Regulation 2**. The Code requires a **Bulk Cargo Shipping Name (BCSN)** and a **Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS)** for every shipment, and groups cargoes into three categories: **Group A (liable to liquefy), Group B (chemical hazard), and Group C (neither A nor B)**.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for three examples, 1 mark for IMSBC Code + categorization.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> What is **IMDG Code**? List its **nine classes** of dangerous goods.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code** is the international regulation governing the **packaged** transport of hazardous materials by sea, published by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**. It is legally binding under **SOLAS Chapter VII**.

**Nine Classes:**
1. Class 1 — Explosives.
2. Class 2 — Gases.
3. Class 3 — Flammable Liquids.
4. Class 4 — Flammable Solids, Spontaneously Combustible, Dangerous When Wet.
5. Class 5 — Oxidizing Substances and Organic Peroxides.
6. Class 6 — Toxic and Infectious Substances.
7. Class 7 — Radioactive Material.
8. Class 8 — Corrosives.
9. Class 9 — Miscellaneous Dangerous Substances (e.g., lithium batteries).

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for IMDG definition + SOLAS reference, 2 marks for nine classes.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Dec 2024 Model**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of cargoes in maritime shipping**, covering dry bulk, liquid bulk, containerized, break-bulk, reefer, Ro-Ro, and project cargo. For each category, state the typical vessel and one applicable IMO regulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipper tenders **30,000 MT of coal** with a bulk stowage volume of **37,500 m³**. The freight rate agreed is **USD 12 per MT on W/M basis** (whichever is higher). Compute the freight payable and classify the cargo by stowage factor. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):** *(7 marks — Examiner's distribution)*
*(1 mark per cargo type — 7 categories = 7 marks)*

| **Cargo Category** | **Typical Vessel** | **Applicable IMO Code** |
|---|---|---|
| Dry Bulk (iron ore, coal) | Capesize / Panamax bulk carrier | **IMSBC Code** |
| Liquid Bulk (crude oil) | VLCC / Aframax tanker | **MARPOL Annex I** |
| Containerized | Container ship (ULCV) | **CSS Code (Container Safe Stowage)** |
| Break-bulk / General | Multi-purpose vessel | **Hague-Visby Rules** |
| Reefer | Reefer ship or reefer container | **ATP Agreement** |
| Ro-Ro | Pure Car Carrier (PCC) | **CSS Code** |
| Project / Heavy Lift | Semi-submersible vessel | **Specialised engineering code** |

**Examiner's Notes:**
- Mention **IMSBC Group A (liquefying cargoes)** like nickel ore and iron ore fines for full marks.
- Distinguish **dry bulk vs wet bulk** clearly.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Given data.**
$$M = 30{,}000 \ \text{MT}$$
$$V = 37{,}500 \ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Stowage Factor calculation.**
$$SF = \frac{V}{M} = \frac{37{,}500}{30{,}000} = 1.25 \ \text{m}^3/\text{MT}$$

**Step 3 — Classification.**
Since **SF = 1.25 < 1.4**, the cargo is **HEAVY BULK**.

**Step 4 — Freightable tons under W/M.**
$$\text{Weight Tons} = 30{,}000 \ \text{MT}$$
$$\text{Measurement Tons} = 37{,}500 \ \text{m}^3 \approx 37{,}500 \ \text{MT}$$

**Step 5 — Apply max function.**
$$\text{Freightable Tons} = \max(30{,}000, 37{,}500) = 37{,}500 \ \text{MT}$$

**Step 6 — Compute freight.**
$$\text{Freight} = 37{,}500 \times 12 = \text{USD } 450{,}000$$

**Final Answer:** **Freight = USD 450,000**, Cargo is **Heavy Bulk**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **IMDG Code** in detail. Explain its nine classes of dangerous goods and the legal consequences of misdeclaring a hazardous cargo on board a vessel. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **Hague-Visby Rules**, explain the concept of **package limitation**. A consignment of **500 packages**, each weighing **750 kg**, is destroyed by a fire caused by the carrier's negligence. The applicable SDR rate is **1 SDR = USD 1.30**. Compute the maximum recoverable compensation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):** *(7 marks)*

The **International Maritime Dangerous Goods (IMDG) Code**, developed by the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)**, governs the **packaged transport** of hazardous materials by sea. It is mandated under **SOLAS Chapter VII** and enforced through the **Container/Vehicle Packing Certificate (CTU Code)**.

**Nine classes** — *(1 mark for naming all 9 classes; awarded only if all are listed)*:

| **Class** | **Description** |
|---|---|
| 1 | Explosives |
| 2 | Gases |
| 3 | Flammable liquids |
| 4 | Flammable solids, spontaneously combustible, dangerous when wet |
| 5 | Oxidizing substances and organic peroxides |
| 6 | Toxic and infectious substances |
| 7 | Radioactive material |
| 8 | Corrosives |
| 9 | Miscellaneous dangerous substances (e.g., lithium batteries) |

**Legal consequences of misdeclaration:**
- **Absolute strict liability** under Article 4(3) of the Hague-Visby Rules: the shipper is liable for **all loss, damage, or expense** resulting from misdeclared dangerous goods.
- **Carrier's right to discharge, destroy, or render harmless** the misdeclared goods at the shipper's risk and expense.
- **Criminal prosecution** under the **Indian Ports Act, 1908** and **EPA, 1986** for environmental damage.
- **Civil suit** by the carrier against the shipper for **general average contribution** if other cargoes are damaged.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Given data.**
$$N = 500 \ \text{packages}$$
$$W_{\text{per pkg}} = 750 \ \text{kg}$$
$$\text{Total Weight} = 500 \times 750 = 375{,}000 \ \text{kg}$$
$$\text{SDR/USD} = 1 \ \text{SDR} = \text{USD } 1.30$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hague-Visby dual-criterion.**

Criterion 1 — **Per package limit:**
$$L_1 = 666.67 \ \text{SDR/package}$$

Criterion 2 — **Per kg limit:**
$$L_2 = 2 \times \text{GW (kg)} = 2 \times 375{,}000 = 750{,}000 \ \text{SDR}$$

**Step 3 — Apply max function across all packages.**

$$L = \max(L_1 \times N, L_2)$$

$$L = \max(666.67 \times 500, 750{,}000)$$

$$L = \max(333{,}335, 750{,}000) = 750{,}000 \ \text{SDR}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to USD.**

$$L_{USD} = 750{,}000 \times 1.30 = \text{USD } 975{,}000$$

**Final Answer:** **Maximum recoverable compensation = USD 975,000** *(since carrier was negligent, Article IV Rule 5 limitations do NOT apply under Article III Rule 8 — full actual loss is recoverable).*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examiner's Note:** If the carrier was **negligent** (fire caused by carrier's fault), the carrier **forfeits limitation of liability** under **Hague-Visby Article III Rule 8** and is liable for the **full actual loss**, not the capped 750,000 SDR. Students who fail to mention this lose **2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing IMSBC with IMDG** — IMSBC governs **unpackaged solid bulk**; IMDG governs **packaged hazardous cargo**. Examiners dock **1 full mark** for this swap.
> 2. **Forgetting SOLAS Chapter VI for IMSBC and Chapter VII for IMDG** — Examiners expect the SOLAS reference.
> 3. **Using the WRONG limit in Hague-Visby** — Students often quote **2 SDR/kg** or **666.67 SDR/package** without applying the **max function**. Always state the dual-criterion and pick the **higher** value.
> 4. **Ignoring negligence forfeiture** — Under Article III Rule 8, negligent carriers lose the right to limit. Forgetting this in a problem where the carrier is at fault costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Wrong Stowage Factor threshold** — Use **1.4 m³/MT** (industry standard), NOT 1.0. Examiners penalize wrong threshold by **1 mark**.
> 6. **Skipping the "max" function in W/M freight** — Always show: $\max(W, M) \times \text{Rate}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cargo** = goods carried under a Bill of Lading or Charter Party; classified by physical state, packaging, hazard class, stowage factor, and handling mode.
- **Five major cargo families**: Dry Bulk, Liquid Bulk, Gas, General (Break-bulk & Containerized), and Specialised (Reefer, Ro-Ro, Heavy Lift).
- **Key IMO Codes (memorize verbatim):**
  - **IMSBC Code** → Dry solid bulk (SOLAS Ch VI).
  - **IMDG Code** → Packaged hazardous goods, 9 classes (SOLAS Ch VII).
  - **IGC Code** → Liquefied gases (LNG/LPG carriers).
  - **IBC Code** → Bulk liquid chemicals (chemical tankers).
  - **MARPOL Annex I** → Oil; **Annex II** → Chemicals in bulk; **Annex VI** → Air pollution.
- **Stowage Factor (SF) classification**: **SF < 1.4** = Heavy; **SF > 1.4** = Light. Formula: $SF = V/M$.
- **W/M freight rule**: freight = $\max(W, M) \times \text{Rate}$.
- **Hague-Visby Package Limitation**: $\max(666.67 \ \text{SDR/pkg}, 2 \ \text{SDR/kg})$.
- **Negligence forfeiture**: Article III Rule 8 strips the carrier of limitation when loss is caused by carrier's fault.
- **LCL vs FCL**: LCL = consolidated, multiple shippers, multiple House B/Ls; FCL = one shipper, one B/L.
- **Container Safe Stowage (CSS) Code** governs container stowage/securing on board.
- **ATP Agreement** governs perishable refrigerated carriage.
- **Always cite the SOLAS Chapter** when naming an IMO Code in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of ships which carry them
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of ships which carry them

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **Maritime Law and Shipping Management**, the term "**Types of ships which carry them**" refers to the **statutory and commercial classification of vessels** designed, constructed, certified, and registered for the carriage of specific categories of cargo, passengers, or both, as governed by international conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, UNCLOS, MLC 2006) and the **Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** (as amended). The classification determines the applicable **safety regulations, liability frameworks, charter party terms, and documentation requirements**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Ship classification is not merely a technical or design-based taxonomy; under maritime law, the **type of ship dictates the legal regime** that applies to bills of lading, charter parties, insurance (H&M, P&I), limitation of liability, and the **Hague-Visby Rules / Hamburg Rules** applicability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Cross-Reference:** This topic directly maps to **CO3** – *Comprehend the regulatory framework governing carriage of goods and passengers by sea* — and aligns with **Bloom's Level: Understand (L2) and Apply (L3)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **postal system**: the **Post Office** is the maritime industry, and **letters, parcels, fragile items, liquids, refrigerated food, and people** are all the "cargoes." Just as the postal service uses **different vehicles** — envelopes, cardboard boxes, insulated coolers, tankers, and passenger buses — the shipping industry uses **specialized vessels** because **oil cannot be transported in a cruise liner, and grain cannot be loaded onto a chemical tanker** without catastrophic legal, safety, and commercial consequences.

- A **Bulk Carrier** is the **"dump truck of the sea"** — built for loose, dry, homogeneous cargo (coal, iron ore, grain).
- A **Tanker** is the **"pipeline of the ocean"** — built for liquid bulk (crude, chemicals, gas).
- A **Container Ship** is the **"delivery van of the sea"** — standardized boxes stacked like Lego.
- A **Passenger Ship** is the **"floating hotel"** — built for human comfort and safety under the strictest regime.

Each vessel type carries with it a **unique legal personality** in the eyes of admiralty courts, classification societies (Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, IRS), and insurers.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Always remember the *mnemonic* — **"D-D-G-B-C-P"**: **D**ry bulk, **D**angerous goods, **G**as, **B**reak-bulk, **C**ontainer, **P**assenger. This covers ~95% of KTU exam answers.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Tonnage Metrics Every KTU Student MUST Know:**

| Metric | Definition | Statutory Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **GT (Gross Tonnage)** | Total enclosed volume of a vessel | IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention |
| **NT (Net Tonnage)** | Usable cargo/passenger volume | IMO 1969 Tonnage Convention |
| **DWT (Deadweight Tonnage)** | Total weight a ship can carry (cargo + fuel + water + stores) | Shipping industry standard |
| **TEU (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit)** | Standard container measurement (20 ft × 8 ft × 8.5 ft) | ISO 668 |

---

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Statutory Anchor Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing Legal Instruments for Vessel Type Classification:**
> 1. **International Convention for the Safety of Life at Sea (SOLAS), 1974** — Chapter II-1, II-2, VII (carriage of dangerous goods).
> 2. **International Convention for the Prevention of Pollution from Ships (MARPOL), 73/78** — Annex I (oil), II (noxious liquids), III (packaged goods), IV (sewage), V (garbage), VI (air pollution).
> 3. **Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** — Sections 3, 333, 352, 356.
> 4. **UNCLOS, 1982** — Articles 91–98 (flag state, nationality, right of innocent passage).
> 5. **MLC 2006** — Maritime Labour Convention (for passenger/crew vessels).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FRAMEWORK

## 2.1 Comprehensive Vessel Classification Framework

Ships are classified along **three orthogonal axes** in KTU 2024 pedagogy:

### Axis A: By Cargo Carried (The "What")
1. **Liquid Bulk Carriers (Tankers)**
2. **Dry Bulk Carriers**
3. **General Cargo / Break-Bulk Carriers**
4. **Container Ships**
5. **Roll-on/Roll-off (Ro-Ro) Ships**
6. **Reefer (Refrigerated) Ships**
7. **LNG / LPG Carriers**
8. **Chemical Tankers**
9. **Livestock Carriers**
10. **Heavy-Lift / Project Cargo Ships**

### Axis B: By Voyage Purpose (The "Why")
1. **Tramp Ships** — voyage charter, no fixed route (e.g., bulk carriers)
2. **Liner Ships** — scheduled routes (e.g., container ships on Asia-Europe)
3. **Industrial Ships** — dedicated to one charterer (e.g., oil majors' tankers)

### Axis C: By Passenger Status
1. **Passenger Ships** (Carrying >12 passengers under SOLAS)
2. **Cargo Ships** (Carrying ≤12 passengers)
3. **Special Purpose Ships** (SPS Code — e.g., research, tender vessels)

---

## 2.2 Detailed Type-by-Type Analysis

### 2.2.1 Oil Tankers (Crude & Product)

- **Cargo:** Crude oil, refined petroleum products (diesel, gasoline, jet fuel).
- **Design:** Double-hull mandatory under **MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 19** (post-Erika, post-Prestige).
- **Sub-Types:**
  * **VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier):** 200,000–320,000 DWT.
  * **Suezmax:** 120,000–200,000 DWT.
  * **Aframax:** 80,000–120,000 DWT.
  * **Panamax / Handysize Product Tankers:** 10,000–60,000 DWT.
- **Legal Regime:** **Hague-Visby Rules** for bills of lading; **TOVALOP / CLC 1969 / Fund Convention 1992** for oil pollution liability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Type Matters:** The **Prestige (2002)** and **Erika (1999)** disasters directly led to **EU Regulation 417/2002** and the **acceleration of the double-hull phase-out for single-hull tankers**, illustrating how vessel type triggers specific mandatory retrofit regulations.

---

### 2.2.2 Bulk Carriers (Dry Bulk)

- **Cargo:** Iron ore, coal, grain, bauxite, cement, sugar (homogeneous, unpackaged).
- **Design:** Single deck, large cargo holds with hatches, no self-loading gear (except Handysize).
- **Critical Statutory Concern:** **SOLAS Chapter XII** — additional safety measures for **bulk carriers** (due to historical loss rate).
- **Key Risk:** **Structural failure due to cargo liquefaction** (e.g., nickel ore, iron ore fines) — regulated under **IMSBC Code**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse a *Bulk Carrier* with a *General Cargo Ship*. Bulk carriers carry **unpackaged, homogeneous** cargo in **single voyages**, whereas general cargo ships carry **varied, packaged** goods.

---

### 2.2.3 Container Ships

- **Cargo:** Standardized ISO containers (20 ft, 40 ft, 45 ft, reefer containers).
- **Generations:** 1st gen (1,500 TEU) → 24,000+ TEU (e.g., MSC Irina class).
- **Legal Regime:** **Hague-Visby Rules** apply; **UCP 600** governs the **Bill of Lading** as a transport document and collateral security.
- **Specialized Sub-Types:**
  * **Feeder ships** (300–1,000 TEU, regional)
  * **Panamax / Post-Panamax / New-Panamax** (can/cannot transit Panama Canal)
  * **ULCV (Ultra Large Container Vessel)** >14,000 TEU

---

### 2.2.4 LNG / LPG Carriers (Gas Carriers)

- **Cargo:** Liquefied Natural Gas (-162°C) or Liquefied Petroleum Gas.
- **Design:**
  * **Moss (spherical) tanks** — independent prismatic type.
  * **Membrane tanks** — GTL (Gaztransport & Technigaz) and CS1 (Technigaz Mark III).
- **Statutory Code:** **IGC Code (International Code for the Construction and Equipment of Ships carrying Liquefied Gases in Bulk)** — mandatory under SOLAS Chapter VII.
- **Liability:** **HNS Convention 1996** (Hazardous and Noxious Substances) for damage.

---

### 2.2.5 Chemical Tankers

- **Cargo:** Organic and inorganic chemicals (acids, alcohols, vegetable oils).
- **Statutory Code:** **IBC Code (International Bulk Chemical Code)** + **BCH Code** (older ships).
- **Tank Coating:** Stainless steel, zinc silicate, epoxy — varies by chemical compatibility.
- **KTU Exam Hook:** Often tested under **"Type of ship determines the Carriage of Dangerous Goods regime."**

---

### 2.2.6 Ro-Ro Ships (Roll-on / Roll-off)

- **Cargo:** Cars, trucks, trailers, military vehicles.
- **Design:** Bow/stern doors, internal ramps, multiple decks (PCC — Pure Car Carrier; PCTC — Pure Car and Truck Carrier).
- **Statutory Concern:** **SOLAS Reg. II-1/40-45** — vehicle deck fire protection, fixed fire-extinguishing systems (CO₂, water mist).

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The **Feldwebel Gerhard Lübsen (2019)** Ro-Ro fire highlighted the catastrophic risk of carrying electric vehicles (EVs) — a current KTU-relevant regulatory debate under **MSC.1/Circ.1622** (Lithium Battery Fires).

---

### 2.2.7 Passenger Ships & Cruise Liners

- **Definition under SOLAS:** A ship carrying **more than 12 passengers** (Section II-1/2(g), SOLAS 1974).
- **Statutory Regime:**
  * **SOLAS Chapter III** (Life-Saving Appliances)
  * **MLC 2006** (crew conditions)
  * **Athens Convention 1974 / 2002 Protocol** (Passenger Liability — limit ~SDR 250,000 per passenger for death/personal injury).
- **Indian Law:** **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958, Part XA** — Carriage of Passengers.

---

### 2.2.8 Refrigerated Ships (Reefers)

- **Cargo:** Perishables (meat, fish, fruits, dairy).
- **Design:** Insulated holds with refrigeration plants.
- **Modern Trend:** Reefer containers on conventional container ships have largely replaced dedicated reefer ships.

---

### 2.2.9 General Cargo / Multi-Purpose / Heavy-Lift Ships

- **General Cargo:** Break-bulk, bagged, baled, drummed goods.
- **Multi-Purpose:** Combination of bulk, container, and heavy-lift capability.
- **Heavy-Lift:** Onboard cranes, strengthened decks, capable of lifting 500–20,000+ tonnes (e.g., vessels for offshore platform modules).

---

## 2.3 KTU 2024 Formula / Classification Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comprehensive Vessel-Type vs. Cargo Matrix (Examiner-Ready Table):**

| Ship Type | Cargo Carried | Governing Code / Convention | Liability Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oil Tanker | Crude & petroleum | MARPOL Annex I, CLC 1969, Fund Conv. 1992 | Strict liability of shipowner (CLC) |
| Chemical Tanker | Bulk chemicals | IBC Code, MARPOL Annex II | Hague-Visby + HNS 1996 |
| LNG/LPG Carrier | Liquefied gases | IGC Code, SOLAS Ch. VII | Hague-Visby + HNS 1996 |
| Dry Bulk Carrier | Ore, coal, grain | SOLAS Ch. XII, IMSBC Code | Hague-Visby |
| Container Ship | ISO containers | SOLAS Ch. VII (DCT), IMDG | Hague-Visby / Hamburg Rules |
| General Cargo | Break-bulk, bags | SOLAS Ch. II-1, II-2 | Hague-Visby |
| Ro-Ro | Vehicles, wheeled cargo | SOLAS Reg. II-1/40-45 | Hague-Visby |
| Passenger Ship | >12 passengers | SOLAS Ch. III, MLC 2006, Athens Conv. | Athens Convention (SDR 250,000 limit) |
| Reefer | Perishables | SOLAS Ch. III (relevant LSA) | Hague-Visby |
| Heavy-Lift / Project | Oversized cargo | SOLAS Ch. VII, CSS Code | Hague-Visby |

> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Since maritime law is non-quantitative, the "formulas" are replaced by **statutory articles, conventions, and section references** — the **legal equivalents of equations** in this discipline.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Management Fields

1. **Ship Finance & Mortgage Law:** Vessel type determines the **"Permissible Mortgage"** scope under **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958, Part V**.
2. **Marine Insurance (P&I Clubs):** Premiums, deductibles, and **"P&I cover"** eligibility (e.g., Gard, UK P&I, Steamship Mutual) differ sharply by vessel type.
3. **Port State Control (PSC):** **Paris MoU, Tokyo MoU, Indian MoU** prioritize inspections based on **vessel type and age risk profile** (e.g., older bulk carriers, tankers are "high-risk").
4. **Flag State Administration:** Indian vessels (Classed by **IRS — Indian Register of Shipping**) must comply with type-specific survey cycles.
5. **Charter Party Selection:** Voyage, time, and demise charter — terms differ by ship type (e.g., **Shellvoy 6** for tankers, **Gencon 94** for general cargo, **NYPE 2015** for time charter).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK & LEGAL DERIVATION

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodological Note:** Since this is a **Law + Management** course (PENST863), the "derivations" are **statutory logic chains** and **legal syllogisms**, not algebraic equations. Each step below mirrors a "deduction" in legal reasoning.

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Legal Syllogism: "Identifying the Applicable Law from Ship Type"

> **Step 1 — Identify the Vessel Type (Major Premise)**
>
> The student must first classify the ship as one of: *Tanker, Bulk Carrier, Container Ship, Passenger Ship, Ro-Ro, LNG Carrier, General Cargo, Reefer, or Heavy-Lift.*

> **Step 2 — Identify the Cargo (Minor Premise)**
>
> Determine the *physical state* (solid, liquid, gas), *hazard class* (IMDG Class 1–9), and *quantity* (bulk, packaged).

> **Step 3 — Map to the Applicable Code (Statutory Deduction)**
>
> Apply the *IF-THEN* legal rule:
> - IF *Tanker + Liquid Bulk + Persistent Oil* → THEN apply **MARPOL Annex I + CLC 1969/1992**.
> - IF *Chemical Tanker + Noxious Liquid* → THEN apply **MARPOL Annex II + IBC Code**.
> - IF *Gas Carrier + Liquefied Gas* → THEN apply **IGC Code + SOLAS Ch. VII**.
> - IF *Dry Bulk + Solid Homogeneous* → THEN apply **SOLAS Ch. XII + IMSBC Code**.
> - IF *Passenger Ship + >12 pax* → THEN apply **SOLAS Ch. III + MLC 2006 + Athens Convention**.

> **Step 4 — Apply the Liability Framework (Conclusion)**
>
> Determine the *limitation of liability*, *carrier's burden of proof*, and *mandatory insurance* based on Step 3.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Legal Reasoning Chain (KTU-Style)

**Case Fact:** *The vessel "MV Coral Star," a chemical tanker of 25,000 DWT, is loading 18,000 MT of phenol in Mumbai port. During loading, a tank overflow occurs, and phenol enters the harbor waters. Adjacent fishing boats suffer damage. Determine the legal regime, the polluter's liability, and the applicable limitation amount.*

### Step 1 — Identify Vessel Type
MV Coral Star is a **Chemical Tanker** (dedicated IMO Type 2/3 vessel).

### Step 2 — Identify Cargo
**Phenol (C₆H₅OH)** — a **toxic, persistent, Noxious Liquid Substance (NLS)** classified under **MARPOL Annex II, Cat. Y** (or X, depending on grade).

### Step 3 — Applicable Code Mapping

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Chemical Tanker} \;\land\; \text{NLS Cargo} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{MARPOL Annex II} \\
&\text{AND} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{IBC Code (construction standards)} \\
&\text{AND} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{HNS Convention 1996 (liability for hazardous \& noxious substances)} \\
&\text{AND (Bunkers)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Bunker Convention 2001} \\
&\text{AND (Bill of Lading)} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Hague-Visby Rules (if COA)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Liability Determination
- The **shipowner is strictly liable** for pollution damage under **HNS Convention 1996** (Article 7) once the Convention enters into force for the flag State.
- Until HNS enters into force, the matter is governed by **national law** (e.g., **Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958, Part XIB** for civil liability).
- **Limitation amount** under HNS 1996 = calculated per the **tonnage formula** similar to CLC 1992:

$$
\text{Limit (SDR)} = \text{flat amount} + \text{per GRT incremental amount}
$$

For a 25,000 GRT chemical tanker, the limit would be approximately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{HNS}} &\approx (\text{flat base for NLS tankers}) + (\text{per-tonne increment}) \\
&\approx 10 \text{ million SDR (base)} + (25{,}000 \times 1{,}500 \text{ SDR}) \\
&\approx 10\text{M} + 37.5\text{M} \text{ SDR} \\
&\approx 47.5 \text{ million SDR (ceiling for NLS tankers)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note:** The exact SDR figures are **tiered and convention-specific**; the KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to cite the **formula structure**, not memorize the exact tonnage bands. Reference: **HNS Convention 1996, Article 9**.

### Step 5 — Conclusion (Model Answer Framework)
> The **applicable legal regime** is **MARPOL Annex II + IBC Code (technical) and HNS Convention 1996 / Indian Merchant Shipping Act Part XIB (civil liability)**. The **shipowner is strictly liable**, subject to the **compulsory insurance certificate** requirement under HNS Article 12, and the **limitation of liability is calculated per tonnage-based SDR formula** capped at the convention ceiling.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Liability Table — Why Ship Type Matters

| Ship Type | Strict Liability? | Compulsory Insurance? | Convention Cap on Liability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Oil Tanker | **Yes** (CLC) | **Yes** (Blue Card) | ~89.77 million SDR (CLC 1992) |
| Chemical Tanker | **Yes** (HNS, post-entry) | **Yes** (HNS, post-entry) | ~120 million SDR ceiling |
| Bulk Carrier | **No** (Fault-based) | **No** (Voluntary) | Per Hague-Visby Art. IV(5): 666.67 SDR/package or 2 SDR/kg |
| Container Ship | **No** (Fault-based) | **No** (Voluntary) | Same Hague-Visby limit |
| Passenger Ship | **Yes** (Athens Conv.) | **Yes** (Blue Card equivalent) | 250,000 SDR/passenger (death/injury) |
| LNG/LPG | **Yes** (HNS) | **Yes** (HNS) | Per HNS tiers |

---

## 3.4 Charter Party Linkage (Sub-Derivation)

The **type of ship determines the standard charter party form**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{If} \; \text{Ship Type} = \text{Tanker} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Charter} \in \{\text{Shellvoy 6, BPVOY 5, Asbatankvoy}\} \\
&\text{If} \; \text{Ship Type} = \text{General Cargo} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Charter} \in \{\text{Gencon 94, Gencon 2016}\} \\
&\text{If} \; \text{Ship Type} = \text{Bulk Carrier} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Charter} \in \{\text{NYPE 2015 (time), GENCON, BALTIME 2001\}} \\
&\text{If} \; \text{Ship Type} = \text{Passenger} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Contract} = \text{Ticket + Athens Convention regime}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **"legal supply chain"** — ship type → governing code → liability regime → charter form → insurance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Vessel Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Maritime Vessels] --> nodeB[Cargo Carriers]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Passenger Carriers]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Special Purpose Ships]

    nodeB --> nodeE[Liquid Bulk Tankers]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Dry Bulk Carriers]
    nodeB --> nodeG[General Cargo Ships]
    nodeB --> nodeH[Container Ships]
    nodeB --> nodeI[Gas Carriers]
    nodeB --> nodeJ[Ro-Ro Vessels]
    nodeB --> nodeK[Reefer Ships]
    nodeB --> nodeL[Heavy Lift Project Ships]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Crude Oil Tankers]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Product Tankers]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Chemical Tankers]

    nodeE1 --> nodeE1a[VLCC 200k to 320k DWT]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE1b[Suezmax 120k to 200k DWT]
    nodeE1 --> nodeE1c[Aframax 80k to 120k DWT]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[Capesize 100k plus DWT]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Panamax 60k to 80k DWT]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[Handymax 35k to 60k DWT]
    nodeF --> nodeF4[Handysize 10k to 35k DWT]

    nodeG --> nodeG1[Single Deck General Cargo]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[Multi Purpose Vessels]
    nodeG --> nodeG3[Heavy Lift Vessels]

    nodeH --> nodeH1[Feeder 300 to 1000 TEU]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[Panamax 3000 to 5000 TEU]
    nodeH --> nodeH3[Post Panamax 5000 to 10000 TEU]
    nodeH --> nodeH4[ULCV 14000 plus TEU]

    nodeI --> nodeI1[LNG Carriers]
    nodeI --> nodeI2[LPG Carriers]

    nodeJ --> nodeJ1[PCC Pure Car Carrier]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ2[PCTC Pure Car and Truck Carrier]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ3[ConRo Container RoRo Hybrid]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Cruise Liners]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Ferries]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Ocean Liners Historic]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Research Vessels]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Tugboats and Tenders]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Offshore Support Vessels]
    nodeD --> nodeD4[Drilling Rigs MODU]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Legal Regime Flow (Ship Type → Applicable Law)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeShip[Identify Ship Type] --> nodeCargo[Identify Cargo]
    nodeCargo --> nodeCode[Map to Statutory Code]
    nodeCode --> nodeLiab[Determine Liability Regime]
    nodeLiab --> nodeCharter[Apply Charter Party]
    nodeCharter --> nodeIns[Apply Insurance Cover]

    nodeShip --> code1[Tanker] 
    nodeShip --> code2[Bulk Carrier]
    nodeShip --> code3[Container Ship]
    nodeShip --> code4[Passenger Ship]
    nodeShip --> code5[Gas Carrier]
    nodeShip --> code6[RoRo Ship]

    code1 --> l1[MARPOL Annex I plus CLC 1992 plus Fund Convention]
    code2 --> l2[SOLAS Chapter XII plus IMSBC plus Hague Visby]
    code3 --> l3[Hague Visby plus IMDG plus UCP 600]
    code4 --> l4[SOLAS Chapter III plus MLC 2006 plus Athens Convention]
    code5 --> l5[IGC Code plus SOLAS Chapter VII plus HNS Convention]
    code6 --> l6[SOLAS Reg II 1 40 to 45 plus Hague Visby]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Risk Profile by Ship Type (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    riskA[Ship Type Risk Profile] --> riskB[Cargo Risk]
    riskA --> riskC[Operational Risk]
    riskA --> riskD[Pollution Risk]
    riskA --> riskE[Structural Risk]

    riskB --> riskB1[Tanker Highest due to flammability]
    riskB --> riskB2[Chemical Tanker High due to toxicity]
    riskB --> riskB3[LNG Highest due to cryogenic and flammable]
    riskB --> riskB4[RoRo High due to EV fire and vehicle deck risk]
    riskB --> riskB5[Bulk Carrier Medium due to cargo shift and liquefaction]
    riskB --> riskB6[Container Medium due to IMDG declared goods]
    riskB --> riskB7[Passenger Highest due to human life]

    riskC --> riskC1[Tramp Service High due to voyage variation]
    riskC --> riskC2[Liner Service Medium due to fixed schedule]

    riskD --> riskD1[Oil Tanker Major]
    riskD --> riskD2[Chemical Tanker Major]
    riskD --> riskD3[Gas Carrier Major]
    riskD --> riskD4[Container Minor unless IMDG]
    riskD --> riskD5[Bulk Carrier Negligible if dry]

    riskE --> riskE1[Bulk Carrier Critical due to hold collapse history]
    riskE --> riskE2[Container Moderate due to stack stress]
    riskE --> riskE3[Tanker Low due to double hull mandate]
```

                            |
                            v
