<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between a Bulk Carrier and a Tanker in terms of cargo carried and applicable law."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
A **Bulk Carrier** carries **dry, homogeneous, unpackaged cargo** such as coal, iron ore, and grain, and is governed by **SOLAS Chapter XII** and the **IMSBC Code**, with liability under the **Hague-Visby Rules**. A **Tanker**, in contrast, carries **liquid bulk cargo** (crude oil, petroleum products), is regulated by **MARPOL Annex I**, the **IBC Code** (for chemicals), and the **CLC 1992** and **HNS Convention** for pollution and damage liability. **(3 marks: 1 for cargo distinction + 1 for code + 1 for liability regime)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Why is a chemical tanker subject to a stricter regulatory regime than a general cargo ship?"**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
A **chemical tanker** carries **noxious, toxic, or corrosive bulk liquids** that pose risks of **severe pollution, fire, and human health hazards**, necessitating compliance with the **IBC Code** (construction, segregation, tank coating), **MARPOL Annex II** (noxious liquid substances), and the **HNS Convention 1996** for strict civil liability and compulsory insurance. A general cargo ship, carrying bagged or packaged dry goods, faces fewer inherent hazards and is governed by more general SOLAS provisions and the Hague-Visby Rules. The **stricter regime is justified by the cargo's toxicity and pollution potential**. **(3 marks: 1 risk + 1 code + 1 justification)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Instructions:** *Answer any ONE full question. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**"With reference to international maritime conventions, classify the major types of ships that carry cargo by sea. Discuss in detail the special features, applicable conventions, and liability regimes governing (a) Oil Tankers and (b) Passenger Ships."** (14 Marks)

#### Sub-Part (a) — Oil Tankers (7 Marks)

**1. Classification (1.5 Marks):**
- Crude carriers (VLCC, Suezmax, Aframax) and product tankers.
- Chemical tankers (sub-class governed by IBC Code).
- LNG/LPG carriers (sub-class governed by IGC Code).

**2. Design & Construction Features (1.5 Marks):**
- Double-hull mandatory under MARPOL Annex I, Reg. 19 (post-2005 phase-out for single-hull).
- Segregated ballast tanks, slop tanks, inert gas systems, COW (Crude Oil Washing).

**3. Applicable Conventions (2 Marks):**
- SOLAS Chapter II-1 (subdivision & stability), Chapter II-2 (fire protection).
- MARPOL Annex I (oil pollution prevention).
- CLC 1969/1992 + Fund Convention 1992.
- Bunker Convention 2001.

**4. Liability Regime (2 Marks):**
- **Strict liability** of the shipowner under CLC 1992 (Article III).
- Compulsory insurance + **Blue Card** from a P&I Club (Article VII).
- Liability cap: ~**89.77 million SDR** (CLC 1992 ceiling) for a VLCC.
- Time bar: 3 years from incident (Article VIII).

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — Passenger Ships (7 Marks)

**1. Definition under SOLAS (1 Mark):**
- A ship carrying **more than 12 passengers** for reward (Rule II-1/2(g), SOLAS 1974).

**2. Design & Construction Features (1.5 Marks):**
- Subdivision into watertight compartments (damage stability, SOLAS Ch. II-1).
- Multiple life-saving appliances (lifeboats, life rafts — Ch. III).
- High fire protection standards (Ch. II-2).

**3. Applicable Conventions (2 Marks):**
- SOLAS Chapter III (Life-Saving Appliances).
- MLC 2006 (crew working conditions, food, accommodation).
- Athens Convention 1974 / 2002 Protocol (carriage of passengers and luggage by sea).
- Indian Merchant Shipping Act, 1958 — Part XA.

**4. Liability Regime (2.5 Marks):**
- **Strict liability** of the carrier for death/personal injury of passengers (Athens Conv. Art. 3).
- Liability limit: **250,000 SDR per passenger** for death/injury (Athens 2002).
- **Compulsory insurance** (Athens Conv. Art. 4bis) — Blue Card equivalent.
- Time bar: **2 years** from disembarkation (Art. 16).

---

### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**"Critically examine how the type of a ship determines the legal regime applicable to the carriage of goods by sea. Illustrate with reference to (a) Dry Bulk Carriers and (b) Container Ships."** (14 Marks)

#### Sub-Part (a) — Dry Bulk Carriers (7 Marks)

**1. Type & Cargo Profile (1.5 Marks):**
- Carry **dry, homogeneous, unpackaged cargo** in single-deck holds (iron ore, coal, grain, bauxite, cement).

**2. Design Features (1 Mark):**
- Large hatches, no self-loading gear (except Handysize), single or double skin construction, ballast tanks.

**3. Applicable Codes (2.5 Marks):**
- **SOLAS Chapter XII** — Additional safety measures specifically for bulk carriers.
- **IMSBC Code** — International Maritime Solid Bulk Cargoes Code (carriage & dangers of liquefaction).
- **Load Lines Convention, 1966** (compatibility with zone/season).
- **Hague-Visby Rules** for the bill of lading (default if no other convention applies).

**4. Legal Concerns (2 Marks):**
- **Cargo liquefaction** (e.g., nickel ore, iron ore fines) — historical cause of vessel loss.
- **Cargo shift and stability** under IMSBC Code.
- **Marpol Annex V** — Garbage from cargo residues.
- **Charter party:** GENCON 94 / NYPE 2015.
- **Insurance:** Standard H&M + P&I for cargo claims.

---

#### Sub-Part (b) — Container Ships (7 Marks)

**1. Type & Cargo Profile (1.5 Marks):**
- Carry **ISO-standardized containers** (20 ft, 40 ft) — multi-modal transport backbone.

**2. Design Features (1.5 Marks):**
- Cellular guides in holds and on deck.
- Bay/row/tier stacking plan; ULCVs with 24,000+ TEU capacity.
- Engine aft, large bridge forward (visibility concerns).

**3. Applicable Codes (2 Marks):**
- **SOLAS Chapter VII** — Carriage of dangerous goods (IMDG Code integration).
- **IMDG Code** (International Maritime Dangerous Goods Code).
- **CTU Code** (Container Packing — IMO/ILO/UNECE).
- **Hague-Visby Rules** for the bill of lading.
- **UCP 600** (documentary credits).

**4. Legal Concerns (2 Marks):**
- **Container misdeclaration** (esp. IMDG goods) — major cause of fires (e.g., MSC Zebra, 2020).
- **Weight misdeclaration** under SOLAS Ch. VI Reg. 2 (VGM — Verified Gross Mass).
- **Stowage planning** and terminal liability.
- **Charter party:** NYPE 2015 (time), GENCON (voyage).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Tonnage" and "Cargo Capacity":** GT/NT is volume; DWT is weight. Examiners specifically deduct **0.5–1 mark** for this error.
> 2. **Always cite the Convention + Article number** in Part B answers (e.g., *Hague-Visby Art. III(2)*, *MARPOL Annex I Reg. 19*). Bare statements without article references lose **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Do not generalize:** A "tanker" is not the same as a "chemical tanker" or an "LNG carrier." Examiners test this distinction for **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **In 14-mark answers, structure = marks:** Use headings (Design → Code → Liability → Charter) to ensure each component is visible and earns its share.
> 5. **Time bar** and **limitation amount** are favorite 2-mark sub-questions — memorize them.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Anchors:**

- [x] **Vessel type determines the legal regime** — never separate ship and law.
- [x] **Tanker** → MARPOL Annex I + CLC 1992 + strict liability + Blue Card insurance.
- [x] **Chemical Tanker** → IBC Code + MARPOL Annex II + HNS Convention 1996.
- [x] **LNG/LPG Carrier** → IGC Code + SOLAS Ch. VII + HNS Convention.
- [x] **Dry Bulk Carrier** → SOLAS Ch. XII + IMSBC Code + cargo liquefaction risk.
- [x] **Container Ship** → IMDG Code + CTU Code + VGM rule + UCP 600 (B/L).
- [x] **Ro-Ro** → SOLAS II-1/40-45 + EV fire risk (MSC.1/Circ.1622).
- [x] **Passenger Ship** → SOLAS Ch. III + MLC 2006 + Athens Convention (250,000 SDR/pax).
- [x] **GT vs NT vs DWT vs TEU** — these are NOT interchangeable metrics.
- [x] **Liability regimes are tiered**: Strict (CLC, Athens, HNS) > Fault-based (Hague-Visby).
- [x] **Charter parties by ship type**: Shellvoy 6 (tanker), Gencon 94 (general), NYPE 2015 (time), NYPE 1946/93 (historical).
- [x] **Indian law anchor**: Merchant Shipping Act 1958 — Part V (Mortgages), Part X (Carriage of Goods), Part XA (Passengers), Part XIB (Civil Liability for Oil Pollution).
- [x] **SOLAS Chapter VII** is the *master chapter* for dangerous goods and gas carriers.
- [x] **Classification society** (IRS, Lloyd's, DNV) is a quasi-regulatory body for ship type survey.
- [x] **Time bars**: Hague-Visby = 1 year; CLC = 3 years; Athens = 2 years.
- [x] **Mnemonic for exam writing**: **DDCBLG-RP** = **D**esign, **D**imensions, **C**ode, **B**ill of Lading, **L**iability, **G**overning Act, **R**isks, **P**itfalls.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Tip:** In a 14-mark question, follow the **5-block structure**: (1) Definition & Classification, (2) Design & Construction, (3) Applicable Codes/Conventions, (4) Liability Regime, (5) Risk/Commercial Concerns. This guarantees a balanced, marks-rich answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship's tonnages (GT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ship's tonnages (GT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gross Tonnage (GT)** is the unitless measure of the overall size of a ship, determined in accordance with the **International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969 (TONNAGE 1969)**. It represents a mathematical function of the *total volume of all enclosed spaces* of a vessel and forms the legal basis for issuing the **International Tonnage Certificate (ITC)** under **IMO (International Maritime Organization)** regulations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Gross Tonnage is **not a unit of weight** and carries **no physical units** (it is a pure number). It replaced the older **Gross Register Tonnage (GRT)** system, which was based on volume measured in units of **100 cubic feet**.

The **TONNAGE 1969 Convention** entered into force on **18 July 1982** and is mandatory for:
- All **new ships** keel-laid on or after **18 July 1982**.
- All **existing ships** from **18 July 1994** onwards.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Understanding:** Think of **Gross Tonnage (GT)** as the **"Universal Size Certificate"** of a ship — similar to how a **Passport Size** photo standardizes identification across all countries, GT standardizes a vessel's "size" globally so that port authorities, insurers, regulators, and charterers in Tokyo, Rotterdam, and New York all *measure the ship the exact same way*.

Imagine two ships:
- **Ship A** is a long, low container carrier.
- **Ship B** is a shorter, tall cruise liner.

To a layman, deciding which is "bigger" is hard. **GT solves this** by mathematically converting total enclosed volume into a single, comparable number. The genius of Tonnage 1969 is that it uses a *logarithmic correction factor* (K) — meaning that as a ship gets bigger, the relative weight of each additional cubic meter decreases, reflecting diminishing returns in operational utility per unit of added volume.

### 1.3 Why Tonnage 1969 Was Adopted

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Historical Context — Why a New System?**
> Before 1982, the world had **conflicting tonnage systems**:
> - **British Moorsom System (1854)**
> - **Oslo Convention (1947)**
> - **Suez Canal Net Tonnage (SCNT)**
> - **Panama Canal Universal Measurement System (PC/UMS)**
>
> This meant a single ship might show **four different tonnages** depending on where it was measured. Tonnage 1969 unified this.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Bold Constants)

The Tonnage 1969 system uses these **internationally fixed constants**:

- **K (constant) = 0.2 + 0.02 × log₁₀(V)** — Logarithmic reduction factor.
- **Anchor point:** When **V = 10,000 m³**, the value of **K = 0.28**, and **GT = 2,800**.
- **Reference dimension: 2/3 power law** — Tonnage is proportional to the **2/3 power** of volume (not linear).
- **Unit of volume used: cubic meters (m³)** for the gross tonnage system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The Gross Tonnage determination follows a strict **three-tier logical structure**:

1. **Step 1 — Define the Enclosed Space Boundary (Moulded Volume):**
   - The total volume **V** is measured between the **moulded lines** of the ship (the inside surface of the hull and structural plating).
   - All **enclosed spaces above and below the upper deck** are included.
   - Includes: cargo holds, machinery spaces, crew quarters, navigation spaces, deckhouses, superstructures, and tanks.

2. **Step 2 — Apply the Reduction Factor (K):**
   - The factor **K** is a function of **log₁₀(V)**, which is a logarithmic transformation.
   - The logarithmic form means **larger ships pay relatively less per cubic meter** in regulatory tonnage than smaller ships.

3. **Step 3 — Multiply K × V to Obtain GT:**
   - The result is a **dimensionless number** used for port dues, IMO safety regulations, manning scales, classification, and chartering.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Logarithmic Factor

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use a logarithmic correction?**
> The original **Moorsom System (1854)** was criticized because large ships were "over-tonnaged" relative to their earning capacity. A logarithmic reduction reflects the engineering reality that **doubling the volume of a ship does not double its operating value** — the value scales sub-linearly. This was a deliberate policy choice when Tonnage 1969 was negotiated at **IMCO (now IMO)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Definition / Formula** | **Unit** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| **V** | Total Volume of all enclosed spaces | Sum of moulded volumes below + above upper deck | **m³** |
| **K** | Reduction Factor | $K = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V)$ | Dimensionless |
| **GT** | **Gross Tonnage** | $GT = K \times V$ | **Dimensionless (Unitless)** |
| **Vc** | Cargo Volume | Volume of all cargo spaces | m³ |
| **D** | Moulded Depth | Vertical distance from keel to upper deck | m |
| **d** | Moulded Draft | Maximum design draft | m |
| **N1** | Passengers in cabins ≤ 8 berths | Number | — |
| **N2** | Other passengers | Number | — |
| **K₂** | NT cargo factor | $K_2 = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V_c)$ | Dimensionless |
| **K₃** | NT passenger factor | $K_3 = 1.25 \times \frac{(GT + 10{,}000)}{10{,}000}$ | Dimensionless |
| **NT** | Net Tonnage | $NT = K_2 \times V_c \times \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^{2} + K_3 \times \left(N_1 + \frac{N_2}{10}\right)$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM NOTE:** The KTU paper often tests whether students confuse GT (which is based on *all enclosed spaces*) with NT (which is based on *cargo volume and passenger capacity*). Remember: **GT ≥ NT** for all ships, since GT considers more space.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of GT in Maritime Operations

| **Application Area** | **Use of GT** |
|:---|:---|
| **Port Dues & Harbour Charges** | Computed using GT in most international ports |
| **SOLAS Safety Regulations** | Many SOLAS Chapter II-1, II-2 rules apply based on GT thresholds (e.g., 500 GT) |
| **MARPOL Annex VI** | Air pollution rules apply to ships ≥ **400 GT** |
| **Manning Requirements (STCW)** | Officer certifications differ by GT bands |
| **Classification Society Rules** | Hull and machinery rules use GT bands |
| **Insurance (H&M and P&I)** | Premiums partly calculated on GT |
| **Chartering & Sale & Purchase** | GT is the standard "size" descriptor in sale agreements |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reduction Factor K

**Given:** The Tonnage 1969 Convention requires that for a **reference volume V = 10,000 m³**, the resulting GT must be **2,800**.

**Step 1 — Set up the relationship:**

We require:
$$GT = K \times V = 2{,}800 \text{ when } V = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Express K as a function of V:**

The IMO working group proposed the linear-log form:
$$K = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V)$$

**Step 3 — Verify the anchor condition:**

Substituting $V = 10{,}000$ m³:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= 0.2 + 0.02 \times \log_{10}(10{,}000) \\
  &= 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4 \\
  &= 0.2 + 0.08 \\
  &= 0.28
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute GT at the anchor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GT &= K \times V \\
   &= 0.28 \times 10{,}000 \\
   &= 2{,}800 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The anchor condition is **satisfied**, confirming the formula.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Determining GT of a Bulk Carrier

**Problem:** A bulk carrier has a total enclosed volume of all spaces (below + above upper deck) of **V = 25,000 m³**. Calculate its Gross Tonnage.

**Step 1 — Compute the log value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(V) &= \log_{10}(25{,}000) \\
             &= \log_{10}(2.5 \times 10^{4}) \\
             &= \log_{10}(2.5) + \log_{10}(10^{4}) \\
             &= 0.39794 + 4 \\
             &= 4.39794
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute K:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.39794 \\
  &= 0.2 + 0.08796 \\
  &= 0.28796
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute GT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
GT &= K \times V \\
   &= 0.28796 \times 25{,}000 \\
   &= 7{,}199
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The vessel's **GT = 7,199** (unitless).

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| **Ship Type** | **Typical V (m³)** | **log₁₀(V)** | **K** | **Typical GT** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Small fishing vessel | 500 | 2.70 | 0.254 | 127 |
| General cargo ship | 10,000 | 4.00 | 0.280 | 2,800 |
| Handysize bulker | 25,000 | 4.40 | 0.288 | 7,199 |
| Panamax container | 65,000 | 4.81 | 0.296 | 19,250 |
| VLCC tanker | 300,000 | 5.48 | 0.310 | 92,860 |
| ULCC supertanker | 500,000 | 5.70 | 0.314 | 157,000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation from the matrix:** As V increases from **500 m³** to **500,000 m³** (a 1,000× increase), GT only increases by a factor of ~1,236×. This sub-linear scaling is the mathematical fingerprint of the **2/3 power law embedded in K**.

### 3.4 Exempted Spaces Under Tonnage 1969

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Per TONNAGE 1969 Regulation 2:** The following spaces are **excluded** from V:
> - Spaces **outside the perpendiculars** (e.g., bowsprit, bumkin spaces).
> - Spaces that are **open to the sea** (e.g., open weather deck areas not enclosed).
> - **Air spaces** above cargo lockers.
> - **Water ballast spaces** in tankers when used solely for ballast.

This is a **high-yield KTU topic** — the precise list of *included* versus *excluded* spaces is frequently asked in **Part A**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Determination of Gross Tonnage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Ship to be Measured] --> B[Identify Upper Deck Line]
    B --> C[Measure All Enclosed Spaces]
    C --> D{Categorize Each Space}
    D -->|Included| E[Cargo Holds]
    D -->|Included| F[Machinery Spaces]
    D -->|Included| G[Crew Spaces]
    D -->|Included| H[Deckhouses / Superstructures]
    D -->|Excluded| I[Open Decks]
    D -->|Excluded| J[Spaces Outside Perpendiculars]
    D -->|Excluded| K[Solas Lifeboat Spaces]
    E --> L[Sum All Included Volumes]
    F --> L
    G --> L
    H --> L
    L --> M[Compute Total V in Cubic Meters]
    M --> N[Compute K = 0.2 + 0.02 log10 V]
    N --> O[Compute GT = K times V]
    O --> P[Round to Nearest Integer]
    P --> Q[Issue International Tonnage Certificate by Flag State]
    Q --> R[End: GT Recorded on ITC]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — GT/NT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Enclosed Volume V] --> B[Gross Tonnage GT]
    A --> C[Cargo Volume Vc]
    C --> D[Net Tonnage NT]
    B --> D
    E[Passenger Numbers N1, N2] --> D
    F[Moulded Depth D] --> D
    G[Moulded Draft d] --> D
    B --> H[Port Dues]
    B --> I[SOLAS Applicability]
    B --> J[Manning Scales STCW]
    B --> K[Classification Rules]
    D --> L[Canal Dues]
    D --> M[Cargo Carrying Capacity Indicator]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Hierarchical View — Tonnage Systems Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Historical Tonnage Systems Pre 1982] --> B[British Moorsom 1854]
    A --> C[Oslo Convention 1947]
    A --> D[US System 1864]
    A --> E[Suez Net Tonnage]
    A --> F[Panama Canal PC/UMS]
    B --> G[Inconsistencies Between Systems]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[IMCO Negotiation 1969]
    H --> I[TONNAGE 1969 Convention]
    I --> J[Entry into Force 18 July 1982]
    J --> K[New Ships from 1982]
    J --> L[Existing Ships from 1994]
    I --> M[Single Global Standard GT and NT]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Gross Tonnage (GT) as per the International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969. State the formula used to compute GT.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Gross Tonnage is a unitless measure of the overall size of a ship, determined in accordance with the **International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969 (TONNAGE 1969)**. It is a function of the total volume of all enclosed spaces of the vessel.
- **Formula (1 mark):**
$$GT = K \times V$$
where $K = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V)$ and **V** is total enclosed volume in **m³**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State any **three exempted spaces** that are excluded from the calculation of total volume V under TONNAGE 1969.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. Spaces **outside the perpendiculars** (bowsprit, etc.).
2. Spaces **open to the sea** (open weather decks, uncovered platforms).
3. **Air spaces** above cargo lockers and certain **navigation spaces** excluded by Regulation 2.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **historical background and necessity** that led to the adoption of the International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969. Discuss the **shortcomings** of pre-1969 tonnage systems. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A vessel has a total enclosed volume **V = 50,000 m³**. Determine its **Gross Tonnage (GT)** using the Tonnage 1969 formula. Show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Historical Background and Necessity (7 marks):**

*(1 mark)* Before 1969, the maritime world had multiple **conflicting tonnage measurement systems**:
- **British Moorsom System (1854)** — measured internal volume in units of 100 cubic feet, called "register tons."
- **Oslo Convention (1947)** — adopted by Northern European nations, differed from Moorsom in machinery space deduction rules.
- **Suez Canal Net Tonnage (SCNT)** — specially used for transit dues through Suez Canal.
- **Panama Canal Universal Measurement System (PC/UMS)** — yet another formula, specific to Panama Canal transit.
- **US Tonnage System (1864)** — used dual tonnage (gross and net) with different rules.

*(2 marks)* The major **shortcomings** were:
- A single vessel could show **four different tonnage values** depending on where measured, leading to disputes in port and canal dues.
- The Moorsom system was based on a **linear** relation with volume, meaning that as ships grew larger (especially supertankers of the 1960s–70s), the tonnages became disproportionately large relative to earning capacity.
- There was **no uniform safety regulation framework**, since many IMO conventions (then IMCO) had tonnage thresholds, and inconsistent measurements created regulatory confusion.

*(2 marks)* IMCO (now IMO) therefore convened a diplomatic conference in **London in 1969**, which adopted the **International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969 (TONNAGE 1969)**. The convention introduced:
- A **single universal tonnage system** applicable to all ships engaged in international voyages.
- The concept of **Gross Tonnage (GT)** and **Net Tonnage (NT)** as unitless measures.
- A **logarithmic reduction factor (K)** ensuring sub-linear scaling.
- A **mandatory International Tonnage Certificate (ITC)**.

*(2 marks)* **Entry into force and implementation:**
- Convention entered into force on **18 July 1982**.
- Applies to **new ships** keel-laid on or after 18 July 1982.
- Applies to **existing ships** from 18 July 1994 onwards (12-year transition period).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation of GT (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V = 50{,}000$ m³

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the log value:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(V) &= \log_{10}(50{,}000) \\
             &= \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{4}) \\
             &= \log_{10}(5) + \log_{10}(10^{4}) \\
             &= 0.69897 + 4 \\
             &= 4.69897
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute K:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.69897 \\
  &= 0.2 + 0.09398 \\
  &= 0.29398
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute GT:
$$
\begin{aligned}
GT &= K \times V \\
   &= 0.29398 \times 50{,}000 \\
   &= 14{,}699
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Rounding and final answer:
GT is expressed as an **integer**. The vessel's **Gross Tonnage = 14,699** (unitless).

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question — Alternative]**
> **(a)** With the help of neat sketches and a flowchart, explain the **methodology** for computing Gross Tonnage (GT) of a ship under TONNAGE 1969. Mention the **spaces included** and **spaces exempted**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The total enclosed volume of a vessel is **V = 80,000 m³**. (i) Calculate its **Gross Tonnage**. (ii) If the **moulded depth D = 18 m**, **moulded draft d = 12 m**, **cargo volume Vc = 40,000 m³**, and the vessel carries **N1 = 0** cabin passengers and **N2 = 100** other passengers, determine the **Net Tonnage (NT)**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Methodology, Included and Exempted Spaces (7 marks):**

*(1 mark)* The Tonnage 1969 methodology follows these steps:
- **Step 1:** Identify the **upper deck** of the ship.
- **Step 2:** Measure **all enclosed spaces** between the **moulded lines** (inner hull surface).
- **Step 3:** **Sum** the volumes below the upper deck and above the upper deck to obtain **V**.
- **Step 4:** Apply the formula $K = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V)$ and compute $GT = K \times V$.

*(2 marks)* **Spaces Included in V:**
- **Below upper deck:** Cargo holds, machinery spaces (engine room, boiler room), chain lockers, tunnels, double bottoms, peak tanks, cofferdams within the hull envelope.
- **Above upper deck:** Deckhouses, superstructures, poop, bridge, accommodation blocks, navigation spaces, all permanently enclosed decks.

*(2 marks)* **Spaces Exempted (Per Regulation 2):**
- Spaces **outside the perpendiculars** (e.g., bowsprit spaces, fairings).
- Spaces **open to the sea** (e.g., uncovered parts of weather decks, hatch openings when uncovered).
- **Anchor chain spaces** (defined per Reg 2(7)).
- **Air spaces** between cargo lockers and the deck.
- Spaces used solely for **water ballast** in tankers (per specific rules).

*(2 marks)* **Flowchart (drawn in answer sheet, summarized):**
Start → Identify upper deck → Measure moulded volume of each enclosed space → Categorize (Include / Exclude) → Sum included volumes → Compute K → Compute GT → Round → Issue International Tonnage Certificate.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation of GT and NT (7 marks):**

**(i) Compute GT (3 marks):**

**Given:** $V = 80{,}000$ m³

**Step 1:** Compute $\log_{10}(V)$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(80{,}000) &= \log_{10}(8 \times 10^{4}) \\
                    &= \log_{10}(8) + 4 \\
                    &= 0.90309 + 4 \\
                    &= 4.90309
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute K:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.90309 \\
  &= 0.2 + 0.09806 \\
  &= 0.29806
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute GT:
$$
\begin{aligned}
GT &= 0.29806 \times 80{,}000 \\
   &= 23{,}845
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** **GT = 23,845** (unitless).

---

**(ii) Compute NT (4 marks):**

**Given:** $V_c = 40{,}000$ m³, $D = 18$ m, $d = 12$ m, $N_1 = 0$, $N_2 = 100$.

**Step 1:** Compute $K_2$ for NT:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_2 &= 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V_c) \\
    &= 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(40{,}000) \\
    &= 0.2 + 0.02 \times 4.60206 \\
    &= 0.2 + 0.09204 \\
    &= 0.29204
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the draft-depth ratio term:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^{2} &= \left(\frac{4 \times 12}{3 \times 18}\right)^{2} \\
                              &= \left(\frac{48}{54}\right)^{2} \\
                              &= (0.8889)^{2} \\
                              &= 0.7901
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $K_3$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
K_3 &= 1.25 \times \frac{(GT + 10{,}000)}{10{,}000} \\
    &= 1.25 \times \frac{(23{,}845 + 10{,}000)}{10{,}000} \\
    &= 1.25 \times 3.3845 \\
    &= 4.2306
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute NT:
$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= K_2 \times V_c \times \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^{2} + K_3 \times \left(N_1 + \frac{N_2}{10}\right) \\
   &= 0.29204 \times 40{,}000 \times 0.7901 + 4.2306 \times \left(0 + \frac{100}{10}\right) \\
   &= 0.29204 \times 31{,}604 + 4.2306 \times 10 \\
   &= 9{,}228.4 + 42.3 \\
   &= 9{,}270.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** **NT ≈ 9,271** (rounded to nearest integer).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Tonnage (GT) Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting that GT is unitless** — writing "GT = 7,199 tons" is **wrong**. The correct phrasing is "GT = 7,199" or "7,199 GT" without any unit.
> 2. **Confusing GT with NT formulas** — NT is much more complex and involves $K_2$, $K_3$, the cargo volume, and passenger numbers. Many students apply the GT formula twice.
> 3. **Including exempted spaces** — Students often include open decks, anchor chain spaces, or water ballast tanks, leading to inflated V and wrong GT.
> 4. **Not verifying the anchor condition** — For $V = 10{,}000$ m³, GT must equal 2,800. Use this as a quick sanity check.
> 5. **Rounding errors in log values** — $\log_{10}(50{,}000) = 4.69897$, not $4.7$. Carrying 4–5 decimal places is essential.
> 6. **Not stating the convention name explicitly** — Examiners expect the answer to reference **"TONNAGE 1969 Convention"** or **"International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969"**, not just "IMO rules."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **GT is a unitless measure** of a ship's size based on enclosed volume.
- ✅ It is calculated under the **International Convention on Tonnage Measurement of Ships, 1969 (TONNAGE 1969)**.
- ✅ The convention entered into force on **18 July 1982**; mandatory for existing ships from **18 July 1994**.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $GT = K \times V$, where $K = 0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10}(V)$.
- ✅ **Anchor check:** $V = 10{,}000$ m³ → $K = 0.28$ → $GT = 2{,}800$.
- ✅ **V is in cubic meters (m³)**, measured to **moulded lines** (inner hull surface).
- ✅ **Included spaces:** cargo holds, machinery spaces, accommodation, deckhouses, superstructures.
- ✅ **Excluded spaces:** outside perpendiculars, open weather decks, anchor chain spaces, certain air spaces.
- ✅ GT is used for: **port dues, SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW manning, classification, insurance, sale & purchase.**
- ✅ **NT formula:** $NT = K_2 \times V_c \times \left(\frac{4d}{3D}\right)^{2} + K_3 \times \left(N_1 + \frac{N_2}{10}\right)$.
- ✅ **NT ≤ GT** always holds.
- ✅ **K₃ formula:** $K_3 = 1.25 \times \frac{(GT + 10{,}000)}{10{,}000}$.
- ✅ The **International Tonnage Certificate (ITC)** is the legal document issued by the **flag state** confirming GT and NT.
- ✅ Tonnage 1969 replaced the **Moorsom, Oslo, Suez, Panama, and US systems** to create a single global standard.
- ✅ Logarithmic K reflects the **2/3 power law** between volume and operational utility.
- ✅ For numerical problems, **always carry 4–5 decimal places** in log calculations to avoid rounding errors.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cargo carrying capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cargo carrying capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cargo Carrying Capacity (CCC)** of a vessel is defined as the **maximum quantity of cargo** (measured in metric tonnes, long tons, or cubic metres) that a ship can safely load, carry, and discharge while maintaining compliance with the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL 1966, as amended by LL Protocol 1988)**, the **SOLAS Convention**, and the vessel's structural strength limits.

In KTU Board examination language, cargo carrying capacity is expressed as a **tripartite relationship** between:

1. **Deadweight Capacity (DWT)** — the total weight the ship can carry.
2. **Volumetric Capacity (Bale / Grain)** — the physical space available for cargo.
3. **Stability & Load Line Constraints** — the regulatory and safety upper bound.

The **operative** cargo carrying capacity is therefore the **lowest** of the three limits — a ship is limited by whichever constraint binds first.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Moving Truck

Imagine a **fully loaded intercity truck**:
- It has a **weight limit** (axle load / road regulations).
- It has a **box volume** (the trailer's internal dimensions).
- It has a **height limit** (overhead bridges and flyovers).

You cannot carry more goods than the truck can **physically hold** (volume) **or legally weigh** (axle limit) **or geometrically accommodate** (bridge clearance).

A ship is identical. The skipper cannot load more cargo than:
- The **hull buoyancy** permits (displacement minus lightweight = DWT).
- The **cargo holds** can enclose (bale / grain capacity).
- The **Plimsoll line** allows (load line freeboard).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** When asked "what is the cargo capacity of a vessel?", never give a single number. Always discuss **weight, volume, and regulatory** dimensions separately, then state the **binding (lowest) constraint**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Standard Metrics (Memorise These in Bold)

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deadweight Tonnage** | **DWT** | **MT** (Metric Tonnes) | Total weight of cargo + fuel + water + stores + crew |
| **Lightweight / Light Ship Weight** | **LWT / LSW** | **MT** | Weight of empty ship (hull + machinery) |
| **Displacement** | **Δ** | **MT** | Total weight of ship and everything aboard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Governing Equation:**
> $$\text{DWT} = \Delta - \text{LWT}$$
> Every subsequent cargo calculation in this module is derived from this identity.

---

## 1.4 Why Cargo Carrying Capacity Matters in Maritime Operations

- **Commercial Bidding:** Charter parties quote freight on **per MT or per tonne-mile** basis; a wrong estimate of CCC is a direct loss of revenue.
- **Stability Safety:** Overloading violates **SOLAS Chapter II-1** and risks capsize.
- **Legal Liability:** Under **Hague-Visby Rules Article III**, the carrier must provide a seaworthy ship — and seaworthiness includes correct loading.
- **Port State Control (PSC):** Violating load line marks is a **detainable deficiency** under the Paris MoU and Tokyo MoU.
- **Insurance:** Hull and machinery insurers (e.g., **Lloyd's of London**, **IACS P&I Clubs**) reject claims arising from overloading.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of Deadweight

The **DWT** itself is **not all usable for cargo**. It contains four sub-components, each of which must be subtracted to obtain the **true cargo carrying capacity**:

$$\boxed{\text{DWT} = \text{Cargo (CDWT)} + \text{Bunker Fuel (FO + DO + LO)} + \text{Fresh Water (FW)} + \text{Stores + Crew + Miscellaneous}}$$

Where:
- **CDWT** = Cargo Deadweight (the only *commercial* component)
- **Bunker** = Fuel Oil (FO) + Diesel Oil (DO) + Lubricating Oil (LO)
- **FW** = Fresh Water (drinking + boiler feed) + sometimes Ballast Water (during voyage)
- **Stores** = Provisions, spare parts, medical supplies
- **Crew + Effects** = Crew weight + luggage

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** When a question gives a single value of DWT, the student is expected to **deduct constants** (bunker, water, stores) and arrive at **CDWT**. Skipping this deduction is a guaranteed 3-mark loss in KTU evaluation.

---

## 2.2 Volumetric Capacity — Bale vs Grain

A ship's holds have two distinct volumetric measures, both defined in **m³** or **ft³**:

- **Grain Capacity (GC):** Total volume of the cargo hold measured to the **interior of the ship's frame**, including all corners and gaps. (Grain flows into every crevice.)
- **Bale Capacity (BC):** The same hold volume but with **stiff sails / bales** that cannot fill corners. Measured by subtracting broken stowage.

$$\boxed{\text{Bale Capacity} = \text{Grain Capacity} - \text{Broken Stowage Volume}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Broken Stowage (\%)} = \frac{\text{GC} - \text{BC}}{\text{GC}} \times 100}$$

For general cargo ships, broken stowage typically ranges from **10% to 20%**. For container ships, the equivalent concept is the **TEU slot utilization** and **revenue tonnes**.

---

## 2.3 Stowage Factor (SF) — The Bridge Between Weight and Volume

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stowage Factor (SF)** is defined as the **volume occupied by one unit weight** of cargo.
> $$\text{SF} = \frac{\text{Volume of cargo (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Weight of cargo (MT)}}$$
> Units: **m³/MT** (or ft³/long ton — note the conversion factor **35.315 ft³ = 1 m³**)

**Examples of typical SFs:**
- Steel: **0.4 m³/MT** (heavy, dense — limited by DWT)
- Wheat: **1.4 m³/MT** (moderate)
- Cotton (bales): **2.5 m³/MT** (bulky — limited by Bale Capacity)

**Decision rule:**
- If **Weight > Volume limit → Ship is "Deadweight Limited"**
- If **Volume > Weight limit → Ship is "Volume / Bale Limited"**

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **routinely tested** in KTU ESE Module 3 questions. Print this table before the exam.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DWT} = \Delta - \text{LWT}$ | Deadweight Tonnage | MT |
| 2 | $\text{CDWT} = \text{DWT} - (\text{Bunker} + \text{FW} + \text{Stores} + \text{Crew})$ | Cargo Deadweight | MT |
| 3 | $\text{Bale Cap.} = \text{Grain Cap.} - \text{Broken Stowage}$ | Volumetric hold capacity | m³ |
| 4 | $\text{Broken Stowage \%} = \dfrac{\text{GC} - \text{BC}}{\text{GC}} \times 100$ | Wasted hold volume | % |
| 5 | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{V}{W}$ | Stowage Factor | m³/MT |
| 6 | $\text{Number of parcels that can be stowed} = \dfrac{\text{Bale Cap.}}{\text{SF of cargo}}$ | Parcel count | parcels |
| 7 | $\text{TPC} = \dfrac{\text{WPA}}{100}$ (in salt water) | Tonnes Per Centimetre immersion | MT/cm |
| 8 | $\Delta W = \text{TPC} \times \Delta d$ | Weight change for draft change | MT |
| 9 | $\text{Cargo Loaded} = \text{DWT}_{\text{required}} - (\text{Constants})$ | Net cargo | MT |
| 10 | $\text{Plimsoll Mark: Load Draft} = T_{\text{design}} - \text{Freeboard}$ | Max permissible draft | m |

> ⚠️ **Table-Formatting Rule:** The vertical pipe character `|` is intentionally **avoided** inside table rows; all mathematical separators are written using **LaTeX `\dfrac` and `\vert` commands** to keep markdown table syntax intact.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **TPC (Tonnes Per Centimetre)** is a **direct operational tool** used by Chief Officers during the **Draft Survey** at the load port:

- A ship sits at **initial draft** $d_1$.
- After loading, **final draft** is $d_2$.
- Cargo loaded = $\text{TPC} \times (d_2 - d_1) - \text{constants consumed in transit}$.

This is regulated under the **UN ECE Code of Uniform Standards for Shipowners' Freight Containers and Cargo** and is the **only legally admissible method** of cargo weight determination in many jurisdictions when a shore scale is unavailable.

---

## 2.6 The Concept of Freeboard and Plimsoll Mark

The **freeboard** is the **vertical distance** between the **waterline** (load draft) and the **upper edge of the deck line** (a fixed line on the ship's side). It is **not the same** as the distance from keel to deck.

$$\boxed{\text{Freeboard} = \text{Depth (moulded)} - \text{Load Draft}}$$

The **Plimsoll Mark** (introduced by **Samuel Plimsoll MP** in the UK **Merchant Shipping Act 1875**, now codified in **ICLL 1966**) is a set of horizontal lines on the ship's side indicating **maximum permissible drafts** under various water and seasonal conditions:

| Line Mark | Condition |
|---|---|
| **TF** | Tropical Fresh Water |
| **F** | Fresh Water |
| **T** | Tropical Sea Water (above summer) |
| **S** | Summer Sea Water (**principal mark**) |
| **W** | Winter Sea Water |
| **WNA** | Winter North Atlantic (most severe) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Summer Load Line (S)** is the design reference. Cargo carrying capacity quoted in **DWT (summer)** is computed using the summer freeboard. The **deadweight at winter load line is greater** because the ship is allowed to load deeper (lower freeboard in winter for stability margin in colder, denser water).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — How a Ship's Cargo Carrying Capacity is Built Up

We will now derive the cargo carrying capacity **algebraically** from first principles, using standard naval architecture identities.

---

### Step 1 — Definition of Displacement

The **displacement** ($\Delta$) of a ship is the **weight of water displaced by the underwater volume** of the hull. By **Archimedes' Principle**:

$$\Delta = \rho_{\text{water}} \times \nabla$$

Where:
- $\nabla$ = **Volume of displaced water** (in m³)
- $\rho_{\text{water}}$ = **Density of water** (in MT/m³)
  - **Sea water:** $1.025$ MT/m³
  - **Fresh water:** $1.000$ MT/m³

> Conversion note: Since $1 \text{ m}^3$ of fresh water weighs $1$ MT, in **fresh water** the displacement in MT **equals** the displaced volume in m³. In **salt water**, displacement in MT = $1.025 \times \nabla_{\text{m}^3}$.

---

### Step 2 — Decomposition of Displacement

By definition:

$$\Delta = \text{LWT} + \text{DWT}$$

Where:
- **LWT** (Lightweight) = weight of the empty ship including hull, machinery, permanent ballast, outfit.
- **DWT** (Deadweight) = everything variable: cargo, bunker, water, stores, crew.

Subtracting LWT:

$$\text{DWT} = \Delta - \text{LWT}$$

This is the **defining identity** from Section 1.3.

---

### Step 3 — Subtracting Non-Cargo Items from DWT

Let the constants aboard be:
- $B$ = Total Bunker fuel (FO + DO + LO) in MT
- $FW$ = Fresh water in MT
- $S$ = Stores and provisions in MT
- $C$ = Crew and effects in MT

Then the **Cargo Deadweight (CDWT)** is:

$$\text{CDWT} = \text{DWT} - (B + FW + S + C)$$

Expanding:

$$\text{CDWT} = \Delta - \text{LWT} - B - FW - S - C$$

This is the **maximum cargo mass** the ship can lift subject to **weight** constraints.

---

### Step 4 — Applying the Volume Constraint

The **maximum cargo volume** that the holds can enclose (Bale Capacity) is $V_B$ m³. For a cargo with stowage factor $SF$ m³/MT, the **maximum cargo mass** limited by volume is:

$$W_{\text{volume}} = \frac{V_B}{SF}$$

---

### Step 5 — The Binding Constraint (Tight-Fit Rule)

A ship can carry cargo only up to the **lesser** of the two limits:

$$\boxed{W_{\text{cargo}} = \min\left(\text{CDWT}, \ \frac{V_B}{SF}\right)}$$

This is the **central result** of the entire module.

---

### Step 6 — Derivation of TPC from Hydrostatics

The **Waterplane Area (WPA)** of a ship is the cross-sectional area of the hull at the waterline. When the ship sinks by a tiny depth $\delta d$, the **additional displaced volume** is:

$$\delta \nabla = \text{WPA} \times \delta d$$

The **additional displacement (weight)** is:

$$\delta W = \rho_{\text{sw}} \times \text{WPA} \times \delta d$$

If we measure $\delta d$ in **centimetres** and $\rho = 1.025$ MT/m³:

$$\delta W = 1.025 \times \text{WPA} \times \frac{\delta d_{\text{cm}}}{100} \text{ MT}$$

The **TPC** is the additional weight for a **1 cm sink**:

$$\boxed{\text{TPC} = \frac{1.025 \times \text{WPA}}{100} \ \frac{\text{MT}}{\text{cm}}}$$

This explains the table entry "TPC = WPA / 100 (in salt water)".

---

### Step 3.2 — Worked Numerical Derivations

#### **Worked Example 1 — Deadweight and Cargo Calculation**

**Problem:** A bulk carrier has the following hydrostatic data at the design draft:
- Displacement $\Delta$ = **45,000 MT**
- Lightweight LWT = **8,200 MT**
- Bunker fuel B = **1,500 MT**
- Fresh water FW = **350 MT**
- Stores S = **120 MT**
- Crew and effects C = **30 MT**
- Bale Capacity $V_B$ = **52,000 m³**
- Cargo stowage factor SF = **1.25 m³/MT**

Find: **(a)** Deadweight Tonnage, **(b)** Cargo Deadweight, **(c)** Volume-limited cargo, **(d)** Actual cargo capacity.

**Solution:**

**(a) Deadweight Tonnage:**

$$\text{DWT} = \Delta - \text{LWT} = 45{,}000 - 8{,}200 = 36{,}800 \ \text{MT}$$

**(b) Cargo Deadweight (Weight limit):**

Constants = $B + FW + S + C = 1{,}500 + 350 + 120 + 30 = 2{,}000$ MT

$$\text{CDWT} = 36{,}800 - 2{,}000 = 34{,}800 \ \text{MT}$$

**(c) Volume-limited cargo:**

$$W_{\text{vol}} = \frac{V_B}{SF} = \frac{52{,}000}{1.25} = 41{,}600 \ \text{MT}$$

**(d) Actual cargo capacity (binding constraint):**

$$W_{\text{cargo}} = \min(34{,}800; \ 41{,}600) = 34{,}800 \ \text{MT}$$

> **Conclusion:** The vessel is **DEADWEIGHT LIMITED** (weight binds before volume). Loading more than **34,800 MT** would submerge the Plimsoll mark — a load line violation.

---

#### **Worked Example 2 — TPC and Draft Change**

**Problem:** A vessel has waterplane area WPA = **1,800 m²**. The Chief Officer needs to know how much cargo must be loaded to sink the ship by **6 cm** in sea water.

**Solution:**

$$\text{TPC} = \frac{1.025 \times 1{,}800}{100} = 18.45 \ \text{MT/cm}$$

For 6 cm sink:

$$\Delta W = \text{TPC} \times \Delta d = 18.45 \times 6 = 110.7 \ \text{MT}$$

> The vessel must receive approximately **110.7 MT** of cargo to settle by 6 cm.

---

#### **Worked Example 3 — Broken Stowage**

**Problem:** A general cargo hold has grain capacity GC = **12,000 m³** and bale capacity BC = **10,500 m³**. Calculate the broken stowage percentage.

**Solution:**

$$\text{Broken Stowage \%} = \frac{12{,}000 - 10{,}500}{12{,}000} \times 100 = 12.5\%$$

This 12.5% volume is "lost" because bales cannot fill corners — wasted space.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Cargo Carrying Capacity Hierarchy

The diagram below maps the **causal flow** between ship design parameters and the final cargo carrying capacity. It is a **block-level functional architecture** rather than a physical drawing.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Hull Form and Principal Dimensions] --> A2[Underwater Volume nabla]
    A3[Waterplane Area WPA] --> A4[TPC and Stability]
    A2 --> A5[Displacement Delta]
    A6[Lightweight LWT] --> A5
    A5 --> A7[DWT Calculation]
    A8[Tank and Bunker Capacities] --> A7
    A7 --> A9[Cargo Deadweight CDWT Weight Limit]
    A10[Hold Dimensions Grain and Bale] --> A11[Volumetric Limit]
    A12[Cargo Stowage Factor SF] --> A11
    A11 --> A13[Volume Limited Cargo]
    A9 --> A14[Binding Constraint Selector]
    A13 --> A14
    A15[Plimsoll Mark and Load Line] --> A16[Regulatory Draft]
    A16 --> A14
    A14 --> A17[Net Cargo Carrying Capacity MT]
    A4 --> A15
    A17 --> A18[Commercial Freight Planning]
    A17 --> A19[Stability and Trim Booklet]
    A17 --> A20[SOLAS Seaworthiness Compliance]
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The **left column** collects **raw ship-design data** (hull form, tank capacities, hold dimensions, waterplane area).
- The **middle column** converts these to the **two physical limits** — weight and volume.
- The **right column** applies the **regulatory limit** (Plimsoll mark) and **binds** everything into the **Net Cargo Carrying Capacity**.
- The **bottom row** is the **output — what the operator, charterer, and regulator actually use**.

---

## 4.2 Concept Map — Bale vs Grain Capacity

```mermaid
graph LR
    Hold[Empty Cargo Hold] --> Grain[Grain Capacity GC]
    Hold --> Bale[Bale Capacity BC]
    Grain --> Diff[Subtract Corners and Gaps]
    Diff --> Bale
    Bale --> Cargo[Stow Cargo as Bales Sacks Bags]
    Grain --> CargoG[Stow Cargo as Free-Flowing Grain]
    Cargo --> SF1[Higher Stowage Factor Used]
    CargoG --> SF2[Lower Stowage Factor Used]
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram of Plimsoll Mark Layout on the Ship's Side

```mermaid
graph TD
    HULL[Ship Side Amidships Starboard] --> DK[Deck Line Top Reference]
    DK --> TF1[TF Line Tropical Fresh]
    DK --> F1[F Line Fresh]
    DK --> T1[T Line Tropical Sea]
    DK --> S1[S Line Summer Principal]
    DK --> W1[W Line Winter]
    DK --> WNA1[WNA Line Winter North Atlantic]
    S1 --> Note[Summer S is Design Reference for DWT]
    W1 --> Note2[Winter W Permits Greater Loading]
    WNA1 --> Note3[WNA is Most Severe Restricted Zone]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each Plimsoll line is **separated by a precise distance** (typically a few cm) calibrated during sea trials. The **statutory authority** issuing the load line certificate is the **flag state administration**, often delegated to **classification societies** such as **Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, or ABS**.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Define the term "Cargo Carrying Capacity" of a vessel. List the three principal constraints that limit it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Cargo Carrying Capacity is the maximum quantity of cargo a ship can safely load, carry, and discharge while remaining within her structural, volumetric, and regulatory limits.
>
> **Three Principal Constraints (2 marks):**
> 1. **Weight Limit (Deadweight Tonnage, DWT):** Set by the difference between displacement at load draft and the lightweight of the vessel, less all onboard constants.
> 2. **Volume Limit (Bale or Grain Capacity):** Set by the physical dimensions of the cargo holds and the stowage factor of the intended cargo.
> 3. **Regulatory Limit (Load Line / Plimsoll Mark):** Set by the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL)** as a function of water density and season.

---

### **Q2. What is "Stowage Factor" of a cargo? Why is it critical for loading decisions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Stowage Factor (SF) is the volume in cubic metres occupied by one metric tonne of cargo. Units: **m³/MT**.
>
> **Critical Role (1.5 marks):** Stowage Factor determines whether the ship will be **weight-limited** (heavy cargo, e.g., steel — SF ≈ 0.4) or **volume-limited** (light bulky cargo, e.g., cotton bales — SF ≈ 2.5). The Chief Officer uses SF to decide **how much cargo to load** and to identify the **binding constraint** before arrival at the load port.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE Module 3 Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Numerical & Analytical Focus

**(a)** A Panamax bulk carrier has a summer displacement of **52,000 MT**, lightweight of **9,500 MT**, bunker of **1,800 MT**, fresh water of **400 MT**, stores of **150 MT**, and crew plus effects of **50 MT**. The vessel has three cargo holds with a total **grain capacity of 64,000 m³** and a **broken stowage of 14%**. The intended cargo is **iron ore** with a stowage factor of **0.45 m³/MT**.

Calculate:
1. Deadweight Tonnage. **(3 marks)**
2. Cargo Deadweight. **(3 marks)**
3. Bale Capacity of the holds. **(2 marks)**
4. Volume-limited cargo that can be stowed. **(2 marks)**
5. The actual cargo carrying capacity and identify the binding constraint. **(2 marks)**
6. If the **TPC of the vessel is 22 MT/cm**, how much cargo must be loaded to **settle the ship by 8 cm** at the load port? **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) DWT Calculation:**

$$\text{DWT} = \Delta - \text{LWT} = 52{,}000 - 9{,}500 = 42{,}500 \ \text{MT}$$

**(2) Cargo Deadweight (CDWT):**

Total constants = $1{,}800 + 400 + 150 + 50 = 2{,}400$ MT

$$\text{CDWT} = 42{,}500 - 2{,}400 = 40{,}100 \ \text{MT}$$

**(3) Bale Capacity:**

$$\text{BC} = \text{GC} - 14\% \times \text{GC} = 64{,}000 \times 0.86 = 55{,}040 \ \text{m}^3$$

**(4) Volume-Limited Cargo:**

$$W_{\text{vol}} = \frac{\text{BC}}{\text{SF}} = \frac{55{,}040}{0.45} = 122{,}311 \ \text{MT}$$

**(5) Actual Cargo Capacity:**

$$W_{\text{cargo}} = \min(40{,}100; \ 122{,}311) = 40{,}100 \ \text{MT}$$

> **Binding Constraint: DEADWEIGHT.** The vessel is heavily weight-limited because iron ore is dense.

**(6) Cargo for 8 cm sink:**

$$\Delta W = \text{TPC} \times \Delta d = 22 \times 8 = 176 \ \text{MT}$$

> The vessel must receive **176 MT** of cargo to sink by 8 cm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Conceptual & Legal Focus (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Plimsoll Mark** in detail. Discuss all the load line marks prescribed by the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL) 1966** and the conditions under which each is applied. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Plimsoll Mark** is a set of horizontal lines marked on **both sides of the ship's hull amidships**, indicating the **maximum permissible drafts** under varying conditions of water density and climate. It was introduced by the **UK Merchant Shipping Act 1875** following the campaigning of **Samuel Plimsoll MP**, and is now internationally mandated by the **International Convention on Load Lines (ICLL), 1966**, as amended by the **1988 LL Protocol**.

**The lines and their application (7 marks):**

1. **Deck Line (Reference):** A horizontal line 12 inches (300 mm) long, marked at the level of the upper edge of the freeboard deck. Serves as the reference for measuring freeboard.
2. **Summer Load Line (S):** The principal line, used for normal sea water (ρ = 1.025 MT/m³) in temperate seasons. **DWT quoted in charter parties is calculated at this line.**
3. **Tropical Load Line (T):** Used in tropical waters (typically above 30°N or below 30°S latitude) when voyage is in the **tropical zone**. Permits loading deeper than summer because the ship will not encounter severe weather in tropical conditions.
4. **Winter Load Line (W):** Used in colder waters. Allows **less** loading than summer because rougher seas are expected, requiring higher freeboard for safety reserve buoyancy.
5. **Winter North Atlantic Load Line (WNA):** The **most restrictive** line. Applied during winter voyages in the **North Atlantic** (above 36°N latitude) where storm conditions are severe.
6. **Fresh Water Load Line (F):** Used in **fresh water** (ρ = 1.000 MT/m³). Located **above** the Summer line because a ship floats higher in fresh water for the same weight.
7. **Tropical Fresh Water Load Line (TF):** Used in fresh water in the tropical zone. The **highest** of all lines — greatest freeboard permitted.

The spacing between these lines is determined by **statutory calculation** based on the ship's length, block coefficient, sheer, and bow height.

---

**(b)** A vessel is loaded in **tropical sea water (ρ = 1.024 MT/m³)** to her **Tropical Load Line**, and thereafter proceeds to a discharge port in **winter North Atlantic (ρ = 1.027 MT/m³)**. Discuss the **legal and operational implications** of this voyage under the **ICLL 1966** and **SOLAS Chapter II-1**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Legal Implication (3 marks):**
- The vessel, although lawfully loaded in the tropics, will, upon entering the **Winter North Atlantic zone**, exceed her **WNA load line**. Under **ICLL 1966 Article 14**, the master must be in possession of a **valid International Load Line Certificate (1966)**. If the vessel submerges the WNA mark, she is operating in violation of the convention — a **detainable deficiency** under **Paris MoU and Tokyo MoU Port State Control** regimes.
- The flag state may revoke or suspend the Load Line Certificate.

**Operational / Safety Implication (2 marks):**
- Submerging the WNA mark reduces **reserve buoyancy**, increasing the risk of **green seas on deck** and progressive flooding of freeing ports during severe North Atlantic storms.
- Stability margin against capsizing is reduced; the ship may be **structurally overstressed** (hogging/sagging bending moments exceed permissible limits in heavy weather).

**SOLAS Chapter II-1 Implication (2 marks):**
- Under **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 4**, the master must ensure that **stability information** (approved by the Administration) is available, and the loading condition must remain within the approved **stability booklet** limits at all times during the voyage.
- The Chief Officer must perform **trim and stability calculations** at each waypoint to demonstrate compliance. Failure may constitute **unseaworthy condition** under **Hague-Visby Rules Article III Rule 1**, exposing the carrier to **cargo claims and general average disputes**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Marks-Deducting Pitfalls in Module 3:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to deduct constants (bunker, water, stores, crew)** from DWT before reporting cargo capacity. The examiner specifically tests the distinction between **DWT and CDWT**. **Lose 3 marks instantly.**
> 
> 2. **Conflating Bale and Grain capacities.** The student must state which one is being asked and the appropriate formula. Bale = Grain − Broken Stowage.
> 
> 3. **Using sea-water density 1.000** (fresh water) instead of **1.025** in TPC and displacement calculations. The 2.5% error propagates.
> 
> 4. **Not stating the binding constraint.** The examiner allocates 2 marks specifically to the phrase "the ship is weight-limited / volume-limited". Without this, the answer is incomplete.
> 
> 5. **In Plimsoll questions**, mixing up the order: students often write "WNA > W > S" alphabetically but the correct order from the bottom (most loading) to top (least loading) is **WNA < W < S < T < F < TF**.
> 
> 6. **Unit errors** — tonnes vs long tons vs short tons. KTU questions always assume **Metric Tonnes (MT)** unless stated otherwise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam:**

- ☐ **DWT = Δ − LWT** — the foundation equation. Memorise it.
- ☐ **CDWT = DWT − Constants** — never skip constants (bunker + FW + stores + crew).
- ☐ **Bale Capacity = Grain Capacity − Broken Stowage.**
- ☐ **Stowage Factor SF = V / W** in m³/MT. Determines binding constraint.
- ☐ **Binding rule:** $W_{\text{cargo}} = \min(\text{CDWT}, \ \frac{V_B}{SF})$.
- ☐ **TPC = 1.025 × WPA / 100** in MT/cm. Use **ρ = 1.025 for sea water**, **1.000 for fresh water**.
- ☐ **Plimsoll Mark order (lowest to highest on hull):** WNA → W → S → T → F → TF.
- ☐ **Summer (S) is the design reference** for DWT quoted in charter parties.
- ☐ **Weight-limited** ship → heavy, dense cargo (iron ore, steel, coal).
- ☐ **Volume-limited** ship → light, bulky cargo (cotton, wool, agricultural bagged goods).
- ☐ **Draft Survey** uses TPC and draft change to verify cargo weight.
- ☐ **Overloading** is a **detainable deficiency** under Paris MoU and Tokyo MoU.
- ☐ **Legal chain:** ICLL 1966 + SOLAS Ch II-1 + Hague-Visby Art III = Seaworthiness.
- ☐ **Archimedes' Principle:** $\Delta = \rho \times \nabla$ — basis of all displacement calculations.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Cargo Carrying Capacity**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ship registration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ship registration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Registration** is the legal process by which a vessel is recorded in the official marine registry (shipping register) of a particular State, thereby acquiring the **nationality** of that State and the right to fly its **flag**. Under international law, specifically **Article 91 of the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS), 1982**, every State, whether coastal or land-locked, has the right to fix the conditions for the registration of ships flying its flag. Registration creates a juridical link between the ship and the flag State, establishing the foundation for jurisdiction, control, and the application of that State's maritime laws.

The act of registration is not merely a procedural formality; it is the **legal cornerstone** of the ship's identity at sea. Once registered, the vessel assumes the **national character** of its flag State, and is subject to the jurisdiction and protection of that State both on the high seas and in foreign ports.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Ship registration falls under the broader theme of *Ship Finance and Security* and is a recurring question area in Part B (14-mark) questions, often linked with flag State jurisdiction, ownership rules, and UNCLOS principles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of ship registration as analogous to a **vehicle registration (RC) process** combined with **citizenship/nationality** in your daily life:

- Just as every car in India must be registered with the Regional Transport Authority (RTO) and display a number plate indicating the State, every merchant ship must be registered with a country's maritime authority and display its **flag**.
- Just as your Indian citizenship determines which laws apply to you and which embassy will protect you abroad, the ship's **flag State** determines which laws govern the ship and which country's navy/consular officials can protect it on the high seas.
- Just as some countries make it easy to obtain citizenship (by investment) — think of flags of convenience like **Panama, Liberia, Bahamas** — some registries are "open" and welcome foreign-owned vessels, while others are "closed" and restrict registration to nationals only.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Example:** When you see a large container ship with "PANAMA" painted on its hull sailing from Singapore to Rotterdam, the ship is likely owned by a Japanese or Greek shipping company, crewed by Filipinos and Indians, financed by a German bank — but registered in **Panama** because of favourable tax rates and lenient regulations. This is the essence of **Flags of Convenience (FOC)**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Flag State** | The country whose flag a vessel is legally entitled to fly. |
| **Nationality of Ship** | The legal status conferred by registration with a flag State. |
| **Registry / Register** | The official record maintained by the maritime authority listing all registered ships. |
| **Bareboat Charter Registration** | Temporary registration of a foreign-owned ship under a different flag while it is chartered. |
| **Open Registry** | A registry that permits registration of foreign-owned vessels with minimal conditions. |
| **Closed Registry** | A registry restricted to vessels owned and operated by nationals. |
| **Flag of Convenience (FOC)** | A vessel registered in a country with favourable tax/regulatory conditions. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **registration** with **documentation** or **licensing**. Registration specifically refers to the formal entry in the maritime register that confers nationality. A **Certificate of Registry** is the documentary proof of registration — not registration itself.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CONCEPT SHEET

## 2.1 The Legal Framework Governing Ship Registration

### 2.1.1 International Legal Basis

Ship registration is governed by a layered framework:

1. **UNCLOS, 1982 — Article 91 (Nationality of Ships)**
   - Every State has the right to fix conditions for granting nationality to ships.
   - Ships have the nationality of the State whose flag they are entitled to fly.
   - There must be a **genuine link** between the State and the ship.

2. **UNCLOS, 1982 — Article 92 (Status of Ships)**
   - Ships fly the flag of one State only and are subject to its exclusive jurisdiction on the high seas (except in cases of piracy, slave trade, etc.).

3. **UN Convention on Conditions for Registration of Ships, 1986 (Geneva Convention)**
   - This convention attempted to establish minimum standards linking registration to genuine economic and managerial presence. However, it has **not entered into force** due to insufficient ratifications, reflecting the resistance of major shipping nations to binding rules.

4. **IMO Standards**
   - The International Maritime Organization (IMO) adopts conventions (SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC) that flag States must enforce on their registered vessels.

### 2.1.2 National Legal Basis

Each country has its own Merchant Shipping Act or equivalent legislation:

- **India:** The **Merchant Shipping Act, 1958** (as amended) governs ship registration through the **Directorate General of Shipping (DGS)**.
- **United Kingdom:** Merchant Shipping Act 1995.
- **Panama:** Law No. 32 of 1927 (as amended) — one of the oldest open registry laws.
- **United States:** 46 U.S.C. (Vessel Documentation) — uses a documentation system rather than traditional registration.

## 2.2 Types of Ship Registration Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Classification:** Ship registries worldwide fall into three broad categories based on the eligibility criteria they impose.

### 2.2.1 Closed Registry (National Registry)

- Restricts registration to vessels owned and controlled by **nationals** of the flag State.
- Requires substantial ownership, crewing, and management within the country.
- **Examples:** United States, China, Brazil, India (historically).
- **Advantage:** Ensures genuine link; better enforcement of labour and safety standards.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher operating costs; less competitive for shipowners.

### 2.2.2 Open Registry (Flag of Convenience / FOC)

- Permits registration of vessels owned wholly or partly by **foreigners**.
- Offers low taxes, low registration fees, minimal crewing requirements, and relaxed regulations.
- **Examples:** **Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Bahamas, Malta, Cyprus, Bermuda**.
- **Advantage:** Cost-effective for shipowners; globally competitive.
- **Disadvantage:** Often criticized for weak enforcement, leading to substandard shipping.
- **Statistical Note:** As of 2024, **open registries collectively account for over 70% of the world fleet by gross tonnage** — a critical fact for KTU exams.

### 2.2.3 Hybrid / International Registry

- A compromise between closed and open registries.
- Allows foreign ownership but requires some domestic presence (management office, training of nationals, or partial crewing).
- **Examples:** Norway's **Norwegian International Ship Register (NIS)**, Denmark's **DIS (Danish International Ship Register)**, Germany's **GIS (German International Ship Register)**, **Indian International Ship Register (IIS)** concept.

## 2.3 The "Genuine Link" Doctrine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** The concept of a "genuine link" between the flag State and the ship originated in the **1958 Geneva Convention on the High Seas (Article 5)** and was reinforced in **UNCLOS Article 91**.

The genuine link requires:
- **Administrative connection** — flag State exercises effective jurisdiction and control.
- **Economic connection** — ownership, manning, or management ties.
- **Social connection** — crew nationality and welfare standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** The genuine link doctrine is a **frequently asked 14-mark question**, often combined with discussions on Flags of Convenience and the failure of the 1986 UN Registration Convention.

## 2.4 Conditions for Registration (Typical Requirements)

While conditions vary by jurisdiction, the **general international consensus** includes:

1. **Ownership Requirement** — the applicant must be the legal owner or charterer.
2. **Vessel Survey** — the ship must pass technical and safety inspections.
3. **Naming of Ship** — the proposed name must be approved by the registry.
4. **Marking of Ship** — the vessel must display its name, port of registry, and IMO number visibly on the hull.
5. **Tonnage Measurement** — official measurement of gross and net tonnage.
6. **Mortgage/Encumbrance Disclosure** — declaration of any existing financial charges.
7. **Payment of Fees** — registration fees, annual tonnage taxes, etc.

## 2.5 Rights Conferred by Registration

| Right | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Right to fly the flag** | The vessel is entitled to fly the national flag of the registry State. |
| **Right to protection** | The flag State's navy and consular officials must protect the vessel on the high seas. |
| **Right of innocent passage** | The vessel enjoys the right of innocent passage through foreign territorial waters. |
| **Right to maritime services** | Access to the flag State's search and rescue, meteorological services, etc. |
| **Jurisdictional immunity** (for State-owned ships) | Warships and certain government vessels enjoy sovereign immunity. |

## 2.6 Duties Arising from Registration

The flag State has **affirmative duties** under UNCLOS Article 94:

1. Maintain a **register of ships** and assume jurisdiction over them.
2. Ensure **safety at sea** — adherence to SOLAS, MARPOL, STCW.
3. Prevent **pollution** from vessels.
4. Enforce **labour standards** — MLC 2006 compliance.
5. Conduct **surveys and inspections**.
6. Ensure **manning and qualification** of crew.
7. Prevent **substandard shipping** under flag State jurisdiction.

## 2.7 Bareboat Charter Registration

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Bareboat Charter Registration** (also called "charter registration" or "parallel registration") is the registration of a vessel in a second State while the original registration is suspended for the duration of the charter. The charterer (lessee) assumes temporary flag control for the period of the bareboat charter.

**Conditions:**
- The ship is temporarily deleted from its original registry.
- The charterer must be a qualified person under the second registry's law.
- The original mortgage holder's consent is usually required.
- On termination of the charter, the ship reverts to the original flag.

**Governed by:** UN General Assembly Resolution on Bareboat Charter Registration (A/RES/46/39) and bilateral agreements between flag States.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This is your **one-page cheat sheet** for quick revision before any KTU ESE on this topic.

| # | Concept | Key Detail |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **UNCLOS Article 91** | Right of State to fix conditions for registration; genuine link requirement. |
| 2 | **UNCLOS Article 92** | Ships fly one flag; subject to exclusive jurisdiction on high seas. |
| 3 | **UNCLOS Article 94** | Flag State duties — safety, pollution, manning, surveys. |
| 4 | **Genuine Link** | Required between flag State and ship; codified in 1958 & 1982 conventions. |
| 5 | **Open Registry (FOC)** | Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Bahamas, Malta — over 70% of world tonnage. |
| 6 | **Closed Registry** | USA, China, Brazil — restricted to nationals. |
| 7 | **Hybrid Registry** | Norway NIS, Denmark DIS, Germany GIS. |
| 8 | **Bareboat Charter** | Temporary re-registration during charter period. |
| 9 | **Certificate of Registry** | Documentary proof of registration; must be carried on board. |
| 10 | **1986 UN Registration Convention** | Not yet in force; aimed at strengthening genuine link. |
| 11 | **Flag State Jurisdiction** | Exclusive on high seas; concurrent in territorial waters. |
| 12 | **Port State Control** | Right of port State to inspect foreign ships for compliance. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: ANALYTICAL MATRIX — STEP-BY-STEP ANALYSIS OF REGISTRATION PROCEDURE

## 3.1 Standard Ship Registration Procedure (Analytical Walkthrough)

While specific procedural steps vary by jurisdiction, the **universal logical sequence** for ship registration is as follows:

### Step 1: Eligibility Determination

The applicant must qualify under the flag State's law. This includes:
- Citizenship / incorporation status of the owner.
- Type of entity (individual, partnership, corporate body).
- For FOC registries: even foreign-owned companies qualify.

### Step 2: Reservation of Ship Name

- The proposed name is submitted to the registrar.
- The registrar checks for duplication and policy compliance (e.g., no offensive names).
- Once approved, the name is **reserved** for a fixed period (typically 90 days).

### Step 3: Technical Survey and Inspection

- A classification society (e.g., **Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, DNV, ABS, IRS**) inspects the vessel.
- Checks include hull integrity, machinery, safety equipment, pollution prevention systems.
- A **Classification Certificate** is issued upon satisfactory survey.

### Step 4: Tonnage Measurement

- The ship's **gross tonnage (GT)** and **net tonnage (NT)** are measured per the **International Tonnage Convention (ITC), 1969**.
- Gross Tonnage = measure of total enclosed volume of the ship.
- Net Tonnage = measure of cargo-earning capacity.

$$GT = K_1 \cdot V$$
$$NT = K_2 \cdot GT \cdot \left(\frac{V}{100D}\right)^{2/3}$$

where:
- $K_1$ = constant (typically **0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V**) for vessels of size $V$.
- $K_2$ = constant (typically **0.2 + 0.02 \log_{10} V**) for the cargo earning capacity.
- $V$ = total volume of all enclosed spaces in cubic metres.
- $D$ = moulded depth in metres.

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are simplified representations; the full ITC 1969 calculation is more complex, but KTU questions typically ask only for conceptual understanding of tonnage measurement.

### Step 5: Documentation Submission

The applicant files the following with the registrar:
- Application form with vessel particulars.
- Builder's certificate or bill of sale (proof of ownership).
- Deletion certificate from previous registry (if any).
- Classification certificate.
- Tonnage certificate.
- Mortgage/encumbrance declarations.
- Power of attorney (if filed by agent).

### Step 6: Payment of Fees

- Registration fee.
- Annual tonnage tax (especially relevant in FOC registries — extremely low).
- Light dues, consular fees, etc.

### Step 7: Issuance of Certificate of Registry

- The registrar enters the ship in the **Register Book**.
- A **Certificate of Registry** is issued, stating the ship's name, official number, particulars, and ownership.
- This certificate must be **carried on board at all times**.

### Step 8: Marking of the Ship

- The ship's **name** is painted on each side of the bow and on the stern.
- The **port of registry** and **IMO number** are displayed.
- The **national flag** is flown from the stern when in port or at sea.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Registration Systems Compared

| Parameter | Closed Registry | Open Registry (FOC) | Hybrid Registry |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ownership** | Nationals only | Any nationality | Mostly foreigners allowed |
| **Tax burden** | High | Very low | Moderate |
| **Crew requirements** | Mostly nationals | Any nationality | Some national quotas |
| **Regulatory enforcement** | Strict | Often lax | Moderate to strict |
| **Examples** | USA, China, India (trad.) | Panama, Liberia, Marshall Is. | Norway (NIS), Denmark (DIS) |
| **Share of world fleet** | ~10% | ~70%+ | ~20% |
| **Legal criticism** | Low — high standards | High — substandard ships | Low — balanced |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analysis: Bareboat Charter Registration Logic

The logical steps of bareboat charter registration under the **UN Resolution on Bareboat Charter Registration (A/RES/46/39, 1991)** are:

1. **Original Registration Exists** — The ship is registered in State A.
2. **Bareboat Charter Agreement** — Owner enters into a bareboat charter with a charterer in State B.
3. **Consent of Mortgagees** — Existing financial encumbrancers consent to the move.
4. **Deletion from State A Registry** — The ship is temporarily deleted from State A's register.
5. **Application to State B** — Charterer applies to register the ship in State B.
6. **Registration in State B** — State B registers the ship and issues a charter registration certificate.
7. **Dual/Hybrid Status** — The ship flies State B's flag during the charter period.
8. **Termination** — On charter expiry, ship is re-registered in State A; State B deletes the entry.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often describe bareboat charter registration as a "permanent" re-registration. It is **temporary** — the original registration is only suspended, not cancelled.

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Analysis: Why Flags of Convenience Exist (The "Why" Behind FOC)

Understanding the rationale behind FOC requires tracing the economic incentives:

1. **High labour costs in developed nations** push shipowners to seek cheaper crewing options.
2. **High corporate taxes** in home countries reduce profitability.
3. **Stringent safety and environmental regulations** increase compliance costs.
4. **Marshall Plan and post-WWII shipping surplus** (USA vessels) led to the creation of the Panamanian registry in 1927 to accommodate surplus tonnage under foreign flags.
5. **Liberia** followed in 1948, **Bahamas** in 1976, and other small nations joined to earn registry fees.
6. **Result:** Today, open registries dominate world shipping, but are criticized by ITF (International Transport Workers' Federation), ILO, and various maritime welfare organizations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
