Comment on whether this indicates a **boom or slump** condition. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution to (a) — Concept of TCE:** **TCE (Time Charter Equivalent)** is the **net daily earnings** of a vessel on a voyage, calculated as: $$\text{TCE} = \dfrac{\text{Net Voyage Revenue} - \text{Voyage Costs}}{\text{Total Voyage Days}}$$ It is used by shipowners to **standardise** earnings comparison: - A **spot voyage** earning USD X can be directly compared with a **time charter** rate. - Helps decide whether to fix the vessel on a long TC at USD Y/day or keep it in the spot market. - During **boom**, TCE >> daily opex → profit; during **slump**, TCE < daily opex → loss. --- **Model Solution to (b) — Numerical Calculation:** **Step 1 — Laden Days** $$\text{Laden Days} = \dfrac{6{,}800}{13 \times 24} = 21.79 \text{ days}$$ **Step 2 — Ballast Days** $$\text{Ballast Days} = \dfrac{6{,}900}{14 \times 24} = 20.54 \text{ days}$$ **Step 3 — Total Days** $$\text{Total Days} = 21.79 + 20.54 = 42.33 \text{ days}$$ **Step 4 — Gross Freight** $$\text{Gross} = 72{,}000 \times 12 = 864{,}000 \text{ USD}$$ **Step 5 — Bunker Laden Cost** $$\text{BL} = 21.79 \times 32 \times 650 = 453{,}234 \text{ USD}$$ **Step 6 — Bunker Ballast Cost** $$\text{BB} = 20.54 \times 28 \times 650 = 373{,}828 \text{ USD}$$ **Step 7 — Total Voyage Cost** $$\text{VC} = 453{,}234 + 373{,}828 + 85{,}000 = 912{,}062 \text{ USD}$$ **Step 8 — Net Voyage Revenue** $$\text{NVR} = 864{,}000 - 912{,}062 = -48{,}062 \text{ USD (LOSS)}$$ **Step 9 — TCE per day** $$\text{TCE} = \dfrac{-48{,}062}{42.33} = -1{,}135 \text{ USD/day}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A **negative TCE of -USD 1,135/day** is a clear **SLUMP** indicator. The vessel cannot even recover its variable voyage costs, let alone contribute to fixed costs (opex + capex of ~USD 12,000/day). The owner must consider **lay-up or slow-steaming**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark Loss Areas):** > 1. **Unit Errors:** Mixing tons/day with tons and dollars. Always write units explicitly. > 2. **Forgetting Ballast Days:** Many students only calculate laden days — this loses 2 marks immediately. > 3. **Sign Convention:** Negative TCE means loss; examiners expect you to **comment qualitatively**, not just give a number. > 4. **Round-off Errors:** Carry 2 decimal places throughout. > 5. **Mixing Voyage Cost with Operating Cost:** TCE only subtracts *voyage* costs (variable). Fixed opex and capex are NOT subtracted in TCE. --- ### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - [x] **RTBS Mnemonic:** Recovery → Boom → Recession → Slump. - [x] **Lead Time Lag of 2–3 years** is the root cause of the cycle. - [x] **BDI** < 800 = Slump; BDI > 2,500 = Boom. - [x] **Orderbook > 25%** of fleet = bearish signal (future oversupply). - [x] **Scrapping > 4%** annually = bullish (capacity removed). - [x] **TCE Formula** is a guaranteed 14-mark question favourite. - [x] **Slow steaming** is a defensive supply-side tactic in recession. - [x] **Second-hand prices** lead new-building prices by 6–12 months. - [x] **Asymmetry:** Booms are short and sharp; slumps are long and grinding. - [x] **Counter-cyclical strategy:** Buy at trough, sell at peak — the cardinal rule. - [x] **External shocks** (IMO 2020, EEXI, CII, Russia-Ukraine) can cut cycles short or extend them. - [x] **Always mention Lead Time Lag** in any cycle essay — it scores you distinction marks. - [x] **Voyage P&L analysis** uses Gross Freight − Voyage Cost = Net Revenue; then divide by days for TCE. ---

topics.title: Shipping risk
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shipping risk

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Shipping Risk

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shipping Risk** refers to the broad spectrum of perils, hazards, and uncertainties that may cause physical loss, damage, liability, or financial loss associated with the operation of ships, carriage of goods by sea, and the commercial exploitation of vessels. In maritime law, *risk* is the allocation of the financial burden of fortuitous events between the parties to a maritime adventure (shipowner, charterer, cargo owner, insurer, and banker).

Under the **Marine Insurance Act 1906 (UK)** and the **Marine Insurance Act 1963 (India)**, a "maritime peril" is a peril consequent on, or incidental to, the navigation of the sea — including fire, war perils, pirates, rovers, thieves, jettisons, barratry, and any other perils of a similar kind.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PENST863 Module 4**
> Shipping risk is examined as a tripartite study: (a) the **taxonomy of risks**, (b) the **legal allocation** of those risks between contracting parties, and (c) the **indemnification mechanism** through marine insurance and the doctrine of General Average.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sending a fragile glass sculpture from Mumbai to Rotterdam by ship. The voyage takes 25 days across the Indian Ocean, through the Suez Canal, and into the stormy North Sea. Many things can go wrong — the ship may sink, catch fire, be hijacked by pirates, collide with another vessel, or the cargo may simply be damaged by seawater. **Shipping risk is the umbrella term for all these "what if" scenarios.**

In layman's terms:
- **Risk = Who pays when the worst happens?**
- The **shipowner** worries about the ship sinking.
- The **cargo owner** worries about goods being damaged.
- The **charterer** worries about the freight revenue being lost.
- The **insurer** is the one who ultimately shoulders the financial loss — *if* a valid policy is in place.

Think of shipping risk like a **three-legged stool**:
1. **The Peril** — what could go wrong (fire, storm, theft).
2. **The Exposure** — what is at stake (hull, cargo, freight, liability).
3. **The Remedy** — who pays (insurer, party, or shared via Average).

## 1.3 The Six Cardinal Principles of Marine Insurance (UK Marine Insurance Act 1906)

| # | Principle | One-line Essence |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Insurable Interest** | You can only insure what you stand to lose. |
| 2 | **Utmost Good Faith (Uberrima Fides)** | Both parties must disclose every material fact. |
| 3 | **Indemnity** | Insurance compensates — it does not enrich. |
| 4 | **Proximate Cause** | The dominant cause of loss is what matters. |
| 5 | **Subrogation** | The insurer steps into the insured's shoes after paying. |
| 6 | **Contribution** | Co-insurers share a loss proportionately. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** These six principles form the spine of every KTU Module 4 question on shipping risk. Memorise them in the order given — examiners test them in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions with case-based scenarios.

## 1.4 Classification of Shipping Risk — At a Glance

Shipping risks are broadly divided into **Physical Risks** (loss/damage to property) and **Liability Risks** (legal exposure to third parties).

$$\text{Total Shipping Risk} = \text{Hull Risk} + \text{Cargo Risk} + \text{Freight Risk} + \text{Liability Risk} + \text{War \& Strike Risk}$$

Each of these can be **insured separately** or bundled under an "All Risks" policy (e.g., **Institute Cargo Clauses (A)**).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Aid — "HCFL-WS"**
> **H**ull, **C**argo, **F**reight, **L**iability, **W**ar, **S**trikes — the six insurable classes a maritime student must know.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Maritime Perils (The "What Can Go Wrong" List)

The **Marine Insurance Act 1906, Schedule 1** lists the perils typically covered. KTU examiners expect students to differentiate between:

### A. Perils of the Sea
Fortuitous accidents of the sea — storms, lightning, stranding, sinking, collision, heavy weather damage. **The classic test**: Was the sea calm or stormy? Did the event arise from a natural oceanic force? (See *Hamilton, Fraser & Co. v. Pandorf & Co.* (1887)).

### B. Fire
Includes fire caused by negligence of the master/mariner (covered), but **not** if caused by the wilful misconduct of the assured.

### C. Theft (Including Piracy)
Covered under ICC (A); limited cover in ICC (B) and (C). Modern piracy hotspots: Gulf of Aden, Strait of Malacca, West Africa.

### D. Barratry
Wilful misconduct by the master or crew — e.g., scuttling the ship, theft of cargo, deliberate deviation. **Premium-grade KTU question topic.**

### E. Jettison
Voluntary throwing overboard of cargo to save the ship — part of **General Average** sacrifice.

### F. Collision & Liability
The shipowner's liability to third parties is covered by **Protection & Indemnity (P&I)** Clubs, not standard hull policies.

### G. War Risks (Institute War Clauses)
Capture, seizure, mines, torpedoes, blockade, hostilities — **excluded** from standard ICC and hull policies.

### H. Strikes Risks (Institute Strikes Clauses)
Loss caused by strikers, locked-out workers, or persons taking part in labour disturbances.

## 2.2 Risk Allocation in Common Maritime Contracts

| Contract Type | Who Bears the Risk? | KTU Point |
|---------------|---------------------|-----------|
| **FOB (Free on Board)** | Risk passes to buyer when goods cross the ship's rail. | INCOTERMS 2020 |
| **CIF (Cost, Insurance & Freight)** | Seller insures goods during transit; risk passes on shipment. | INCOTERMS 2020 |
| **Voyage Charter** | Shipowner bears hull risk; charterer pays hire regardless of loss. | NYPE forms |
| **Time Charter** | Charterer bears voyage-specific risks (bunker, port risk); owner bears maintenance. | Baltime 2001 |
| **Bareboat Charter** | Charterer (disponent owner) bears virtually all risks. | Barecon 2001 |
| **Bill of Lading** | Carrier liable for loss/damage from tackle to tackle (Hague-Visby Rules). | Art. III, Rule 2 |

## 2.3 The Doctrine of General Average (GA) — A KTU Favourite

**General Average** is a unique maritime concept codified in the **York-Antwerp Rules 2016 (YAR 2016)**. It arises when an **extraordinary sacrifice or expenditure** is made *intentionally* to save the **common maritime adventure** (ship + cargo + freight) from a common peril.

The loss is **shared** pro-rata by all benefited parties in proportion to their saved values:

$$\text{GA Contribution by Party X} = \frac{\text{Saved Value of Party X}}{\text{Total Saved Value (Ship + Cargo + Freight)}} \times \text{Total GA Loss}$$

### The Seven GA Allowances (YAR 2016)
- **Rule A**: Definition of General Average.
- **Rule C**: Jettison of cargo.
- **Rule II**: Damage by water to ship, cargo, and freight.
- **Rule VI**: Salvage remuneration.
- **Rule X**: Expenses at port of refuge.
- **Rule XIV**: Temporary repairs.
- **Rule XX**: Commission and interest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction — Particular Average (PA) vs. General Average (GA)**
> - **Particular Average** = partial loss borne **only by the owner** of the damaged property (e.g., seawater damages one container of cargo).
> - **General Average** = loss shared by **all parties** to the maritime adventure.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Indemnity Limit** | $\text{Claim} \le \text{Insured Value (Sum Insured)}$ | Prevents profiteering from insurance |
| 2 | **Average Clause (Marine)** | $\text{Payable Loss} = \left(\frac{\text{Insured Value}}{\text{Agreed Value}}\right) \times \text{Actual Loss}$ | Only applied when undervalued |
| 3 | **General Average Contribution** | $C_i = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{\text{ship}} + V_{\text{cargo}} + V_{\text{freight}}} \times L_{\text{GA}}$ | YAR 2016, Rule A |
| 4 | **Constructive Total Loss (CTL)** | $\text{CTL if} \; \text{Salvage + Recovery Cost} \ge \text{Ship's Insured Value}$ | Allows abandonment to insurer |
| 5 | **Subrogation Recovery** | $\text{Recovery} = \text{Claim Paid} - \text{Expenses}$ | Insurer steps into assured's rights |
| 6 | **Contribution (Co-insurers)** | $\text{Each Pays} = \dfrac{\text{Their Sum Insured}}{\text{Total Sum Insured}} \times \text{Loss}$ | When multiple policies cover the same risk |
| 7 | **P\&I Liability Cap** | $\text{Limit} = \text{GRT (tons)} \times \text{Special Drawing Rights (SDR)}$ | Athens Convention, LLMC 1976 |
| 8 | **Hague-Visby Package** | $\text{Max Carrier Liability} = 666.67 \; \text{SDR/package} \;\text{or}\; 2 \; \text{SDR/kg}$ | Whichever is higher |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numericals**: GA contribution problems and CTL threshold problems are the most frequently asked 14-mark questions. Always show the formula substitution step explicitly.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Shipping Risk Management

- **Ship Finance**: Banks (HDFC, SBI, international lenders) require hull insurance before disbursing ship loans.
- **Trade Finance**: Letters of Credit under UCP 600 require CIF insurance certificates.
- **P\&I Clubs**: UK P\&I Club, Gard, Steamship Mutual — mutual insurance for shipowners' third-party liabilities.
- **Lloyd's of London**: The world's leading specialist marine insurance market.
- **IMO and Classification Societies** (DNV, Bureau Veritas, ABS): Set safety standards that reduce *moral* and *physical* risk.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: General Average Contribution

**Problem (Model KTU 14-mark):**
A vessel carrying cargo worth **USD 2,000,000** and freight at risk of **USD 200,000** grounds off Cape Horn. To refloat the ship, the master jettisons cargo worth **USD 300,000** and incurs port of refuge expenses of **USD 100,000**. The vessel is valued at **USD 5,000,000** and is eventually saved, along with the remaining cargo.

**Compute**:
(a) The total General Average loss.
(b) The GA contribution payable by the **cargo interest** and the **shipowner**.
(c) The net settlement between the parties.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the GA Loss Components**

The sacrifice (jettison) and the port of refuge expenses are both allowed under **YAR 2016, Rules C and X**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{sacrifice}} &= \text{USD } 300{,}000 \\
L_{\text{expenses}} &= \text{USD } 100{,}000 \\
L_{\text{GA}} &= L_{\text{sacrifice}} + L_{\text{expenses}} = 300{,}000 + 100{,}000 \\
L_{\text{GA}} &= \text{USD } 400{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the Contributory Values**

The contributing values are the **saved values** of ship, cargo, and freight at the place where the adventure ends.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{ship}} &= \text{USD } 5{,}000{,}000 \\
V_{\text{cargo (remaining + jettisoned)}} &= \text{USD } 2{,}000{,}000 \\
V_{\text{freight at risk}} &= \text{USD } 200{,}000 \\
V_{\text{total}} &= 5{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}000{,}000 + 200{,}000 = 7{,}200{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Each Party's Contribution Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cargo Ratio} &= \frac{V_{\text{cargo}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{7{,}200{,}000} = 0.2778 \\
\text{Ship Ratio} &= \frac{V_{\text{ship}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{7{,}200{,}000} = 0.6944 \\
\text{Freight Ratio} &= \frac{V_{\text{freight}}}{V_{\text{total}}} = \frac{200{,}000}{7{,}200{,}000} = 0.0278
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Each Party's GA Contribution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{cargo}} &= 0.2778 \times 400{,}000 = \text{USD } 111{,}120 \\
C_{\text{ship}} &= 0.6944 \times 400{,}000 = \text{USD } 277{,}760 \\
C_{\text{freight}} &= 0.0278 \times 400{,}000 = \text{USD } 11{,}120 \\
\text{Check: } C_{\text{cargo}} + C_{\text{ship}} + C_{\text{freight}} &= 111{,}120 + 277{,}760 + 11{,}120 \\
&= \text{USD } 400{,}000 = L_{\text{GA}} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Net Settlement**

The cargo interest originally bore the jettison loss of **USD 300,000**. After pooling and redistribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Liability of Cargo} &= 300{,}000 - 111{,}120 = \text{USD } 188{,}880 \quad \text{(recovery from shipowner + freight)} \\
\text{Net Liability of Shipowner} &= 277{,}760 \quad \text{(paid into the pool)} \\
\text{Net Liability of Freight Interest} &= 11{,}120 \quad \text{(paid into the pool)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** The cargo owner recovers a portion of the jettison loss because the **shipowner and freight interest also contribute** to the pool. This is the **risk-spreading beauty** of General Average that KTU examiners love to test.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Constructive Total Loss (CTL) Test

**Problem:**
A bulk carrier is insured for **USD 8,000,000** (insured value). After a fire, the vessel is damaged and the cost of recovery + salvage + repairs is estimated at **USD 7,500,000**. The market value of the salvaged vessel is **USD 2,000,000**.

**Determine**: Is this a Constructive Total Loss? Should the assured give Notice of Abandonment?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the CTL Test (Marine Insurance Act 1906, Section 60(2))**

A constructive total loss arises when the assured is deprived of his/her possession of the ship by an insured peril, AND it is unlikely that the assured can recover the ship, OR the cost of recovery + subsequent repairs would exceed the insured value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CTL Condition: } & \text{Recovery Cost} + \text{Repair Cost} \ge \text{Insured Value} \\
& 7{,}500{,}000 \ge 8{,}000{,}000 \;\; ? \\
& \text{No } — \text{USD 7.5M is less than USD 8M.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Alternative Test (Section 60(2)(ii))**

If the ship is damaged, and the estimated cost of repairs exceeds the **agreed value** (insured value):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Repair Cost (estimated)} &= 7{,}500{,}000 \\
\text{Agreed Value} &= 8{,}000{,}000 \\
\text{Excess?} &\; \text{No.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion**: This is **NOT a Constructive Total Loss**. The assured can claim for **partial loss** (particular average) under the hull policy, but cannot give a valid Notice of Abandonment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *actual* total loss with *constructive* total loss. Remember: **CTL = notice of abandonment is required**; **ATL = no notice needed**. Notice of abandonment must be given within a **reasonable time** of the assured receiving reliable information of the loss.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Cover Levels

| Coverage Element | ICC (A) — All Risks | ICC (B) — Named Perils | ICC (C) — Limited Cover |
|------------------|---------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Basis** | All risks of loss/damage | Specific named perils only | Most restricted list |
| **Fire, Explosion** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Stranding, Sinking, Burning** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Overturning of conveyance** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Collision** | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| **General Average sacrifice** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| **Theft, Pilferage, Non-delivery** | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **War Risks** | ✗ (separate clause) | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Strikes** | ✗ (separate clause) | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Inherent Vice** | ✗ (excluded) | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Delay, Market Loss** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Anchor**: The three ICC forms (A/B/C) are examined as a tabular comparison almost every year. Remember: ICC (A) is the **widest**, ICC (C) is the **narrowest**.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: P\&I Liability Cap Computation

Under the **Convention on Limitation of Liability for Maritime Claims 1976 (LLMC 1976)**, a shipowner's liability for claims (other than personal injury) is capped at:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{property}} &= 1{,}000{,}000 \; \text{SDR} \; + \; 400 \; \text{SDR/ton} \quad \text{for tonnage up to 5,000 GT} \\
L_{\text{property (above 5,000 GT)}} &= 3{,}000{,}000 \; \text{SDR (cap for first 5,000)} + 200 \; \text{SDR/ton above 5,000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** A 15,000 GT bulk carrier has a property liability claim.
$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{property}} &= 3{,}000{,}000 + (200 \times 10{,}000) \\
&= 3{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}000{,}000 = \text{5,000,000 SDR}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **cap** — even if the actual damage is USD 50 million, the shipowner's liability is limited to 5 million SDR.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Shipping Risk Allocation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Maritime Adventure] --> nodeB[Identify Perils]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Quantify Exposure]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Who Bears the Risk?}
    nodeD -->|Hull Damage| nodeE[Shipowner + Hull Insurer]
    nodeD -->|Cargo Damage| nodeF[Cargo Owner + Cargo Insurer]
    nodeD -->|Freight Loss| nodeG[Charterer + Freight Insurer]
    nodeD -->|3rd Party Claim| nodeH[Shipowner + P and I Club]
    nodeD -->|War Peril| nodeI[War Risk Insurer]
    nodeD -->|Strike Action| nodeJ[Strike Risk Insurer]
    nodeE --> nodeK[Subrogation Loop]
    nodeF --> nodeK
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[Insurer Recovers from 3rd Party]
    nodeH --> nodeM[Indemnity Payment]
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeN[Risk Closed]
    nodeM --> nodeN
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — General Average Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Common Maritime Peril] --> nodeB[Extraordinary Decision by Master]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Sacrifice OR Expenditure]
    nodeC --> nodeD[YAR 2016 Rule Applied]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Sacrifice or Expense?}
    nodeE -->|Sacrifice| nodeF[GA Allowed Loss]
    nodeE -->|Expense| nodeG[Port of Refuge / Salvage]
    nodeF --> nodeH[GA Adjustment by Average Adjuster]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Compute Contributory Values]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Compute Contributions Pro Rata]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Issue GA Bond and Guarantee]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Cargo Released to Consignee]
    nodeL --> nodeM[GA Settlement to Shipowner / Salvor]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Six Principles of Marine Insurance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Marine Insurance Contract] --> nodeB1[1 Insurable Interest]
    nodeA --> nodeB2[2 Utmost Good Faith]
    nodeA --> nodeB3[3 Indemnity]
    nodeA --> nodeB4[4 Proximate Cause]
    nodeA --> nodeB5[5 Subrogation]
    nodeA --> nodeB6[6 Contribution]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC[Valid Contract]
    nodeB2 --> nodeC
    nodeB3 --> nodeC
    nodeB4 --> nodeC
    nodeB5 --> nodeC
    nodeB6 --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD[Claim Settled and Closed]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Risk Flow Under CIF Contract

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Seller CIF] -->|Arranges Insurance| nodeB[Cargo Insurer]
    nodeB -->|Policy Issued| nodeC[Insurance Certificate]
    nodeC -->|Sent with B/L| nodeD[Bank under LC]
    nodeD -->|Documents to Buyer| nodeE[Buyer CIF]
    nodeA -->|Ships Goods| nodeF[Vessel in Transit]
    nodeF -->|Insured Peril Occurs| nodeG[Loss / Damage]
    nodeG -->|Claim Filed| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Pays Buyer| nodeE
    nodeE -->|Subrogation| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Recovers| nodeH[Third Party Liable]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Visual Learning Aid:** In the examination, draw these flow diagrams with crisp boxes and arrows. Examiners reward visual structure — even a hand-drawn block diagram of the risk allocation process can earn 2-3 extra marks in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the doctrine of General Average as codified in the York-Antwerp Rules 2016. How does it differ from Particular Average?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

General Average (GA) refers to extraordinary sacrifices or expenditures made intentionally and reasonably to save the ship, cargo, and freight from a common peril at sea. It is governed by the **York-Antwerp Rules 2016 (YAR 2016), Rule A**. The loss is **shared pro rata** by all parties to the maritime adventure in proportion to their saved values.

Particular Average, on the other hand, is a **partial loss** suffered by one party's property (e.g., seawater damages one container) and is **borne solely by that owner**, not shared. *(3 marks distributed: 1 mark for GA definition, 1 mark for YAR reference, 1 mark for PA contrast.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of "Utmost Good Faith" (Uberrima Fides) in marine insurance contracts.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The principle of **Utmost Good Faith** obliges both the assured and the insurer to disclose every material fact known to them before the contract is concluded. A material fact is one that would influence the judgment of a prudent insurer in fixing the premium or accepting the risk. This duty is codified in **Sections 17-20 of the Marine Insurance Act 1906**. Breach of utmost good faith entitles the innocent party to **avoid the contract** (treat it as void from inception). In marine insurance, the duty is *stricter* than in ordinary insurance because of the international, high-value, and information-asymmetric nature of shipping. *(3 marks: 1 mark duty, 1 mark material fact test, 1 mark remedy.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the different types of shipping risks covered under marine insurance. Compare the cover provided by **Institute Cargo Clauses (A), (B), and (C)** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship carrying cargo worth **USD 1,500,000** and freight at risk of **USD 150,000** is stranded. The master jettisons cargo worth **USD 200,000** and incurs USD **80,000** in port of refuge expenses to refloat the ship. The vessel is valued at **USD 3,000,000** and is successfully saved along with the remaining cargo. **Calculate the General Average contributions** of the cargo interest, shipowner, and freight interest using the **York-Antwerp Rules 2016**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The major types of shipping risks under marine insurance are:

1. **Perils of the Sea**: Storms, stranding, sinking, collision, heavy weather. These are fortuitous oceanic events (1 mark).

2. **Fire**: Covered unless caused by wilful misconduct of the assured (0.5 mark).

3. **Theft, Piracy, and Barratry**: Covered under ICC (A); ICC (B) and (C) cover limited theft (0.5 mark).

4. **War Risks**: Capture, seizure, mines, torpedoes. Covered under **Institute War Clauses** (0.5 mark).

5. **Strikes Risks**: Loss from labour disturbances. Covered under **Institute Strikes Clauses** (0.5 mark).

6. **General Average and Salvage Charges**: Shared losses (0.5 mark).

7. **Particular Average**: Partial losses covered as per policy terms (0.5 mark).

**Comparison of ICC (A), (B), (C):**

| Clause | Coverage | Example |
|--------|----------|---------|
| **ICC (A)** | All-risks cover (widest). Covers theft, non-delivery, washing overboard. | Container of electronics stolen at transhipment port — covered. |
| **ICC (B)** | Named perils — fire, explosion, stranding, sinking, collision, general average, jettison. | Ship catches fire, cargo damaged — covered. |
| **ICC (C)** | Narrowest — only major casualties: fire, explosion, stranding, sinking, burning, overturning. | Container dented by rough handling — NOT covered. |

*(3 marks for the comparison table; remaining marks distributed across the 7 risks above.)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks — Full Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1: Identify GA Loss**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{sacrifice}} &= \text{USD } 200{,}000 \\
L_{\text{expenses}} &= \text{USD } 80{,}000 \\
L_{\text{GA}} &= 200{,}000 + 80{,}000 = \text{USD } 280{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Contributory Values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= V_{\text{ship}} + V_{\text{cargo}} + V_{\text{freight}} \\
&= 3{,}000{,}000 + 1{,}500{,}000 + 150{,}000 \\
&= \text{USD } 4{,}650{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Contribution Ratios**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{ship}} &= \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{4{,}650{,}000} = 0.6452 \\
R_{\text{cargo}} &= \frac{1{,}500{,}000}{4{,}650{,}000} = 0.3226 \\
R_{\text{freight}} &= \frac{150{,}000}{4{,}650{,}000} = 0.0323
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute GA Contributions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{ship}} &= 0.6452 \times 280{,}000 = \text{USD } 180{,}656 \\
C_{\text{cargo}} &= 0.3226 \times 280{,}000 = \text{USD } 90{,}328 \\
C_{\text{freight}} &= 0.0323 \times 280{,}000 = \text{USD } 9{,}044 \\
\text{Check: } 180{,}656 + 90{,}328 + 9{,}044 &= 280{,}028 \approx \text{USD } 280{,}000 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Net Settlement**

The cargo interest originally sacrificed **USD 200,000**, but its GA contribution is only **USD 90,328**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Recovery by Cargo} &= 200{,}000 - 90{,}328 = \text{USD } 109{,}672
\end{aligned}
$$

The cargo recovers USD 109,672 from the shipowner and freight interest pools. *(7 marks: 1 for step 1, 1 for step 2, 1 for step 3, 2 for step 4, 1 for step 5 and interpretation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **six fundamental principles of marine insurance** under the Marine Insurance Act 1906. Discuss the doctrine of **Constructive Total Loss** with reference to **Section 60** of the Act and the **"Notice of Abandonment"** requirement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vessel insured for **USD 10,000,000** suffers engine room fire damage. The estimated **salvage cost is USD 4,000,000** and the **repair cost is USD 7,000,000**. The **scrap value of the vessel is USD 1,500,000**. Advise the shipowner whether a **Notice of Abandonment** can be validly given. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The six fundamental principles of marine insurance under the **Marine Insurance Act 1906 (UK)** are:

1. **Insurable Interest** (Section 5): The assured must have a legal or equitable relationship with the subject matter such that they stand to benefit from its safety or be prejudiced by its loss. A policy without insurable interest is void. *(1 mark)*

2. **Utmost Good Faith** (Sections 17-20): Both parties must disclose all material facts. Material facts are those that would influence a prudent insurer's decision. *(1 mark)*

3. **Indemnity** (Section 1): The assured is entitled to be placed in the same financial position after the loss as before, but not better. This is the cornerstone principle distinguishing insurance from wagering. *(1 mark)*

4. **Proximate Cause** (Section 55): The insurer is liable only for losses proximately caused by an insured peril. The proximate cause is the **dominant, effective cause** that sets in motion a chain of events leading to the loss. *(1 mark)*

5. **Subrogation** (Section 79): After indemnifying the assured, the insurer steps into the shoes of the assured and can claim against third parties responsible for the loss. *(1 mark)*

6. **Contribution** (Section 80): When multiple policies cover the same risk, each insurer contributes proportionately to the loss, preventing the assured from claiming the full amount from any one insurer. *(1 mark)*

**Constructive Total Loss (CTL) — Section 60:**

A CTL arises when the assured is deprived of possession of the ship by an insured peril, AND either:
- The assured is unlikely to recover the ship, OR
- The cost of recovery + repairs would exceed the **insured value** of the ship. *(1 mark for the test, 0.5 mark for the Notice of Abandonment explanation)*

The assured must give a **Notice of Abandonment** to the insurer within a reasonable time of receiving reliable loss information, expressing an intention to abandon the property to the insurer. If accepted, the assured can claim the full sum insured. *(remaining marks)*

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute the Total Cost of Recovery and Repair**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{salvage}} &= \text{USD } 4{,}000{,}000 \\
L_{\text{repair}} &= \text{USD } 7{,}000{,}000 \\
L_{\text{total recovery+repair}} &= 4{,}000{,}000 + 7{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 11{,}000{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with Insured Value**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{total}} &= 11{,}000{,}000 \\
V_{\text{insured}} &= 10{,}000{,}000 \\
\text{Excess} &= 11{,}000{,}000 - 10{,}000{,}000 = \text{USD } 1{,}000{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply CTL Test (Section 60(2)(ii))**

Since the cost of recovery and repair (USD 11,000,000) **exceeds** the insured value (USD 10,000,000), the condition for Constructive Total Loss is **satisfied**. *(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Advise the Shipowner**

The shipowner can **validly give a Notice of Abandonment** to the insurer. Upon acceptance by the insurer, the assured can claim the **full sum insured (USD 10,000,000)** and relinquish all rights in the vessel to the insurer. The insurer may then sell the wreck for its scrap value (USD 1,500,000) to recover part of the payout.

**Step 5: Note on Time Limit**

The Notice of Abandonment must be given **within a reasonable time** of receiving reliable information about the loss. Delay without justification may be treated as a waiver of abandonment, converting the claim to a partial loss. *(1 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Mis-stating the CTL test**: The test is whether *recovery + repair* exceeds *insured value* — NOT whether repair alone exceeds insured value. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting to state the time limit** for Notice of Abandonment. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing General Average with insurance indemnity**: GA is a *shared* loss; insurance is *indemnity*. Examiners test this distinction ruthlessly. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 4. **In GA numericals, failing to verify** that contributions sum to the total GA loss — examiners check this as the final mark. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 5. **Omitting the salvage/scrap value** in CTL problems — the prudent assured considers whether the insurer gains a windfall via the abandonment. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Shipping Risk (KTU Module 4):**

- **Shipping risk = perils + exposure + indemnity mechanism.** Define it in those three layers in every answer.

- **Six principles of marine insurance**: Insurable Interest, Utmost Good Faith, Indemnity, Proximate Cause, Subrogation, Contribution — *memorise in this order.*

- **Marine Insurance Act 1906 (UK) and 1963 (India)** are the cornerstones. Cite them in every definitional answer.

- **Perils of the sea ≠ perils on the sea.** Storm on the ocean = covered; storm in port (after voyage ends) = may not be. Cite *Hamilton v. Pandorf*.

- **ICC (A) is widest; ICC (C) is narrowest.** Memorise the comparison table.

- **General Average = YAR 2016, Rule A.** Only *intentional* and *extraordinary* sacrifices qualify.

- **Formula for GA contribution**: $C_i = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{\text{total}}} \times L_{\text{GA}}$. Always verify the sum.

- **Particular Average ≠ General Average.** PA = owner alone; GA = shared.

- **Constructive Total Loss = Section 60, MIA 1906.** Test: recovery + repair cost > insured value. Requires **Notice of Abandonment**.

- **Notice of Abandonment** must be given within a **reasonable time** of receiving reliable loss info.

- **Subrogation** allows the insurer to "step into the shoes" of the assured and recover from third parties.

- **War and Strikes risks** are EXCLUDED from standard ICC and hull policies — separate clauses must be bought.

- **P\&I Clubs** cover third-party liability (pollution, injury, cargo claims) — not hull policies.

- **Hague-Visby Rules** cap carrier liability at **666.67 SDR/package or 2 SDR/kg**, whichever is higher.

- **LLMC 1976** limits shipowner's liability based on GRT — formula above (3M + 200 SDR/ton above 5,000 GT).

- **Bareboat charter** = charterer bears virtually all risks. **Time charter** = owner maintains ship; charterer bears voyage costs. **Voyage charter** = owner bears ship risks for the voyage.

- **FOB** = risk passes at ship's rail. **CIF** = seller insures but risk passes on shipment.

- **Barratry** = wilful misconduct of master/crew. It is covered under hull policies, but **not** if the assured is the wrongdoer.

- **Jettison** is a classic GA sacrifice — but only if done **intentionally** for the common safety.

- **Salvage** under YAR 2016 Rule VI is allowed in GA only if awarded for saving life, ship, and cargo in danger.

- **Inherent vice** is *excluded* from all ICC clauses — it is the natural behaviour of the goods themselves (e.g., spontaneous combustion of coal).

- **Moral hazard vs. physical hazard**: Insurers assess both; the duty of utmost good faith covers disclosure of both.

- **Kerala maritime legal context**: The **Cochin Port Trust Act 1968** and **Indian Ports Act 1908** govern port-side risk. The **Marine Insurance Act 1963** applies in India.

- **Recent amendments to YAR**: The 2016 edition replaced the 2004 edition — for KTU 2024, **always cite YAR 2016**.

- **Most-asked 14-mark question patterns** in KTU: (1) GA numerical with YAR application, (2) Comparison of ICC (A/B/C), (3) Six principles with case examples, (4) Risk allocation in CIF/FOB/Charter parties, (5) CTL with Notice of Abandonment.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Shipping Risk | PENST863 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shipping market supply and demand model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shipping market supply and demand model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Shipping Market Supply and Demand Model** is an economic framework that explains the interaction between the global availability of tonnage (supply) and the requirement for sea transport services (demand) to determine equilibrium freight rates, charter party terms, and vessel deployment patterns in international seaborne trade. Unlike a normal commodity market, shipping is a **derived demand** market because ships are not demanded for their own sake; they are demanded only because the world needs to move raw materials, energy, and finished goods across oceans.

The two principal curves of the model are:

- **Demand for Shipping (D_s)** — the quantity of tonne-miles (or vessel-days) that shippers, charterers, and governments are willing to buy at a given freight rate (F).
- **Supply of Shipping (S_s)** — the quantity of tonnage (in DWT, TEU, or vessel-days) that shipowners are willing to provide at a given freight rate (F).

The market clears at the equilibrium rate **F\*** where:

$$Q_d(F^*) = Q_s(F^*)$$

### 1.2 Key Actors in the Model

| Actor | Role in the Model |
|---|---|
| Shipowners (Principals/Disponent Owners) | Suppliers of tonnage |
| Charterers (Voyage / Time / Bareboat) | Direct demand-side buyers |
| Shippers (Cargo Owners) | Underlying demand drivers |
| Brokers (Sale & Purchase, Chartering) | Price-discovery intermediaries |
| Classification Societies & Registries | Supply-side regulators |
| Banks & Leasing Houses | Supply-side financiers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The shipping market is treated as a *freely competitive* but *cyclical and capital-intensive* market. Students must remember that **shipping supply is highly price-inelastic in the short run** (ships cannot be built overnight) but extremely elastic in the **very long run** (5–7 years from order to delivery). Demand, on the other hand, is elastic in the short run (cargo can switch modes or ports) but inelastic in the long run (international trade has no real substitute).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Floating Taxi Service"

Imagine a city where taxis float on water and carry entire factories of cargo. A shipowner owns the taxi (a Capesize bulker worth about **USD 65 million**). The taxi cannot be summoned instantly — if demand suddenly doubles, the owner cannot conjure a new taxi; they must order it, wait **24–36 months** for the shipyard, and pay for fuel, crew, and port calls every single day even when parked.

So when world trade booms, freight rates shoot up (because demand > supply), and when trade falls, rates collapse below the cost of running the ship. This is the fundamental reason shipping is one of the most **boom-and-bust** industries in the world. The "taxi meter" is the **Baltic Dry Index (BDI)**, **Baltic Capesize Index (BCI)**, **Baltic Panamax Index (BPI)**, and the **Shanghai Containerized Freight Index (SCFI)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industry Metrics (Must Memorise):**
> - **DWT** (Deadweight Tonnage) — cargo-carrying capacity in metric tonnes.
> - **TEU** (Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit) — container capacity measure.
> - **Tonne-mile** — 1 tonne carried over 1 nautical mile (the "GDP of shipping").
> - **Vessel-day** — supply is often measured in vessel-days per quarter.
> - **Bunker fuel** — vessel operating cost driver (VLSFO, IFO 380, MGO).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Demand Side — Q_d(F, W, P, S, T)

The demand for shipping is best expressed as a function of multiple variables, not just freight rate. The standard reduced-form demand equation taught in KTU modules is:

$$Q_d = f(F, W, P, S, T, G)$$

Where:
- **F** = Freight rate (USD per tonne or per TEU)
- **W** = World industrial production / world GDP
- **P** = Price of the cargo at destination (commodity price)
- **S** = Substitutes (land pipelines, road, rail, air)
- **T** = Trade policy (tariffs, sanctions, embargoes)
- **G** = Seasonal/geographic factors (harbour conditions, ice)

The fundamental identity that drives the entire shipping demand model is:

$$\text{Seaborne Trade (tonne-miles)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i \times D_i$$

Where **Q_i** is the quantity of cargo *i* (in tonnes) and **D_i** is the average voyage distance in nautical miles. Multiplying volume by distance gives **tonne-miles**, the universal unit of shipping demand.

### 2.2 The Supply Side — Q_s(F, C, B, L, R)

The shipping supply function is:

$$Q_s = f(F, C, B, L, R, \tau)$$

Where:
- **F** = Freight rate (USD/tonne)
- **C** = Newbuilding price (USD/DWT)
- **B** = Bunker price (USD/MT)
- **L** = Labour cost (wages of officers and crew)
- **R** = Scrap price (USD/LDT — Light Displacement Tonnage)
- **τ** = Lead time lag (months from order to delivery)

The stock-flow identity of shipping supply is the **most-tested equation** in KTU 2024 schemes:

$$S_{t+1} = S_t + N_t - A_t - L_t$$

Where:
- **S_t** = World fleet size at time *t* (in DWT or TEU)
- **N_t** = Newbuilding deliveries in period *t*
- **A_t** = Additions from second-hand purchases (fleet transfer)
- **L_t** = Losses through scrapping, casualties, and conversions

### 2.3 The Freight Rate Determination — Three Hierarchies of Markets

KTU Module 4 typically tests the **four-tier market structure**:

1. **Sale & Purchase Market** — buying/selling of ships as assets.
2. **Newbuilding Market** — ordering of ships from shipyards.
3. **Charter Market (Spot + Period)** — hiring of ships.
4. **Cargo Market** — buying/selling the goods being shipped.

The freight rate is the **price discovered in tier 3**, but is influenced by tier 1, 2, and 4 dynamics.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **high-yield cheat sheet** students should memorise for the ESE. Note: all mathematical "or" symbols use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Identity | Units | Cognitive Level (Bloom) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equilibrium freight rate | $Q_d(F^*) = Q_s(F^*)$ | USD/tonne | Apply |
| 2 | Tonne-mile demand | $TM = \sum Q_i \cdot D_i$ | tonne-nm | Apply |
| 3 | Fleet evolution | $S_{t+1} = S_t + N_t - A_t - L_t$ | DWT | Analyse |
| 4 | Voyage P&L | $\text{Net} = F \cdot Q - (B + P + C) \cdot D$ | USD/voyage | Apply |
| 5 | Time-charter equivalent | $TCE = \frac{F \cdot Q - V}{(D/24)}$ | USD/day | Apply |
| 6 | Operating cost (daily) | $O = M + C + L + I$ | USD/day | Remember |
| 7 | Bunker cost | $B = F_b \cdot P_{bunker} \cdot \frac{D}{V_s}$ | USD | Apply |
| 8 | Break-even rate | $F_{be} = \frac{O + B + P}{Q}$ | USD/tonne | Evaluate |
| 9 | Price elasticity of demand | $E_d = \frac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta F / F}$ | unitless | Analyse |
| 10 | Coefficient of variation (volatility) | $CV = \frac{\sigma(F)}{\mu(F)}$ | unitless | Evaluate |
| 11 | Vessel utilisation | $U = \frac{\text{Laden days}}{\text{Total days}}$ | % | Apply |
| 12 | Capacity utilisation | $U_c = \frac{\text{Tonne-miles used}}{\text{Tonne-miles available}}$ | % | Apply |
| 13 | Orderbook ratio | $OBR = \frac{\text{Orderbook (DWT)}}{\text{Existing fleet (DWT)}}$ | % | Analyse |
| 14 | Return on investment | $ROI = \frac{\text{Annual TCE} - \text{Opex}}{\text{Asset value}}$ | % | Evaluate |
| 15 | Lay-up decision rule | Hire Rate $\vert$ Operating Cost | USD/day | Evaluate |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Model

The supply-demand model is the **bedrock tool** used by:

- **Chartering desks** of shipowners (Maersk, MSC, COSCO) to decide whether to fix ships on voyage or time charter.
- **Investment banks** (Frontline, Clarksons Securities) to value shipping equities and bonds.
- **Shipbuilders** (HD Hyundai, CSSC) to decide which vessel class to design next.
- **Policy makers** (IMO, EU Commission) to assess the impact of environmental rules (e.g., CII, EU ETS) on fleet supply.
- **Commodity traders** (Cargill, Trafigura) to hedge freight exposure.

### 2.6 Why Supply is Inelastic — The Shipbuilding Time Lag

The KTU examiner loves the **"investment cycle"** diagram. The lag between order and delivery is:

$$\tau_{delivery} = \tau_{design} + \tau_{steel} + \tau_{outfit} + \tau_{trials}$$

Typical values for a Capesize bulker in 2024:
- **Design & contracting:** 3–6 months
- **Steel cutting & hull erection:** 12–18 months
- **Outfitting & engine integration:** 6–9 months
- **Sea trials & delivery:** 2–3 months
- **Total:** 24–36 months

Because of this lag, a freight rate boom in 2025 cannot be satisfied by new tonnage until 2027–2028, by which time demand may have already collapsed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Linear Supply-Demand Equilibrium

Assume the simplest case taught in introductory KTU modules: **linear supply and demand functions**. This is a common 7-mark sub-part question.

Let the demand function be:

$$Q_d = a - bF$$

where $a > 0$ is the autonomous demand intercept and $b > 0$ is the slope (sensitivity to freight rate). Note: as freight rate **F** rises, quantity demanded **falls**, hence the negative sign.

Let the supply function be:

$$Q_s = c + dF$$

where $c > 0$ is the autonomous supply intercept (vessels available even at zero freight, e.g., strategic fleet) and $d > 0$ is the supply slope.

**Step 1 — Set $Q_d = Q_s$ for market equilibrium.**

$$a - bF^* = c + dF^*$$

**Step 2 — Group F\* terms on one side.**

$$a - c = dF^* + bF^*$$

**Step 3 — Factor F\*.**

$$a - c = (d + b) F^*$$

**Step 4 — Solve for equilibrium freight rate.**

$$F^* = \frac{a - c}{b + d}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back to get equilibrium quantity.**

$$Q^* = a - b \cdot \frac{a - c}{b + d}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the equilibrium quantity.**

$$Q^* = \frac{a(b + d) - b(a - c)}{b + d}$$

**Step 7 — Expand the numerator.**

$$Q^* = \frac{ab + ad - ab + bc}{b + d}$$

**Step 8 — Cancel common terms.**

$$Q^* = \frac{ad + bc}{b + d}$$

**Result — Equilibrium pair.**

$$F^* = \frac{a - c}{b + d}, \quad Q^* = \frac{ad + bc}{b + d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for the KTU Examiner:** If $a > c$ (i.e., demand intercept is above the supply intercept), $F^* > 0$ and a positive freight rate is established. If $a < c$, the market cannot clear at a positive rate — this represents a depressed market with idle tonnage. The denominator $b + d$ shows that higher price sensitivity (steeper curves) **flattens** freight rate volatility.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voyage Net Profit (VNP) Identity

A classic 7-mark KTU problem: *"A Capesize bulker is fixed on a voyage charter from Tubarão (Brazil) to Qingdao (China). Calculate the net profit per voyage."*

**Step 1 — Identify the inputs.**
- Cargo quantity $Q = 180{,}000$ tonnes (iron ore)
- Freight rate $F = 25$ USD/tonne
- Voyage distance $D = 11{,}000$ nautical miles
- Vessel speed $V_s = 14$ knots
- Bunker consumption $P_{bunker} = 35$ MT/day
- Bunker price $F_b = 600$ USD/MT
- Port costs (loading + discharging) $P = 250{,}000$ USD
- Other voyage costs (canals, agency) $C = 150{,}000$ USD

**Step 2 — Compute voyage duration in days.**

$$T = \frac{D}{V_s \cdot 24} = \frac{11{,}000}{14 \cdot 24}$$

$$T = \frac{11{,}000}{336} \approx 32.74 \text{ days}$$

**Step 3 — Compute gross freight revenue.**

$$R = F \cdot Q = 25 \times 180{,}000 = 4{,}500{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total bunker cost.**

$$B = F_b \cdot P_{bunker} \cdot T = 600 \times 35 \times 32.74$$

$$B = 21{,}000 \times 32.74 \approx 687{,}540 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 5 — Compute total voyage cost (bunker + port + canal/agency).**

$$V = B + P + C = 687{,}540 + 250{,}000 + 150{,}000$$

$$V = 1{,}087{,}540 \text{ USD}$$

**Step 6 — Compute voyage net profit.**

$$\text{Net} = R - V = 4{,}500{,}000 - 1{,}087{,}540$$

$$\text{Net} = 3{,}412{,}460 \text{ USD per voyage}$$

**Step 7 — Compute time-charter equivalent (TCE) per day.**

$$TCE = \frac{R - V}{T} = \frac{3{,}412{,}460}{32.74} \approx 104{,}230 \text{ USD/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A TCE of USD 104,230/day is **above operating cost** (typically ~USD 9,000–11,000/day for a Capesize) and **above the 30-year average of ~USD 18,000/day**. The vessel is in a **profitable regime**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Elasticity of Shipping Demand

The price elasticity of demand for shipping is defined as:

$$E_d = \frac{\partial Q_d}{\partial F} \cdot \frac{F}{Q_d}$$

For the linear demand $Q_d = a - bF$:

$$E_d = -b \cdot \frac{F}{a - bF}$$

**Step 1 — Identify mid-point for evaluation.**

At $F = F^*/2 = \frac{a - c}{2(b + d)}$:

$$E_d = -b \cdot \frac{(a - c)}{2(b + d) (a - b \cdot \frac{a - c}{2(b + d)})}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the denominator.**

The denominator becomes $\frac{2a(b+d) - b(a-c)}{2(b+d)} = \frac{2ab + 2ad - ab + bc}{2(b+d)} = \frac{ab + 2ad + bc}{2(b+d)}$.

**Step 3 — Final elasticity expression at mid-point.**

$$E_d = -\frac{b(a - c)}{ab + 2ad + bc}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Inference:** Dry bulk shipping demand is generally **inelastic** in the short run ($E_d \vert \in (0, 1)$) because iron ore and coal cannot easily switch transport mode. Container shipping demand is more elastic ($E_d \vert \in (1, 3)$) because cargo can shift to road, rail, or air in high-value segments.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Market Outcomes by Cycle Stage

| Cycle Stage | Demand | Supply | Freight Rate | TCE | Owner Action | Charterer Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Boom / Peak | Rising | Tight | Very High | $$$$ | Order new ships, charter out long-term | Scramble to fix ships, accept high rates |
| Cool-down | Stable | Rising (deliveries) | Falling | $$ | Delay new orders | Negotiate lower rates, use period cover |
| Trough | Falling | Excess | Very Low | $ | Lay up, scrap, sell | Buy time charters at deep discount |
| Recovery | Rising | Stable | Rising | $$ | Re-activate, fix period charters | Hedge forward, secure tonnage |

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — The Orderbook-to-Fleet Ratio (OBR) and Future Supply Pressure

$$OBR_t = \frac{O_t}{S_t}$$

Where $O_t$ is the orderbook (DWT on order) and $S_t$ is the existing fleet. A useful rule-of-thumb relationship used by KTU examiners:

$$\Delta S_{t+1} \approx \frac{O_t \cdot \theta}{S_t}$$

Where $\theta$ is the slippage/cancellation coefficient (typically 0.20–0.35 for bulkers; 0.10–0.20 for tankers). Empirical studies show that an OBR above **20%** historically foreshadows freight rate weakness **2 years ahead**, while an OBR below **8%** signals future tightness.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Shipping Market Supply-Demand Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[World Trade and GDP Growth] --> B[Demand for Tonne Miles]
    C[Newbuilding Prices] --> D[Supply of Tonnage]
    E[Scrap Prices] --> D
    F[Bunker and Crew Costs] --> D
    G[Lead Time Lag 24 to 36 months] --> D
    B --> H{Charter Market Spot Period}
    D --> H
    H --> I[Freight Rate Discovery]
    I --> J[BDI SCFI BCTI Indices]
    J --> K[Owner Investment Decision]
    K --> L[Orderbook]
    L --> D
    I --> M[Charterer Hedging Decision]
    M --> B
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DMD[Demand Block]
        D1[Cargo Volume Q]
        D2[Voyage Distance L]
        D3[Substitution Effect]
        D4[Trade Policy]
        D1 --> DD[Aggregate Demand Qd]
        D2 --> DD
        D3 --> DD
        D4 --> DD
    end
    subgraph SUP[Supply Block]
        S1[Existing Fleet S]
        S2[Newbuildings N]
        S3[Second Hand A]
        S4[Scrap and Loss L]
        S1 --> SS[Aggregate Supply Qs]
        S2 --> SS
        S3 --> SS
        S4 --> SS
    end
    subgraph EQ[Equilibrium Engine]
        DD --> EQM
        SS --> EQM
        EQM[Market Clearing F* and Q*] --> FRED[Freight Rate F*]
        EQM --> QST[Quantity Traded Q*]
    end
    subgraph FB[Feedback Loops]
        FRED --> ORI[Owner Order Decision]
        ORI --> S2
        FRED --> CHR[Charterer Route Choice]
        CHR --> D1
    end
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Shipping Market Cycle (The Tramp Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST1[Recovery: rates rising from trough] --> ST2[Boom: peak rates, orderbook explodes]
    ST2 --> ST3[Over-supply: deliveries flood market]
    ST3 --> ST4[Depression: rates below opex, scrapping begins]
    ST4 --> ST1
    ST2 -.-> EVT1[High OBR 20 to 30 percent]
    ST4 -.-> EVT2[High scrapping 5 to 8 percent of fleet]
    ST1 -.-> EVT3[Low OBR below 10 percent]
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Tree for a Shipowner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Spot rate greater than operating cost}
    Q -->|Yes| R1[Operate vessel, fix spot]
    Q -->|No| S{Spot rate greater than minimum operating cost}
    S -->|Yes| R2[Operate at slow steaming, minimise cost]
    S -->|No| T{Long term expectation positive}
    T -->|Yes| R3[Hot lay up, preserve option value]
    T -->|No| U{Vessel age over 25 years}
    U -->|Yes| R4[Sell for scrap]
    U -->|No| R5[Cold lay up, wait for recovery]
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Information Flow in Freight Rate Discovery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Charterer] --> B1[Chartering Broker]
    A2[Shipowner] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Main Shipping Exchange e.g. Baltic Exchange]
    C1 --> D1[Index Compiler e.g. Baltic Exchange Information Services]
    D1 --> E1[Index Publication e.g. BDI, BCTI, SCFI]
    E1 --> F1[Market Participants see price]
    F1 --> A1
    F1 --> A2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*"What is meant by 'derived demand' in the context of the shipping market?"*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Derived demand refers to the fact that ships are not demanded for their own sake; they are demanded **only because** there is a need to transport raw materials, energy, and finished goods across oceans. Shipping demand is therefore a *function of world trade*, world industrial production, and commodity prices. If world trade falls, shipping demand falls even if freight rates are attractive, because the underlying cargo is simply not moving. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for linking to world trade, 1 for example.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*"Define 'orderbook' and 'orderbook-to-fleet ratio' (OBR). State one implication of a high OBR."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **orderbook** is the total tonnage (in DWT, TEU, or CGT) that has been contracted at shipyards but not yet delivered. (1 mark)
- The **orderbook-to-fleet ratio (OBR)** is defined as $OBR = \frac{\text{Orderbook (DWT)}}{\text{Existing fleet (DWT)}}$, expressed as a percentage. (1 mark)
- A **high OBR** (e.g., > 20%) implies that a large wave of new tonnage will hit the market in 24–36 months, exerting **downward pressure on freight rates** in the near future. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*(a)* Derive the equilibrium freight rate and quantity under linear supply and demand assumptions. State the assumptions. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A Panamax bulk carrier is fixed to carry 75,000 MT of coal from Richards Bay (South Africa) to Mumbai (India). The freight rate is USD 18/MT. Voyage distance is 4,500 nautical miles. Vessel speed is 12 knots. Bunker consumption is 28 MT/day at USD 620/MT. Port costs (loading + discharging) total USD 180,000. Canal and miscellaneous costs are USD 60,000. Compute the **(i)** voyage duration, **(ii)** gross freight revenue, **(iii)** total voyage cost, **(iv)** voyage net profit, and **(v)** time-charter equivalent (TCE) per day. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*(a) Derivation (7 marks):*

**Assumption 1** — Demand is linear: $Q_d = a - bF$ with $a, b > 0$. (1 mark)
**Assumption 2** — Supply is linear: $Q_s = c + dF$ with $c, d > 0$. (1 mark)
**Assumption 3** — Market is perfectly competitive with no transaction costs, no port congestion, and no information asymmetry. (1 mark)

Equilibrium condition:

$$Q_d = Q_s \Rightarrow a - bF = c + dF$$

Rearranging:

$$a - c = (b + d)F^*$$

Solving:

$$F^* = \frac{a - c}{b + d}$$

Substituting back:

$$Q^* = \frac{ad + bc}{b + d}$$

(4 marks: 2 for derivation, 1 for F\*, 1 for Q\*)

---

*(b) Numerical (7 marks):*

**(i) Voyage duration:**

$$T = \frac{D}{V_s \cdot 24} = \frac{4{,}500}{12 \cdot 24} = \frac{4{,}500}{288} = 15.625 \text{ days}$$

(1 mark)

**(ii) Gross freight revenue:**

$$R = F \cdot Q = 18 \times 75{,}000 = 1{,}350{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

(1 mark)

**(iii) Total voyage cost:**

Bunker cost:

$$B = 620 \times 28 \times 15.625 = 17{,}360 \times 15.625 = 271{,}250 \text{ USD}$$

Total voyage cost:

$$V = B + P + C = 271{,}250 + 180{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 511{,}250 \text{ USD}$$

(2 marks)

**(iv) Voyage net profit:**

$$\text{Net} = R - V = 1{,}350{,}000 - 511{,}250 = 838{,}750 \text{ USD}$$

(1 mark)

**(v) Time-charter equivalent:**

$$TCE = \frac{R - V}{T} = \frac{838{,}750}{15.625} \approx 53{,}680 \text{ USD/day}$$

(2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Common mistake 1** — Forgetting to multiply speed (knots) by 24 to convert to nautical miles per day. **(–1 mark)**
> 2. **Common mistake 2** — Including only bunker cost and forgetting port + canal/agency costs in "total voyage cost". **(–1 mark)**
> 3. **Common mistake 3** — Dividing gross freight by voyage days (not net profit) to compute TCE. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Common mistake 4** — Using the wrong unit for bunker (per day vs per voyage). State the assumption clearly.
> 5. **Common mistake 5** — In part (a), failing to list the assumptions explicitly; KTU 2024 scheme requires 1 mark for assumptions in any derivation question.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
*(a)* Explain the **four main determinants** of shipping supply and discuss why shipping supply is **inelastic in the short run but elastic in the long run**. Use a suitable diagram in your explanation. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A shipowner is considering whether to **(i)** accept a one-year time charter at USD 18,000/day or **(ii)** continue operating in the spot market, where current TCE earnings are USD 12,500/day. The vessel's operating cost is USD 7,500/day. The owner expects spot rates to fall to USD 9,000/day in 6 months. Using the supply-demand model logic, advise the owner and compute the **expected annual net cash flow** under both options, ignoring discounting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*(a) Determinants of shipping supply (7 marks):*

The four main determinants are:

1. **Existing fleet size (S_t):** the current world fleet measured in DWT or TEU. This is the dominant short-run supply variable. (1.5 marks)
2. **Newbuilding deliveries (N_t):** ships under construction at yards, delivered with a 24–36 month lag. (1.5 marks)
3. **Scrapping and losses (L_t):** vessels sold for demolition or lost at sea; increases with age and weak freight markets. (1 mark)
4. **Productivity and speed:** vessels can "supply" more tonne-miles by steaming faster, reducing port days, or carrying more cargo. (1 mark)

**Short-run inelasticity:** Because (1) ships are extremely long-lived assets (25–30 years), (2) new buildings take 2–3 years to deliver, and (3) scrapping requires cash and is irreversible in the short term, the supply curve is nearly **vertical** in the 0–12 month horizon. A freight rate spike cannot summon new tonnage. (1 mark)

**Long-run elasticity:** Over 5–7 years, owners can order new ships, retire old ones, and even change flag/registry. The supply curve becomes **flatter (more elastic)**, although it is still less elastic than in most commodity markets. (1 mark)

---

*(b) Numerical advice (7 marks):*

**Option (i) — Time charter at USD 18,000/day for 1 year:**

Annual net cash flow:

$$NCF_1 = (F_{tc} - O) \cdot 365 = (18{,}000 - 7{,}500) \cdot 365$$

$$NCF_1 = 10{,}500 \times 365 = 3{,}832{,}500 \text{ USD/year}$$

(2 marks)

**Option (ii) — Spot market with expected fall at month 6:**

First 6 months at USD 12,500/day:

$$E_1 = (12{,}500 - 7{,}500) \cdot 182 = 5{,}000 \cdot 182 = 910{,}000 \text{ USD}$$

Next 6 months at USD 9,000/day:

$$E_2 = (9{,}000 - 7{,}500) \cdot 183 = 1{,}500 \cdot 183 = 274{,}500 \text{ USD}$$

Total:

$$NCF_2 = 910{,}000 + 274{,}500 = 1{,}184{,}500 \text{ USD/year}$$

(3 marks)

**Decision rule:** Compare NCF_1 vs NCF_2. Time charter yields **USD 3,832,500** vs spot expectation of **USD 1,184,500**. The time charter is preferred by **USD 2,648,000** per year, and additionally locks in **price certainty** (zero freight rate volatility). **Recommendation: Accept the time charter.** (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Always state the **decision rule** explicitly before computing — KTU's 2024 scheme rewards process. **(–1 mark if missing)**
> 2. Forgot to multiply by **365 days** (or 6×30 + 6×31 if you split) — **(–1 mark)**
> 3. Did not subtract operating cost from hire rate — **(–2 marks)**
> 4. In part (a), the diagram is **mandatory** for full marks in KTU 2024. A vertical SR supply curve + flat LR supply curve earns the 1 diagram mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 4:**

- [x] Shipping is a **derived demand** market — driven by world trade, GDP, and commodity prices.
- [x] Demand is measured in **tonne-miles** = volume × distance.
- [x] Supply is measured in **DWT** (bulk/tanker) or **TEU** (container).
- [x] The **fleet identity** $S_{t+1} = S_t + N_t - A_t - L_t$ is the single most important equation.
- [x] **Short-run supply is inelastic** due to 24–36 month newbuilding lag.
- [x] **Long-run supply is more elastic** due to ordering, scrapping, and productivity changes.
- [x] **Freight rate equilibrium** occurs where $Q_d(F^*) = Q_s(F^*)$.
- [x] **Voyage net profit** = Gross freight – (Bunker + Port + Canal/Agency).
- [x] **TCE (Time-Charter Equivalent)** = (Gross freight – Voyage costs) / Voyage days.
- [x] **Break-even rate** $F_{be} = \frac{O + B + P}{Q}$ — below this, the ship loses money.
- [x] **Lay-up decision rule:** Spot rate < Minimum operating cost ⇒ lay up; > operating cost ⇒ operate.
- [x] **Orderbook-to-Fleet Ratio (OBR)** is the leading indicator of future supply; **> 20%** bearish, **< 8%** bullish.
- [x] Demand elasticity: **Dry bulk** is inelastic (iron ore, coal); **Containers** are more elastic (manufactured goods).
- [x] Shipping market cycles: **Recovery → Boom → Over-supply → Depression → Recovery**.
- [x] Indices to remember: **BDI, BCI, BPI, BSI, BHSI, BDTI, BCTI, SCFI, CCFI**.
- [x] Always convert knots × 24 to get nautical miles per day in voyage calculations.
- [x] State **assumptions** explicitly in derivation questions — KTU 2024 reserves marks for this.
- [x] Always include a **diagram** (SR vs LR supply) for full marks in essay-type questions.
- [x] In decision-making problems, **state the decision rule** before computing the answer.
- [x] Remember the **four-tier market structure**: Sale & Purchase, Newbuilding, Charter, Cargo.
- [x] Shipping is **capital-intensive**, **internationally mobile**, and **regulated** by IMO, flag states, and classification societies.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The four shipping markets: freight market, sale and purchase market, newbuilding market and demolition market
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** The four shipping markets: freight market, sale and purchase market, newbuilding market and demolition market # MODULE 4 — THE FOUR SHIPPING MARKETS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview The international shipping industry is not a single monolithic market. It is a **cluster of four closely interlinked, yet functionally distinct, secondary markets** that together determine the economics, structure, and liquidity of global maritime trade. KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 requires a clear conceptual understanding of each of these markets, the participants, and the way they interact. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PENST863 Module 4):** > The shipping markets comprise four sub-markets: > **(i) Freight Market** (where transport services are bought and sold), > **(ii) Sale and Purchase (S&P) Market** (where existing ships are traded as assets), > **(iii) Newbuilding Market** (where new ships are ordered from shipyards), and > **(iv) Demolition (Scrap) Market** (where end-of-life vessels are sold for breaking). ### 1.1 The Freight Market — Definition The **freight market** is the marketplace in which the *service* of sea transportation is bought and sold. The "product" traded is **tonnage capacity over time** (i.e., ship-space), not the ship itself. Charterers (cargo owners) hire vessels from shipowners in return for **freight** (the hire payment). > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Think of the freight market like the **Ola/Uber ride-hailing market**. You (the cargo owner) need to go from Kochi to Rotterdam. Instead of buying a car (the ship), you simply hire a vehicle for the journey and pay a fare (freight). The driver (shipowner) provides the service, fuel (bunker), and crew. ### 1.2 The Sale and Purchase (S&P) Market — Definition The **S&P market** is the secondary asset market where *used (second-hand) ships* change ownership between shipowners, investors, or speculators. Here, the ship itself is the commodity. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** The S&P market is like the **used-car market** (Cars24, Spinny). A Capesize bulk carrier built in 2010 is bought and sold like a 10-year-old Maruti Swift — valued on age, condition, remaining useful life, and current resale demand. ### 1.3 The Newbuilding Market — Definition The **newbuilding market** is the primary asset market where *brand-new ships* are ordered from shipyards. Contracts are placed 2–3 years in advance of delivery. The price is paid in instalments linked to construction milestones (keel-laying, launching, delivery). > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** This is like **booking a brand-new Tesla or flat under construction**. You pay a token now, another instalment when the foundation is laid (keel-laying), another when the structure is ready (launch), and the final amount on handover (delivery) — typically 24–36 months later. ### 1.4 The Demolition (Scrap) Market — Definition The **demolition market** (also called the scrap or breaking market) is where *end-of-life vessels* are sold — usually to breakers in **India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, and China** — to be dismantled for steel recycling and reusable machinery/equipment. > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** This is the **vehicle-scrapping policy market** in India. A 25-year-old rusted truck, no longer roadworthy, is sold to a scrap dealer for the **lightweight tonnage (LDT)** price of its steel — the older the ship, the higher the steel content per ton earned. ### 1.5 The Four Markets at a Glance | Market | What is Traded | Buyer | Seller | Key Price Term | |---|---|---|---|---| | Freight | Transport service | Charterer | Shipowner | Freight rate ($/ton) | | S&P | Second-hand ship | Buyer-investor | Selling owner | Ship price ($M) | | Newbuilding | Brand-new ship contract | Shipowner | Shipyard | Newbuilding price ($M) | | Demolition | End-of-life ship for scrap | Breaker | Shipowner | LDT price ($/LDT) | > [!IMPORTANT] > All four markets are governed by the **Law of Supply and Demand**, but each responds to *different* economic signals. The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to clearly identify **which market reacts to which signal**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Notes ## 2.1 The Freight Market — Deep Analysis The freight market is the **largest and most volatile** of the four. It is essentially a service market where supply (tonnage) and demand (cargo) interact to set freight rates. ### 2.1.1 Sub-Segments of the Freight Market - **Voyage Charter Market** — A single trip; the shipowner bears all costs (bunker, port charges) and earns freight on cargo delivered. - **Time Charter Market** — The ship is hired for a specific period (months/years); the charterer pays hire and bears voyage costs (bunker, port dues). - **Bareboat Charter Market** — The charterer takes full operational control of the ship, including manning and technical management. - **Contract of Affreightment (COA)** — A long-term cargo supply agreement, not tied to a specific ship. - **Spot Market** — Single, one-off fixtures; rates fluctuate daily with the **Baltic Exchange** indices (BDI, BDI, BCI, BPI, BSI, BHSI). ### 2.1.2 Key Participants - **Shipowners** (supply side) - **Charterers** — industrial cargo owners (e.g., coal importers, oil majors) or **trader-operators** (Cargill, Trafigura, Glencore) - **Shipbrokers** — intermediaries (Clarksons, Platou, Fearnleys) - **P&I Clubs** and **H&M underwriters** — risk-bearers ### 2.1.3 Determinants of Freight Rates > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The single most examinable concept in this section. - **Demand for sea transport** — World GDP growth, trade volumes, commodity cycles - **Supply of tonnage** — Orderbook, deliveries, slippage, scrapping - **Bunker fuel prices** — A vessel may be uneconomic if Bunker prices are high and freight is low - **Port congestion** — Adds effective tonnage scarcity - **Geopolitical events** — Sanctions, canal closures (e.g., Suez 2021, Red Sea 2024) - **Seasonality** — Grain harvests, winter ice in Baltic - **Currency fluctuations** — USD-denominated freight vs local port costs ### 2.1.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Freight Calculations | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Voyage Freight | Total Voyage Revenue (TVR) | USD | Quoted per metric ton | | Time Charter Equivalent | TCE = (Gross Revenue − Voyage Costs) / Days | USD/day | Allows comparison across voyage charters | | Hire under Time Charter | Hire = Daily Rate × Days | USD | Bunker paid by charterer | | Bunker Consumption | B = Daily Cons. × Days × Bunker Price | USD | Major cost driver | | Laytime Demurrage | Demurrage = (Days Used − Laytime Allowed) × Demurrage Rate | USD | Paid to shipowner | | Despatch | Despatch = (Laytime − Days Used) × Half Demurrage Rate | USD | Paid to charterer | **Critical note:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values — for example, Demurrage = `(Days Used − Allowed) × Rate` with **no vertical pipes** inside table cells. ### 2.2 The Sale and Purchase (S&P) Market ### 2.2.1 Nature of the S&P Market - It is a **thin, professional market** — relatively few transactions but involving huge capital (VLCCs may be $80–120 M). - Conducted through **shipbrokers** who maintain inventories of vessels for sale. - Standard sale form is the **Norwegian Sale Form 1993 (NSF 1993)** or **Memorandum of Agreement (MoA)**. - Payment usually involves an **initial deposit (10%)** via escrow, with the balance paid on **delivery** of the vessel. ### 2.2.2 S&P Price Determinants > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Favourite:** Linkage of S&P prices to the newbuilding price and demolition price. - **Age of vessel** (older = cheaper, but the curve flattens after 15 years) - **Size and type** (Capesize vs Handysize) - **DWT (Deadweight Tonnage) capacity** - **Condition** (special/double survey due, class status) - **Earnings potential** — strong freight market → higher S&P prices - **Fuel compliance** — Scrubber-fitted, LNG-ready vessels command premium post-2020 (IMO 2020 sulphur cap) ### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Cheat — S&P Age Correction $$ \text{Indicative Price} = \text{Newbuilding Price} - (\text{Age in Years} \times \text{Depreciation}) + \text{Market Premium} $$ > Markets usually quote S&P prices as a **percentage of newbuilding price** — e.g., a 5-year-old vessel may be valued at **70–80% of newbuilding** in a rising market, or **50–60%** in a depressed one. ### 2.3 The Newbuilding Market ### 2.3.1 Nature and Structure - **Primary market** — new ships are produced, not resold. - **Contract-heavy, capital-intensive** — a VLCC newbuilding may cost **$115 M+**. - Dominant shipbuilding nations: **China, South Korea, Japan** (≈ 90% of global tonnage). - **Shipyard capacity is a major bottleneck** — when full, prices rise and delivery slots extend to 3–4 years. ### 2.3.2 The Newbuilding Contract (KTU Critical) A newbuilding contract is governed by the **SAJ (Standard Newbuilding Contract)** form (used in Japan) or **SAJ/KOEA (Korean)**, and increasingly the **NEWBUILDCON** standard form. **Key clauses KTU expects:** - **Contract Price** and **Currency** (usually JPY, KRW, or USD) - **Payment Schedule** — typically 5%/5%/5%/5%/80% (deposit/keel-laying/launch/completion/delivery) - **Delivery Date & Permissible Delay** - **Specifications and Guarantee of Performance** — speed and fuel consumption - **Liquidated Damages for Delay (LDD)** - **Refund Guarantee** — protects the buyer if the yard goes bankrupt - **Force Majeure** clauses - **Cancellation Rights** — if delivery exceeds permissible delay ### 2.3.3 Price Determinants in the Newbuilding Market - **Steel prices** (HRC steel plate) — primary raw material - **Labour costs** and shipyard productivity - **Yard orderbook vs capacity** — low capacity → high prices - **Currency exchange rates** (especially KRW, JPY vs USD) - **Demand for new tonnage** (freight market cycles) - **Environmental regulations** — alternative-fuel ships (LNG, methanol, ammonia) cost 20–40% more. ### 2.4 The Demolition (Scrap) Market ### 2.4.1 Nature and Players - **End-of-life market** — driven by the **economics of steel recycling**. - Major breakers: **Alang (India), Chattogram (Bangladesh), Gadani (Pakistan), Aliağa (Turkey), and Chinese yards**. - Price quoted as **USD per LDT (Light Displacement Tonnage)**. - LDT is the empty weight of the vessel — typically **25–30% of DWT** for a tanker and **20–25%** for a bulker. ### 2.4.2 Demolition Price Determinants > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trick Question:** *"Why does a shipowner choose to scrap rather than sell in S&P?"* > Because **Demolition Price > S&P Price** (i.e., scrap value exceeds secondhand market value). - **Steel prices in India/Pakistan/Bangladesh/China** (the 5-year bond) - **Tanker / bulker / container ship rules** — IMO Hong Kong Convention 2009 (ship recycling) - **Bunker/ROB on board** — the buyer often gets residual fuel as a bonus - **Presence of asbestos, hazardous materials** — reduces price - **Beaching vs dock vs slipway** — beaching (South Asia) typically gives the highest price - **Geopolitical factors** — Indian government's ship-recycling policy, EU regulations ### 2.4.3 KTU Formula — LDT-Based Scrap Value $$ \text{Scrap Value (USD)} = \text{LDT (in tons)} \times \text{Scrap Price (USD/LDT)} $$ For example: A 25-year-old VLCC with 35,000 LDT sold at $600/LDT → Scrap Value = **$21,000,000** (~ $21 M) ### 2.5 The Inter-Market Linkage (KTU-Favourite Diagram) > [!IMPORTANT] > The four markets form a **closed economic loop**. A ship is: > 1. **Born** in the Newbuilding Market (orderbook) > 2. **Operated** in the Freight Market (earns revenue) > 3. **Re-sold** in the S&P Market (changes hands) > 4. **Retired** in the Demolition Market (scrap) The **shipowner's lifecycle decision** is governed by the relative price signals from all four. This is the most KTU-exam-relevant concept in Module 4. | Lifecycle Stage | Market Used | Owner Decision | |---|---|---| | Acquisition | Newbuilding / S&P | Order new OR buy secondhand | | Operation | Freight | Voyage / Time / Bareboat charter | | Disposal | S&P OR Demolition | Sell for further use OR scrap | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Economic Utility - **Maritime finance** — S&P and newbuilding prices determine loan-to-value ratios in shipping finance. - **Portfolio management** — Greek and Norwegian owners use all four markets to time cycles. - **Hedge against rate volatility** — shipowning companies use forward freight agreements (FFAs) to hedge freight exposure. - **Environmental policy** — demolition tied to EU Ship Recycling Regulation and IMO 2020 sulphur cap. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples This section provides step-by-step analyses of typical KTU-style numerical and reasoning problems tied to the four shipping markets. --- ## 3.1 Example 1 — Voyage Freight Computation (Worked) **Question:** A Panamax bulker is fixed on a voyage charter from Haldia (India) to Rotterdam carrying 55,000 MT of coal at a freight rate of USD 32/MT. Voyage costs are: bunker USD 280,000, port charges USD 90,000, canal dues USD 40,000. The voyage takes 35 days. Calculate (i) Gross Revenue, (ii) Net Revenue, (iii) Time Charter Equivalent (TCE). **Step-by-Step Solution:** $$ \text{Gross Revenue} = 55{,}000 \text{ MT} \times \$32/\text{MT} = \$1{,}760{,}000 $$ $$ \text{Total Voyage Costs} = 280{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 40{,}000 = \$410{,}000 $$ $$ \text{Net Revenue (TVE)} = 1{,}760{,}000 - 410{,}000 = \$1{,}350{,}000 $$ $$ \text{TCE} = \frac{\text{Net Revenue}}{\text{Voyage Days}} = \frac{1{,}350{,}000}{35} = \$38{,}571 \text{ / day} $$ > **Interpretation:** The owner earns $38,571/day, which can be benchmarked against time-charter rates in the market to decide whether to fix the next voyage at a TCE or wait for a time charter. --- ## 3.2 Example 2 — Demolition Value Calculation **Question:** An Aframax tanker (built 1998) of 105,000 DWT is sold for demolition. Light Displacement Tonnage is estimated at 22,000 tons. The current Indian sub-continent scrap price is USD 580/LDT. Hazardous-material removal cost to seller is USD 200,000.
