Calculate net demolition proceeds. **Step-by-Step:** $$ \text{Gross Scrap Value} = 22{,}000 \times \$580 = \$12{,}760{,}000 $$ $$ \text{Net Scrap Value} = 12{,}760{,}000 - 200{,}000 = \$12{,}560{,}000 $$ $$ \text{Net per LDT} = \frac{12{,}560{,}000}{22{,}000} = \$570.9 / \text{LDT} $$ > **Interpretation:** The owner nets ~$12.56 M, which is often higher than what a 25-year-old Aframax would fetch in the S&P market — hence the decision to scrap. --- ## 3.3 Example 3 — S&P Age-Adjusted Valuation **Question:** A newbuilding Capesize bulker costs USD 65 M today. Using the rule of thumb that a vessel depreciates linearly over 20 years to a scrap residual of 15% of newbuilding, calculate the indicative S&P price for a 7-year-old vessel in a *neutral market*. Compare with a *rising market* where the depreciation factor is multiplied by 1.2. **Step-by-Step (Neutral Market):** $$ \text{Annual Depreciation} = \frac{65 - (0.15 \times 65)}{20} = \frac{65 - 9.75}{20} = \$2.76 \text{ M/year} $$ $$ \text{Value at 7 years} = 65 - (7 \times 2.76) = 65 - 19.32 = \$45.68 \text{ M} $$ **Rising Market (factor 1.2 → slower depreciation):** $$ \text{Adjusted Depreciation} = 2.76 \times 0.8333 = \$2.30 \text{ M/year} $$ $$ \text{Value at 7 years} = 65 - (7 \times 2.30) = 65 - 16.10 = \$48.90 \text{ M} $$ > **Interpretation:** The 7-year-old vessel is worth $45.7 M in a neutral cycle and $48.9 M in a rising market — a 7% premium purely on market sentiment. --- ## 3.4 Example 4 — Newbuilding Instalment Schedule (KTU Numerical) **Question:** A VLCC newbuilding contract price is USD 120 M with the standard payment pattern 20/20/20/20/20 (deposit, steel-cutting, keel-laying, launch, delivery). What amount is paid at each milestone? **Step-by-Step:** $$ \text{Each Instalment} = 120{,}000{,}000 \times 0.20 = \$24{,}000{,}000 $$ | Milestone | % Paid | Amount (USD M) | |---|---|---| | On signing | 20% | 24.0 | | Steel cutting | 20% | 24.0 | | Keel laying | 20% | 24.0 | | Launch | 20% | 24.0 | | On delivery | 20% | 24.0 | | **Total** | **100%** | **120.0** | > **KTU Insight:** The buyer must also secure a **Refund Guarantee** for the pre-delivery instalments. If the yard goes bankrupt, the bank refunds these amounts to the buyer. --- ## 3.5 Example 5 — Laytime & Demurrage **Question:** A vessel is fixed with allowed laytime of 5 days at the discharge port. Loading takes 6.5 days total. Demurrage rate is USD 25,000/day. Calculate demurrage payable to shipowner. **Step-by-Step:** $$ \text{Extra Days} = 6.5 - 5 = 1.5 \text{ days} $$ $$ \text{Demurrage} = 1.5 \times 25{,}000 = \$37{,}500 $$ > If the operation had finished in 4 days (1 day saved), **Despatch** = (1 × 12,500) = **$12,500** paid by the shipowner to the charterer (despatch is half demurrage by convention). --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Ship Disposal | Condition | Best Disposal Choice | |---|---| | Scrap price > S&P price | Demolition | | S&P price > Scrap price | Sale & Purchase | | S&P = Scrap, vessel > 25 yrs | Demolition (lower compliance cost) | | Vessel passes all surveys, < 15 yrs old | Sale & Purchase (recyclable life left) | | Environmental non-compliance, EU flagged | S&P (non-EU buyers will take it) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Lifecycle of a Ship Across the Four Markets ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Shipyard
Newbuilding Market] -->|Delivery
2-3 yrs| B[Operating Owner
Freight Market] B -->|Earnings:
Voyage / Time / Bareboat| C{Earnings Period
15-25 years} C -->|Strong freight
or aged vessel| D{Sale and
Purchase Market} C -->|End of life
scrap price high| E[Demolition
Market] D -->|Secondhand buyer| F[New Owner
Freight Market] D -->|Cascade sale
to smaller trade| G[Regional Trade] E -->|Steel and
machinery recycling| H[Steel Mills] F --> C classDef marketNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decisionNode fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef terminalNode fill:#E8F8E8,stroke:#2CA02C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,B,D,F marketNode class C decisionNode class E,G,H terminalNode ``` ## 4.2 Interaction Map — The Four Shipping Markets ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NB[Newbuilding Market] NB1[Shipyard builds new ship] NB2[Orderbook: 2-3 years ahead] NB3[Steel + Labour = Price] end subgraph FM[Freight Market] FM1[Voyage Charter] FM2[Time Charter] FM3[Bareboat Charter] FM4[Spot vs Period] FM5[Baltic Indices: BDI / BDI / BCTI] end subgraph SP[Sale and Purchase Market] SP1[Secondhand ships traded] SP2[Norwegian Saleform 1993] SP3[Brokers: Clarksons, Platou] SP4[10% deposit in escrow] end subgraph DM[Demolition Market] DM1[End-of-life vessels] DM2[LDT x Scrap Price = Value] DM3[Breakers: India / Bangladesh / Pakistan] end NB1 -->|Delivered vessel| FM1 FM1 -->|Strong market, owner invests| NB1 FM1 -->|Sale during operation| SP1 SP1 -->|Resale to another operator| FM1 FM1 -->|Aged, uneconomic| DM1 SP1 -->|Old tonnage no buyers| DM1 DM1 -->|Steel price spike| SP1 classDef marketBox fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class NB,FM,SP,DM marketBox ``` ## 4.3 Price Signal Flow — How the Markets React ```mermaid flowchart TD E1[World GDP Growth] -->|Demand for sea transport| F1[Freight Rates Rise] E2[Steel Plate Price HRC] -->|Higher input cost| N1[Newbuilding Price Rises] E3[Indian Subcontinent Demand] -->|Steel scrap demand| D1[Demolition Price Rises] E4[Orderbook Full] -->|Limited yard slots| N2[Newbuilding Price Rises] F1 -->|Strong earnings = can afford| S1[S&P Price Rises] F1 -->|Less incentive to scrap| D2[Demolition Price Falls] S1 -->|Asset values up, credit easier| N3[New orders increase] D1 -->|Old tonnage worth more scrap| S2[S&P of older ships rises] classDef inputNode fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef marketNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class E1,E2,E3,E4 inputNode class F1,N1,D1,N2,S1,D2,N3,S2 marketNode ``` ## 4.4 Decision Matrix for a Shipowner ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ship reaching
end of useful life] --> B{Scrap Price
vs S&P Price?} B -->|Scrap > S&P| C[Send to
Demolition Market] B -->|S&P > Scrap| D{Buyer available
in S&P market?} D -->|Yes| E[Negotiate MoA
with S&P buyer] D -->|No| F[Wait / Lay up
vessel] E --> G[Delivery &
Payment via escrow] C --> H[Cash inflow
USD per LDT] F --> I[Continue in
freight market] I --> B classDef decisionBox fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef actionBox fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#6A5ACD,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class B,D decisionBox class A,C,E,F,G,H,I actionBox ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q: Define the freight market and list any THREE sub-segments of it.** **Model Answer:** The **freight market** is the market where sea-transport services are bought and sold. The "product" traded is **tonnage capacity over time** (i.e., ship-space), not the ship itself. Sub-segments: 1. **Voyage Charter Market** — single-trip hiring 2. **Time Charter Market** — period-based hiring (months/years) 3. **Bareboat Charter Market** — full operational control given to the charterer *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for sub-segments)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q: What is the LDT-based pricing mechanism in the demolition market?** **Model Answer:** The demolition (scrap) market uses **LDT — Light Displacement Tonnage** as the basis of pricing. LDT is the empty weight of the vessel, typically **20–30% of DWT**. The price is quoted as **USD per LDT** and reflects **steel scrap prices in the buying country** (India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, China). The formula is: $$ \text{Scrap Value (USD)} = \text{LDT (tons)} \times \text{Scrap Price (USD/LDT)} $$ *(3 marks: 1 for defining LDT + 1 for mechanism + 1 for formula)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not confuse** the *Freight Market* (service) with the *S&P Market* (asset). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this. > 2. **Always state the price determinant** when explaining a market — vague answers score low. > 3. **In demolition questions**, you must mention LDT, not DWT. Many students wrongly quote scrap price in DWT. > 4. **In newbuilding questions**, mention **Refund Guarantee** — most students forget this critical clause. > 5. When listing factors, group them (demand-side / supply-side) for clarity. --- ### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]** **(a) Explain in detail the FOUR shipping markets, with their key participants and the price determinants in each. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The four shipping markets are: 1. **Freight Market** — Service market; trades sea-transport capacity. Participants: shipowners, charterers, shipbrokers. Determinants: world trade volumes, tonnage supply, bunker prices, port congestion, geopolitics. 2. **Sale & Purchase (S&P) Market** — Asset market for secondhand ships. Participants: shipowners, investors, financial buyers, S&P brokers. Determinants: vessel age, condition, earnings outlook, newbuilding price ratio, regulatory compliance (scrubber, dual-fuel). 3. **Newbuilding Market** — Primary market for newly-built ships. Participants: shipowners (buyers), shipyards (sellers), classification societies, financiers. Determinants: steel prices, yard capacity, orderbook, currency exchange rates, environmental regulations. 4. **Demolition (Scrap) Market** — End-of-life disposal market. Participants: shipowners, cash buyers, breakers (India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, China, Turkey). Determinants: steel scrap prices, vessel LDT, hazardous material content, IMO Hong Kong Convention compliance. *(7 marks: ~1.5 marks per market for definition, participants, and determinants)* --- **(b) Discuss the inter-linkage between the four shipping markets with a suitable example. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The four markets are **economically interlinked** through a ship's lifecycle. A new vessel: - **Starts** in the Newbuilding Market (orderbook) - **Operates** in the Freight Market (earns revenue) - **May be re-sold** via the S&P Market - **Ends** in the Demolition Market **Example:** A Capesize bulker ordered in 2010 for $80 M (newbuilding) earns high freight in 2010–14 (rate > $30,000/day). Owner sells in 2015 for $50 M (S&P) to a Greek operator. After 20 years, the vessel is sold for scrap at $580/LDT × 25,000 LDT = $14.5 M (demolition). **Key principles of inter-linkage:** - High freight → high S&P prices → more new orders → eventual oversupply - High scrap price → vessels scrapped early → freight tonnage reduces → freight rises - Low newbuilding prices → more orders → eventual freight downturn This is the **shipping cycle** — explained by **SS Yorque, Stopford, and others** as a self-correcting feedback loop. KTU 2024 expects students to recognize this **causal loop** in their answers. *(7 marks: 3 for inter-linkage explanation, 2 for example, 2 for cycle-theory connection)* --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]** **(a) Explain the newbuilding market in detail. Discuss the key clauses of a shipbuilding contract. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **The Newbuilding Market** is the primary asset market where new ships are constructed by shipyards. Dominant shipbuilders are in **China, South Korea, and Japan**. The contract is typically placed 2–3 years before delivery. **Key Clauses of a Newbuilding Contract (NEWBUILDCON / SAJ form):** | Clause | Significance | |---|---| | Contract Price & Currency | Defines total price in JPY/KRW/USD | | Payment Schedule | Usually 20/20/20/20/20 instalments | | Delivery Date | Latest permissible delivery date | | Permissible Delay | Yard can delay by X months without penalty | | Liquidated Damages for Delay | Yard pays LDD if delivery crosses permitted delay | | Refund Guarantee | Critical — bank guarantees refund of pre-delivery instalments if yard defaults | | Speed & Fuel Guarantee | Yard guarantees performance; penalty for shortfall | | Classification & Flag | Class society and flag state agreed | | Force Majeure | Excuses delay due to war, strikes, etc. | | Cancellation Right | Buyer can cancel if delivery crosses maximum delay | *(7 marks: 2 for newbuilding market description + 5 for clauses)* --- **(b) "A shipowner's decision to scrap or re-sell is purely economic." Critically examine this statement with reference to the demolition and S&P markets. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The statement is **broadly true but incomplete**. A shipowner's disposal decision is driven by multiple factors: **Economic factors (in support of the statement):** - If **Scrap Price > S&P Price**, demolition is chosen (e.g., 25-year-old VLCC scrapped for $20 M instead of sold for $12 M in S&P). - If **S&P Price > Scrap Price**, sale for further use is preferred. - Net Present Value (NPV) of continued operation vs disposal cost is the primary economic test. **Non-economic factors (refining the statement):** 1. **Regulatory compliance** — IMO 2020 sulphur cap, EEXI, CII rules may force older ships out of service regardless of price. 2. **Flag state restrictions** — some flags ban vessels over 25 years. 3. **Class survey costs** — Special Survey (every 5 years) may be uneconomic for old tonnage. 4. **Insurance** — P&I and H&M premiums spike after 20 years. 5. **Charterer preference** — major charterers (oil majors) refuse to charter aged vessels. 6. **ESG and CSR** — listed shipping companies face investor pressure to scrap polluting tonnage. **Conclusion:** While the *immediate trigger* is economic (price comparison), the *structural reason* is a combination of **economic, regulatory, and ESG factors**. KTU 2024 expects a **balanced, multi-dimensional** answer. *(7 marks: 3 for economic factors, 2 for non-economic, 2 for conclusion)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PENST863 Module 4** ### ⭐ The Four Markets — At a Glance - **Freight Market:** service of transport bought/sold; freight = hire/fare - **S&P Market:** secondhand ships traded as assets; NSF 1993, MoA, 10% escrow deposit - **Newbuilding Market:** new ships ordered from yards; 2–3 yr delivery; NEWBUILDCON/SAJ contract; **Refund Guarantee is critical** - **Demolition Market:** end-of-life vessels; price = **LDT × $/LDT**; breakers in India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, China, Turkey ### ⭐ Key Price Determinants (Memorize) - **Freight** ← world trade + tonnage supply + bunker + port congestion - **S&P** ← age + condition + newbuilding ratio + earnings outlook - **Newbuilding** ← steel + labour + yard capacity + currency - **Demolition** ← scrap steel price + LDT + hazardous material cost ### ⭐ Critical Formulas (Use Absolute Value Carefully) - Scrap Value = LDT × $/LDT - TCE = (Gross Revenue − Voyage Costs) / Days - Demurrage = (Days Used − Allowed) × Demurrage Rate - Despatch = (Laytime − Days Used) × (½ × Demurrage Rate) - Newbuilding Instalment = Total Price × Instalment % ### ⭐ Inter-Market Linkage (KTU Favourite) The four markets form a **ship's lifecycle loop**: **Newbuilding → Freight → S&P → Demolition** (or direct to Demolition). The shipping cycle is a self-correcting feedback loop: high freight → high S&P → new orders → oversupply → low freight → scrapping → tonnage shortage → high freight. ### ⭐ Standard Contract Forms to Remember - **Norwegian Sale Form 1993** — S&P - **NEWBUILDCON / SAJ** — Newbuilding - **GENCON 1994** — Voyage Charter - **NYPE 2015 / BALTIME 2001** — Time Charter - **BARECON 2017** — Bareboat Charter ### ⭐ Top 5 Examiner Traps 1. Confusing **freight market (service)** with **S&P market (asset)**. 2. Quoting demolition price in **DWT** instead of **LDT**. 3. Forgetting **Refund Guarantee** in newbuilding answers. 4. Saying "ship is bought in freight market" — it is *hired*, not bought. 5. Not distinguishing **Spot** from **Period** charter. ### ⭐ KTU 2024 Top Exam Tip Always end any answer on the four markets with a **closing line on the inter-linkage and the shipping cycle** — this is the single most-marked "synthesis" point in the 2024 scheme.

topics.title: Classification of costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST863 - MARITIME LAW AND SHIPPING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost Classification** is the systematic process of grouping costs according to their common characteristics, behavior patterns, traceability, function, or relevance to decision-making, in order to facilitate **cost control, pricing, profit planning, performance evaluation, and managerial decision-making** in a shipping organization.

In the context of **Maritime Law and Shipping Management**, the classification of costs is the foundational step of **Voyage Estimation, Vessel Operating Budgets, Freight Rate Determination, and Charter Party Negotiations** under both **Time Charter** and **Voyage Charter** frameworks. The **UNCTAD** and **BIMCO** standardized shipping cost nomenclatures are widely adopted by classification societies and ship operators globally.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Family Monthly Budget"

Imagine you are the **Chief Purser (CFO)** of a household running a cargo ship:
- Some bills are **fixed** (like the mortgage = ship loan EMI) — they don't change whether the ship sails or stays at port.
- Some bills **change with every voyage** (like fuel and port charges) — these rise and fall with the kilometers sailed.
- Some costs are **directly tied to a specific cargo** (loading/unloading labor for a particular consignment).
- Some are **shared by the whole fleet** (HO salaries, marketing, insurance).

Without **classification**, your accounts are a tangled mess. Classification is like sorting your mail — *bills, invitations, junk* — into separate trays. Once sorted, you can **plan, budget, and decide** intelligently.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Must-Know)

| Term | Meaning in Shipping Context |
|---|---|
| **Cost Object** | Anything for which costs are measured — e.g., a specific voyage, a vessel, a charter party, or a single cargo lot. |
| **Cost Centre** | A location/function where costs are accumulated — e.g., Engine Room, Bridge, Port Operations Office. |
| **Cost Unit** | The unit of measurement — e.g., **per nautical mile**, **per TEU**, **per voyage**, **per deadweight ton (DWT)**. |
| **Cost Behaviour** | How a cost responds to changes in activity level (miles sailed, days operated). |
| **Sunk Cost** | An irrecoverable past expenditure — *relevant* only to book-keepers, not to managers. |
| **Opportunity Cost** | The benefit foregone by choosing one alternative over the next best — the "shadow cost". |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly maps cost classification to **voyage estimation, charter party decisions, and financial reporting under UNCTAD conventions**. Memorize both the **classification basis** AND the **shipping-specific example** for each.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Classify Costs? (The "Five Whys")

A shipping manager classifies costs to:

1. **Decide Voyage Profitability** — distinguish voyage costs from time costs.
2. **Fix Competitive Freight Rates** — know the minimum variable cost to set a floor rate.
3. **Budget & Control** — separate fixed from variable for break-even analysis.
4. **Comply with Statutory Reporting** — IFRS / Companies Act / UNCTAD formats.
5. **Evaluate Managerial Performance** — assign controllable costs to specific departments (Engine, Deck, Catering).

### 2.2 Major Bases of Cost Classification

#### A. **By Behaviour (Activity-Based)** — *The Most Important for KTU*

| Type | Definition | Shipping Example | Behaviour Curve |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Cost** | Total remains constant within a relevant range of activity. | Ship loan EMI, vessel insurance, crew salaries (permanent), depreciation. | Horizontal straight line |
| **Variable Cost** | Total varies proportionately with activity; per-unit is constant. | Bunker fuel, port charges, agency fees, canal dues, fresh water. | Linear upward line through origin |
| **Semi-Variable (Mixed) Cost** | Has both fixed and variable components. | Telephone at HO (fixed line rent + call charges), vessel maintenance (standing cost + running cost). | Intercept on Y-axis, then slopes up |
| **Step-Fixed Cost** | Fixed in "steps" — jumps at capacity thresholds. | Hire of additional crew when 2nd vessel added; rent of 2nd warehouse. | Staircase pattern |

> [!TIP]
> **The "Relevant Range" Trap:** Fixed costs are only fixed *within* a relevant range of activity. Adding a new vessel shifts the fixed cost to a higher plateau.

#### B. **By Traceability / Directness**

- **Direct Cost** — Identifiable with one cost object. Example: Bunker consumed by *MV Kerala Voyager* on Voyage No. 17, Mumbai to Rotterdam.
- **Indirect Cost** — Common to several cost objects; needs apportionment. Example: Head Office rent shared by 5 vessels.

#### C. **By Function (in a Shipping Company)**

1. **Operating / Production Cost** — Running the ship.
2. **Administrative Cost** — HO expenses.
3. **Selling & Distribution Cost** — Freight canvassing, marketing, agency commissions.
4. **Finance Cost** — Interest on vessel loans.

#### D. **By Nature / Element** *(Prime Cost = Material + Labour + Direct Expenses)*

- **Material** — Bunkers, lubricants, spares, stores.
- **Labour** — Crew wages, port labour.
- **Expenses** — Insurance, depreciation, communication.

#### E. **By Decision Relevance (Managerial Costing)**

| Cost | KTU Definition | Shipping Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Sunk Cost** | Past, irrecoverable. Ignore for decisions. | Original purchase price of an old vessel now scrapped. |
| **Opportunity Cost** | Benefit of next-best alternative foregone. | If a vessel is chartered-in instead of used for owner's cargo, the lost freight earning. |
| **Differential (Incremental) Cost** | Difference in total cost between two alternatives. | Cost difference between going via Suez vs Cape route. |
| **Marginal Cost** | Cost of producing *one additional* unit. | Cost of carrying *one extra* tonne of cargo. |
| **Relevant Cost** | Future cost that differs between alternatives. | Bunker, port charges for a new voyage. |
| **Irrelevant Cost** | Doesn't change with the decision. | Sunk cost, committed fixed cost. |
| **Imputed / Notional Cost** | No cash outflow but a real cost. | Depreciation on an owner-operated vessel. |
| **Joint Cost** | Incurred in producing two or more products simultaneously. | Crude oil tanker — fuel oil + lubricants from same crude. |
| **Common Cost** | Shared across cost objects but separable. | Insurance premium for the whole fleet. |

#### F. **By Time Period (Capital vs Revenue)**

- **Capital Expenditure** — Benefit > 1 accounting period. *Example:* Purchase of vessel, dry-docking cost.
- **Revenue Expenditure** — Benefit ≤ 1 accounting period. *Example:* Bunker, voyage stores, port charges.

#### G. **Shipping-Specific Classification — The **TCV Framework** (Time · Capital · Voyage)

This is the **most exam-relevant framework** for a maritime management course:

| Cost Bucket | Components | Incurs When |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Cost (Capital Cost)** | Depreciation, interest on loan, insurance, crew wages (sometimes), administration overhead. | Ship is **owned**; incurred every day, sailing or at port. |
| **Operating Cost** | Manning, stores, lubricants, maintenance & repair (M&R), insurance, administration. | Every day; partly fixed, partly variable. |
| **Voyage Cost** | Bunker fuel, port charges, canal dues, pilotage, tugs, agency commission, cargo handling. | **Only** when the ship sails — purely variable. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Charter Equivalent (TCE)** formula unifies these classifications into a single comparable metric (covered in detail in Section 3).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TC = FC + VC$ | Total Cost = Fixed + Variable | Voyage cost build-up |
| 2 | $TC = FC + (VC_{u} \times Q)$ | Total Cost with variable per unit | Cost projection |
| 3 | $VC = VC_{u} \times Q$ | Total Variable Cost | Bunker planning |
| 4 | $AC = \dfrac{TC}{Q}$ | Average Cost per unit | Competitive quoting |
| 5 | $MC = \dfrac{\Delta TC}{\Delta Q}$ | Marginal Cost | Pricing extra cargo |
| 6 | $Contribution = Sales - VC$ | Sales minus variable cost | Profit-volume analysis |
| 7 | $CM_{ratio} = \dfrac{Contribution}{Sales} \times 100$ | Contribution Margin ratio | Pricing decisions |
| 8 | $BEP_{units} = \dfrac{FC}{SP - VC_{u}}$ | Break-even in units | Voyage viability |
| 9 | $BEP_{sales} = \dfrac{FC}{CM_{ratio}}$ | Break-even in revenue | Fleet planning |
| 10 | $TCE = \dfrac{Net\ Voyage\ Revenue - Voyage\ Costs}{Total\ Voyage\ Days}$ | Time Charter Equivalent | Cross-route comparison |
| 11 | $Opportunity\ Cost = Best\ Foregone\ Alternative\ Return$ | Shadow cost | In-house vs charter decision |
| 12 | $Sunk\ Cost = Irrelevant\ for\ decision$ | Always zero in decision analysis | Discontinue route decision |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **love** testing whether students confuse *sunk* with *committed fixed* costs. They are different! Sunk = past, gone. Committed fixed = future, contractual, but unavoidable.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Shipping

- **Freight Rate Setting:** Knowing the **marginal cost** allows a liner to set a rate that at least covers the extra cost of an additional container.
- **Chartering Decisions:** A shipowner choosing between a **Voyage Charter** and a **Time Charter** uses differential cost analysis.
- **Lay-up Decision:** If daily revenue < daily time cost, the vessel should be laid up — this is a sunk vs relevant cost application.
- **Route Selection:** Suez vs Cape — comparison of voyage cost differential.
- **Regulatory Disclosures:** IFRS 15 / 16 requires classification of voyage and time charter revenue streams correctly.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because "Classification of Costs" is a **categorical** topic rather than a calculus-heavy derivation, Section 3 focuses on (a) a **rigorous step-by-step algebraic derivation** of the **Time Charter Equivalent (TCE)** model — which is a KTU-favourite numerical question — and (b) an **analytical matrix** that walks through classifying a real shipping scenario.

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Derivation: TCE Rate from Classified Costs

**Problem Setup:** A bulk carrier operator provides the following classified cost data for Voyage No. 47 (Singapore → Hamburg, 35 days round-trip):

| Cost Item | Amount (USD) | Classified As |
|---|---|---|
| Bunker fuel consumed | 220,000 | Voyage cost (Variable) |
| Port charges (both ends) | 80,000 | Voyage cost (Variable) |
| Canal dues (Suez) | 45,000 | Voyage cost (Variable) |
| Agency commission (3.75% of freight) | 37,500 | Voyage cost (Variable) |
| Crew wages (full voyage) | 35,000 | Operating cost (Fixed) |
| Insurance (apportioned) | 15,000 | Time/Capital cost (Fixed) |
| Depreciation (apportioned) | 25,000 | Capital cost (Fixed) |
| Net freight revenue received | 1,000,000 | — |

**Step 1 — Identify the Voyage Costs (Variable) — these are *relevant* for voyage-level decisions.**

$$\begin{aligned}
VC_{voyage} &= \text{Bunker} + \text{Port charges} + \text{Canal dues} + \text{Agency commission} \\
&= 220{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 37{,}500 \\
&= 382{,}500\ USD
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Voyage costs are incurred **only when the ship moves** and **vary directly with distance/port calls**. Therefore, they are *revenue-earning-day* dependent. They are the **relevant costs** for a voyage-level decision.

**Step 2 — Identify the Time/Capital Costs (Fixed) — these are *not* relevant for voyage-level TCE.**

$$\begin{aligned}
FC_{time} &= \text{Crew wages} + \text{Insurance} + \text{Depreciation} \\
&= 35{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 25{,}000 \\
&= 75{,}000\ USD
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* These costs would have been incurred **even if the ship stayed at anchorage** for 35 days. They are *time-based*, not *voyage-based*.

**Step 3 — Compute the Voyage Surplus (Net Voyage Revenue − Voyage Costs).**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Voyage Revenue} &= \text{Freight} - \text{Agency Commission on Freight} \\
&= 1{,}000{,}000 - 37{,}500 \\
&= 962{,}500\ USD
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* The owner's *gross freight* is reduced by the broker's commission before the owner realises it. The *net* figure is what the company actually receives.

**Step 4 — Deduct Voyage Costs to get the Time-Charter Equivalent Gross.**

$$\begin{aligned}
TCE_{gross} &= \text{Net Voyage Revenue} - \text{Variable Voyage Costs (ex-commission)} \\
&= 962{,}500 - (220{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 45{,}000) \\
&= 962{,}500 - 345{,}000 \\
&= 617{,}500\ USD
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* The TCE gross represents what the voyage earned *over and above* the variable costs of moving — a synthetic "time-charter" rate applicable to the same vessel-days.

**Step 5 — Divide by Total Voyage Days to get Daily TCE Rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
TCE_{daily} &= \dfrac{TCE_{gross}}{\text{Voyage Days}} \\
&= \dfrac{617{,}500}{35} \\
&= 17{,}642.86\ USD/day
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Verify Profitability using the BEP framework.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Required Daily TCE} &= \dfrac{FC_{time}}{\text{Voyage Days}} + \text{Operating margin} \\
&= \dfrac{75{,}000}{35} + 1{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}142.86 + 1{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}142.86\ USD/day
\end{aligned}$$

*Logic:* Since the **actual TCE of 17,642.86 USD/day ≫ 3,142.86 USD/day**, the voyage is highly profitable on a relevant-cost basis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Students often subtract the *commission* twice (once in net freight, once in voyage costs). The commission is a *voyage cost*, but it has already been netted in Step 3. So, in Step 4, deduct only *non-commission* voyage costs from net revenue.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Classifying a Real Shipping Scenario

The following matrix applies **all 7 classification bases** to the same set of real shipping expenditure items, demonstrating how a single transaction can be classified in multiple ways.

| Expenditure Item | By Behaviour | By Traceability | By Function | By Nature | By Time | By Relevance | Shipping (TCV) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bunker fuel** | Variable | Direct | Operating | Material | Revenue | Relevant | Voyage |
| **Crew wages (permanent)** | Fixed (step) | Indirect | Operating | Labour | Revenue | Relevant (long-run) | Time / Operating |
| **Vessel loan EMI** | Fixed | Direct (to vessel) | Finance | Expense | Capital | Committed (relevant) | Capital (Time) |
| **Port charges** | Variable | Direct | Distribution | Expense | Revenue | Relevant | Voyage |
| **Hull & Machinery Insurance** | Fixed | Indirect | Operating | Expense | Capital | Committed | Time / Capital |
| **Dry-docking expenditure** | Step-fixed | Direct | Operating | Expense | Capital | Sunk (after incurred) | Capital (Time) |
| **Suez Canal dues** | Variable | Direct | Distribution | Expense | Revenue | Relevant | Voyage |
| **Lubricating oil** | Variable (semi) | Direct | Operating | Material | Revenue | Relevant | Voyage (treat as Voyage) |
| **Lighterage at discharge port** | Variable | Direct | Distribution | Service | Revenue | Relevant | Voyage |
| **HO Marketing salary** | Fixed | Indirect | Selling | Labour | Revenue | Controllable | Administrative |
| **Old vessel scrap proceeds (negative cost)** | — | — | — | — | — | Sunk (past) | Not in TCV |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Examiners often give a **5–7-item list of expenditures** and ask students to **classify them on a given basis** (e.g., "Classify as fixed or variable"). The matrix above is the perfect revision template — practice once with each basis.

### 3.3 The "Three-Step Classification Method" (for Short-Answer Questions)

1. **Identify the cost object** (e.g., voyage, vessel, cargo, department).
2. **Choose the classification basis** (behaviour / traceability / function / relevance / TCV).
3. **Apply the test** of that basis (e.g., "Does the total change with nautical miles?" → if yes, *variable*; if no, *fixed*).
